2.1 Introduction
Aromatic compounds are currently used in everyday life. They are used, for
example, as solvents and are components of automotive and aeronautical fuels.
The presence of aromatic compounds in gasoline is desirable because they are
included in the gasoline composition due to their high octane number. Their
percentage in mass in a European gasoline, a Californian reformulated gasoline,
a diesel fuel, and a jet fuel are 35, 31, 30, and 15% respectively.
1 European Union
Directive 2003/17/EC ﬁxed the maximum amount of benzene at 1% in volume in
gasoline and for other aromatic compounds at 35%; in diesel fuel, the amount of
polyaromatic compounds must not exceed 11% by mass.
2 Under certain conditions, combustion systems using fossil fuels can emit aromatic compounds even
if the fuel composition does not include any. Aromatic compounds are at the
RSC Theoretical and Computational Chemistry Series No. 6
Reaction Rate Constant Computations: Theories and Applications
Edited by Keli Han and Tianshu Chu
r The Royal Society of Chemistry 2014
Published by the Royal Society of Chemistry, www.rsc.org
34
 17/11/2013 09:28:31.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00034

## Page 55

origin of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon (PAH) and soot particle formation as
reported in many studies. 3–15 Toxicological studies show that some of these
PAHs (especially benzo(a)pyrene, ﬂuoranthene, and cyclopenta[c,d]pyrene) have
mutagenic and carcinogenic properties.16–18
Useful properties of aromatic compounds and their negative impact on
environment and health have motivated many studies on their oxidation
process. These studies have been mainly devoted to the simplest monoaromatic
compounds, which are benzene and toluene.
19,20 The main objective of these
studies was to identify the formation and/or destruction pathways of aromatic
compounds by means of detailed thermokinetic model validation. Detailed
kinetic mechanisms of these models contain hundreds of elementary reactions
and about a hundred chemical species. The assignation of thermochemical data
to species and kinetic rate constants to reactions is a complicated task due to
the lack of information in the literature. This task is rendered more diﬃcult
because thermokinetic data must be known in large ranges of pressure and
temperature to allow the use of thermokinetic models in conditions encountered in practical combustion systems. For example, the temperature in the
combustion chamber of a spark ignition engine varies from 500 to 2500 K and
the pressure from 1 to about 60 bar. Pressure has an inﬂuence on the rate
constant of reactions exhibiting a fall-oﬀ behaviour and on the rate of reactions
requiring a third body. Temperature inﬂuences almost all reactions and
modiﬁes the oxidation pathways considerably. It is now well demonstrated that
a low-, an intermediate- and a high-temperature oxidation mechanism
exist.
21,22 In automotive and aeronautical engines, the three mechanisms are
implied because of the large range of temperatures encountered.
Many studies have been performed on the development of high-temperature
oxidation thermokinetic models in our laboratory. 19,20,23–26 Some of these
models relate to the formation and/or thermal depletion of benzene 20 and
toluene19 in premixed ﬂames. Low-temperat ure oxidation thermokinetic
models have been also elaborated and concern n-butane,27 n-pentane, 28 pent1-ene,28 n-butylbenzene 29 and cyclohexene. 30 An oxidation thermokinetic
model for methane–benzene and n-heptane–benzene mixtures has been also
built.20 This model has been tested against experimental data obtained at low
and high temperatures and at sub- an d upper-atmospheric pressures. If
benzene does not react at low temper ature as shown in ref. 20, aromatic
compounds with an alkyl side chain (such as toluene, o-xylene and ethylbenzene) exhibit a low-temperature reactivity. 31–33 However, with the exception of n-butylbenzene, 29 only high-temperature oxi dation thermokinetic
models have been published for these aromatic compounds. 34–51 This is not
surprising due to the considerable lack of experimental data on the lowtemperature reactions of aromatic compounds. Determination of thermochemical data and kinetic parameters by means of quantum chemistry tools is
a valuable solution to overcome the absence of experimental data. This
chapter is devoted to the generation of kinetic data for benzylperoxy radical
isomerization using quantum chemistr y methods, statistical thermodynamics
and canonical transition state theory.
Rate Constant Calculation of Benzylperoxy Radical Isomerization 35
 17/11/2013 09:28:31.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00034
View Online

## Page 56

At low temperature, addition of molecular oxygen to aryl radicals
(C6H5 /C0 /C1R) occurs in the oxidation process of alkylbenzenes (C 6H5 /C0 RH):
C6H5 /C0 /C1R þ O2 ¼ C6H5 /C0 R /C1O2 ðR1Þ
This reaction is an equilibrium that is very sensitive to the temperature. It
shifts toward the products C 6H5 /C0 /C1R þ O2 when temperature overcomes the
‘ceiling temperature’, which depends on the structure of the C 6H5 /C0 /C1R and
C6H5 /C0 R /C1O2 radicals and the O 2 partial pressure. 52 Reaction R1 is followed
by an internal H-atom transfer producing a hydroperoxyaryl radical C 6H5/C0/C1QO2H:
C6H5 /C0 R /C1O2 ¼ C6H5 /C0 /C1QO2H ðR2Þ
The internal transfer is easier when the transferred hydrogen atom is
benzylic.29 In the case of toluene, the only possible transfer is a 1,3sb transfer
involving one of the two secondary benzylic hydrogen atoms of benzylperoxy
radical:
C
6H5 /C0 CH2
/C1O2 ¼ C6H5 /C0 /C1CHO2H ðR3Þ
In the notation 1,3sb, number 1 indicates the position of the radical oxygen,
number 3 indicates the relative position of the carbon atom bonded to the
transferred hydrogen atom, the letter s stands for secondary and the letter b for
benzylic. After formation by reaction R3, the 1-hydroperoxybenzyl radical
decomposes to yield benzaldehyde and /C1OH radical by the following reaction:
C
6H5 /C0 /C1CHO2H ¼ C6H5 /C0 CHO þ /C1OH ðR4Þ
Benzaldehyde has been identiﬁed as a major oxidation product of pure
toluene or alkane–toluene mixtures in studies at temperature below 800 K: in a
static reactor between 723 and 788 K,
31 in a jet stirred reactor between 580 and
620 K,32 and in a rapid compression machine at 750 K. 53 The reaction sequence
R1–R3–R4 is often suggested to explain the formation of benzaldehyde in the
low-temperature oxidation mechanism of toluene.
In the reaction sequence R1–R3–R4, reaction R3 is certainly the rate-limiting
step as it involves a strained four-centre cyclic transition state. Thus, a reliable
value for the rate constant of this reaction is needed. In the literature, only one
experimental determination of this rate constant is available.
54 This is a
measurement based on an indirect method using addition of toluene to
mixtures of H2 and O2. The reported rate constant value is 2.8 /C2 103 s/C0 1 at 773 K
under 0.67 bar total pressure. This experimental value will be used to validate
our theoretical methodology for the estimation of the rate constant.
A few theoretical studies have been carried out on the reactions of four-centre
isomerization of the alkylperoxy
55–59 and arylperoxy 60 radicals. Chan et al. 55
36 Chapter 2
 17/11/2013 09:28:31.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00034
View Online

## Page 57

performed BHandHLYP/6-311G(d,p) calculations estimating the activation
energies and pre-exponential factors for a series of intramolecular hydrogenatom abstraction reactions in alkylperoxy radicals containing up to ﬁve
carbons. In particular, the activation energy they calculated was about
209 kJ mol
/C0 1 for the four-centre isomerization reaction of the pentylperoxy
radical. Sheng et al.56 studied the detailed kinetics and thermochemistry for the
reaction between the ethyl radical and molecular oxygen. The isomerization
transition state (TS) of the ethylperoxy adduct (CH 3 /C0 CH2 _O2) leading to
the formation of acetaldehyde (CH 3CHO) and OH radical was characterized
at the B3LYP/6-31G(d,p) level of theory. The energy barrier at 0 K was
estimated to be 174.7 kJ mol
/C0 1 at the CBS-Q//B3LYP/6-31G(d,p) level
of theory. Sun and Bozzelli 57 worked on the reactions of neopentyl and
hydroperoxyneopentyl radicals with molecular oxygen. Part of this study
concerned the four-centre isomerization of the neopentylperoxy radical
(C
4H9 /C0 CH2 _O2) for which the CBS-Q reaction barrier was 174.0 kJ mol /C0 1. Lee
and Bozzelli 58 studied the reaction of the allyl radical with molecular oxygen.
The thermochemical and kinetic parameters were obtained using the CBS-Q
composite method. The energy barrier of the four-centre isomerization was
estimated to be 157.9 kJ mol
/C0 1. Zhu et al. 59 performed theoretical calculations
at the CBS-QB3 level of theory on the kinetics of the intramolecular hydrogen
shift reactions of the butylperoxy (C 3H7 /C0 CH2 _O2) and pentylperoxy
(C4H9 /C0 CH2 _O2) radicals. Both energy barriers at 0 K of the four-centre isomerizations were estimated to be 170.7 kJ mol /C0 1.
It is worth noticing that, in all these aforementioned studies, 56–59 no structure
for the hydroperoxyalkyl radicals was characterized. Clothier et al. 60 worked
on the simulation of diesel fuel ignition by benzyl radicals and showed, using ab
initio molecular orbital calculations, that there is a plausible mechanism by
which benzylperoxy radical thermal decomposition could lead to the production of OH radicals. The calculations were performed at the low level of
theory ROMP2/3-21G//ROHF/3-21G. The energy barrier for the benzylperoxy radical isomerization was calculated to be 121 kJ mol
/C0 1.
A recent theoretical study was performed by Murakami et al.61 to determine
the kinetics, the mechanism, and the product branching ratios of the
benzyl þ O
2 reaction at the CBS-QB3 level of theory. They found that the
reaction proceeded with an exothermic barrierless addition of molecular oxygen to the benzyl radical to form the benzylperoxy radical. The benzylperoxy
radical can: (i) be dissociated in benzyl radical þ O2 with a 93.3 kJ mol/C0 1 energy
barrier (called channel E in their paper); (ii) form the C 6H4CH2OOH radical
with a 135.6 kJ mol/C0 1 energy barrier (channel C); (iii) form the cyclic O 2 adduct
with a 128.0 kJ mol/C0 1 energy barrier (channel D); and (iv) lead to the formation
of benzaldehyde and OH radical with a 161.9 kJ mol /C0 1 energy barrier (channel
B), which is higher than the one predicted by Clothier et al. 60 In the reaction
system of the O 2 addition to the allyl radical, Lee and Bozzelli 58 reported energy barriers for the same channels that were very close to the ones obtained by
Murakami et al.61 The temperature dependence between 300 and 1500 K of the
Rate Constant Calculation of Benzylperoxy Radical Isomerization 37
 17/11/2013 09:28:31.

## Page 58

high-pressure limit rate constant for these reaction channels was computed by
Murakami et al. 61
In our previous study, 62 the rate constant of the benzylperoxy isomerization
reaction was computed using 54 diﬀerent levels of theory and compared to the
literature value reported at 773 K.
54 The aim of this methodology work was to
show that standard theoretical methods were inadequate to obtain quantitative
rate constants for the reaction under study. The use of the elaborated CASPT2
method was essential to estimate a quantitative rate constant. Geometry
optimizations and vibrational frequency calculations were performed using
three diﬀerent methods (B3LYP, MPW1K, and MP2) and six diﬀerent basis
sets [6-31G(d,p), 6-31 þG(d,p), 6-31 þþ G(d,p), 6-311G(d,p), 6-311 þG(d,p)
and cc-pVDZ]. Single-point energy calculations were performed using the
coupled cluster method in the space of single, double, and triple (pertubatively)
electron excitations CCSD(T) using the 6-31G(d,p) basis set and with the
CASPT2 level of theory with the ANOL-VDZP basis set. In this chapter, the
number of levels of theory has been increased to show the inﬂuence of the level
of theory on the calculated rate constant: 69 new levels of theory were used to
complete the ﬁrst study.
Highly correlated quantum chemical calculations were performed to directly
compute the barrier for the reaction R3 without any energy adjustments. The
energetics of the reactant and the TS was used together with transition state
theory (TST) calculations to compute the rate constants in the temperature
range 600–2000 K.
This chapter is organized as follows. Computational methods are reported in
section 2.2, and the results are presented and discussed in section 2.3.
2.2 Computational Methods
Ab initio and DFT calculations were performed using the Gaussian 03 63 and
Molcas 6.0 64 software packages. Reactant and TS structures were fully optimized at HF-DFT (B3LYP), 65,66 MPW1K,67 and MP2 68 levels of theory
using the following 6-31G(d,p), 6-31 þG(d,p), 6-31þþ G(d,p), and 6-311G(d,p)
Pople style basis sets 69 and cc-pVDZ, aug-cc-pVDZ, and cc-pVTZ 70–73
Dunning style basis sets. Product geometries were fully optimized at
MPW1K and MP2 levels of theory with the same seven basis sets. All TSs have
been characterized by one imaginary frequency (ﬁrst-order saddle points) on the
potential energy surface (PES). Special care was taken to determine minimum
energy pathways (MEPs), performing intrinsic reaction coordinate analyses
(IRC)
74 using all levels of theory, to conﬁrm that a speciﬁc TS connects
the diﬀerent local minima. Vibrational frequencies were determined within the
harmonic approximation, at the same level of theory as for geometries. For the
reactant and TS structures, single-point energy calculations were carried out at
diﬀerent high levels of theory using in each case the optimized B3LYP, MPW1K,
and MP2 geometrical parameters. Electron correlation was calculated using
second Møller–Plesset perturbation theory with full annihilation of spin contamination
75–78 as implemented in the Gaussian package (noted in the tables as
38 Chapter 2
 17/11/2013 09:28:31.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00034
View Online

## Page 59

PMP2). Thus, electronic energies were obtained: (i) employing the single and
double coupled cluster theory with inclusion of a perturbative estimation
for triple excitation
79–82 (CCSD(T)) with the 6-311G(d,p), 6-311 þG(d,p),
6-311þþ G(d,p), 6-311 þþ G(3df,2p), and cc-pVTZ basis sets (the frozen-core
approximation has been applied in CCSD(T) calculations, which implies that the
inner shells are excluded at estimating the correlation energy); and (ii)
CASPT283–86 level of theory with the ANO-L-VDZP 87 basis set.
In this work, it is necessary to obtain energy diﬀerences between the TS and
the reactant at a high level of theory such as, for example, CCSD(T) or
CASPT2, in particular to carry out rate constant calculations through TST at a
later stage. The cost of optimizing geometries at these high levels can be very
prohibitive. In these conditions, it is helpful to perform calculations at a higher
level of theory using the geometries optimized at a lower level. The CASPT2
method was carried out to incorporate both dynamic and nondynamic correlation eﬀects on the relative energy ordering of the calculated stationary
points. The CASPT2 approach is based on a second-order perturbation
treatment in which the CASSCF wave function is taken as the reference
function. In this study, CASPT2(3,3) single-point energy calculations were
obtained with the ANO-L-VDZP basis set on the optimized geometries, and
excluding the inner shells and corresponding virtual counterpart from the
perturbation calculation. For the abstraction mechanism, the active space (3,3)
that describes the C–H bond breaking and O–H bond formation includes the
s
C–H bonding molecular orbital (MO) with the associated s*C–H antibonding
MO and the single electron MO.
Canonical TST 88–95 was used to predict the temperature dependence of the
rate constants. Accordingly, the high-pressure limit rate constants, k(T), were
computed using the eqn (2.1):
kðTÞ¼ GðTÞ/C2 kBT
h /C2 QTSðTÞ
Qbenzylperoxy radical ðTÞ /C2 exp /C0 E0
kBT
/C18/C19
ð2:1Þ
where G(T) indicates the transmission coeﬃcient used for the tunnelling correction at temperature T, kB is Boltzmann’s constant and h is Planck’s constant,
and the terms QTS(T) and Qbenzylperoxy radical (T) are the total partition functions
for the TS and the benzylperoxy radical at temperature T, and E0 is the
vibrationally adiabatic barrier height. E0 is computed as the energy diﬀerence
between the TS and the reactant, including zero-point energy corrections.
The calculation of the reaction rate constants using eqn 2.1 requires the
proper computation of the partition functions of reactant and TS. The total
partition function Q
X ðTÞ of a species X can be cast in terms of the translational
Qtrans

Wigner method 96 in the calculation of tunnelling corrections to the rate

2.3 Results and Discussion
2.3.1 Geometric Parameters
Figure 2.1 shows the structure and atom numbering of the reactant (benzylperoxy radical), the isomerization TS, and the product (1-hydroperoxybenzyl
radical). Table 2.1 gathers selected bond lengths of this species calculated at
diﬀerent levels of theory.
2.3.1.1 Benzylperoxy Radical
At all levels of theory, the global minimum for the benzylperoxy radical is
found to be in a conformation in which the dihedral angles C
3C2C1O2 and
C2C1O2O1 are equal to about /C0 901 and 180 1, respectively. The same results
were obtained by Garcia et al.108 using the UHF approximation combined with
the 6-31G(d,p) and 6-31 þG(d,p) basis sets.

## Page 62

It can be observed that the calculated geometric parameters do not vary
much as a function of the level of theory. For example, the O 1–O2 bond lengths
estimated at the B3LYP/6-31G(d,p) and MPW1K/6-311G(d,p) levels of theory
are 1.321 A˚ and 1.285 A˚ , respectively. All the optimized geometric parameters
are consistent to the ones reported by Garcia et al.
108 and Murakami et al. 61
The O 1–O2 and O 2–C1 key bond lengths in B3LYP geometries are slightly
longer than those in MP2 and MPW1K geometries. A similar but not so
marked trend is observed for the C
1–C2 and C 1–H1 bond lengths.
2.3.1.2 Transition State
The TS for isomerization of benzylperoxy to the 1-hydroperoxybenzyl radical is
a four-membered ring. The unpaired electron on the O–O s-bond attacks one
of the C–H s-bonds of the methyl fragment. The two electrons in the C–H
bond become unpaired and one of these pairs with the peroxy function electron
to form the O–H bond in the 1-hydroperoxybenzyl radical. The atoms C
1,H 1,
O1, and O 2 are nearly in the same plane. Structural parameters depend slightly
on the level of theory. The O 1–O2 bond length in the TS is longer than the
O1–O2 bond length in the 1-hydroperoxybenzyl radical, but the H 1–O1 bond in
the TS is shorter than the H 1–O1 bond in the 1-hydroperoxybenzyl radical. The
optimized structures using the B3LYP levels are similar to the one reported by
Murakami et al.
61 at the B3LYP/6-311G(2d,d,p) level of theory.
2.3.1.3 1-Hydroperoxybenzyl Radical
The global minimum is found using the MP2 and MPW1K methods in a
conformation allowing the intramolecular interaction between the oxygen and
hydrogen atoms ( i.e. O
2–H3 and H 2–O1). Geometry optimizations at the
B3LYP levels predict the absence of this stationary point on the potential energy surface. The same results have been observed by Murakami et al.
61 Instead
of the energy minimization, the O 2–O1 bond cleaves to form the molecular
complex C 6H5CHO /C1/C1/C1 OH, although reaction path calculations from the TS
structure indicate that the B3LYP transition state does not connect with the
C
6H5CHO /C1/C1/C1 OH molecular complex. Despite exhaustive searches involving
relaxed scans and reaction path calculations, we were not able to locate a
C6H5
/C1CHO2H structure using the B3LYP method. Theoretical studies on the
four-centre isomerization of some alkylperoxy radicals did not report any
optimized structure for the R /C1CHO2H radical when using the B3LYP
method.56–59 From these results, the B3LYP method is inadequate to obtain
optimized R /C1CHO2H structures.
Bond lengths in MP2 geometries are longer than those in MPW1K geometries
except for C–C bonds in the aromatic ring. 62 The C 1–C2 bond in the
1-hydroperoxybenzyl radical is about 1.406 A˚ , which is far shorter than the MP2/
6-31G(d,p) calculated C–C bond in the benzaldehyde (about 1.478 A˚ 109)a n dt h e
experimental C–C bond in the toluene (about 1.513 A ˚ 110). Diﬀerences in bond
angles and dihedral angles are within 5 1 between MP2 and MPW1K
geometries.62
42 Chapter 2

## Page 63

2.3.2 Energetics
Table 2.2 lists the reaction enthalpies at 0 K, DrH(0 K), computed at diﬀerent
levels of theory. The results obtained with the MPW1K method indicate that
adding diﬀuse functions to the basis sets or increasing the basis set size has a
small eﬀect on the D
rH(0 K) value ( DDrH(0 K) r 2.8 kJ mol/C0 1) with the exception of the MPW1K/cc-pVTZ level of theory. This trend is less important
when using the MP2 method ( DDrH(0 K) o 1.0 kJ mol/C0 1) with the exception of
the MP2/aug-cc-pVDZ level of theory. The reaction is predicted to be endothermic whatever the level of theory. The values of D
rH(0 K) range from 4.9
to 35.6 kJ mol/C0 1 at the MPW1K/cc-pVTZ and CCSD(T)/6-311 þG(d,p)//MP2/
6-31G(d,p) levels of theory, respectively. Increasing the basis set size from
6-311G(d,p) to 6-311 þG(3df,2p) in the single-point calculation changes the
value of D
rH(0 K) by about –6 and –9 kJ mol /C0 1 for the MPW1K and MP2
geometries, respectively. The DrH(0 K) values calculated at the CCSD(T)/
6-311þG(3df,2p) level of theory for the MPW1K geometries are about 18 kJ
mol/C0 1 lower than the value calculated by Clothier et al. 60 at the ROMP2/
3-21G//ROHF/3-21G level of theory (about 33 kJ mol /C0 1), while the values
obtained at the CCSD(T)/6-311 þG(3df,2p) level of theory for the MP2
geometries are about 9 kJ mol /C0 1 lower than that of Clothier et al. 60
Table 2.3 shows the E0 values at diﬀerent levels of theory for the reaction
under study. E0 is calculated using eqn (2.4):
E0 ¼ ETS /C0 ER þ ZPETS /C0 ZPER ð2:4Þ
where ETS and ER are the potential energies of the TS and the reactant, and
ZPETS and ZPER are their corresponding zero-point energy corrections.
The four-centre isomerization has a large E0 value ranging from 137.1 to
238.4 kJ mol/C0 1 at the CASPT2/ANO-L-VDZP//B3LYP/cc-pVDZ and PMP2/
cc-pVTZ levels of theory, respectively. The E0 values calculated with the
B3LYP method are systematically lower by about 14–40 kJ mol /C0 1 than those
obtained with the MPW1K and the PMP2 methods coupled with Pople basis
sets. Larger diﬀerences (26–80 kJ mol
/C0 1) are observed using Dunning basis sets.
Increasing the basis set size in the single-point energy calculation from
6-311G(d,p) to 6-311 þ G(3df,2p) changes the value of E0 by about 5, 5, and
10 kJ mol/C0 1 for the B3LYP, MPW1K, and MP2 geometries, respectively. The
barriers obtained using the CCSD(T) method coupled with the Pople basis sets
are slightly higher than those obtained with the Dunning basis set.
The barriers calculated at the CCSD(T)//B3LYP levels of theory are very
close to the ones computed at the CCSD(T)//MPW1K levels of theory, but are
much lower by about 31–42 kJ mol
/C0 1 than those obtained at the CCSD(T)//
MP2 levels of theory. Similar trends are observed with the CASPT2 method.
The barriers calculated with the CCSD(T) method are signiﬁcantly higher than
those calculated with the CASPT2 method. Increasing the basis set size from 6311G(d,p) to 6-311+G(3df,2p) with CCSD(T) method does not reduce the
diﬀerence with the barriers calculated using CASPT2 method. The E
0 values
computed at the B3LYP levels of theory using Pople basis sets are very close to

## Page 68

the one reported by Murakami et al.61 (161.9 kJ mol/C0 1) at the CBS-QB3 level of
theory. We found also the UMP2 method was unsuitable because of severe spin
contamination for the TS. Values of S(S þ 1) are ranging from 1.21 to 1.25,
instead of 0.75. After spin projection, the S(S þ 1) values are close to 1.10.
Due to the large range of E
0 values obtained with the diﬀerent levels of theory,
it is essential to choose the appropriate level of theory for an accurate rate
constant estimation. In the literature, only one indirect experimental value of the
rate constant at 773 K is available.
54 Thus, the calculation of the rate constants
at 773 K for each level of theory and comparison with the value of Ellis et al.54
is essential to determine this appropriate level of theory (see next section).
2.3.3 Kinetic Parameter Calculations
2.3.3.1 Rate Constants
Table 2.4 lists the calculated high-pressure limit rate constants at 773 K for each
level of theory together with the literature value. The rate constants range from
10/C0 3 to 103 s/C0 1, showing the strong dependence of the rate constant on the level
of theory. As discussed above, the MP2 method is unsuitable due to a severe
spin contamination in the TS. The calculated rate constants using CCSD(T)
energies on the B3LYP and MPW1K density functional geometries are
two or three orders of magnitude lower than the experimental value. Increasing the basis set size from 6-311G(d,p) to 6-311+G(3df,2p) with CCSD(T)
method does not improve signiﬁcantly the agreement with this experimental
value. A very good agreement is obtained between the experimental value of
2.8/C2 10
3 s/C0 1 (obtained at a pressure of 0.67 bar) and our high-pressure limit
calculated values at the CASPT2//B3LYP and CASPT2//MPW1K levels of
theory which range from 6.68 /C2 10
2 to 3.47 /C2 103 s/C0 1. By comparison with the
experimental value, the most appropriate levels of theory are the CASPT2/
ANO-L-VDZP//B3LYP/cc-pVDZ and CASPT2/ANO-L-VDZP//B3LYP/
aug-cc-pVDZ ones; the calculated rate constants at these levels of theory are
2.51/C2 10
3 and 2.93/C2 103 s/C0 1, respectively. Murakami et al.61 calculated the highpressure limit rate constants at six diﬀerent temperatures (300, 500, 700, 1000,
1200 and 1500 K). Using these values, one can derive from a linear regression
the value at 773 K ( k ¼ 1.23/C2 10
1 s/C0 1), which is 200 times lower than the value
calculated using the CASPT2/ANO-L-VDZP//B3LYP/aug-cc-pVDZ level of
theory. To conclude, the CASPT2/ANO-L-VDZP//B3LYP/cc-pVDZ and
CASPT2/ANO-L-VDZP//B3LYP/aug-cc-pVDZ levels of theory are the most
appropriate levels to compute quantitatively the temperature dependence of
the high-pressure limit rate constant for the studied reaction.
2.3.3.2 Arrhenius Parameters
The rate constants k1 and k2 calculated at the CASPT2/ANO-L-VDZP//
B3LYP/cc-pVDZ and CASPT2/ANO-L-VDZP//B3LYP/aug-cc-pVDZ levels
of theory, respectively, have been ﬁtted to a three-parameter Arrhenius
48 Chapter 2

Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00034
View Online

## Page 69

expression by the least-squares method over the temperature range 600–2000 K.
Equations (2.5) and (2.6) were obtained (Table 2.5):
k1ðin s /C0 1Þ¼ð 1:34 /C2 1010ÞT0:79 exp½ð/C0 133:1 in kJ mol /C0 1Þ=RT/C138ð 2:5Þ
k2ðin s /C0 1Þ¼ð 1:85 /C2 1010ÞT0:78 exp½ð/C0 133:9 in kJ mol /C0 1Þ=RT/C138ð 2:6Þ
Given the excellent agreement with the experimental rate constant at 773 K
(see section 3.3.1), eqn (2.5) and eqn (2.6) can be used in oxydation thermokinetic models involving toluene and alkylbenzenes.
2.4 Conclusions
Ab initio and DFT theoretical calculations combined with canonical TST were
performed on the benzylperoxy radical four-centre isomerization reaction. The
geometry parameters for the reactant, the product and the TS were fully optimized at 99 levels of theory with the B3LYP, MPW1K and MP2 methods
combined with seven basis sets. The calculation of reaction energetics proved to
be more dependent on the level of theory than on the nature and extent of the
basis set. The use of the MP2 method is unsuitable because of severe spin
contamination in the TS. The high-pressure limit calculated rate constants at
773 K with a Wigner tunnelling correction using CASPT2//B3LYP and
CASPT2//MPW1K levels of theory are in very good agreement with the value
of Ellis et al.
54 By comparison with this value, the CASPT2/ANO-L-VDZP//
B3LYP/cc-pVDZ and CASPT2/ANO-L-VDZP//B3LYP/aug-cc-pVDZ levels
of theory are the most appropriate ones to calculate accurately the rate constant. Three-parameter Arrhenius expressions of the rate constant have been
calculated using the two former levels of theory. This study has shown that
standard theoretical methods B3LYP, MPW1K, and MP2 as well as elaborated
CCSD(T) methods are not adequate to obtain quantitative rate constants for
the reaction under study. The use of the elaborated CASPT2 method was
crucial to estimate a quantitative rate constant.

CHAPTER 3
Rate Constants and the Kinetic
Isotope Eﬀects in Multi-Proton
Transfer Reactions: A Case
Study of ClONO
2 þ HClHNO3 þ Cl2 Reactions with
Water Clusters with Canonical
Variational Transition State
Theory using a Direct Ab Initio
Dynamics Approach
YONGHO KIM
Department of Chemistry, Kyung Hee University, 1 Seochun-Dong,
Kiheung-Gu, Yongin-Si, Gyeonggi-Do, 446-701, Korea
Email: yhkim@khu.ac.kr
3.1 Introduction
Recently, it has been proposed that the production of photolyzable chlorine
(Cl2) from chlorine nitrate (ClONO 2) and hydrochloric acid (HCl) in
RSC Theoretical and Computational Chemistry Series No. 6
Reaction Rate Constant Computations: Theories and Applications
Edited by Keli Han and Tianshu Chu
r The Royal Society of Chemistry 2014
Published by the Royal Society of Chemistry, www.rsc.org
55
 17/11/2013 09:28:31.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00055

## Page 76

stratospheric clouds is an eﬃcient heterogeneous process involving multiproton transfer that ultimately inﬂuences ozone depletion in the Antarctic
stratosphere.
1–13 Halogen atoms are stored in nearly inert reservoir species
(ClONO2, HCl and HBr) and released into more active forms through several
reactions taking place primarily on the ice surface in polar stratospheric clouds
(PSCs). The amount of Cl and ClO available to participate in the ozonedestroying catalytic reactions is critically dependent on the partitioning of
chlorine between these ‘active’ chlorine radicals and the non-ozone-destroying
‘reservoirs’, HCl and ClONO
2. The rates of formation and destruction of these
reservoir species determine this partitioning. Solomon et al. 10 suggested that
HCl and ClONO 2 might react on the surfaces of PSCs (R1), which leads to
ozone loss in the Antarctic lower stratosphere:
ClONO2 þ HCl ! Cl2 þ HNO3 ðR1Þ
The Cl2 formed would be photolyzed rapidly in sunlight to produce Cl radicals,
which then activate ozone-destroying reactions. Molina et al. 8 and Tolbert
et al. 11 showed experimentally that the reaction of HCl with ClONO 2 indeed
takes place readily on water ice ﬁlms. Hanson and a colleague 14,15 showed that
a portion of the reaction is also occurred by:
ClONO2 þ H2O ! HOCl þ HNO3 ðR2Þ
HOCl þ HCl ! Cl2 þ H2O ðR3Þ
It is useful to note that reaction R1 competes with reaction R2 for the
available ClONO 2 on liquid aerosol surfaces. Thus if HCl has been depleted,
the rate of the latter reaction increases, so that eﬀective heterogeneous activation of chlorine is not dependent upon both HCl and ClONO
2 being present.
Further, the reactions of HCl with HOCl (R3) are also quite eﬃcient on liquid
aerosol under moderately cold and/or wet conditions. ClONO
2 hydrolysis may
proceed via nucleophilic attack at Cl by a lattice water molecule of ice in
concert with proton transfer. 16 It is also possible that Cl /C0 is formed on ice
surfaces, allowing an ion-assisted reaction with ClONO 2.17
Reactions R1, R2 and R3, which are crucial steps in the ﬁnal release of reactive halogen species, are known to occur heterogeneously throughout the
stratosphere, whereas homogeneous gas-phase reactions are considered to be
relatively unimportant. Although many experimental and theoretical studies
have been performed on these reactions,
14,16–31 the detailed mechanism for the
direct activation of ClONO 2 þ HCl or ClONO 2 hydrolysis is not fully understood.1,18,21,27 McNamara et al. 26 investigated the reaction of ClONO 2 with
HCl in water clusters using ab initio methods. They reported that the reaction
barrier was lowered systematically by adding water molecules to the reaction.
The inclusion of these so-called structural or spectator water molecules signiﬁcantly lowered the barrier down to a nearly barrier-less situation. They also
suggested that the reaction of ClONO
2 with HCl on ice can proceed by an ionic
mechanism. Bianco and Hynes 19 reported the reaction path of ClONO 2 þ HCl
in water clusters at the HF level and the energy proﬁle at the MP2 level along
56 Chapter 3
 17/11/2013 09:28:31.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00055
View Online

## Page 77

the HF path. They suggested that H/D isotope eﬀects would help further clarify
the reaction mechanism. 18,19 Recently Liedl and colleagues have calculated
reaction rates for R3, supported by n ¼ 0, 1, 2, and 3 water molecules, using a
variational transition state theory including tunneling contribution. 32 They
reported that an H 2OCl1Cl– like structure is generated at the transition state
(TS) of HOCl þ HCl. No reaction dynamics calculations have been performed
for reactions (R1) and (R3).
Recently, it was found that the surface layers of ice might be disordered by
hydrocarbon contamination 33 or HCl traces. 34 Molina35 ﬁrst postulated the
liquid-like ice surface of type II PSCs at stratospheric temperatures to explain
HCl adsorption, which was supported experimentally by a zeroth-order rate
dependence on HCl partial pressure.
36 Further support comes from molecular
dynamics simulations showing that the water molecules on the ice surface are
substantially disordered even at 200 K, 37 and the dynamics of water molecules
in the uppermost bilayer of hexagonal ice is substantially faster than in the
bulk
38 leading to partial disorder. 39 Therefore, small water clusters can be used
to model the reactions occurring on type II PSCs. Both the nature of the type II
PSCs and the detailed mechanisms of reactions within them have been the
subjects of many studies. Whereas the reactions are reasonably well understood
from a phenomenological point of view, there is still a vast amount of uncertainty in the microscopic view of the reaction mechanism.
In the heterogeneous reactions (R1, R2 and R3) in the type II PSCs, multiproton transfers via hydrogen-bonded lattice water molecules play a very important role in the detailed reaction mechanism. Proton transfer is one of the
simplest and the most fundamental reactions in chemistry and biology and has
been studied extensively.
40,41 The majority of studies on proton transfer have
been carried out for a single proton transfer, in which one proton is transferred
during the reaction. However, multi-proton transfers, where more than one
proton is transferred either stepwise or concertedly, have not been studied as
extensively. The dynamics of multi-proton transfer is more complicated than
that of a single proton transfer and the detailed underlying mechanisms are not
yet fully understood. Examples of multi-proton transfer include proton relay
systems in enzymes, proton transfers in hydrogen-bonded water complexes and
prototropic tautomerisms in nucleic acid base pairs. Limbach and colleagues
studied double proton transfers in prototropic tautomerisms for many formamidine systems and porphyrins using the dynamic nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) technique.
42–48 They reported rates and kinetic isotope eﬀects
(KIEs) for both concerted 43,49 and stepwise 45–47 double proton transfer.
One of the most important issues for multi-proton transfer reactions is their
mechanism: do they occur stepwise or concertedly? Schowen 50 highlighted this
issue with four examples: mutarotation of glucose with 2-pyridone; 51 tautomerization of porphyrin molecules; 42 alcohol-mediated double proton transfer
in 7-azaindole; 52 and isomerization in 7-azaindole dimer. 53 However, in the
7-azaindole dimer, the mechanism of the excited-state double proton transfer
has not been fully resolved, even though careful experimental and quantum
mechanical studies have been performed.
54–57 The excited-state reaction
Rate Constants and the Kinetic Isotope Eﬀects in Multi-Proton Transfer Reactions 57
 17/11/2013 09:28:31.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00055
View Online

## Page 78

dynamics calculation, although not entirely simple, would help inform the
current understanding of the mechanism. Evidence of concerted vs. stepwise
pathways for these reactions is often manifested through the proton inventory
and the rule of the geometric mean (RGM)
43,44,49,52,58–60 for the KIEs
(kHD ¼ (kHHkDD)1/2, i.e. k HH/kHDEkHD/kDD).
The concertedness and synchronicity of multi-proton transfer processes are
based on the minimum potential energy surfaces along the reaction coordinates. Detailed information on the potential energy surface is necessary. Many
theoretical studies with ab initio quantum chemical methods at various levels
have been carried out to predict the potential energy surface for various multiproton transfer processes.
61–74 Because the focus of most of the earlier studies
has been on the geometrical change and energetic information in the hydrogenbonded complexes, the dynamic features of the multi-proton transfer, such as
the synchronicity of bond breaking and formation as well as the tunneling eﬀect
of isotopic substitution, are not very well understood. In particular, there is
little theoretical work on the dynamics of multi-proton transfer.
68,75–83 In this
chapter, we focus on the multi-proton transfer in reaction R1 within water
complexes as a model for a heterogeneous reaction in the type II PSCs.
84 The
reaction path, rate constants and kinetic isotope eﬀects are described to clarify
the underlying mechanisms. The breakdown of the rule of the RGM for the
concerted multi-proton transfer is also discussed.
3.2 Computational Methods
The results of the rate calculations depend greatly on the quality of the
potential energy surfaces. The shape and the height of potential energy barrier for
proton transfer reactions are very sensitive to the size of the basis sets used and
the inclusion of the electron correlation in the electronic structure calculations.
Electronic structure calculations were performed at the MP2 level by using the
Gaussian 03 quantum mechanical package.
85 Geometries for reactants, products
a n dt r a n s i t i o ns t a t e so fC l O N O2 þ HCl þ (H2O)n-HNO3 þ Cl2 þ (H2O)n
reactions with n ¼ 0, 1 and 2 were optimized at the MP2 level of theory using
the 6-31G(d,p) and 6-31 þ G(d,p) basis sets, and frequencies were calculated at
the same level to ensure the optimized structures. Single-point energy calculations were also performed at the MP2/6-311 þ G(3df,3pd) level for stationary
points. Rate calculations were carried out by direct ab initio reaction dynamics
with variational transition state theory using interpolated single point energies
(ISPEs) along the minimum energy path (MEP). The MEP was initially
obtained at the MP2/6-31G(d,p) level, and six or seven high level points along
the MEP were calculated at the MP2/6-311 þþ G(3df,3pd) level so that the
interpolation could generate the needed point of a high-level potential energy
surface for the rate calculations. The rate constants were calculated using
GAUSSRATE,
86 which is an interface of POLYRATE 87 with Gaussian 03.
The reaction rates were calculated using variational transition state theory,
which includes a multidimensional tunneling approximation. 88–90 The Born–
Oppenheimer potential on the MEP is called VMEP(s), where s is the reaction

1/kBT, kB is the Boltzmann constant, h is Planck’s constant, sCVT
/C3 is the value of
s at which kGT is minimum (that is, the location of the canonical variational
transition state), s is the symmetry factor, and QGT and FR are partition
functions for the generalized transition state (GTS) and reactants, respectively.
To include the tunneling eﬀect, the calculated rate constant k
CVT(T) is multiplied by a transmission coeﬃcient, kSCT:
kCVT = SCT TðÞ ¼ kSCT TðÞ kCVT TðÞ ð 3:2Þ
The transmission coeﬃcient is deﬁned as the ratio of the thermally averaged
quantal ground-state transmission probability, P(E), to the thermally averaged
classical transmission probability for the eﬀective potential along the reaction
coordinate. When the reaction path curvature is small, tunneling is assumed to
occur on a path deﬁned by the classical turning points on the concave side of
the MEP. This is an example of corner-cutting tunneling.
90–92 The centrifugaldominant small-curvature semiclassical adiabatic ground state (CD-SCSAG)
tunneling approximation
90–92 is used to calculate P(E). The CD-SCSAG
method is referred to as ‘small-curvature tunneling’ (SCT).
3.3 Results and Discussion
3.3.1 Electronic Structures at the Stationary Points
Some geometrical parameters at the stationary structures for ClONO 2 þ HCl
calculated at the MP2 level are shown in Figure 3.1 along with the B3LYP level
structures from literature.
26 The B3LYP level predicted slightly longer Cl–Cl
distance at the transition state compared with the MP2 level structure. The
post-HF structures of hydrated complexes and the transition states of the
multi-proton transfer in ClONO
2 þ HCl þ H2O, n ¼ 0B3 reactions were reported by McNamara et al. 26 There are no major diﬀerences in the geometric
parameters of this system depending on the level of theory used in this work.
The structures of the stationary points for ClONO 2 þ HCl þ H2O are illustrated in Figure 3.2. The geometric parameters for the MP2 structures agree
very well with each other. The B3LYP level predicted slightly shorter and larger
distances for
2Cl–1H and 1O–1H at the transition state, respectively, compared
with corresponding MP2 values. It is interesting to note that the 1O–1H and
1O–2H distances at the transition state are very short. The 1O–1H bond is nearly
formed but the 1O–2H bond is rarely broken, indicating that the two protons,
1H and 2H, move very asynchronously to result in an H 3O1 like moiety at the
transition state.

## Page 80

The structures for the stationary points of ClONO 2 þ HCl þ 2H2O are
illustrated in Figure 3.3. There are some geometrical diﬀerences between MP2
and B3LYP structures, particularly at the transition state. The
2Cl–1H and
2O–1H distances at the MP2 level are longer and shorter than those at the
B3LYP level, respectively, meaning that the 1H hydrogen is much closer to the
2O atom at the MP2 level. The distance between 2O and 3O atoms at the MP2
level is 0.2 A˚ shorter than at the B3LYP level. The 3H hydrogen at the B3LYP
level is nearly bonded to the 2O atom, while the 3H hydrogen at the MP2 level is
nearly in the middle of the 2O and 3O atoms. As shown in Figure 3.3, the 1O–2H
bond distance is not signiﬁcantly changed at the transition state. At the transition state, the
1H hydrogen was transferred all the way to the 2O atom,
while the 2H hydrogen moved very little, indicating that the motion of these
two hydrogen atoms is very asynchronous. It is interesting to note that the
Figure 3.1 Structures at stationary points of the ClONO 2 þ HCl reaction.
Figure 3.2 Structures at stationary points of the ClONO 2 þ HCl þ H2O reaction.

 17/11/2013 09:28:31.

## Page 81

partial structure of the transition state, consisting of two oxygen and ﬁve
hydrogen atoms, is quite similar to the structure of the Zundel cation, 93–95
H5O2
1.
Natural bond orders and partial charges for stationary points calculated at
the MP2/6-311 þþ G(3df,3pd)//MP2/6-31G(d,p) level are listed in Tables 3.1
and 3.2. For the ClONO 2 þ HCl reaction, the bond order sum of 1Cl–1H and
1H–bO involving transferring hydrogen 1H and that of aO /C0 1Cl and 1Cl-2Cl
involving the chlorine atom ( 1Cl) at the reactants are 0.94 and 0.92, respectively. These values shift to 0.74 and 0.88 at the transition state, indicating that
the transition state is slightly loose. A loose transition state generally induces
larger charge separation, thus the partial charges for 2Cl and 1H become
more negative and positive, respectively, as shown in Table 3.2. For the
ClONO
2 þ HCl þ H2O reaction, the bond order sums of bond pairs connecting
the transferring hydrogen atoms, 1H and 2H, are 0.92 and 0.76 at the reactants
and 0.70 and 0.65 at the TS, respectively, indicating a loose transition state. The
bond orders of
1O–1Ha n d 1O–2H were observed to be much larger than those
of 1H–2C and 2H–bO, respectively, which is consistent with the formation of a
H3O1-like moiety at the transition state. The sum of the partial charges for the
H3O1-like moiety is 0.78. There are three transferring hydrogen atoms in the
ClONO2 þ HCl þ 2H2O reaction. The bond order sums for bond pairs connecting the transferring hydrogen atoms ( 1H, 3H and 2H) are changed from
0.91, 0.73 and 0.76 at the reactant to 0.72, 0.69 and 0.68 at the transition state,
respectively, indicating a slightly loose transition state structure. The bond
orders of
2O–1H and 1O–2H are much larger than those of 2Cl–1H and bO–2H,
respectively, whereas the 2O–3Ha n d 1O–3H bond orders are quite similar to
one another. This is consistent with the formation of a Zundel cation inside the
transition state. The partial charge of the H 5O2
1 like moiety is 0.85. The formation of the Zundel cation at the transition state region had previously been
reported in the hydrated proton transfer in liquid water.

## Page 82

3.3.2 Reaction Dynamics of Multi-Proton Transfers
Potential energy barriers and reaction energies of ClONO 2 þ HCl reactions are
listed according to the number of water molecules in Table 3.3. We observed
that the barrier heights at the MP2 level depend very little on the size of basis
sets when there is no water molecule involved ( n ¼ 0). HF and B3LYP levels
predict higher and lower barrier heights, respectively, compared with the MP2
barriers. However, the reaction energies at the MP2 level depend on the basis
sets; the larger the basis sets the lower the reaction energies. The HF level
predicts the lowest reaction energy of –24.8 kcal mol
/C0 1. McNamara et al. 26
reported on the catalytic eﬀect of water molecule additions to the reaction and
our results are consistent with theirs. The B3LYP level predicted almost no
barrier for the two water molecule reactions. The single-point MP2/
6-311þþ G(3df,3pd) level calculations using MP2 level structures predict a
slightly higher barrier than calculations using the B3LYP level structures,
probably due to the diﬀerences in the geometrical parameters of the transition
states, as discussed previously.
The MP2/6-31G(d,p) level was used to calculate the MEPs for the reactions,
ClONO
2 þ HCl þ nH2O, n ¼ 0, 1, 2. Because the barrier heights at the MP2/
6-31G(d,p) level were higher for the reactions where n ¼ 1 and 2 than those at
the MP2/6-311þþ G(3df,3pd) level, the potential energy curves along the MEP

were further corrected by using MP2/6-311 þþ G(3df,3pd) level calculations for

87 Potential
and vibrationally adiabatic ground-state energies (the sum of potential energy
and zero-point vibrational energies) as well as selected bond distances along the
MEP for the ClONO
2 þ HCl reaction are illustrated in Figure 3.4. The
hydrogenic motion occurs primarily on the reactant side, with almost no
change in the
bO/C0 1H distance when s is larger than approximately 0.5 bohr.
The potential and adiabatic energies as well as selected bond distances along
the MEP for the ClONO 2 þ HCl þ H2O reaction are illustrated in Figure 3.5.
The potential curve near the transition state is very ﬂat with almost no energy
change between s ¼ –1.8 and 1.3 bohr. Two protons are moving in this reaction.
The ﬁrst proton,
1H, moves rapidly on the reactant side around s ¼ –2.0 bohr,
with the transfer near completion at the transition state. In contrast, the second
proton,
2H, moves very little at the transition state and its transfer occurs primarily around s ¼ 1.4 bohr, indicating that the two protons are transferred very
asynchronously. It is very interesting to note that the ﬂat region of the potential
surface begins approximately where the ﬁrst proton transfer is nearly complete
Figure 3.4 Potential and vibrationally adiabatic ground-state energies for the
ClONO2 þ HCl reaction and selected bond distances along the MEP.

## Page 85

and ends where the second proton transfer is about to begin. Therefore, in the
ﬂat region of the potential energy surface, the hydrogenic motion is minimal and
the energetic contributions created by
1O–1H bond shortening and 1O–2Hb o n d
lengthening are approximately canceled out. We also note that the hydrogenic
motions start and end quickly within a short range of the reaction coordinate but
the heavy atoms, such as chlorine, move slowly all along the reaction coordinate,
suggesting separate (asynchronous) motion between light and heavy atoms.
Potential and adiabatic energies as well as selected bond distances along
the MEP for the ClONO
2 þ HCl þ 2H2O reaction are illustrated in Figure 3.6

are transferred in this reaction. The ﬁrst proton ( 1H) is transferred primarily in
a region where s is smaller than –2.8 bohr, while the second proton ( 2H) is
transferred primarily in a region around s ¼ 2.5 bohr. The third proton ( 3H)
is transferred near the saddle point. We note that the hydrogenic motions in

3.3.3 Rate Constants, Tunneling and Kinetic Isotope Eﬀects
The rate constants, tunneling coeﬃcients and KIEs for the ClONO 2 þ HCl
reaction are listed in Table 3.4. At 193 K, which is a temperature in the
stratospheric clouds, the rate constant is 2.9 /C2 10
–49 s–1, which is very small
compared with experimental values 20,36 due to the high reaction barrier. There
is large amount of tunneling involved in the proton and deuterium transfers.
The KIE including tunneling is 19 at 193 K.
The rate constants and tunneling coeﬃcients for proton and deuterium
transfers in the ClONO
2 þ HCl þ H2O reaction are listed in Table 3.5 and the
KIEs are listed in Table 3.6. The rate constant for a double proton transfer
Figure 3.6 Potential and vibrationally adiabatic ground-state energies for the
ClONO2 þ HCl þ 2H2O reaction and selected bond distances along the
MEP. High level energies for seven non-stationary points ( s ¼/C0 2.8, /C0 2.0,
/C0 1.0, 0.7, 1.2, 1.85, 2.8 bohr) calculated at the MP2/6-311 þþ G(3df,3pd)
level were used for interpolation.

 17/11/2013 09:28:31.

## Page 87

including tunneling, k(HH)CVT/SCT, is 1.59 /C2 10–4 s–1 at 193 K. The quasiclassical HH/DH and HD/DD KIEs are smaller than 1, indicating an inverse
KIE. In general, the force constants of isotopically sensitive vibrational modes
at the transition state are smaller than those at the reactants, which gives
normal (larger than 1) KIEs. The inverse primary KIE appears when isotopically sensitive force constants of the transition state are larger than those of
the reactants. At the transition state, the ﬁrst proton (
1H) is nearly transferred
to 1O, which is initially attached to 2Cl at the reactant. Therefore the 2Cl–1H
stretching force constant of the reactant transitions into the 1O–1H stretching
of the transition state with a larger force constant, thus making the HH/DH
and HD/DD KIEs smaller than 1. The force constants associated with the
second proton (
2H) become smaller at the transition state, giving a normal HH/
HD KIE (larger than 1). The small quasiclassical HH/HD KIE is consistent
with a small change in the
1O–2H bond length at the transition state, which
leads to small force constant changes. Because the inverse contribution of the
force constant changes to the KIE from the ﬁrst proton ( 1H) is larger than the
normal contribution from the second proton ( 2H), the overall HH/DD KIEs
remain at less than 1. The tunneling coeﬃcient is very small (nearly 1) due to the
ﬂat potential energy surface.

in the ClONO
2 þ HCl þ 2H2O reaction are listed in Table 3.7, while KIEs are
listed in Table 3.8. The tunneling coeﬃcients are not large, even at very low

temperatures, due to ﬂat potential energy surfaces. It is interesting to note that
the k(HHH)SCT values are smaller than k(HHD)SCT values, which means that
tunneling is more eﬃcient when the third proton ( 3H) is substituted to deuterium. There are in fact some cases where deuterium tunnels more than protium when the barriers are small and ﬂat. 98–100 The rate constant is 1.61 /C2 103 s–1
at 193 K, which agrees very well with experimental results. 20,36 Chu et al. 20
suggested that the direct reaction between ClONO 2 and HCl could be more
important than a two-step mechanism in which reaction R2 is followed by
reaction R3. Agreement between experimental and theoretical rate constants
suggests that the reaction between ClONO
2 and HCl occurs concertedly via
asynchronous multiple proton transfers through hydrogen-bonded water
molecules between them.
3.3.4 Breakdown of the Rule of Geometric Mean
One of the underlying assumptions of this rule is that changes in the
force constants of isotopically sensi tive bonds at the transition state that

## Page 89

are broken or formed during the concer ted reaction are approximately the
same. This assumption is generally good when the two reactive sites are
chemically equivalent and the secondary kinetic isotope eﬀect is negligible.
The most well-known breakdown of the RGM for a concerted multi-proton
transfer is due to tunneling.
44,49,60,101,102 However, in some cases, the
RGM breaks down due to violation of the basic assumption ( i.e. the force
constant changes at the transition state are not the same among the isotopically sensitive bonds). In a synchronous concerted double-bond reaction,
two bond-breaking processes take p lace at the same time, as do the bondforming processes. However, multi- bond reactions do not necessarily take
place synchronously. One can consider se veral diﬀerent types of concerted
multi-proton transfer pr ocesses (Scheme 3.1).
In a concerted synchronous (CS) double proton transfer, the breaking of
A–H and A*–H bonds occurs in unison, along with the bond-forming of B–H
and B*–H. In this process, the sum of the bond orders of A–H and H–B* is not
necessarily conserved. If the A and A* atoms are the same, and if the B and B*
atoms are the same, the transition state of the CS process will have C
2 or
approximately C2 symmetry, at least locally. In a concerted asynchronous (CA)
process, the breaking of A–H and A*–H bonds and the forming of B–H and
B*–H bonds do not occur in unison. At the transition state of the CA1 process,
the bond breaking of A–H is nearly complete, but the bond breaking of the
A*–H has rarely hardly begun. However, B*–H bond formation is also nearly

Rate Constants and the Kinetic Isotope Eﬀects in Multi-Proton Transfer Reactions 69
 17/11/2013 09:28:31.

## Page 90

complete but the B–H bond is not yet formed. The transition state of this
process will have an ion-pair character. The CA2 process is the same as the
CA1 process with the exception of the bond-breaking and bond-making order.
When the A, A*, B and B* atoms are all the same, the transition state will show
approximately C
2v symmetry. This type of transition state structure was predicted for the double proton transfer of a formamidine dimer in a polar
solution.
67
In general, when the reaction goes from reactants to the transition state, the
changes in the force constants of A–H and A*–H (and B–H and B*–H) are
approximately the same for the CS process, but not for the CA processes. As a
result, it is clear that the RGM will generally work for the CS process, but not
for the asynchronous CA1 and CA2 processes. In an extreme case where the
changes in the force constants of A–H and A*–H are very diﬀerent (when two
reactive sites are not chemically equivalent), the RGM may not necessarily
work even for the CS process. In contrast, if the absolute changes in the force
constants for A*–H and B*–H (and A–H and B–H) are approximately the
same in the CA1 process (as shown in the transition state of the formamidine

## Page 91

dimer in a polar solution), 67 the RGM will be valid even when bond-breaking
and bond-making processes are not synchronized. Therefore, the RGM will
work in a special concerted process where the changes in the force constants for
two reactive sites are approximately the same, regardless of their synchronicity.
If the changes in the force constants are not the same and the multi-proton
transfer is asynchronous, the RGM will not necessarily hold even for the
concerted process. Therefore, the breakdown of the RGM may not be a sufﬁcient evidence for the stepwise mechanism.

Rate Constants and the Kinetic Isotope Eﬀects in Multi-Proton Transfer Reactions 71

## Page 92

In the multi-proton transfer of ClONO 2 þ HCl þ H2O reaction, the values of
k(HH)/k(HD) are very diﬀerent from those of k(HD)/k(DD (Table 3.6), and
the [ k(HH)/k(DH)]2 value is much smaller while the [ k(HH)/k(HD)]2 value is
much larger than the k(HH)/k(DD) values, indicating the breakdown of the
RGM for KIEs in the concerted double proton transfer. The breakdown originates primarily from the zero-point energy change at the transition state as a
result of asynchronicity during the double proton transfer and the chemically
non-equivalent reactive sites. This result is not surprising since an underlying
assumption for the RGM is that the force constant changes of two transferring
protons between transition state and reactants are approximately the same for
the concerted mechanism. As mentioned previously the change in the bond
distances and force constants at the transition state for two protons in ﬂight are
very diﬀerent from one another.
Three protons are moving in the multi-proton transfer of ClONO
2 þ
HCl þ 2H2O reaction. There are many possible isotopomers, but we have
presented only the rate constants for the isotopomers without including the
substitution of the ﬁrst proton (
1H) in Figure 3.3. In this case, the initial force
constants of two protons attached to oxygen atoms at the reactant would be
quite similar since the two reactive sites are chemically equivalent. This analysis
will determine if breakdown of the RGM occurs only when the initial force
constants of the two protons in the reactive sites are very diﬀerent. As shown in
Table 3.8, the values of k(HHH)/k(HHD) diﬀer signiﬁcantly from those of
k(HHD)/k(HDD), which indicates again the breakdown of the RGM. The
inﬂuence of tunneling on the KIEs is negligible. Consequently, the breakdown
of the RGM in this case originates solely from the asynchronicity of proton
transfers, regardless of the initial force constants of the hydrogenic bonds.
3.4 Conclusions
This study analyzed the eﬀects of multi-proton transfer in ClONO 2 þ HClHNO3 þ Cl2 reactions on water clusters as a model of the reactions taking place
in the stratospheric clouds. The computed rate constant of the reaction with
two water molecules is 1.6 /C2 10
3 s–1 at 193 K, which agrees very well with experimental values. The agreement between experimental and theoretical rate
constants suggests that the reaction between ClONO
2 and HCl in the PSCs
occurs concertedly by asynchronous multiple proton transfers through the
hydrogen-bonded water molecules between them. The potential energy curve
near the saddle point is very ﬂat, suggesting that the tunneling eﬀect on the
multi-proton transfer in the PSCs is negligible. The primary HH/DH KIEs in
the ClONO
2 þ HCl þ H2O reaction are less than 1 as a result of the larger
hydrogenic force constants at the transition state than at the reactant. This
study revealed that the RGM, which has been used for many years as an experimental criterion for the concerted mechanism, is not valid for highly
asynchronous multiple proton transfers. This breakdown is no surprise since
the underlying assumption in the rule of the geometric mean suggests that the
force constant changes of two transferring protons are approximately the same
72 Chapter 3
 17/11/2013 09:28:31.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00055
View Online

## Page 93

for the concerted mechanism, but they are not always the same, particularly in
cases where the concerted proton transfers are highly asynchronous.

4.1 Introduction
4.1.1 Cluster Physics and Chemistry
As fundamental precursors to the currently booming ﬁeld of nanotechnology,
atomic and molecular clusters have been studied intensively for themselves for
more than three decades.
1 Owing to their very speciﬁc size-dependent properties lying between those of the atom and those of bulk matter, 2 clusters are
relevant in many areas of science and technology. The enhanced magnetic
moments of some metal clusters
3 could have some applications in high-density
storage materials, their high surface to volume ratio is exploited in catalysis 4
and their tunable optical properties make them good candidates as communication devices
5 and even biosensors. 6 Weakly bound clusters are often
considered as ideal media to investigate fundamental quantum processes
involved in energy redistribution or transfer processes.
7 Hydrogen-bonded and
RSC Theoretical and Computational Chemistry Series No. 6
Reaction Rate Constant Computations: Theories and Applications
Edited by Keli Han and Tianshu Chu
r The Royal Society of Chemistry 2014
Published by the Royal Society of Chemistry, www.rsc.org
77
 01/12/2013 15:15:52.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00077

## Page 98

particularly aqueous clusters are relevant to study the emergence of condensed
phase behavior as more solvent molecules are added. 8 Clusters also play a
central role in atmospheric nucleation 9–11 and are regarded as promising candidates for building new self-assembled inorganic materials. 12
Experimental studies of clusters in the gas phase are often carried out using
time-of-ﬂight apparatus, thus allowing mass selection of charged species. 13–15
Although sources and detection devices vary from a research group to another,
the basic principle of a cluster experiment consists of producing clusters with a
given size, exciting them in a speciﬁc way, and monitoring the changes resulting
from this excitation. Fragmentation of the cluster, as detected by variations in
the mass spectra, is the very basic mechanism signaling that excitation was
successful. The occurrence of fragmentation, and its characterization on a
quantitative footing in terms of a probability given the experimental conditions, provided considerable information about the stability of the cluster, its
dissociation energy or other properties such as temperature. In the absence of
atomistic modeling, it is still possible to use purely statistical arguments and
simpliﬁed rate theories to relate the abundances in mass spectra to eﬀective
dissociation rate constants—and ultimately to dissociation energies—for
fragmentation channels that are well-identiﬁed beforehand.
16,17 Except for
homogeneous clusters, however, it is not usually straightforward to even
predict these channels and how they compete with each other depending on
internal energy. Information at the molecular level, possibly completed with
explicit simulations of the dissociation process, is often essential to understand
the mechanisms by which energy is deposited and redistributed in the system
and eventually leads to its fragmentation.
For the theoretician, clusters are also convenient model systems to evaluate
the performance of dissociation rate theories. By comparing the results of
numerically exact molecular dynamics (MD) trajectories to the predictions of
rate theories, the various approximations inherent to these theories can
be unambiguously tested and possibly improved upon. Previous authors
18,19
have critically discussed how the Rice–Ramsperger–Kassel (RRK), 20–22
Weisskopf,23 and Phase Space Theory of Light and Pechukas, 24,25 Nikitin,26,27
Klots,28 Chesnavich and Bowers 29,30 respectively compare for the thermal
evaporation of atomic clusters. This work was subsequently extended by the
present authors to rotating
31–33 and molecular34 clusters. From these eﬀorts it
was concluded that phase space theory (PST), in its orbiting transition state
version, was quantitatively able to describe statistical dissociation. This chapter
is not devoted to a detailed presentation of phase space theory and the reader is
encouraged to consult the cited work.
4.1.2 Dissociation as a Multiscale Process
In time-of-ﬂight experiments, the charged clusters travel across an electric
ﬁeld region over times that exceed microseconds, being thus macroscopic
with respect to the vibrational time scale of the picosecond. Ion storage
devices can reach even longer time scales of a fraction of second. However,
78 Chapter 4
 01/12/2013 15:15:52.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00077
View Online

## Page 99

excitation generally takes place on a very brief time scale, ranging from femtoseconds (intense lasers, collisions with highly charged ions) to nanoseconds
(pulsed lasers, softer collisions) or even longer. The time evolution of the
cluster between its excitation and the detection of its possible dissociation
products is thus fundamentally a time multiscale process, as depicted in
Figure 4.1.
Because of these multiple scales, several descriptions must be adopted to
cover each of the successive processes occurring after excitation. At the shortest
times, and owing to non-adiabatic couplings, energy redistribution between the
electronic degrees of freedom and nuclear motion turns out to be critical in
determining the possible fragmentation on excited states. This is most likely to
occur in small systems having dissociative excited state energy curves. Alternatively, and except for long-lived excitations, the non-adiabatic dynamics
converts the largest portion, if not all, of electronic energy into nuclear kinetic
energy, thereby heating the system by a signiﬁcant amount and on a short
period. The time needed for this hot cluster to thermally dissociate depends on
its internal energy, the dissociation energy but also and as importantly on its
number of degrees of freedom. If the thermal energy is much higher than the
binding energy, fragmentation will be fast and intense, possibly leading to
multiple products within vibrational periods. MD simulations can in principle
describe such events but with some care for statistics. After this conversion of
(vibrational) thermal energy into (translational) kinetic energies, the products
are colder but still able to emit particles over longer times through evaporative
cooling or, over even longer times, by radiative cooling through infrared
photon emission. MD simulations become then of limited use, because evaporation is a rare event taking place during the time-of-ﬂight, beyond the range
of nanoseconds accessible to MD. This is when kinetic rate theories become the
method of choice as they do not present any upper limitation in the time range
they can cover.
Bridging the gap between the production and detection times thus
requires using various computational strategies and connecting them carefully
in suitable time domains. This chapter aims to present the time multiscale
modeling approach
35 in more details in the context of atomic and molecular
Figure 4.1 Series of events taking place in a typical gas-phase experiment on atomic
and molecular clusters, highlighting the various timescales involved from
the excitation stage to the relaxation and fragmentation stages.
Statisticodynamical and Multiscale Modeling of Cluster Dissociation 79

Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00077

## Page 100

clusters. After giving a brief description of the individual methods employed
for each speciﬁc time scale, we emphasize the various connections between
them and highlight the central role of molecular dynamics as the way of providing most of the ingredients required for a quantitative application of rate
theories.
4.2 Time Multiscale Modeling
4.2.1 Basic Principles
The time multiscale approach 35 consists of separating the timescales of the
various processes taking place between excitation and detection. This strategy
is of course relevant in situations where these processes are well-deﬁned and
consecutive to each other, as it assumes that the energy deposited in the system
ﬂows from the electronic degrees of freedom to the nuclei, before being dissipated into direct (non-statistical) fragmentation and ﬁnally into slower (statistical) evaporative cooling. The non-adiabatic dynamics is stopped naturally
when the system has reached its ground electronic state, at which stage the
trajectory continues adiabatically in the Born–Oppenheimer approximation.
A correct modeling of internal conversion requires computational methods
such as trajectory surface hopping (TSH),
36 which treat properly the excited
state surfaces and their couplings without introducing any mixing as the meanﬁeld (Ehrenfest) approach does. 37
Because the excitation energy converted into nuclear kinetic energy can be
high, it is useful to propagate the adiabatic trajectory over several picoseconds,
during which several fragmentation events may possibly take place. After some
time, the energy dissipated by these processes is suﬃcient for the remaining
products to have a much lower thermal energy. Over microseconds, evaporative cooling can then be described by applying a statistical theory with carefully
calculated rates. At even longer times, additional processes such as radiative
emission should be considered.
As mentioned above, dissociations into excited electronic states are not
directly prone to such modeling, although the evolution of the fragmentation
products may well be so. Simulating violent fragmentation events that are likely
to happen at high excitation energies is also not practical because the time
needed for the products to cool down suﬃciently and reach the statistical regime could be long, hampering molecular simulations on a statistical basis.
Another limitation to time multiscale modeling lies in the requirement that the
system under study is prone to simulation. Electronic excited states and
their couplings are usually not available except for speciﬁc compounds, and
non-adiabatic molecular dynamics remains a nontrivial computational
eﬀort, although some general schemes combining time-dependent density
functional theory with the TSH method appear very promising.
38 Unfortunately, MD at the ab initio level remains computationally demanding and it is
still not possible to aﬀord long trajectories (nanosecond) or signiﬁcant statistics. Achieving statistical MD trajectories requires an explicit potential energy
80 Chapter 4

Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00077
View Online

## Page 101

surface and its gradient. Hopefully, advances in polarizable and reactive
force ﬁelds increasingly broaden the range of molecular systems that can be
simulated this way, paving the way toward dissociation studies over long time
scales.
4.2.2 Connecting Atomistic Dynamics with Rate Theories
Carrying molecular dynamics trajectories is valuable to simulate thermally
activated fragmentation, but it is also useful in order to determine
accurately some key ingredients of rate theories that are relevant for evaporation processes, namely densities of vibrational states and prefactors.
4.2.2.1 Elements of Phase Space Theory
Our statistical description of long-time evaporation is based on phase space
theory, for which we shall just recall some of the salient results. PST assumes
that the dissociation does not involve a transition state, the only barrier to cross
being due to centrifugal repulsion (orbiting transition state). The process
considered is the evaporation of an atom or a molecule X from a cluster
containing initially N þ 1 such elements. We assume the available energy, E,
dissociation energy, D
N, and initial angular momentum, J, are known. Phase
space theory characterizes the dissociation process in terms of the total kinetic
energy released (KER) etr ¼ et þ er, where et and er respectively denote the
translational and rotational contributions to etr, and the angular momentum
Jr ¼ J1 þ J2 of the products. PST is based on the microreversibility principle,
which equates the forward and backward phase space ﬂuxes F(E,J) and F0(E,J)
corresponding to the dissociation and reverse nucleation reactions. The forward ﬂux F is the product of the dissociation rate, k, and the vibrational
density of states (DOS), O
N+1, of the parent. An additional factor, Srot, which
essentially depends on the symmetry properties of the parent, is introduced to
account for its rotational degeneracy:
FðE;JÞ¼ kðE;JÞSrotOðE /C0ErotÞð 4:1Þ
In eqn (4.1), Erot denotes the rotational energy of the parent cluster, which in
the case of linear of spherical parents is related to the angular momentum,
J, through E
rot ¼ BJ2. For the inverse nucleation reaction, the backward ﬂux F0
is related to the probability of forming the parent X N11 from the products X
and XN, and depends on both the translational and rotational energies of each
collision event. An explicit form for F0 is given by: 30

An expression for DL requires some knowledge of the centrifugal barrier,

the etr variable.
For atomic systems, Jr can be associated to the main fragment and this
directly yields the rotational energy er. The translational energy et is thus obtained by simple subtraction from etr. In molecular systems, the two products
can rotate and there is no general relation giving the speciﬁc values of angular

4.2.2.2 Basic Ingredients and Densities of States
The application of phase space theory ﬁrst requires a number of static data,
starting with the dissociation energy of the process considered, the rotational
constants of the parent and products, and the interaction between the fragments. The interaction between the products, although usually taken as a
power law form, turns out to be poorly representative of the actual potential of
mean force acting between the fragments at ﬁnite temperature.
18 The ﬁnite
extension of the cluster can be accounted for using a more realistic form
V(r) ¼/C0C/(r /C0r
0)p, with r0 the cluster radius. Constrained molecular dynamics
simulations are then used to evaluate C and r0, but the centrifugal barrier
needed to determine the rotational density of states must be located numerically18 or perturbatively. 39
MD simulations are also of interest to take into account the possible eﬀects
of temperature on the rotational constants, which due to thermal expansion
may slightly contract at temperatures of experimental relevance. One other
application of simulations is for the determination of the densities of states.
Because O
N11 for the parent and ON for the products directly enter all diﬀerential rate constants, it is important to evaluate these quantities as accurately as
possible. Analytical forms for the DOS are only available for simple models,
with primarily the harmonic approximation giving O EðÞ / E
k/C01; k denoting
the number of degrees of freedom. Anharmonic corrections are possible
through perturbation theory
40 or the superposition approximation over isomers,41 however the most general solution to the problem resides in dedicated
computational schemes designed for the purpose of calculating O, notably the
multiple histogram method 42 and multicanonical approaches such as the
Wang-Landau method. 43 A MD version of the histogram has also been proposed.44 We show in Figure 4.2(a) the variations of the classical vibrational
state densities obtained for the rocksalt cluster (NaCl) 32 using parallel tempering Monte Carlo simulations and the histogram reweighting method,
assuming an analytical polarizable potential.
45 In the low energy range covered,
only the most stable 4 /C2 4 /C2 4 cubic isomer is accessible, and the variations
exhibited by O(E) are intrinsic to the shape of the potential energy surface near
this minimum. As can be seen in Figure 4.2(a), the density of states is markedly
higher when anharmonicities are included, a result that could be anticipated
from the known decrease in level spacing for realistic systems at high energy.
If the energy levels are known, for instance through vibrational perturbation
theory, then it is also possible to evaluate the quantum density of states by direct
counting
46 if the oscillators are decoupled, or by Monte Carlo integration
if couplings are important. 47 Quantization gives rise to strong ﬂuctuations
in the microcanonical density of states, but no divergence at low energy.
The quantum densities of states obtained for the Ne13 cluster by superposition of

the isomer-resolved individual densities,48 shown in Figure 4.2(b), illustrate the

4.2.2.3 Prefactors and Calibration
A well-known deﬁciency of rate theories in chemical physics lies in the rather
loose deﬁnition of some of the prefactors involved in absolute quantities,
especially the total rate constant. The rotational degeneracy factors may not be
well-deﬁned for ﬂuxional systems, moreover the densities of states are rarely
known on an absolute scale. The work by Weerasinghe and Amar
18 showed
nicely that PST rate constants for simple evaporation processes were oﬀ by a
factor of at least three, even after carefully evaluating the absolute state
densities and all other factors. These authors were also able to circumvent this
bottleneck by suggesting a practical solution to the problem by scaling the PST
rate constants in order that they match the MD results at some reference energy. While empirical, this procedure is particularly useful to calibrate the rate
constants at high energies, where short MD trajectories are suﬃcient to produce dissociation events, making the rate theory predictive at low energies
where dissociative trajectories could not be aﬀorded.
We illustrate this calibration procedure in Figure 4.3 using the example of the
evaporation of the (H
2O)50 cluster modeled by rigid molecules with the polarizable Kozack–Jordan potential. 49 For this cluster and its product (H 2O)49,
