the anharmonic densities of states were computed by parallel tempering Monte
Carlo and the rotational density of states was evaluated assuming spherical
products. Statistical MD trajectories were performed at three total energies of
250, 270 and 300 kcal mol
/C01 above the global minimum energy. 50 By monitoring the number of trajectories N(t) not having dissociated as a function of
time t since heating, the evaporation rate constant k can be obtained by simple
ﬁtting of the logarithmic decay law, NtðÞ ¼ Nt ¼ 0ðÞ exp /C0ktðÞ : Such a ﬁtting
procedure is depicted as an inset in Figure 4.3 and the resulting rate constants
are shown in the main plot.
After calibration, the dissociation rat e is extrapolated down to low energies,
and in the present situation some not eworthy non-monotonic variations are
found at energies close to 140 kcal mol /C01. These variations are not spurious, but
convey some strong anharmonicities in bo th parent and product clusters associated with the occurrence of melting in t his energy range. That dissociation
properties relate to the thermodynamical behavior can be understood on the basis
that the density of states is important to measure the available phase space open to
dissociation, but is also characteristic of statistical properties at equilibrium, in
particular the partition function and all other canonical observables. Interestingly,
the connection between dissociation properties and thermodynamic features such
as phase transitions has been the subject o f intense experimental and theoretical
research, not only in cluster physics and chemistry, 51,52 but also in nuclear
physics.53

4.3 Validating and Exploiting Rate Theories
4.3.1 Probability Distributions
The calibration procedure advocated in the previous section is useful in the
context of the multiscale approach, but it does not provide any really strong
support for the rate theory used because the dissociation rates accessible to MD
cover a very limited range. Other statistical properties are more suitable for
assessing the validity and performance of the rate theory used, in particular the
averages of the kinetic energy released and angular momentum produced,
together with the corresponding probability distributions. Contrary to experiment, the MD approach is able to produce such detailed information, allowing
stringent tests of the statistical theory without introducing empirical
parameters.
Figure 4.4 shows some typical simulation results obtained for the thermal
dissociation of molecular clusters of nitrogen or methane. These molecules are
simple enough for the rotational densities of states to be computed exactly in
the approximations where the dissociation products are both spherical (methane), or spherical and linear (nitrogen). The details of these simulations, including the intermolecular potentials, are given in ref. 33, but it is important to
Figure 4.4 Probability distributions predicted by phase space theory (PST) in its

evaporation product, for a parent (N 2)14 with 30 kcal mol /C01 internal
energy and an angular momentum of 290 /C22h; and (b) total kinetic energy
released upon the evaporation of a single atom from the 14-molecule CH 4
cluster having a total energy of 70 kcal mol /C01 and angular momenta of 0
or 195 /C22h. For both panels, the distributions are normalized.

all incorporate ﬁnite temperature and anharmonicity contributions.
Figure 4.4(a) shows the distribution of total angular momentum, J
r, produced
upon dissociation of a nitrogen molecule from the (N 2)14 cluster at excess energy of 30 kcal mol /C01, initially rotating with an angular momentum of 290 /C22h:
This distribution is peaked around J ¼ Jr, but in a clearly asymmetric way
correctly captured by phase space theory. Quantitative agreement is also obtained for the kinetic energy released, as illustrated in Figure 4.4(b) in the case
of the (CH
4)14 cluster undergoing thermal evaporation at the excess energy of
70 kcal mol /C01, and for initial angular momenta of 0 and 195 /C22h: The KER distribution is shifted to higher values in the case of the rotating parent, an eﬀect
due to the likely rotation of the products that yields a non-zero contribution e
r
to the total KER etr.
Besides angular momentum and the total kinetic energy release, PST can also
be used to evaluate the distribution and average of the purely translational part
of the KER. This quantity is important because it can be experimentally
measured by velocity map imaging. The spectrum of translational kinetic energy is sensitive to the interaction between the products, but also to their
shape.
33,54 In addition, its connection with the internal temperature of the
products make it a valuable thermodynamic indicator from which phase
transitions can be probed.
55
4.3.2 An Integrated Kinetic Modeling of Sequential Dissociation
Evaporative cooling proceeds by the successive emission of individual atoms or
molecules, each dissociation process removing some of the internal energy.
Having validated phase space theory as a quantitative method with which to
describe cluster dissociation under timescales relevant to MD trajectories, we
integrate the method into a more general scheme able to model an entire
evaporation cascade X
n ! Xn/C01 þ X ! /C1/C1/C1 !Xn/C0p þ pX. Doing so, we neglect the possible emission of larger species such as dimers or larger clusters,
which are only likely to appear at high excitation energies. 56 This is achieved by
a kinetic Monte Carlo (kMC) approach covering arbitrarily long timescales.
32,57,58 The kMC scheme is depicted in Figure 4.5 as a ﬂowchart. The
evaporation cascade is simulated in the microcanonical ensemble by following
the energetics, kinematics and kinetics of each dissociation event.
Thermal dissociation at a canonical temperature, T, can be simulated by
drawing the initial total energy and angular momentum from appropriate
Boltzmann distributions. The probabilities that a dissociation event occurs with
speciﬁc values e
tr of the KER and Jr of the angular momentum are evaluated
consecutively, and from the translational kinetic energy released in the reference frame of the parent, the recoil motion of both fragments is evaluated.
The linear and angular momenta of all fragments can thus be monitored and
kinetic energies in the laboratory frame are computed. From the dissociation
rate, k, the lifetime Dt of the parent is evaluated assuming a purely stochastic
process, namely DtB1/k. The simulation stops when the time exceeds some

detection limit, which is bound to happen because the dissociation rate sharply
increases with each new evaporation.
The kMC approach was tested on model clusters 59 before it was subsequently employed to model thermal dehydration from a nucleotide anion. 60
Its most complete validation against statistical MD simulations was achieved
for a number of Lennard-Jones (LJ) clusters.
39 In Figure 4.6 we show some
typical results obtained for the evaporative cascade of LJ 28, initially excited at
an energy of 35.1 LJ units and without any angular momentum. We monitor
the time evolution of the average cluster size, its angular momentum and its
internal temperature, all properties being given in LJ units (unit of energy
and temperature ¼ 120 K for argon, unit of time ¼ 2.16 ps, unit of angular
momentum ¼ 34:1/C22h). In addition to a full PST treatment of statistical evaporation where anharmonic densities of states are used and the rates are calibrated
Figure 4.5 Flowchart of a kinetic Monte Carlo simulation of the sequential evaporation of an atomic cluster in which the size, internal energy and momenta
of the main fragment are monitored as a function of time.

## Page 109

from short MD trajectories, we also present the predictions of the same model
using harmonic densities, either with prefactors recalibrated for these new
densities or without any calibration procedure whatsoever.
As can be seen from Figure 4.6, the PST approach is accurate in predicting the
detailed variations of all three properties, at least over timescales that MD can
cover. The harmonic approximation mostly impacts on the temperature, leading
to a substantial overestimation. The angular momentum increases smoothly with
the early evaporation events before stabilizing. Although the additional approximations made to the statistical approach do not alter its qualitative predictions, quantitative accuracy relative to MD is thus found to demand dedicated
computational eﬀort in order to determine the anharmonic state densities and the
rate prefactors. However, this should be balanced with the very inexpensive
character of the kMC simulations and hence the price to pay is not so high
considering the major extension in timescales it grants.

4.4 Applications
4.4.1 Dissociation Induced by Electron Impact: Cationic Argon
Clusters
The rare gases have always been favorite elements in chemical physics due to
their simple interactions making them prone to large-scale modeling. Experimentalists have also worked heavily on rare gas clusters, getting insight into
their structure and complex dynamics.
61–63 Electron impact is a primary way to
excite rare gas clusters, producing ionized species that can be conveniently
detected by mass spectrometry. Upon impact, ionization induces strong
changes in the electronic structure with the formation of a covalent molecular
ion at the center.
64 In addition, the cluster is initially electronically excited and
the corresponding dynamics should involve multiple coupled electronic states.
For suﬃciently large clusters, the high number of excited states and their numerous avoided crossings enhances internal conversion to the ground electronic
state, the initial excitation energy being transferred into a high nuclear kinetic
energy possibly leading to some early non-statistical fragmentation. At this
stage, conventional molecular dynamics trajectories can be pursued on the
ground electronic state, until the fragmentation products are suﬃciently cold
for statistical arguments and kinetic rate theories to apply.
We have modeled such a complex dissociation process in the case of cationic
argon clusters, modeled using a diatomic-in-molecules (DIM) approach.
65 The
non-adiabatic dynamics was simulated using the TSH method, 36 and after total
internal conversion the Born–Oppenheimer dynamics was propagated further
over 10 ps. Finally, the evolution of the main fragment having survived at
this time was predicted by assuming sequential evaporation of neutral atoms,
applying the kMC/PST approach over timescales reaching the millisecond.
35
Some results of these simulations are depicted in Figure 4.7 for the clusters Ar30
1
and Ar 20
1, the average size hNi of the main cluster being represented as a
function of time on a logarithmic scale. Besides the multiscale description with
full (anharmonic and calibrated) PST, we also show the results obtained with
harmonic densities of states, as well as the predictions of a more approximate
model neglecting the early dynamics on the ground electronic state surface.
The ﬁrst hundreds of femtoseconds mainly involve the electronic degrees of
freedom and the atoms only undergo some motion at longer times. The dynamics during the subsequent 10 ps exhibits a strong drop in the cluster size,
nearly 50% of the mass being lost in an explosive fashion. Above this time, the
main clusters progressively evaporate atoms in a much more gentle way that is
well described by the statistical approach. Using anharmonic densities of states
does not alter the general picture, although the discontinuities seen in hNi at
10 ps with the modeling changing from atomistic to continuous are indicative
of inaccurate rate constants.
The main result is obtained by considering the neglect of the intermediate
MD trajectories on the ground state electronic surface. This approximation
leads to qualitatively diﬀerent variations in the average cluster size, with two
90 Chapter 4
 01/12/2013 15:15:52.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00077
View Online

## Page 111

major drops corresponding to the initial explosion and some later occurring
sequential dissociation. Clearly the present kMC/PST model is unable to account for the various fragmentation channels taking place during the ﬁrst explosive processes and this deﬁciency is reﬂected on the much overestimated
cluster size at 10 ps.
4.4.2 Thermalization along the Dissociative Chain and the
Decay of Fullerenes
Fullerenes are sp2-bound carbon compounds showing remarkable thermal and
chemical stability. Their peculiar properties have been scrutinized since their
discovery,
66 and from the fundamental viewpoint they are large enough to bridge
the gap between molecular and condensed matter science. Their dissociation can
be induced by high-energy collisions 67 or by exposure to an intense laser. 68 Experiments performed with nanosecond lasers have shown that C 60 decays by
successive emission of carbon dimers.69,70 Empirical models based on simple rate
theories with adjustable parameters reasonably account for the observed mass
spectra,
71 providing some estimates of the dissociation energies and rates.
Our second application of the time multiscale modeling approach aims to
describe the sequential emission of carbon dimers from a purely theoretical
perspective, without any adjustment. Because the absolute dissociation rates
Figure 4.7 Variations of the average size of cationic argon clusters excited by electron
impact at t ¼ 0, as predicted by a time multiscale approach combining
non-adiabatic molecular dynamics trajectories until internal conversion to
the ground electronic state is achieved, followed by classical MD up to
100 ps and ﬁnally a kinetic approach based on phase space theory for
sequential monomer evaporation. The results of approximate treatments
with harmonic densities of states, or with the neglect of the classical MD
relaxation stage, are also shown.
Statisticodynamical and Multiscale Modeling of Cluster Dissociation 91
 01/12/2013 15:15:52.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00077
View Online

## Page 112

predicted by PST could be in error by an order of magnitude or more, we focus
on the energetic and kinematic aspects of the sequential dissociation process
and leave the real-time dynamics aside. Brieﬂy, our modeling employs quantum
densities of states for the successive fullerene fragments using dissociation energies, vibrational frequencies, and rotational constants calculated for structures obtained by density functional theory (B3LYP/6-31 þ G* level).
72
Sequential fragmentation is modeled for the emission of neutral dimers from
neutral or cationic fullerenes, appropriate interactions in 1/ r6 or 1/ r4 being
respectively adopted, and the fragments are treated as spherical and linear tops
for the purpose of evaluating rotational densities of states. The modeling relies
then on the same kMC approach described in the previous section, with only
one signiﬁcant diﬀerence in the evaluation of angular momenta. As already
mentioned, PST predicts the total angular momentum J
r of the products
without specifying their individual angular momenta. Assuming that the rotational energy is shared in comparable proportions by the two products and
that their orientational distributions are isotropic, the individual angular momenta can be roughly evaluated from the simple knowledge of the two rotational constants. This equipartition ansatz was found to work relatively well
against the results of MD simulations of nitrogen clusters, for which the dissociation products also belong to the sphere þ linear class.
73
With this assumption at hand, it is possible to obtain the evolution of the
translational, rotational and vibrational contributions to the energy of the
fullerene fragments, as well as the emitted dimers. In particular, from the respective distributions the corresponding temperatures can be inferred by appropriate ﬁtting onto Boltzmann forms. Figure 4.8 shows the variations of the
three temperatures of carbon dimers emitted successively from C
60
1 after a
Figure 4.8 Vibrational, translational and rotational temperatures of carbon dimers
emitted successively from thermally excited fullerene cations, as predicted
by phase space theory with quantum densities of states.
92 Chapter 4
 01/12/2013 15:15:52.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00077
View Online

## Page 113

brief excitation resembling that of a nanosecond laser approximately equivalent
to 7000 K, as a function of their fullerene of origin.
As expected, the three temperatures are initially the highest and steadily
decrease with decreasing fullerene size. However, they are very diﬀerent from
each other, especially at the ﬁrst dissociation from size 60, the excess energy
ﬂowing more likely into the vibrational degrees of freedom than in the translational and ﬁnally the rotational ones. This lack of energy partitioning is attenuated as further dissociations take place, but the vibrational temperature
drops rather quickly, whereas translational and rotational temperatures are
essentially stable. Although temperatures of atomic clusters can eﬀectively be
estimated from the distribution of translational KER,
55 the situation of molecular clusters seems thus not as straightforward and there is no simple relation
between the diﬀerent temperatures in such an out-of-equilibrium system.
4.4.3 Laser-Induced Spectroscopy of a Messenger-Tagged
Ionic Cluster
Selective excitation of a molecule can be achieved by photon absorption. The
absorption eﬃciency can be monitored directly from mass abundances, and by
tuning the laser frequency, a so-called action spectrum can be determined.
Action spectroscopies can be performed with optical pulses, hence interrogating electronic excited states. With the advent of powerful coherent and
tunable sources using synchrotron light or free-electron lasers, the infrared (IR)
range has become accessible as well, providing some more direct probe of the
vibrational dynamics and, ultimately, of the molecular conformation. IR action
spectra are systematically interpreted by computing the absorption spectrum of
various candidate structures, usually by static quantum chemistry calculations.
However, action spectra are not equivalent to the absorption proﬁle because
the absorption, relaxation and fragmentation processes involve diﬀerent
mechanisms and timescales.
The time multiscale approach oﬀers a convenient way of addressing this
issue, as it provides a natural framework to describe all the relevant processes.
In the example below, we focus on messenger-tagging action spectroscopy.
74
This soft technique consists of attaching a weakly bound atom or molecule
(inert gas, nitrogen, etc.) to the system of interest and monitoring its detachment caused by the laser at much lower intensities than those required for
fragmenting the system itself. It turns out to be also rather ideal for modeling
since a single dissociation channel is involved. Excitation by the laser is described by an explicit homogeneous time-dependent electric ﬁeld with a
Gaussian envelope, ﬁxed intensity and pulse duration.
The multiscale approach involves three stages. Initially, the system is thermalized and its conﬁguration and momenta are drawn from a Boltzmann
distribution generated by a thermostatted MD trajectory at the desired temperature. A new trajectory is then propagated without the thermostat but in
presence of the electric ﬁeld, and it is stopped after the laser pulse has vanished.
Statisticodynamical and Multiscale Modeling of Cluster Dissociation 93
 01/12/2013 15:15:52.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00077
View Online

## Page 114

The energy gained by the system upon exposure to the ﬁeld is then transformed
into a dissociation probability from a careful calibration of the dissociation rate
based on anharmonic densities of states.
We have applied this procedure to the simple ionic cluster (NaCl)
32 tagged to
an argon atom, exposed to laser pulses covering the far IR range, with a
maximum laser intensity of 6.6 10 6 Vc m/C02 and a duration of 100 ps. 75 The
polarizable Welch potential, 45 complemented with additional terms accounting
for the presence of the argon atom was employed for the simulations, and the
cubic structure of the ionic cluster fully justiﬁes the spherical approximation
used for evaluating the rotational density of states. The initial conditions were
sampled at 40 K using a Nose´–Hoover thermostat, and the connection between
the MD trajectories and the kinetic description was made after 1 ns. The
probability of the argon atom to dissociate after 1 ms is depicted in Figure 4.9 as
a function of laser frequency in two spectral ranges. On this ﬁgure we also
superimposed the anharmonic absorption spectrum obtained from an equilibrium MD trajectory of the argon-tagged cluster at 40 K. The IR intensity is
there evaluated by the Fourier transform of the dipole moment time autocorrelation function. Finally, the vertical lines locate the three IR active absorption bands given by a harmonic analysis in the static approximation.
The action spectrum generally shares several features of the anharmonic
absorption spectrum, with a marked broadening and red shifting of the lines.
These features are characteristic of ﬁnite temperature eﬀects. However,
the peaks of the action spectrum are excessively red shifted with respect to
the absorption bands by a few tens of a percent depending on the band.
Figure 4.9 Probability dissociation (action spectrum) of the argon atom from the
weakly bound complex Ar(NaCl) 32 upon exposure to an infrared laser
pulse as a function of laser frequency. The anharmonic absorption spectrum is superimposed in black and the vibrational redshifts relative to the
static absorption frequencies at 0 K (denoted by vertical dashed blue lines)
are indicated by arrows.
94 Chapter 4
 01/12/2013 15:15:52.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00077
View Online

## Page 115

This additional shifting can be traced back to the dynamical heating produced
by the laser. As the system absorbs more and more photons, its heating progressively changes its eﬀective absorption frequencies owing to anharmonicities. The eﬀective temperature is thus higher than the initial 40 K, but depends
on the absorption eﬃciency at the speciﬁc laser frequency. The vibrational
response seen on the action spectrum reﬂects part of this heating and this study
suggests that anharmonicities should generally be ampliﬁed in multiphoton
processes.
4.5 Conclusions
This chapter has shed some light onto the time multiscale approach, which was
introduced to model the various stages of cluster fragmentation by combining
successive schemes appropriate to each stage. This general strategy is expected
to work in general for suﬃciently large systems, in which the initial excitation is
relatively short and can be explicitly described at the atomistic and electronic
levels. The progressive conversion of the excitation energy into vibrations
allows a purely statistical framework to be employed to describe the long-time
kinetics, assuming sequential decay into the lowest channel. However, some
intermediate dynamics on the ground state energy surface may be necessary to
account for the rapid fragmentation events taking place if the thermal energy
largely exceeds cohesion. In any case, the multiscale approach is intrinsically
statistical and in practice requires the ability to perform numerous molecular
dynamics simulations, not only to simulate dissociation itself, but also to calculate the various ingredients that are necessary for a quantitative application
of kinetic rate theories. The main limitation is thus the need for a suitable
potential energy surface and its gradient, which unfortunately is at present
probably too expensive for ab initio descriptions of electronic structure, although semi-empirical treatments could be considered. The speciﬁc modeling of
the non-adiabatic dynamics following electronic excitations is also an involved
task in itself.
Phase space theory appears as relevant to the modeling of unimolecular
evaporation in clusters and it is even quantitative against numerically exact
MD, provided anharmonic densities of states and calibrated prefactors are
used. However, the agreement remains rather good even in absence of
such ingredients, which should motivate the use of this general framework to
describe statistical dissociation. Although the present work only considered
sequential dissociation into the most probable monomer channel, it could
be extended to treat several channels in competition. Such an extension
involves the calculation of absolute diﬀerential rates for all channels, which
implies characterizing all vibrational and rotational densities of states,
possibly with some additional calibration of individual fragmentation ratio
based on simulations. The feasibility has been demonstrated for small carbon
clusters,
76 but it could be tedious for chemically complex or heterogeneous
systems.
Statisticodynamical and Multiscale Modeling of Cluster Dissociation 95
 01/12/2013 15:15:52.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00077
View Online

## Page 116

The applications were concerned with a fully classical treatment of nuclear
motion, which is usually appropriate for suﬃciently massive particles and high
excitations. For large systems, it is rather diﬃcult to assess the importance of
quantum eﬀects because, contrary to the classical equations of motion, the
Schro¨dinger equation cannot be solved exactly. Quantum rate theories can still
be used, with quantum densities of states and some account of tunneling.
77
Developing approximate schemes for the multiscale dissociation dynamics
of quantum systems thus appears a natural perspective requiring theoretical
eﬀorts in various directions.

CHAPTER 5
A Mixed Quantum-Classical
View to the Kinetics of Chemical
Reactions Involving Multiple
Electronic States
AURE´LIEN DE LA LANDE,* BERNARD LE ´VY AND
ISABELLE DEMACHY
Laboratoire de Chimie Physique – CNRS UMR 8000., Universite ´Paris-Sud.
baˆ timent 349, Campus d’Orsay. 15, rue Jean Perrin. 91 405 Orsay Cedex,
France
*Email: aurelien.de-la-lande@u-psud.fr
5.1 Introduction
An important issue when investigating chemical reactions is the determination
of their rates. Calculating chemical rate constants is particularly challenging
owing to the molecular complexity of the condensed phase, which is not necessarily reducible to few dimensional problems. A supplementary level of
complexity arises when more than one electronic state participates in the process. In such situations one has to consider multiple potential energy surfaces
(PESs) and the fact that, in principle, the nuclear dynamics are coupled to that
of the electrons. Depending on the strength of the coupling, the molecular
system may undergo population exchanges between distinct electronic states.
These situations are far from being anecdotic in physical chemistry and some of
the most interesting processes indeed fall into this category. Without being
RSC Theoretical and Computational Chemistry Series No. 6
Reaction Rate Constant Computations: Theories and Applications
Edited by Keli Han and Tianshu Chu
r The Royal Society of Chemistry 2014
Published by the Royal Society of Chemistry, www.rsc.org
99
 01/12/2013 15:15:59.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00099

## Page 120

exhaustive we could mention the cases of electron or electronic excitation
transfers, of spin crossing reactions, of photo-induced processes, of radiation
processes (ﬂuorescence, phosphorescence ... ) and so on.
Historically, statistical methods like transition state theory (TST)
1,2 as well
as its nonadiabatic version 3,4 or the Marcus theory 5 of electron transfers
addressed this issue by considering the collective behavior of large numbers of
molecules in a statistical sense, leading to simple expressions for the rate
constants. In these theories, a Gibbs free energy of activation ( DG a), a characteristic frequency along a suitable reaction coordinates ( n) and eventually a
probability to hop from one electronic state to the other ( hPhi) are the essential
ingredients entering the rate constant expressions. (See eqns (5.1), (5.2) and
(5.3) where g, g and k are corrections factors to account for re-crossings, nonequilibrium eﬀects and tunneling, respectively; DG
/C14
and l are the Gibbs free
energy of reaction and the reorganization energy of Marcus theory.)

 !

Such statistical theories have proven to be extremely useful for rationalizing
experimental rate constants. It is indeed remarkable that such simple theories
succeed in reducing the molecular complexity and provide a simple framework
to think about the molecular mechanisms governing the kinetics of chemical
reactions in terms of easily accessible concepts. A chemical reaction is regarded
as a statistical process by which atoms are moving on the potential energy
surface from one potential well to another one by passage over a transition
state (deﬁned in TST as a saddle point on the PES) or through the crossing
points between the PESs in Marcus theory. It is assumed that molecular
populations at the transition state (TS) and in the reactant potential follow
Boltzmann equilibrium law.
It has been recognized that corrections sometimes need to be brought to
account for particular eﬀects such as tunneling through the barrier, re-crossing
at the TS or non-equilibriums eﬀects (the g, g, and k terms).
6 An aspect that
may not have received suﬃcient attention in the past is the precise procedure by
which the notion of hopping is deﬁned and how the hopping probability enters
the rate constant expression. These interrogations are relevant when more than
one PES is involved. In fact the notion of hopping probability is intimately
related to the quantum-to-classical transition and one may argue that this issue
is not satisfactorily handled in nonadiabatic TST or the Marcus theory.
This chapter deals with the presentation of a mixed quantum–classical
framework which our group has developed to address this issue. Many eﬀorts

## Page 121

have been spent by various groups since the 1990s to identify the role of
decoherence or nuclear dephasing in physical–chemical problems. 7–8 We have
used the notion of decoherence to operate phenomenologically the smooth
transition from the fully quantum to semi-classical picture in a kinetics
model.
9,10 The outcomes of these investigations have fed our reﬂection in the
establishment of a conceptual framework as intuitive as TST or Marcus theory
but including the notion of quantum decoherence.
The ﬁrst part of the chapter focuses on the derivation of a mixed quantum–
classical theory for rationalizing chemical reactions involving two electronic
states. The central piece of this mixed quantum–classical rate constant is the
appearance of a characteristic decoherence time. The second part of the chapter
deals with numerical approaches at the atomic level that can be carried out to
decipher the molecular mechanisms governing decoherence in real systems of
biological interest.
5.2 A Mixed Quantum–Classical Framework for
Multiple PES Reactions
At our most fundamental level of description we consider molecular systems to
be composed of atomic nuclei and electrons, all obeying quantum mechanical
laws. The question of the kinetics of a physicochemical event is therefore
related to the time evolution of such composite systems. In the ﬁrst sub-section
we recall the basic quantum mechanical equation-of-motions relevant in this
context. We then consider approximations that can be operated to simplify the
nuclear-electronic dynamics, leading to the derivation of the mixed quantum–
classical rate constant expression.
5.2.1 Time Evolution of a Molecular System using a
Time-dependent Schro¨dinger Equation
We start by considering a general expression for the wave function of the
system. In cases where several electronic states are involved, it is convenient to
expand the wave function of the system in the form:
C q; Q; tðÞ ¼
X

jkðq; Qfg Þ fkðQ; tÞð 5:4Þ
where q stands for the set of all electronic coordinates, Q for the set of all
nuclear coordinates and t is the time. In that speciﬁc form of C; the jk
functions are the electronic wave functions. They depend parametrically on the
nuclear coordinates Q but not on the time and they can be assumed to be
orthonormal, just like the outcome of standard quantum chemistry (QC)
programs. The f
k functions represent the nuclear wave functions (or wave
packets). They hold the dependence in time of the wave function and their
norms carry the information on the relative weights of the diﬀerent electronic
states at time t.

It should to be noted that the form chosen here for C is somewhat arbitrary.
For instance, one could use electronic wave functions in the form of plane
waves (exp ik:qðÞ ) independent of Q. Similarly one could use nuclear wave
functions independent of time (a basis set of steady states) multiplied by a
time-dependent scalar factor. In fact, this last form of C might be more
convenient for numerical purposes than the one proposed here, that is,
eqn (5.4). However, it turns out that eqn (5.4) is convenient for the qualitative
discussion on the quantum-to-classical transition described below.
Our objective is to model chemical kinetics, meaning that the evolution of
C with time must be examined. The most direct way to do so is to consider the
time-dependent Schro¨dinger equation (TDSE):

## Page 123

TDSEs reﬂecting that each quantum states evolve independently. This is the
so-called Born–Oppenheimer approximation:
Ek QðÞ ¼ h jk jbTq þ bV j jkiq ð5:11Þ
Having written down these equations, the diﬃculty is to obtain a general
analytical solution for any system of interest. The numerical simulation of eqn
(5.6) is itself a formidable task. Speciﬁc methodologies such as the ab initio
multiple spawning
11 or the multi-conﬁguration-time-dependent Hartree–
Fock12 methods have been devised to that end over the past years. Systems
containing up to 100 degrees of freedom can be treated by such approaches.
However, these computations remain extremely expensive and are not currently
applicable to large molecular systems like those encountered, for example, in
biology. As an alternative one may operate approximations in the equationsof-motion with the aim of facilitating their numerical simulations and to provide
intuitive conceptual frameworks in which it is possible to rationalize non-Born–
Oppenheimer processes. These approximations are the subject of the next
subsection.
5.2.2 Simpliﬁcation of the Nuclear–Electronic Dynamics
Problem
5.2.2.1 Motivations for the Quantum-to-Classical Transition
We consider here a series of approximations that rely on distinction between
time sequences where classical mechanics provide an acceptable theory for
describing the nuclear dynamics and time sequences where a quantum dynamical
description is necessary. Such a distinction is motivated by the qualitative
analysis of the potential and dynamical coupling terms entering eqn (5.6).
We ﬁrst remark that the functions f
k are always bounded in the present
context. They vanish rapidly when Q increases and they are square summable.
Note that we do not try to describe collision processes or the asymptotic
behavior of the fragments resulting from a dissociation. In the situations we are
currently interested in it may happen that two wave packets are such that one
vanishes in the region where the other does not. Such a situation implies that
the potential coupling hj
k jbTq þ bV j jl iq has no eﬀect on fkjj 2. Indeed, the time
derivative of fkjj 2 (obtained by substituting the time derivative of fk, eqn (5.6)
into that of fkjj 2 and by combining with the complex conjugate) can be
written as:

dynamical coupling, the exponents ‘*’ refer to the complex conjugate. It is has
assumed that j
k and jl are real. It is easily seen that eqn (5.12) implies that the
potential coupling between fk and fl does not contribute to the variation of fk
A Mixed Quantum-Classical View to the Kinetics of Chemical Reactions 103
 01/12/2013 15:15:59.

## Page 124

if fkvanishes where fl does not. A similar deduction can be made with the
dynamical coupling. We conclude that two wave packets become independent if
their product vanishes; more precisely, if the product of the wave packets is
small enough and both couplings are small compared with the potential energy
diﬀerence. This qualitative analysis leads to a sequence of diﬀerent approaches
that can be described as follows.
We assume that one starts at t ¼ 0 with a system in a given electronic state k,
eﬃciently coupled to another electronic state l. Eﬃcient electron–nuclear
couplings arise when the potential coupling is not small compared with the
potential energy diﬀerences, as around the crossings seams
3 or conical intersections between Born–Oppenheimer surfaces. The eﬃciency of the dynamical
coupling may also arise from the velocities of the wave packets as expected by
the form of the operator appearing in the deﬁnition of D
k;l (eqn (5.7)). In all
these cases the time evolution of the system must be described by quantum
mechanics. We usually refer to this evolution as a coherent evolution of the
total wave function. However the two wave packets, which are initially localized in the same region, follow diﬀerent coupled trajectories and eventually
diverge.
Secondly, when the coupling has vanished, the evolution of the two wave
packets is no longer coherent. In such situations molecular mechanics is an
acceptable framework to describe the nuclear dynamics and one may assume
the nuclear wave packet evolves independently. This is of course an approximation that may not always be valid.
It should be noted that the separation of the two wave packets implies that
they become independent but not that the coupling vanishes: if it does not, one
is back to the ﬁrst situation—the wave packet localized in the region with a
coupling gives rise to two wave packets both localized in the same region. In
fact the use of classical mechanics rather than quantum mechanics is acceptable
provided (i) the wave packets are separated and (ii) that they are localized in
non-overlapping regions where the couplings vanish or become ineﬃcient due
to a large energy diﬀerence.
In summary it seems possible to devise a procedure where the time evolution
is split into shorter windows in which quantum or classical mechanics are
applied alternatively. When classical mechanics is applicable, the picture of the
time evolution of the system is greatly simpliﬁed. The nuclei can then be
regarded as following well-deﬁned trajectories in the phase space. This type of
procedure has been applied in many situations, for example, to devise
algorithms to simulate non-Born–Oppenheimer processes using semi-classical
dynamics (see, for example, the fewest switching surface hopping scheme
13).
Statistical theories like the Marcus theory or the nonadiabatic version of
TST also rely on such a treatment, where the molecular system is modeled
alternatively with classical dynamics (to estimate the probability to reach a
seam) or quantum mechanics (to estimate the hopping probability with
Landau–Zener
14,15 or more sophisticated models 16–17). Historically little attention had been paid to the process by which the quantum-to-classical transition could be operated and it was in fact assumed to operate suddenly. One
104 Chapter 5

Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00099
View Online

## Page 125

may alternatively resort to the notion of decoherence as a means to operate this
transition.
5.2.2.2 Qualitative Discussion about the Quantum-to-Classical
Transition
The introduction of the notion of decoherence in physical chemistry was
pioneered by Rossky and co-workers in the late 1990s 7,18,19 and further
developed in the beginning of the 21st century by other groups. 8,20–21 Rossky
and co-workers developed these ideas to calculate the nonadiabatic decay of
excited electrons in water and heavier solvents based on the Fermi golden rule.
Building on the works of Heller
22 and of Neria and Nitzan, 23 they could deﬁne
a decoherence function that bridges exactly the expressions for the fully
quantum and the quantum–classical golden rule.
22 We actually recommend
readers to read these illuminating articles on the importance of decoherence in
the condensed phase.
We consider for the moment an over-simpliﬁed model to present this notion
in the continuation of the previous sections. The model consists of an isolated
system with a single nuclear degree of freedom Q (one-dimensional system), a
bond length, and just two electronic states 1 and 2 giving rise to two potential
energy functions E
1 and E2 and two wave packets f1 and f2
(see Figure 5.1).
Initially, at time t ¼ 0, the system is assumed to be in state 1 and the wave
packet f1 is normalised to unity and centered on the equilibrium position of E1
while the wave packet f2 is null. But one should take into account the fact that
the time derivative of f2 at t ¼ 0 is not zero due to the coupling D21 with f1.
Just after t 0 at time (t 0 þ e), f2 is no longer zero and is centered in the
same region as f1 (the region where the product D21f1 does not vanish).
In Figure 5.1 the wave packet f2ðt0 þ eÞ is assumed to be identical with f1; this

particle 2.104 a. u.; frequency in the two potential wells of 1000 cm /C0 1; and

## Page 126

is just for convenience, the only important aspect is that it is localized in the
same region as f1
In later times, the two wave packets start moving under the eﬀect of the
gradients of E1, E2 and of the coupling. But neither of the wave packets can
move far from its own equilibrium geometry due to energy conservation. In
Figure 5.1 it is assumed for simplicity that f1 remains ﬁxed while f2 moves
toward the equilibrium geometry of E2. In any case they cannot be both far
from their equilibrium geometry. Therefore, decoherence is governed by the
relative magnitude of the distance between the two equilibrium geometries
[Q
1 eqðÞ /C0 Q2 eqðÞ ] and of the width of the wave packets. The variations in bond
length between two electronic states of a molecule (in the simple cases where
non bond breaking occurs) are usually of the order of 10
/C0 2 A˚ while the widths
of wave packets are instead in the range of 10 /C0 1 A˚ . (In Figure 5.1 the width of
the Gaussian—deﬁned as the standard deviation—is equal to 0.2 A ˚ ). This
means that the two wave packets cannot be well separated, they always overlap
and their product never vanishes. In other words this mechanism does not
explain decoherence in a one-dimensional model.
We consider now the case of an nDimensional model similar to the previous
one (two electronic states, harmonic potentials and real Gaussian wave packets) except that the number of degrees of freedom or bonds now equals n. Since
we assume nodeless wave packets, the separation between the wave packets can
be actually measured by the scalar product equal to the product of the scalar
products of the individual wave packet of each degree of freedom:
hf
1j f2i¼
Y
i ¼ 1;n
h f1;i j f2;iið 5:13Þ
where f1and f2 are the global wave packets of the system in the two electronic
states, and f1;i and f2;i the wave packets of the degree of freedom i in these two
states. We may evaluate hf1j f2i assuming that all the f1;i remain ﬁxed while all
the f2;i oscillate with an initial zero velocity and that all the fk;i functions are
identical real Gaussian functions. The scalar product is then the nth power of
the overlap between two Gaussian functions with a distance in the form of a
cosine function of time (Figure 5.2). It is seen that the scalar product measuring
the degree of coherence is highly dependent of n, the number of degrees of
freedom of the system. As already mentioned if n ¼ 1 the coherence never
disappears. If n41 it is seen that the scalar product may actually vanish and
that the coherence time length—in the range of 5–10 fs in Figure 5.2—decreases
when n increases.
These two examples lead to the conclusion that, in these over-simpliﬁed
models, the coherence time length is governed by at least two factors: the variation of the equilibrium geometry between the two states compared with the
width of the wave packets of each state; and the number of degrees of freedom
that are displaced. Both factors may vary signiﬁcantly from a molecular system
to the other. Actual systems are investigated in the second part of the chapter.

5.2.3 Mixed Quantum–Classical Kinetics Theory
5.2.3.1 Qualitative for the Quantum-to-Classical Transition
Recently we have adapted the ideas set out in the previous sections to the
question of chemical kinetic theories. While it is clear that the development of
eﬃcient simulation algorithms that include decoherence eﬀects is a worthwhile
objective of current research, the development of simple kinetics theories is, to
our point of view, of equal importance. Indeed the diﬀusion of the notions of
quantum decoherence to non-specialists would be facilitated if these ideas could
be translated into accessible concepts and the outcomes of computations could
be directly connected to experimental data.
A ﬁrst step in that direction was achieved in ref. 24 where we modiﬁed the
expression of the nonadiabatic expressions given by eqns (5.2) and (5.3) which
relate the rate constant of a chemical reaction to the product of three terms: a
frequency along a suitable reaction coordinate ( e.g. the energy gap); a
Boltzmann probability to reach regions on the PES where the electronic states
are degenerated; and an average hopping probability at the crossing points.
Historically the Landau–Zener
15,16 or more sophisticated models have been
used to estimate these probabilities.
Inspired by the work of Prezhdo and Rossky 22 and of Jasper and Truhlar, 8
we proposed an alternative derivation, built on the notion of decoherence to
ensure physical sound quantum-to-classical transition at the seam. Our
approach is schematized in Figure 5.3. We set t ¼ 0 when the system enters a
region where the coupling between the quantum states is eﬃcient, that is, when
the energy gap is small compared with the coupling. We consider N
T copies of
Figure 5.2 Decay of the function f1 j f2hi in the harmonic models as a function of
the dimension n.

the system (note that since we are working with the ensemble interpretation of

probability amplitude for the system to be in the second electronic state evolves
according to the TDSE. Following the qualitative discussion of section 5.1.2,
the decaying decoherence function D(t) may be interpreted as the fraction of
the N
T copies that remains in a superposition of states while the complementary function [1 – D(t)] represents the fraction of the population that has
decohered in either state 1 or state 2. At each time t, the coherent reservoir
supplies the classical reservoirs depending on the actual value of P
12(t). The
ﬁnal population N2 of molecules in state 2 is thus obtained by integrating
the classical kinetic law given in Figure 5.3. When D(t) has vanished to zero, the
system can be considered to behave semi-classically again. The hopping
probability that has been accumulated during the decoherence process is given
by eqn (5.14):

5.2.3.2 Chemical Implications of the Mixed Quantum–Classical
View
In eqn (5.20), the parameter m determines the probability of hopping from one
quantum state to the other. It is thus apparent that the strength of the electronic
coupling and the characteristic decoherence time have equal importance in
deﬁning this quantity. For strong coupling or for long decoherence times, eqn
(5.20) reduces to eqn (5.21).

For weak coupling or for short decoherence times, eqn (5.20) reduces to
eqn (5.22). This is analogous to the nonadiabatic regime of Marcus theory
(eqn (5.3)) or the nonadiabatic TST (eqn (5.2)), except that the notion of
decoherence is absent in these theories. Under this regime the rate decays
exponentially with the distance, a trend that is observed, for example, for
biological electron transfer occurring in the super-exchange regime.
27 We see
also in Figure 5.4 that the transition between the two limit regimes is rapid.
A remarkable feature of the mixed quantum–classical formulation of the rate
[eqn (5.20)] is that it enables rationalization of both the adiabatic and
nonadiabatic regimes with a single mathematical expression.

5.3 Atomistically Resolved Decoherence in Molecular
Systems
In this second section we illustrate how decoherence can be simulated in large
molecular systems using numerical approaches. These approaches are aimed
at providing, on one hand, ﬁrst order estimations of the characteristic
Figure 5.4 Transition from the adiabatic to non-adiabatic regime for an electron
transfer between a donor and an acceptor as a function of the separating
distance and the characteristic decoherence time.

## Page 131

decoherence times and, on the other hand, some insights on the molecular
mechanisms that lead to decoherence. We present examples taken from our
own work, although citations to other studies are given. We ﬁrst detail in
section 5.2.1 the computational protocol we have employed in our studies. We
then present three molecular systems where decoherence has been investigated,
highlighting similarities but also diﬀerences between these systems.
5.3.1 Modeling Decoherence from Semi-Classical Molecular
Dynamics Simulations
5.3.1.1 General Lines for Avoiding Fully Quantum Simulations
The notion of decoherence is usually formulated in the density matrix formalism
which provides a convenient framework to discuss this notion. To help the reader
making connections with other studies we brieﬂy reformulate the reasoning of
section 5.2.2 using density matrices. Our starting point is the state vector that
characterizes the entire molecular system, which is similar to eqn (5.4):

wave function in the nth electronic state. The oﬀ-diagonal elements represent
the so-called coherences between the electronic states. To use the same
notations as in ref. 24 and ref. 28 we introduce the nuclear overlap/phase
function J
mn by rewritingP

electronic matrix reads:

ð5:29Þ
The term decoherence describes the process by which the oﬀ-diagonal
elements of the reduced density matrix tend to zero when evolving with time. 29
Our objective is to reach an understanding of the molecular mechanisms governing decoherence with an atomic resolution. In addition we wish to be in a
position to treat systems consisting of tens to thousands of atoms since the
brute force simulation of the time evolution of ^r
e tðÞ by the Liouville–von
Neuman equation ( _re tðÞ ¼ i/C22h ^H; ^re/C2/C3
), the equivalent of the TDSE in the density
matrix formalism, is out of question for such molecular systems.
We have considered in our group an alternative strategy for simulating the
oﬀ-diagonal elements of the reduced density matrix inspired by the work of
Rossky and co-workers.
7 Facing the impossibility of performing quantum
dynamics fully, these authors proposed estimating the function JmnðtÞ and its
evolution building on semi-classical molecular dynamics (MD) trajectories.
Such MD simulations are straightforward to carry out since the nuclei follow
Newtonian trajectories and simulation algorithms are available in many computational packages. The quantum nature of the nuclei is introduced
a posteriori in the modeling by assigning to each degree of freedom (three per
nuclei) a Gaussian function G
j mimicking the nuclear wave packet. The full
NWP (eqn (5.28)) may be expressed as a product of the 3N /C0 6 individual
Gaussian functions and the phase term: 28,29

In these equations DE12 and DK12 are respectively the potential and kinetics
energy diﬀerences between the electronic states 1 and 2, aj is related to the
Gaussian width of the degree of freedom j (the width is a/C0 1 = 2
j ), and xmj and pmj
are the position and momentum vectors of the degree of freedom j in the
state m.
The representation of the nuclear wave function and its evolution by a semiclassical trajectory is of course an approximation of the real dynamics of the
nuclear wave packet. However, following Heller, it can be justiﬁed by considering the fact that Gaussian wave packets evolving on harmonic potentials
preserve their Gaussian shape with the wave packet centers following classical
trajectories. If the decoherence process we are modeling occurs on a suﬃciently
short timescale it is then reasonable to consider the PES as locally harmonic.
In fact the principal limitation of the approach proposed by Rossky and
co-workers is to be restricted to short time-windows (says tens of few
femtoseconds). In the example presented below this is eﬀectively the case,
although this limitation must be borne in mind when applying the method to
new situations. However, a major advantage is that one can use the wide range
of methodologies developed over the past decades in computational chemistry
to drive the motion of the classical nuclei. Classical molecular mechanics
(MM), quantum mechanics (QM) and hybrid QM/MM
30 methodologies can be
used depending on the desired level of accuracy and on the electronic–nuclear
speciﬁcities of the system of interest.
We have recently carried out a systematic comparison of the density
function theory (DFT),
31 the self-consistent-charge density-functional tightbinding (SCC-DFTB) method 32 and classical MM approaches for modeling
decoherence within an organic redox cofactor. 33 All methods produced similar
results in terms of molecular mechanisms governing decoherence and, as a
consequence, similar characteristic decoherence times. Of particular interest
were the tests of the MM approach (a particularly attractive approach for large
molecular systems) that was shown to be well adapted provided a careful
calibration of the underlying force ﬁelds was carried out beforehand.
A Mixed Quantum-Classical View to the Kinetics of Chemical Reactions 113
 01/12/2013 15:15:59.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00099
View Online

## Page 134

However, we will not dwell any further into the comparison of the diﬀerent
methodologies that could be used for evaluating the potential energies. In the
reminder of this chapter we consider only DFT and hybrid DFT/MM based
approaches.
5.3.1.2 Practical Details
The central point to simulate decoherence is to generate independent MD
simulations on the PESs of the two electronic states of interest. To have a nonzero overlap at time t ¼ 0 (a coherent superposition of states), these MD
simulations will share the same initial conditions (positions and momentum). In
later times the classical nuclei will feel diﬀerent forces on the two PESs and
will diverge over time. Once the two trajectories have been carried out, the
function J
12 can be calculated using the values of x1j , x2j , p1j , x2j , DE12 and
DK12 evaluated along the diverging trajectories. This operation is repeated with
diﬀerent initial conditions to sample the desired thermodynamics ensemble.
In summary the simulation protocol is schematized on Figure 5.5. It consists
of three steps. The ﬁrst one generates a so-called initial MD trajectory on a
single PES. When possible we apply a harmonic bias on the energy gap DE12 to
impose the molecular system to sample only the seam. With DFT or DFT/MM
approaches the initial MD simulations can be run for a few tens of picoseconds
with the current available computers. Of course if computationally less expansive approaches are possible ( e.g. SCC-DFTB/MM or MM), the initial
sampling can be run for several nanoseconds or more. These MD simulations
furnish the initial conditions for running the sets of independent diverging
trajectories on the two PESs. These MD simulations have to be run for several
tens of femtoseconds, i.e. the time necessary for complete decoherence to occur.
The MD trajectories are ﬁnally treated with a home-made program written by
Dr Jan Rˇ eza´cˇ , of the Academy of Science of the Czech Republic.
30
Figure 5.5 Schematic representation of the three-step simulation protocol employed
for simulating decoherence in large molecular systems.

## Page 135

There is one point that we have not discussed so far, that is, the deﬁnition of
the widths a/C0 1 = 2
j entering the deﬁnition of the functions Gnj . This is a point that
has to be addressed in any method that uses Gaussian functions for approximating a real nuclear wave packet. Various prescriptions can be found in the
literature based, for example, on high temperature expressions
29 or on quantum
chemistry reference computations. 34 In our previous work we have tested
diﬀerent values for a/C0 1 = 2
j with no signiﬁcant inﬂuence on the time evolution of
the function J12. Indeed, given the intrinsic approximation of the simulation
protocol we use, the conclusions we are in position to draw regarding the
mechanism of decoherence are not qualitatively aﬀected by the particular
choice made for choosing a
j . Therefore all the results given in the examples
below have been obtained using high-temperature expressions for the widths
(aj ¼ 6Mj kBT/C22h/C0 2, with Mj being the mass of the nuclei j). Examples are given in
Table 5.1.
5.3.2 Activation of Dioxygen by Cuprous Complexes
As a ﬁrst applicative example we consider the case of a spin crossing reaction
between a singlet and a triplet electronic state within a copper dioxygen
complex. This process is relevant to the question of the activation of molecular
oxygen by coordination to a metallic inorganic complex. We ﬁrst provide a
general biochemical background of this type of reaction and we then report our
analysis of quantum decoherence.
5.3.2.1 Biochemical Background
Aerobic organisms take advantage of the high oxidative power of dioxygen to
functionalize a wide variety of metabolites they need.
35,36 Yet the use of
molecular oxygen in vivo is not a trivial choice for various reasons. First, the
kinetics of the reactions between O 2 and organic molecules are generally slow,
as noticed by everyday life experience: combustion is initiated only when a
source of thermal energy is present. This is a consequence of the so-called
spin-mismatch between free dioxygen, a triplet molecule, and most of organic
metabolites it reacts with which are singlet species.
37,38 Consequently

deﬁnition of the nuclear wave packets.
Element j a j/Bohr/C0 2 a/C0 1 = 2
j /A˚
C 124.7 0.192
H 10.4 0.856
O 166.3 0.147
N 145.6 0.157
Cu 658.7 0.074
A Mixed Quantum-Classical View to the Kinetics of Chemical Reactions 115
 01/12/2013 15:15:59.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00099
View Online

## Page 136

oxidoreductive processes involving triplet molecular oxygen should involve
spin-crossing reaction steps which are formally forbidden, and thus are slow.
Second the partial reduction of dioxygen is accompanied with the risk of
releasing reactive oxygen species (ROS) that are deleterious for tissues and
cells.
41 Evolution permitted the emergence of enzymes that are able to catalyze
spin-crossing reactions in a controlled way to cope with these diﬃculties.
Typically dioxygen can be made reactive toward exogenous substrates
(i.e. activated) by coordination to metal ions (mostly Fe(
II) or Cu( I) protein
active sites) or by covalent linkage to organic prosthetic groups like ﬂavins.
Curiously despite the ubiquity of enzymatic processes relying on O
2 activation,
the studies of the deep molecular bases governing these reactions, and in
particular how enzymes catalyze them, remain sparse in the literature.
We have been interested in the family of non-coupled dicopper monooxygenases such as peptidylglycine alpha-hydroxylating monooxygenase
(PHM) or dopamine beta-monooxygenase (D bM). These enzymes have the
remarkable ability to hydroxylate inert C–H bonds through the activation of
dioxygen molecules at a mononuclear cuprous active site (named Cu
M).39
Much experimental and theoretical eﬀort has been spent to unravel the
hydroxylation mechanism by PHM and D bM. The considerable interest for
this type of reaction has also triggered intense research on the development of
mononuclear copper sites mimicking the chemistry of the enzymatic parents.
Recently we have considered some in silico models of the Cu
M active site of
PHM (Figure 5.6) and addressed the question of the kinetics of the spincrossing reaction within these complexes.
30,32 We have previously shown that
these complexes are decent minimal models of the enzymatic cupric superoxide
adducts.
40 In the enzyme the coordination sphere of the Cu M active site is
Figure 5.6 [Cu(MimN3)O2] þ and [Cu(MimN3S)O2] þ copper dioxygen adducts. The
numbers are the lengths and their standard deviations in parenthesis for
the important chemical bonds involved in decoherence. The values are
averaged over few picoseconds of DFT-BOMD simulations. The ﬁrst and
second lines correspond to the singlet and triplet spin-states respectively.

View Online

## Page 137

composed of two histidine residues and one methionine one. The latter introduces a thioether function in the coordination sphere of a biological copper ion,
which is not common for copper oxidoreductases. Indeed its role in the catalysis of the PHM or D bM reactions has been questioned by various groups
without, though, a conclusive answer. Here to test the inﬂuence of the sulfur
ligand on the characteristic decoherence times we consider two complexes built
on the ligands Min
N3 and Mim N2S.
5.3.2.2 Molecular Description of Decoherence
Following the computational protocol described in section 5.3.1 we performed
Born–Oppenheimer molecular dynamics (BOMD) simulations using density
functional theory with the Perdew–Becke–Ernzerhof (PBE) functional
41 with
the relativistic eﬀective core potential (RECP) technique for all atoms apart
from hydrogens for which the DZVP-GGA (double-zeta-valence-withpolarization, optimized for generalized-gradient-approximation functional) basis
set was used. DFT computations are realized with a locally modiﬁed version of
the software deMon2k.
42 The details of the computational protocol can be found
in the original publications 30,32 and here we only recall its general lines.
For each complex, two initial BOMD trajectories were run for around
10 picoseconds on the triplet and singlet surfaces. These initial MD simulations
were biased by a harmonic potential applying on the triplet–singlet energy gap
to maintain it within k
BT around zero. In this way decoherence was evaluated
for molecular conformations close to the seam. The positions and momentums
of the nuclei, as well as the Kohn–Sham determinants, were extracted every
75 fs from the initial trajectories to furnish the starting conditions of the
diverging trajectories. A total of 290 and 256 sets of diverging trajectories were
accumulated for the [Cu(Mim
N3)O2]1 and the [Cu(Mim N2S)O2]1 complexes
respectively. The coeﬃcients c1 and c2 were evolved along the diverging trajectories according to the TDSE and a fourth-order Runge–Kutta algorithm.
The initial values c
1(0) and c2(0) were set to 1 and 0 respectively. The results
were not sensitive to the direction of the reaction (T -So rS -T ) and are
presented together in the following discussion.
The average decoherence curves for the [Cu(Mim N3)O2]1 complex are
shown in Figure 5.7. Similar curves were found for the sulfurous complex and
are not represented. We ﬁrst remark on the Gaussian shapes of the functions
J, J
phase and Joverlap. After this initial decay the function J reaches zero
within ca. 25 fs. A characteristic decoherence time of 7.9 fs, deﬁned
by Jðt ¼ tdecÞ¼ exp /C0 t2
2t2
dec
/C18/C19
/C25 0:61, is found. A similar value is found for the
[Cu(MimN2S)O2]1 complex. We also remark that both the phase and the
overlap contributions play a role in the decay at shorter times. However, it is
the divergence of the overlap term that ensures an irreversible decay to zero at
longer times. Since J
overlap is a product of 3 N /C0 6 individual overlap terms, the
probability of getting back to a non-zero overlap after loss is very small in a
molecular system.

## Page 139

coupling term seems acceptable, although it has a noticeable contribution to the
total overlap term and to decoherence. As noted in ref. 30, the dominant
contribution of the CuO
2 core can be understood by considering the variations
in the average and standard deviations of the Cu–O and O–O bond lengths
between the singlet and triplet states. The divergence of the nuclear wave
packets along the sets of diverging trajectories stems principally from the diverging motion of the copper and oxygen atoms. The diﬀerence in bond lengths
is well understood from the viewpoint of the equilibrium electronic structures
and has been described by various groups. When going from the triplet to the
singlet spin state, the charge transfer from the copper d-orbitals to the f*
dioxygen orbital is enhanced and induces a lengthening of the O–O bond and a
shortening of the Cu–O bond (see Figure 5.6).
44,45

the other. We recall that in our simulation we set c
1(t ¼ 0) ¼ 1 and c2(t ¼ 0) ¼ 0.

probability calculated with the analytical expression, eqn (5.19). Finally Pnum

The graphs shown in Figure 5.9 illustrate the interplay between the oscillations of the probability to measure the system in the second electronic state
(curve hc2
2i) and the loss of electronic coherences (curve hJ12i). Indeed the latter
function reaches a ﬁrst maximum around 0.08 at t ¼ 25 fs. Nevertheless decoherence has already completely occurred at that time, thereby damping the
actual hopping probability. The curve hc2
2:J12i reﬂects the contribution of each

for t ¼ 11 fs.
The comparison of the hopping probabilities for the two complexes is instructive. The replacement of one –NH 2 group by a –SMe function does not
have any inﬂuence on the characteristic decoherence times (Table 5.2). However, the hopping probability increases by a factor of almost three with the
mixed quantum–classical approaches. This diﬀerence is entirely attributed to
the stronger spin-orbit coupling for the [Cu(Mim
N2S)O2]1 complex (160 cm /C0 1
vs. 85 cm /C0 1) in line with the well-known capability of heavier atoms to
favor spin-orbit couplings. The shapes of the curves plotted on Figure 5.9
for the [Cu(Mim
N2S)O2]1 complex are strikingly similar to those of the
[Cu(MimN2N)O2]1 complex. However, the amplitude of the oscillations for hc2
2i
is larger for the former. Given that the decoherence function is similar for both
system the function hc2
2:J12i is larger for sulfurous complex that for the nitrogenous complex (0.06 vs. 0.017).
Finally we ﬁnd for both complexes that the Landau–Zener probabilities are
much larger than the ones obtained in the mixed quantum–classical approach
(Table 5.2). These values would lead to reactions occurring in the adiabatic

## Page 141

regime. In addition the Pnum
LZ values fail to reproduce the diﬀerence between the
MimN2S and MimN2N based adducts. This point that can be rationalized easily
since the hopping values reﬂects an adiabatic-like reaction, i.e. they are not
sensitive to the exact value of the coupling above a certain threshold.
Taken together the number results could suggest that the introduction of the
methionine residue within the coordination sphere of the Cu M active site helps
in catalyzing the activation of dioxygen by allowing stronger coupling between
the triplet and singlet states. Nevertheless the numbers presented in Table 5.2
have been obtained for a minimal model of the enzymatic active site and the
simulations have been performed in the gas phase! More work is needed to
address the relevance of this hypothesis in the real enzyme.
We now turn to the modeling of decoherence in another important class of
physical chemistry, that of electron transfers.
5.3.3 Decoherence and Long-Range Electron Transfers
5.3.3.1 Biochemical Context
Together with proton transfers, electron transfers are one of the most common
physicochemical reactions encountered in the biological medium. Electron
transfers may process over short distances (1–4 A ˚ ) as seen frequently in enzymatic chemical reactions. However, they may also occur over more than
10 A˚ . Such long-range electron transfers (LRET) are ubiquitous along the
cellular respiratory chain or in the photosynthetic system. Both rely on the
transport of electrons over tens of nanometers from proteins to proteins with
the ﬁnality to supply chemical energy for the production of adenosine triphosphate (ATP) molecules in ATP synthases. More generally, many enzymes need
to be supplied by electrons to fulﬁll their catalytic roles and such electron deliveries are accomplished through LRET.
We have been interested in the biological electron transfer between the
cofactor tryptophan tryptophylquinone (TTQ) and a type I copper center.
46,47
The former is part of the membrane enzyme, methyl amine dehydrogenase
(MADH), while the latter is the redox center of the blue copper protein,

A Mixed Quantum-Classical View to the Kinetics of Chemical Reactions 121
 01/12/2013 15:15:59.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00099

## Page 142

amicyanin. The electron transfer between MADH and amicyanin is the ﬁrst
link of the respiratory chain of the bacterium Paracoccus denitriﬁcans when
growing under methylotrophic conditions ( i.e. with solely one-carbon-atom
molecules for food). 48
The modeling of biological electron transfer presents its own challenges
compared with the modeling of other biophysical processes. Among them is the
participation of a large number of atoms that are actors in the electron transfer
process per se , that is, they require to be treated at the quantum mechanical
level. Indeed it is often necessary to consider the intervening molecular fragment alongside the electron donor and acceptor.
We have chosen to investigate decoherence in this biological electron transfer
process following a line of increasing complexity. Thus our ﬁrst step has focused on the TTQ cofactor.
39 We plan to add more and more elements in our
modeling in future work. We have performed our MD simulations at the DFT
and hybrid DFT/MM level of computation. We have carried out a systematic
comparison of diﬀerent methodologies on the modeling of decoherence for the
TTQ cofactor. We refer the reader to the original article and we restrict our
attention here to the DFT-based modeling.
33
5.3.3.2 The Local Origin of Decoherence within TTQ
We start our study by investigating a model of the biological TTQ cofactor in
the gas phase (Figure 5.10). We consider two redox states, the fully reduced,
Figure 5.10 Model of the TTQ cofactor. The average distances of the bonds whose
length vary by more than 0.01 A˚ between the two redox states are shown.
These distances were obtained from around 25 ps of gas phase DFTBOMD simulations respectively of the anionic and radical forms (ﬁrst
and second lines).
122 Chapter 5
 01/12/2013 15:15:59.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00099
View Online

## Page 143

anionic form and the partially oxidized, radical form, denoted TTQ /C0 and TTQ/C15
respectively. The initial DFT-BOMD simulations were realized with deMon2k.
The energies and gradients were computed with the PBE98-PBE 49 exchangecorrelation (XC) function with empirical corrections to account for dispersion
eﬀects.
50,51 All the computations are realized with the DZVP-GGA atomic
basis set and the Dunning GEN-A2 auxiliary basis set 52 to expand the auxiliary
function densities. An adaptive grid of medium accuracy was employed for the
numerical integration of the XC energies and potentials, while an asymptotic
multipolar expansion technique was used for the calculation of the long-range
three-center Coulomb integrals.
53 For each redox states three simulations were
launched with diﬀerent initial conditions. After an equilibration phase of few
picoseconds, the data were accumulated for around 17 ps. In total 25 ps BOMD
simulations were run for both the TTQ
/C0 and the TTQ /C15redox forms, furnishing
an ensemble of 500 set of diverging trajectories. Like for the spin-crossing reaction described in section 5.2.2, the results are similar for both the forward and
backward redox reactions and are grouped together in the following discussion.
The analysis of the structures along the MD simulations shows that the two
redox forms diﬀer essentially by the lengths of the four covalent bonds localized
around the quinol function (Figure 5.10). The hydrogen bond length between
the oxygen atoms is also unsurprisingly lengthened when passing from the
anionic to the radical form. The other bond lengths are not signiﬁcantly affected by the ionization process (the diﬀerences for all the other bonds are less
than 0.01 A˚ ). Despite the aromatic character of TTQ this BOMD-based
structural analysis indicates that oxidation of TTQ aﬀects a group of few
nuclei.
The overlap contribution to decoherence is plotted in Figure 5.11. We recall
that since the full electron transfer system (TTQ and the copper center) is not
included in the modeling the phase term ( hJ
phaseiÞ and the contributions of
other atoms ( e.g. that of the amicyanin) are not taken into account. For ease of
description the characteristic decay constant of the function hJX;P;XP
overlap i will be
assimilated to the characteristic decoherence time. A time of 7.7 fs is found.
The analysis in terms of atomic contributions is also reported in Figure 5.11,
with an apparently good approximation (compare the full and the circled
curves). The group of four atoms deﬁning the quinol function (‘group 1’, see
Figure 5.11, right) is the initiator of decoherence at short times ( o15 fs). This is
obviously directly connected with the diﬀerence in equilibrium structures of the
TTQ
/C0 and the TTQ/C15forms. Taken alone, however, these atoms do not account
for the decay of the overlap function to zero. Eﬀectively at t ¼ 15 fs
hJX;P
group1i 4 0:6 and at t ¼ 50 fs, the function still amounts to almost 0.4.
A partial rephasing with a period of 22 fs is seen. This probably corresponds to
the C–O and C–C nuclear vibrations of the quinol function. Moving to the
contributions of the other groups of atoms we remark that they are mandatory
in order to lead to complete and irreversible decoherence. Individually each
atom of groups 2, 3 or 4 in Figure 5.11 contributes only moderately to the decay
of overlap. However due to their large number they produce a mass eﬀect.
A Mixed Quantum-Classical View to the Kinetics of Chemical Reactions 123
 01/12/2013 15:15:59.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00099
View Online

## Page 144

Moreover, a certain delay is apparently necessary for atoms of group 2 to 4 to
start contributing to decoherence. This delay is all the more important that
atoms are distant from the quinol group (see for example, groups 3 and 4).
The mechanism of decoherence that emerges from these simulations is that
decoherence is initiated by the divergent motions of few atoms, the ones directly
concerned by the change of electronic state and that the divergence propagates
dynamically to the remaining atoms even though the equilibrium position of
these latter atoms is not aﬀected by the electronic transition. Decoherence thus
combines structural and dynamical aspects of the system. Structural aspects tell
us which atoms are primarily aﬀected by the transition, their number, their
masses, etc. and determines if they might explain decoherence alone (as found
in the case of the copper dioxygen adduct) or not (as found in TTQ); this aspect
was discussed in section 5.2.2. Structure also determines how the group of
atoms which is primarily aﬀected is coupled to the remaining ones, and how the
initial divergence might propagate to the whole system. Dynamical aspects tell
us how the number of atoms with divergent motions increases progressively
with time and how these atoms ﬁnally contribute to decoherence, but only some
time after the primary group.
5.3.3.3 Inclusion of Solvatation Eﬀects
Having identiﬁed the intramolecular mechanism of decoherence within the
TTQ cofactor in gas phase, we now investigate the inﬂuence of its environment.
Within the MADH enzyme, the TTQ cofactor is surrounded on one hand by
protein residues and on the other hand by water molecules. To include these
Figure 5.11 Decay of the overlap contribution to decoherence within the TTQ
cofactor in gas phase. Left: total overlap function (full line). Right:
deﬁnition of contributions of the three atom groups (dashed/dotted
lines). The circled line is the product of the diﬀerent contributions. All
the curves are real parts of the function averaged over the sets of
diverging DFT-BOMD trajectories.
124 Chapter 5
 01/12/2013 15:15:59.

## Page 145

eﬀects explicitly we employed a so-called hybrid DFT/MM approach 36,54 where
the TTQ is treated by means of DFT and the surrounding molecules are treated
by MM. Our DFT/MM approach follows a subtractive scheme with electrostatic embedding. We use the ﬂexible simple point charge (SPCF) water
model.
55 Given the short timescales of decoherence within the TTQ cofactor, it
is probably important not to freeze the O–H stretches in the BOMD simulations. We chose this model because it has been shown to correctly reproduce
the various important dynamical properties of water ( e.g. the hydrogen bond
lifetimes and the Debye relation times).
61 The curves reported on Figure 5.12
indicate the weak inﬂuence of the water environment on the decay of the nuclear overlap function. The water molecules only play a role for around 20fs,
apparently as a supplementary contributor to the mass eﬀect already described
for the TTQ atoms belonging to groups 2 to 4 in Figure 5.11. This reinforces the
conclusion that decoherence has a very local origin for the molecular systems
investigated so far.
Lockwood and colleagues performed an analysis of decoherence in a
ruthenium-modiﬁed blue copper protein similar to amicyanin.
23 They found a
short characteristic decoherence time of 2.4 fs, which they attributed on one
hand to the diverging motion of the protein nuclei and on the other hand to the
solvent molecules. Their conclusion was that ‘both solvent and protein dynamics can aﬀect both the rate and mechanism of electron transfer’,
23 which is
diﬀerent to our conclusions on solvated TTQ where the solvent does not seem
to play any role in decoherence. More precisely, solvent molecules start to play
a role once decoherence has already occurred due to the intramolecular motions within the TTQ. Lockwood et al. used a classical force ﬁeld for all the
atoms, including those of the copper and ruthenium complexes, and a rigid SPC
water model. In addition they did not carry out large ensembles of diverging
Figure 5.12 Average decay of the overlap term for the TTQ cofactor time in the gas
phase and in water. SPCF, ﬂexible simple point charge.
