## Page 146

MD simulations from the initial classical MD simulation assuming a short time
process (see section 5.2.3.4 for more details on this type of approximation),
which might not have been able to observe the type of delayed mechanism we
saw for TTQ. Beyond the fact that the two studies investigated diﬀerent
systems, a pair of metal complexes and an organic cofactor respectively, insight
would be gained by repeating previous investigations using more accurate
ab initio approaches.
5.3.3.4 Decoherence and Bridge Lengths in Long Range
Electron Transfers
To go one step further into the modeling o f decoherence for biological electron
transfers, it is necessary to include the entire donor–bridge–acceptor (D-B-A)
system and its environment in the computation. Although hybrid QM/MM approaches will clearly continue to be a key tool, special care needs be taken over the
choice of the QM technique. In our group we have invested eﬀort in the constrained DFT (cDFT) in the formalism introduced by Dederichs et al .
56 and
further developed by Wu and Van Voorhis57 based on optimized potential theory.
The purpose of the cDFT approach is to deﬁne diabatic states that resemble
as much as possible the phenomenological Marcus states. To this end, a
Lagrange multiplier technique is employed to apply a constraint on the charge
distribution within the supramolecule. In the case of an electron transfer
reaction, an adequate functional F subjected to minimization is written as:
F r½/C138 /C17 E r½/C138 þl

where E r½/C138is the usual DFT functional, s is the electronic spin, and wD-A(r) is a
function that ensures that the integration of the electronic density between the
donor and acceptor group is equal to plus or minus the charge diﬀerence
between the redox cofactors N
cnsðD/C0 AÞ. The atomic charge within molecules is
not unambiguously deﬁned in quantum mechanics and so various population
schemes can be used in cDFT computations. By adjusting the value of
N
cnsðD/C0 AÞ, one can estimate the cDFT energy of the two diabatic states of the
chemical reaction of interest and also the electronic coupling term. The cDFT
scheme is compatible with geometry optimizations and ab initio MD, therefore
opening the door toward the modeling of decoherence. We have implemented
this method in the DFT program deMon2k. 58,59 The main advantage of our
implementation is its high speed, which is achieved thanks to the intensive use
of auxiliary electronic densities for the computation of the coulomb, exchangecorrelation, and constraint energies and potentials.
60
A biologically relevant (donnor-bridge-acceptor) system we have investigated is represented in Figure 5.13. The electron donor and acceptor roles are
played by two lithium ions while the bridge is composed of a polyglycine chain
of increasing length. The ionization potential (IP) of lithium amounts to 5.39
eV which is close to IPs found in nature for biological redox cofactors, making

this element a descent minimal model of real D-A systems. In recently published work, 65 our objective was to evaluate the inﬂuence of the bridge length
on the electronic coupling between the two diabatic states and on the characteristic decoherence times. cDFT-BOMD simulations were carried out in gas
phase and in SPC water molecules to estimate average values of these two
quantities over 1 ps.
If one is interested solely in the estimation of a characteristic decoherence
time, an alternative to the generation and the analysis of a large set of diverging

## Page 148

behind the mathematical formulae. Indeed the approach we have followed
attributes one of the main source of decoherence to the diverging motion of the
NWP between diﬀerent electronic states. Eventually this information propagates from neighbors to neighbors. However, in the examples described above,
decoherence has a very local origin involving groups of few atoms.
5.4 Conclusions
The time evolution of molecular systems, including multiple electronic states, is
in principle described by complicated sets of equations which include the dynamical coupling between the electrons and the nuclear motions. Given the
huge number of particles usually encountered in condensed phase systems,
these equations have no simple analytical solutions and cannot be easily
simulated by numerical approaches. In addition, their mathematical complexity
hampers the establishment of intuitive mental representations of the physicochemical processes they describe. A valuable route to simpliﬁcation is to use
classical mechanics to describe the nuclear motion. Indeed although they are of
critical importance for the understanding of the kinetics of multiple electronic
states reactions, non-Born–Oppenheimer eﬀects are manifest only for brief
periods of time (say on the fs timescales), i.e. when the coupling between the
quantum states become signiﬁcant, while most of the time the semi-classical
description remains perfectly acceptable.
In this chapter we have focused on the application of a mixed quantum–
classical approach for rationalizing the kinetics of chemical reactions involving
more than one electronic state. While previous theoretical frameworks like
those of Marcus or Lorquet considered a complete decoupling between the
quantum and classical phases of evolution of the molecular system, we have
proposed an original path where the quantum-to-classical transition operates in
a smooth fashion. As a result we have ended up with a new expression for
estimating the probability for the system to hop from one step to the other
when decoherence occurs. In the second part of this chapter we have shown
how the characteristic decoherence times could be evaluated by atomistic
simulations on large molecular systems (from 30 to 40 000 atoms in the
Table 5.3 Characteristic decoherence times toverlap (fs) in the short time
approximation (eqn (5.44)). The inter-lithium distances (R Li-Li)
are given in A˚ .

128 Chapter 5
 01/12/2013 15:15:59.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00099
View Online

## Page 149

presented cases) or for complicated electronic structures (see the copper complex case).
The role of decoherence and its manifestations started to be appreciated in
physical chemistry only in the last decade of the 20th century. Although we
have focused here on the importance of decoherence in the establishment of
statistical kinetic theories of chemical reactions, much eﬀort is currently being
spent by various groups in diﬀerent contexts. We believe that the knowledge
obtained by the type of approaches used in this chapter, which provide a
molecular understanding of the mechanism governing decoherence in complex
systems, are likely to feed other approaches such as the semi-classical nonBorn–Oppenheimer MD simulations techniques ( e.g. decoherence correction to
the surface hopping scheme
27,61–62 or decay of mixing types of algorithm 63)
where decoherence eﬀects need to be introduced.
Together with other previous studies our results provide initial elements with
which to think about the interplay between decoherence and the structure and
the dynamics of a molecular system. It is clear that more work is needed in that
direction and decoherence may well be controlled in other systems by yet unknown molecular mechanisms. We are currently pursuing our eﬀorts on real
molecular systems like proteins. Some interesting questions can nevertheless be
formulated. One may think for example of the development of simple models
allowing quick estimations of the characteristic decoherence times without the
need to perform MD simulations. This would obviously be useful for experimental groups. One may also ask to which extent it is possible to modulate the
rate of a chemical reaction playing on the rapidity of the decoherence process
through the modiﬁcations of the structure and of the dynamics of the molecular
matrix. More generally it is probably worthwhile to think about the chemistry
that would emerge for systems lying at the border between the classical and the
quantum domains.
Owing to their long evolutionary hist ory, biological systems may be good
candidates for such a mixed quantum–classical behavior. In the recent years it
has been suggested that long lived coherences ( 4500 fs) might be at play in the
eﬃciency of the light harvesting in bacteria,
64,65 posing the question of the
possible role of the protein matrix in s uch a process. Rela ted questions are
currently emerging from theoreti cal works on donor–bridge–acceptor
electron transfer in both synthetic and biological systems. It is now well
established that electron transfer by tunneling may follow diﬀerent mechanisms ranging from fully cohere nt to incoherent (hopping) regimes.
66–67 The
transition between these limit mechani sms is intimately related to the notion
of decoherence (or dephasing) and dese rves thorough investigation in the
future. The question of proton tunneling in enzymatic reactions is yet another
type of processes whose satisfactory d escription might require the notions of
decoherence to be included in the kinetic models. 68 Indeed a recent model
based on the ideas developed in ref. 30 and in this chapter has been recently
proposed for this type of chemical reaction. 69 In conclusion, we anticipate
that investigations about the relevan ce of quantum decoherence in physicochemical processes are likely to be very active in the coming years, eventually

leading to spectacular outcomes in t erms of new concepts and technological
applications.

## Page 153

CHAPTER 6
Adiabatic Treatment of
Torsional Anharmonicity and
Mode Coupling in Molecular
Partition Functions and
Statistical Rate Coeﬃcients:
Application to Hydrogen
Peroxide
ZEB C. KRAMER AND REX T. SKODJE*
Department of Chemistry and Biochemistry, University of Colorado,
Boulder, CO 80309-0215, USA
*Email: rex.skodje@colorado.edu
6.1 Introduction
The computation of internal state densities and partition functions for polyatomic molecules is an essential task in the theoretical treatment of molecular
gases. A ﬁrst principles approach to the statistical thermodynamics of polyatomic gases requires the computation of the internal molecular energy levels
based on an ab initio quantum mechanical (QM) determination of portions of the
potential energy surface.
1 Likewise, statistical theories of chemical reactions,
such as Rice–Ramsberger–Kassel–Marcus (RRKM) theory or transition state
RSC Theoretical and Computational Chemistry Series No. 6
Reaction Rate Constant Computations: Theories and Applications
Edited by Keli Han and Tianshu Chu
r The Royal Society of Chemistry 2014
Published by the Royal Society of Chemistry, www.rsc.org
133
 01/12/2013 15:16:09.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00133

## Page 154

theory (TST), permit the computation of reaction rate coeﬃcients without the
need for dynamical modeling provided partition functions for the activated
complex and the reagent molecules can be accurately obtained.
The traditional approach to the calculation of molecular partition functions
makes use of a separable normal mode approximation for molecular vibrations
and a rigid rotor treatment of molecular rotations obtained from single point
QM determination of energies, structures and frequencies. This simple approach is often found to be adequate for many practical purposes, even though
signiﬁcant numerical errors may be incurred. With the advent of powerful
computers, it has become possible to compute QM energies for hundreds or
thousands of molecular geometries of reasonably large molecules. In principle,
this makes possible the computation of partition functions that go beyond the
harmonic oscillator–rigid rotor (HO-RR) approximation. Despite these advances, it is still not possible to obtain an accurate rotational–vibrational
eigenvalue spectrum for most polyatomic molecules. It is, however, of great
importance to develop methods that at least partly incorporate the eﬀects of
anharmonicity, mode coupling and non-rigidity into ﬁrst principles computations of statistical quantities.
A particularly challenging problem in the statistical thermodynamics of gases
involves the treatment of torsional motions such as internal rotations.
2 These
torsional motions are often of low frequency and high amplitude and are poorly
described by a normal mode description at most elevated temperatures. Furthermore, it is quite common for multiple minima to occur along the torsional
coordinate. When the barrier to torsional isomerization is not too high, the inﬂuence of these conformers must be fully included. For chemical reactions, this
may lead to multiple non-equivalent transitions states that contribute in toto to
the overall rate. In general, the frequencies of the remaining vibrational modes
will be functions of the torsional coordinate, and hence the conformational
isomer, as will be the rotational constants. The anharmonic coupling to the
torsional motion is often found to lead to much larger breakdowns of the
HO-RR model than coupling between other higher frequency normal modes.
There have been numerous attempts to treat torsional motion in molecular
statistical mechanics and we do not attempt to review this vast literature here.
We do note, however, the ground breaking early work of Pitzer and Gwinn
3
who proposed a separable hindered rotor model. This approach, and closely
related variations, is still widely used today as the most common method to go
beyond the HO-RR model.
4,5 We also point out that several non-separable
methods have recently been developed that appear to be promising. 6–11
Our approach to the treatment of torsional anharmonicity makes use of the
vibrationally adiabatic approximation. We originally introduced this analysis
in our previous treatment of the HO
2 þ HO2 reaction where torsional motion
played a key role. 12 The adiabatic approximation is a common scheme to treat
mode coupling for systems that exhibit a separation of timescales. 13,14 Since
torsional motion is typically a very low frequency molecular vibration, the
adiabatic approximation appears well suited to this problem. Hence we take the
torsional coordinate, t, as ‘slow’ while the remaining normal modes are ‘fast’.
134 Chapter 6
 01/12/2013 15:16:09.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00133
View Online

## Page 155

We adopt the most basic version of the adiabatic approximation in which
nonadiabatic couplings between the vibrational modes and to the rotational
degrees of freedom are ignored. For problems that exhibit chemical reaction,
we include a second slow coordinate, the reaction coordinate s. We need make
no additional assumptions about the relative timescales of t and s. For reactive
problems, we note that the conventional saddle point, X
0, associated with the
transition state is replaced by a ridge line, X0(t). The ridgeline passes through
the conventional saddle point as well as any other conformers of the transition
state. The reactive ﬂux is computed through a generalized dividing surface that
coincides with X
0(t). In this scheme, the reactive ﬂux occurring at all values of
the torsional angle are included in an even-handed way. The rotational partition functions are likewise parameterized by the torsional coordinate in
addition to the reaction coordinate. When necessary, the eﬀects of quantum
tunneling can be included in terms of tunneling through a series of barriers
computed for frozen values of the torsional angle.
While it is generally straightforward to construct the adiabatic Hamiltonian
for a bound or reactive problem, the full computation of the coupled partition
function can be problematic. For each set of quantum numbers describing
excitations of the fast vibrational modes, a distinct vibrationally adiabatic
potential curve is generated for the torsion. In principle, this may require the
solution of thousands or even millions of one-dimensional Schro ¨dinger equations to obtain the required level positions. To circumvent this computational
bottleneck, we introduce a semiclassical method where the torsion is treated
classically by phase space integration while the remaining fast modes are
modeled as fully quantum mechanical. As we demonstrate, this approach
generally yields excellent results.
In this work we investigate the inﬂuence of the torsional motion on the
statistical description of the hydrogen peroxide molecule. Hydrogen peroxide
provides a good system for study since it is the simplest molecule to exhibit
torsion and has been thoroughly studied in all its isotopic variants.
15 Furthermore, a high quality potential energy surface (PES) is available due to
Kuhn et al.
16 The equilibrium geometry of the HOOH molecule is non-planar,
which leads to a double barrier potential energy proﬁle along the dihedral
angle. The higher barrier to internal rotation, in the cis conﬁguration, is approximately 7.6 kcal mol
/C0 1. The O–O bond can rupture at excitation energies
of 48.4 kcal mol/C0 1 and higher. In additional to numerous theoretical treatments
of HOOH, 17–21 including our recent six-dimensional wave packet treatment of
the reaction dynamics, 22 there have been experimental studies carried out for
this system which provide useful data for comparison. 23a–d,24a–d,25–27
In section 6.2, we present the formal theory of the adiabatic approach to the
computation of thermodynamic functions with the emphasis on the high
amplitude torsional motion. For simplicity, only cases with a single torsional
degree of freedom are addressed. An algorithm is presented for the computation of rotational–vibrational state sums with the torsional motion adiabatically coupled to the remaining rotational-vibrational degrees of freedom.
The calculations are made feasible with the introduction of a semiclasssical
Adiabatic Treatment of Torsional Anharmonicity and Mode Coupling 135
 01/12/2013 15:16:09.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00133
View Online

## Page 156

technique for the torsional state sum that obviates the need for repeated
diagonalization of the vibrationally adiabatic Hamiltonian. The methodology
required for reactive problems is introduced in section 6.3. In this case, the
density of states is now written as explicit functions of two slow coordinates,
the torsion and the reaction coordinate. A technique to include quantum
tunneling through the reactive ridge is discussed. In section 6.4, the numerical
results for the hydrogen peroxide molecule are presented. It is found that
molecular partition functions and thermodynamic functions are modiﬁed by
factors on the order of 10% when the torsional coupling is included. The
RRKM reaction rate coeﬃcient is modiﬁed by a similar amount. Section 6.5
presents a brief conclusion.
6.2 Adiabatic Theory of Molecular State Density
for Non-Separable Large Amplitude Motion:
Bound Motion
The basic approach we adopt in the adiabatic method is quite simple and we
present the formalism brieﬂy for bound molecules in this section and then, in
section 6.3, for reactive systems. Further details may be found in our earlier
work.
12
For a bound nonlinear polyatomic molecule consisting of n atoms, we imagine that a well-deﬁned large amplitude coordinate, such as a torsion, can be
deﬁned based on physical considerations. For a typical internal rotation, this
coordinate might be selected to be a dihedral angle. In other applications, we
might pick the large amplitude coordinate to follow a numerically determined
path of steepest descent from a saddle point of the barrier to internal rotation.
We denote this coordinate as t and make no strict mathematical requirement
that this coordinate is periodic. For simplicity in this presentation, we assume
that the curvilinear torsional coordinate coincides with a normal mode vibration near the minimum of the potential well, although it is not strictly necessary. We decompose the total 3 n-dimensional quantum Hamiltonian as:
bH
tot ¼ bHtrans þ bHrot tðÞ þ bHvib bpt; t;bp; qðÞ ð 6:1Þ
where the vectors p and q describe the 3 n-7 small amplitude vibrations. The
coupling between rotation and vibration is neglected except for the (possibly
large) dependence of the rotational constants on t. Focusing on the vibrational
dynamics, we invoke the adiabatic approximation by assuming that each vibrational eigenstate takes the product form cðtÞfðq; tÞ. (For simplicity we
drop the ‘vib’ subscript.) The constituent wavefunctions satisfy:
bH bp
t ¼ 0; t;bp; qðÞ fn q; tðÞ ¼ en tðÞ fn q; tðÞ ð 6:2Þ
bTt þ en tðÞ
/C16/C17
cn;nt tðÞ ¼ En t; nðÞ cn;nt tðÞ ð 6:3Þ
where bTt is the kinetic energy operator for t and n ¼ (n1, n2, ... n3n-7) are the
quantum numbers for the ‘fast’ vibrational degree of freedom.

Of interest here is the cumulative density of states N(E) and the local density
of states r(E), formally deﬁned by the exact expressions:

where Ei are the exact eigenvalues, Y is the Heaviside step function and d is

transform of r(E). The vibrationally adiabatic potential functions en(t)m a y
be approximated by a separable model for the 3 n-7 fast vibrational modes
(invoked at each t) or through a low order perturbation treatment. Unfortunately, the low frequency mode t is a high amplitude degree of freedom,
such as a torsion, that is not expected to be separable at all. To ﬁnd the
density of vibrational states within the vi brationally adiabatic approximation,
it is necessary to solve the one dimension Schro ¨dinger equation (Equation
(2.3)) for each set of quantum numbers n up to a suﬃciently high level of
excitation. While this is not overly bur densome for triatomic molecules, for
larger polyatomics the number of required Schro ¨dinger equations can become
astronomical. Hence, we propose a method to obtain the state densities which
is more computationally feasible while not resorting to unrealistic separable
approximations.
We recall that the classical approximation to the cumulative density of vibrational states can be written as:

The inclusion of overall molecular rotation into the state sums may be carried out using either classical or quantum mechanics using the symmetric top
approximation in which two of the moments of inertia are set equal to their
average. We present here the explicit formulae for the quantum approach. The
key assumption necessary for this treatment is that the rotational constants are
instantaneous functions of the large amplitude t coordinate but that the rotation is otherwise separable from vibration. The symmetric top moments of
inertia I

6.3 Adiabatic Theory for Reactive Systems
The adiabatic model for reactive problems is formulated in a manner similar to
that outlined above for bound polyatomic molecules. The basic distinction is

140 Chapter 6
 01/12/2013 15:16:09.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00133
View Online

## Page 161

that the reactive problem requires the use of two slow large amplitude degrees
freedom, the torsion, t, and the reaction coordinate, s. The reaction coordinate
may be described as the path of steepest descent from the saddle point or
through some other convenient method. Both of degrees of freedom ( s,t) are
treated adiabatically and thus are assumed to be slow compared to the q
vibrations, although no particular timescale separation is presumed to exist
between t and s themselves.
To obtain the rate coeﬃcient, the cumulative density of states must be
computed as a function of s, i.e. N (E, J;s). In particular, for unimolecular reaction such as HOOH -2OH, if the transition state bottleneck occurs at s ¼ s
0,
so that Nz(E, J) ¼ N(E,J;s0), the ( E, J) dependent rate coeﬃcient is: 30
kE ;JðÞ ¼ Nz E;JðÞ
hr E;JðÞ ð6:21Þ
where r(E, J) is the reagent density of states.
The key construction for the reactive problem is the reaction path as a
function of the torsional coordinate t, i.e. X(s,t). Here, X is a 3 n-dimensional
vector and X(s,t) is assumed to be a smooth surface. Hence, in place of usual
saddle point X0, one has a ridge line of saddle points, X0(t), deﬁned through
eV ¼ 0 when t is held ﬁxed. The reaction surface can be viewed as composed of
individual paths of steepest descent (at ﬁxed t) that issue from the reduced
saddle points along the ridgeline. Along each path of ﬁxed t, we carry out an
instantaneous normal mode analysis by projecting out both the torsion and the
reaction coordinate motions from the Hessian, i.e. Fproj ¼ PFP/C0 1. Assuming
the torsion and the reaction coordinate are treated classically while the
remaining 3 n-8 vibrations are treated quantally, we have the semiclassical
vibrational Hamiltonian:

Although quantum tunneling is not important for the HOOH system, for
completeness we note the methodology for incorporating tunneling into the
adiabatic model. Including quantum tunneling into the RRKM determination
of the rate is typically accomplished by replacing the classical ( i.e. no tunneling)
cumulative density of states N

where the state density is rz(e) ¼ dNz(e)/de and P(E– e) is the quantum transmission probability at the reaction coordinate translational energy E–e.W e
deﬁne the energy e as the total excitation energy of the 3 n-8 vibrational modes.
Tunneling occurs through the ridge surface in the adiabatic approximation. For
simplicity, we assume that the tunneling dynamics occurs at ﬁxed t, i.e. the
torsion is dynamically frozen. Thus, a tunneling probability at each t value,
P(E–e,t), may be determined by applying the tunneling method to the torsionally constrained Hamiltonian, H(t ¼ constant). We can calculate the
quantum transmission probability at ﬁxed t by simply applying Equation (6.25)
separately to each t value. Thus, in place of the classical ~NE ; tðÞ we have the
quantum expression:
~Nz

where ~r E; yðÞ ¼ d ~NE ; yðÞ =dE. The choice of tunneling method, such as the
small curvature method, 31 should be adapted to the problem at hand.
6.4 Results for HOOH and HOOD
The HOOH molecule provides a thoroughly studied and well understood
problem that possesses a non-separable torsional coordinate which we will use

modest, the deviation from separable behavior is clear and, potentially, observable. Furthermore, this system is eﬃcient for study since an accurate global

16 using spectroscopic observation and ab initio calculations.

equilibrium geometry. Indeed, the frequency of small amplitude motion along
the dihedral angle is 397 cm
/C0 1, within about 1% of the value for the torsional
normal mode. For heuristic purposes, we have constructed a simple separable
model of the torsion that is described by the one-dimension Schro ¨dinger
equation in the dihedral angle t:

## Page 167

obtained using the separable approximations discussed previously. It is seen
that the conventional HO-RR approximation is in error by roughly 10% at
high energy. Although HO-RR apparently undercounts the state density at low
energy, it overcounts the state density at high energy since it omits the torsional
anharmonicity.
We have also constructed the separable hindered rotor representation of the
state density. This method treats all vibrational modes as harmonic oscillators
with frequencies set to those as the equilibrium geometry except for the torsion;
the torsional energy eigenvalues of the one-dimensional torsional Schro ¨dinger
equation, shown in Equation (6.27), are explicitly included in the state count.
The rotational constants are set to their values at the equilibrium geometry.
It is seen that at low energies the hindered rotor model gives results similar
to the HO-RR method, but the two methods begin to clearly deviate from
each other at energies above the barrier to internal rotation of 7.6 kcal mol
/C0 1.
The hindered rotor model undercounts the state density at high energy by
about 10% due to its neglect of mode coupling. While the diﬀerence from the
adiabatic model and the conventional treatments is fairly modest, it is still
potentially observable. For example, the ‘microcanonical entropy’, k
Blnr(E)
shown in Figure 6.8 indicates that deviations from the separable models are
apparent. The inﬂuence of torsional non-separability is found to be equally
important for canonical ensembles. The canonical partition function Q(T)i s
obtained from the Laplace transform of r(E). Its behavior versus T is shown in
Figure 6.9. The non-separable eﬀects are again seen to be on the order of 10%
over the full temperature range.
Using the same methodology, it is a straightforward exercise to compute the
state density for the isotopomer HOOD. In addition to the frequency shifts and
changes in rotational constants due to the mass substitution m
H-mD, there is
also a modiﬁcation due to the loss of symmetry. In Figure 6.10, we show the
state density versus the excitation energy above the zero point. It is seen that the
separable HO-RR and hindered rotor approximation show a somewhat higher
level of error that was seen for the HOOH species.

Clearly, the harmonic approximation is inappropriate for the transition state.
The separable hindered rotor approximation gives an improvement over the

dependence of the vibrational frequencies and rotational constants is seen to
have a clear and potentially observable eﬀect upon the rate coeﬃcient.
Finally, the inﬂuence of overall rotation can be easily determined by evaluating

## Page 171

6.5 Conclusions
This work proposes a new method for the inclusion of coupling between a
torsional mode and other degrees of freedom in a valid statistical model of a
molecule. While totally separable approximations like the HO-RR method are
quite often useful, they should be used cautiously in quantitative work. The
semiclassical adiabatic method developed here accounts for torsional coupling
while circumventing the necessity for repeated quantum determinations of
torsional eigenenergies at all levels in internal excitation of the molecule. Since
the torsional motion is typically of low frequency, this semiclassical approximation is generally good.
Application of the semiclassical adiabatic method to a model system of
coupled harmonic oscillators showed that the usual separable model severely
overestimates the number of states at higher energy while the semiclassical
method is indistinguishable from the exact state count. The method was successfully tested for many choices of system parameters indicating that the state
counting algorithm was reliable.
The density of states for the hydrogen peroxide, and its isotopomer HOOD,
were calculated using the semiclassica l adiabatic method and the separable
HO-RR and hindered rotor models. Bot h separable models showed a discrepancy from the semiclassical adiaba tic model that included the torsional
coupling. At low energies, the HO-RR m odel underestimated the state density
due to the omission of mode coupling whi le, at high energies, it overcounted
the state density due to it omission of torsional anharmonicity. The hindered
rotor approximation predicted a state density that was too low due to the
neglect of mode coupling. It is clear tha t, while the diﬀerences between the
adiabatic model and the separable models are on the order of 10%, these
diﬀerences to appear in observable quantities amenabl e to experimental
veriﬁcation.
The RRKM rate coeﬃcients for the dissociation of hydrogen peroxide
into OH radicals were calculated using the semi-classical adiabatic,
hindered rotor and HO-RR models. The rate coeﬃcients predicted by the
methods were signiﬁcantly diﬀerent. The HO-RR underestimated the rate coeﬃcient by a factor of 2 or 3. The harmonic treatment of torsion at the transition state is clearly a poor approximation. The hindered rotor approximation
reduces the error to about a factor of 1.5. The deviations between the
separable models and the semiclassical adiabatic model are much greater
for the rate coeﬃcient than for the thermodynamics of the bound molecule.
This is due to both the higher level of torsion coupling at high energies
in the reagent and to the importance of torsional coupling at the transition
state.
While the adiabatic model has been shown to work well for the case of
one slow torsional mode, it is an open question how it will perform for
systems with additional torsional modes. Since multiple torsional coordinates
exist commonly for many polyatomic systems this is an important avenue for
future work.

## Page 174

CHAPTER 7
Dynamics of Chemical Reaction
around a Saddle Point: What
Divides Reacting and NonReacting Trajectories?
SHINNOSUKE KAWAI* AND TAMIKI KOMATSUZAKI
Research Institute of Electronic Science, Hokkaido University, Kita-20
Nishi-10, Kita-ku, Sapporo, Japan 001-0020
*Email: skawai@es.hokudai.ac.jp
7.1 Introduction
Chemical reactions can be regarded as a motion of a mass point on a multidimensional potential energy surface (PES). The reactant and the product
correspond to valleys in the PES where the system is stably trapped before and
after the reaction process, respectively. Between the two valleys corresponding
to the reactant and the product, there can often be found a point called a
(rank-one) saddle point. A rank-one saddle point is deﬁned as a point at which
the potential energy is maximum along one direction and minimum along the
others. The former direction corresponds to the reaction process, connecting
the reactant valley and the product valley. The dynamics around the saddle
point play a primary role in determining whether the system with a given initial
condition will go through the reaction process to reach the product region or be
reﬂected back into the reactant region, in other words, whether the reaction
happens or not.
RSC Theoretical and Computational Chemistry Series No. 6
Reaction Rate Constant Computations: Theories and Applications
Edited by Keli Han and Tianshu Chu
r The Royal Society of Chemistry 2014
Published by the Royal Society of Chemistry, www.rsc.org
154
 01/12/2013 15:16:24.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00154

## Page 175

An attempt to calculate the reaction rate constant by counting the trajectories passing the saddle region has been initiated in the context of transition
state theory (TST).
1–12 There a dividing surface was postulated in the vicinity of
the saddle point to separate the reactant and the product regions. The reaction
rate was evaluated by the ﬂux crossing this surface from the reactant side to the
product side; an important assumption was made that a trajectory having
crossed the surface should not cross the surface again. This is a crucial assumption that enables the evaluation of the reaction rate as the ﬂux through the
dividing surface because otherwise the ﬂux includes the trajectories that cross
the surface several times and ﬁnally return to the reactant side, resulting in
overestimation of the rate. A dividing surface with this non-recrossing property
is called ‘transition state’ (TS). Figure 7.1 shows a schematic picture for the
concept of the transition state. Figure 7.1(a) shows example trajectories that are
allowed by the non-recrossing assumption: if the trajectory crosses the transition state, it must go into the product region without returning to the transition
state and, if the trajectory is to return to the reactant side, it should not cross
the transition state. Figure 7.1(b) shows trajectories that cross the dividing
surface several times. These trajectories are forbidden by the non-recrossing
assumption, or, in other words, we do not call the surface transition state if
such trajectories exist. It was not before the extensive studies of the saddle
region dynamics in the past decades that the existence of such a non-recrossing
dividing surface (transition state) has been guaranteed with a ﬁrm mathematical grounding under moderate assumptions.
13–16
A primary goal of TST is to provide a simple intuitive picture of the chemical
reaction, elucidating what is actually taking place in the reaction system, how
(a)
(b)
Figure 7.1 Schematic illustrations for the concept of transition state. (a) The transition state is a dividing surface between the reactant and the product
regions in the phase space, which any reacting trajectory crosses only once
and any non-reacting trajectory does not cross. (b) Illustration of recrossing trajectories. Such recrossings are prohibited by the deﬁnitions of the
transition state.
Dynamics of Chemical Reaction around a Saddle Point 155
 01/12/2013 15:16:24.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00154
View Online

## Page 176

the system overcomes the barrier to reach the product region, what makes the
diﬀerence between reacting and non-reacting trajectories, what causes the
chemical reaction to happen, etc. Such fundamental knowledge can then be
applied to oﬀer eﬃcient methods to calculate reaction rate constants that
require only a limited amount of information about the system, for example,
the morphology of the PES only in the vicinity of the saddle point. Following
several developments in experiments
17,18 and theories,13,14,19–26 the dynamics in
the saddle region have been extensively studied in the past decades. 27–46
Various researchers elucidated the dynamical structures in the saddle region
including the transition state and several other manifolds in the phase space, as
well as a ﬁrm mathematical grounding for their existence. The theory of the
saddle region dynamics is applicable not only to chemical reaction, but also to
any dynamical system with saddle points such as isomerization of
clusters,
27,32–37 ionization of Rydberg atoms in external ﬁelds, 38,39,43 structural
transformation of proteins, 47 capture of asteroids in the solar system, 48 orbit
designing of space crafts, 49–51 and so on.
In addition to the transition state, the theory on the dynamics in the saddle
region has elucidated other important objects in the phase space. An invariant
manifold is a set of points in the phase space such that any point in that set will
remain in that set perpetually during the time evolution. As a consequence, if an
invariant manifold divides the whole phase space into two disjoint regions, the
system can never cross the invariant manifold from one region to the other.
An important invariant manifold in the context of reaction dynamics is the
one that separates the trajectories going into the product region from those
going into the reactant region. Once we know which side of the manifold a
given initial condition is, we can tell without any further time propagation
whether the reaction occurs or not. In what follows, this manifold between the
reactive and the non-reactive trajectories is be called a ‘reactivity boundary’,
distinguishing it from a ‘reaction boundary’ which may signify the transition
state, the no-recrossing surface between the reactants and the products.
Recently it was shown that the invariant manifold dividing the trajectories into
reactive and non-reactive ones can be extracted analytically even when the
nonlinearity of the system is so large that the concept of no-return transition
state is not applicable.
52,53
We begin our investigation of the saddle region dynamics in section 7.2 with
a simple system of one-dimensional parabolic barrier potential. We then
introduce anharmonicities, including the deviation of the one-dimensional
potential from the parabola as well as couplings among multiple degrees of
freedom. By some example trajectories in a simple model system, it will be seen
how the couplings induce recrossings on the transition state dividing surface
taken simply by the quadratic approximation. Importance of the velocities
(or equivalently the momenta) of the system in the reaction dynamics are
clariﬁed and the dividing surface in the phase space rather than the conﬁguration space is introduced. Section 7.3 is dedicated to a special technique,
called canonical perturbation theory (CPT) or normal form (NF) theory in the
literature, to enable the extraction of the dynamical structures including the
156 Chapter 7
 01/12/2013 15:16:24.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00154
View Online

## Page 177

transition state and the reactivity boundaries under the existence of couplings.
Simple example applications to the chemical reaction system are demonstrated
in section 7.4.
7.2 Phase Space Picture of Chemical Reaction Systems
in the Saddle Region
7.2.1 Quadratic Approximation
If the energy of the system is only slightly above the saddle point, then the
Hamiltonian can be satisfactorily approximated by:

## Page 178

Suppose q1 ¼/C01 corresponds to the ‘reactant’ ( i.e. before the reaction) and
q1 ¼þ1 to the ‘product’ (after the reaction). The trajectories with x1 4 0 and
x1 4 0 are ‘forward reactive’ trajectories because they start in the reactant
region, overcome the barrier and go into the product region (in other words,
‘the reaction occurs’). The trajectories with x
1o0 and x140 are ‘forward nonreactive’ trajectories because they start in the reactant region, but are reﬂected
by the barrier and go back into the reactant region. The trajectories with x
1o0
and x1o0 are ‘backward reactive’ trajectories because they start in the product
region and go into the reactant region (the backward reaction). Similarly, the
trajectories with x1 4 0a n d x1o0 are ‘backward non-reactive’ trajectories.
Note that the sign of the action I1 ¼ x1x1 determines the reactivity of the
trajectory. If I1 4 0, then it is reactive (forward or backward). If I1 o 0, then it
is non-reactive.
Figure 7.2 Phase space ﬂow in the neighborhood of a saddle point when the
normal mode approximation is valid. The horizontal axis is the position
coordinate q
1 along the reaction mode. The vertical axis is its conjugate
momentum, p1. The left side corresponds to the reactant region and
the right side to the product. The whole space is divided into four parts
with respect to the reactivity of each trajectory. The boundaries of the
reactivity are given by sets of points that converge to the origin in t-N or
t- /C0 N.

 01/12/2013 15:16:24.

## Page 179

The set:
Ws ¼ð q; pÞjx1 ¼ 0fg ð7:5Þ
represented as the x1-axis in Figure 7.2 is called the stable invariant manifold.
This object is the boundary between reactive and non-reactive trajectories:
points on W
s remain on Ws without going either to the reactant or the product
regions. Points with x1 4 0 ðx1 o 0Þ go into the product (reactant) region as
t !þ 1. Thus the manifold Ws divides the ‘future’ of the trajectory.
The set:
Wu ¼ð q; pÞjx1 ¼ 0fg ð7:6Þ
is called the unstable invariant manifold. Points on Wu remain on Wu. Points
with x1 4 0 ðx1 o 0Þ came from the reactant (product) region in t !/C0 1 . Thus
the manifold Wu divides the ‘past’ of the trajectory.
The transition state discussed in section 7.1 can in this case be taken as:
T ¼ð q; pÞjq1 ¼ 0fg ð7:7Þ
As seen in Figure 7.2, once a forward-going trajectory crosses the transition
state, it must go straight into the product region, without ever returning into
the reactant region. Thus the transition state surface can be regarded as the
boundary between the reactant and the product regions. Note, as mentioned in
section 7.1, the transition state lies between the reactant and the product
regions, whereas the stable manifold W
s and the unstable manifold Wu divide
the reactive trajectories and the non-reactive trajectories. In the following we
call Ws and Wu the ‘reactivity boundary’, distinguishing it from a ‘reaction
boundary’ which may signify the transition state.

The Hamiltonian eqn (7.1), which was a quadratic approximation, must now be
modiﬁed as:

of l ¼ o ¼ 1, a ¼ 0.5. Figure 7.3 shows two example trajectories together with

2, which corresponds to the

crosses the harmonic transition state again to go back into the reactant region.
This trajectory can be understood by the fact that the actual position of the
maximum is on the right side of the space when the vibrational coordinate q
2 is
large. The trajectory is reﬂected by the ridge of the potential. This observation
indicates that the TS dividing surface must be taken as a curved surface depending on the vibrational coordinates, reﬂecting the coupling between the
reactive and the vibrational modes.

(b)
Figure 7.3 Example trajectories that cross the naı ¨vely taken dividing surface ( q1 ¼ 0)
more than once. The horizontal and vertical axes are the normal mode

160 Chapter 7
 01/12/2013 15:16:24.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00154
View Online

## Page 181

One may imagine taking the curve of maximum q1 ¼ aq2
2 as the dividing
surface. However, this does not work either, as shown in Figure 7.3(b). In this
example, the trajectory once crosses the curve of maximum, but then goes
vertically to cross the curve again and returns to the reactant side. To understand this trajectory, one notes that the dynamical motion in the classical
mechanics depends on the velocity as well as the potential. The trajectory has a
velocity component along the vertical direction when it crosses the dotted curve
in Figure 7.3. After the crossing, while the gradient of the potential pulls the
system toward the product side, the vertical component of the velocity remains
due to the law of inertia until the system reaches the dotted curve again.
Considering this dependence of the motion on the velocity, the TS dividing
surface must be taken in the phase space rather than in the conﬁguration space
in order to satisfy the non-recrossing assumption.
Figure 7.4 shows a three-dimensional view of the trajectories in the coordinates q
1, q2 and arctan ðp2=p1Þ for ﬁxed energy E ¼ 0:4 corresponding to
the trajectories shown in Figure 7.3. Note that because of the energy conservation H ¼ E, specifying q
1, q2 and arctan ðp2=p1Þ determines the phase space
point of this system uniquely. The two trajectories in Figure 7.3 are plotted in
this three-dimensional space together with two semi-transparent surfaces
showing the naı¨ve ‘TS’ q
1 ¼ 0 and the transition state calculated by the method
presented in section 7.3. As seen in Figure 7.3, the two trajectories cross the
q
1 ¼ 0 surface twice and return to the reactant region, whereas they do not cross
the true transition state obtained by the method that incorporates the anharmonicities. Thus the problem of recrossing is resolved by taking the proper
Figure 7.4 Three-dimensional phase space plot of the dividing surfaces and the
example trajectories. The two non-reacting trajectories shown in
Figure 7.3 are now plotted in the phase space with one axis representing
the momenta. While they cross the naı ¨vely taken dividing surface ( q
1 ¼ 0)
more than once, they do not cross the true transition state taken as a
surface in the phase space. Note the momentum dependence of the
true TS.

## Page 182

dividing surface in the phase space. Note also the bumpy shape of the true
transition state surface showing its momentum dependence.
7.3 Canceling the Eﬀect of Coupling: Normal Form
Theory
7.3.1 Lie Transformation
In section 7.2, we saw that the phase space structures including the nonrecrossing transition state and the reactivity boundaries can be identiﬁed easily
for harmonic approximation, but the eﬀect of couplings complicates the
dynamics and makes the identiﬁcation of transition state diﬃcult. In this
section we introduce a method to elucidate the phase space structures in the
existence of coupling. The method is called normal form (NF) theory or
canonical perturbation theory (CPT).
The idea of NF theory is to introduce a new set of coordinates by a
coordinate transformation q; pðÞ 7! /C22q; /C22pðÞ , with which the number of coupling
terms is as small as possible. The scheme is based on the canonical perturbation
theory proposed by Deprit.
54 It has been recently introduced in the ﬁeld of
molecular dynamics in the context of transition state theory and also reaction
dynamics.
The Hamiltonian of the system is decomposed into a series expansion with a
formal parameter E of perturbation, which we set E ¼ 1 after performing all the
following calculation:
H ¼

The assignment of the perturbation order is usually done by referring to the
polynomial order, though this is not always the case. Thus the zeroth order
consists of quadratic terms and the n th order part is a polynomial of degree
n þ 2:

The coordinate transformation from q; pðÞ to /C22q; /C22pðÞ is constructed by the
method of the Lie transformation. 55 The transformation is given by solving the
following ordinary diﬀerential equations:
d
dE q EðÞ ¼ /C0 @
@p EðÞ F q EðÞ ; p EðÞðÞ

corresponding to the original coordinate:
q 0ðÞ ¼ q
p 0ðÞ ¼ p
ð7:14Þ
The transformed coordinates are given as the ﬁnal values at E ¼ 1:
/C22q ¼ qð1Þ
/C22p ¼ pð1Þ
ð7:15Þ
Eqn (7.13) is in the form of the Hamiltonian equations of motion as if E were
‘time’ and /C0 F were ‘Hamiltonian’. As a result, the transformation from q; pðÞ
to /C22q; /C22pðÞ as given by eqns (7.14) and (7.15) is automatically a canonical transformation. Moreover, the construction of the coordinate transformation is now
equivalent to giving the function F.

Hamiltonian.

 01/12/2013 15:16:24.

## Page 187

appeared to overcome the barrier once but return to the reactant side by
crossing the barrier again. When viewed in the transformed coordinates,
however, they do not actually cross the barrier. They bounce back before
reaching the true dividing surface of /C22q
1 ¼ 0. This is because the NF transformation correctly incorporates all the eﬀects of the anharmonicity and renders
the dynamics identical with the harmonic case when viewed in the transformed
coordinates.
The form of eqn (7.33) is termed full normal form (full NF) here.
Figure7.6(c) depicts the phase space ﬂow in the case where the full NF theory is
applicable. In addition to the reaction mode action /C22I1, all the vibrational mode
Figure 7.6 Schematic illustration of the phase space structure of the chemical reaction
around a saddle point. (a) For yet higher energy, we cannot extract a
separable reaction mode. The reactivity boundaries can still be located by
the minimal normal form. For much higher energy, there is a region where
the present method cannot extract any structure in the phase space.
(b) For higher energy, the anharmonic couplings mix the vibrational
modes and the motion can become complex. The reaction mode extracted
by the normal form transformation, however, still maintains the clear
structure. (c) When the energy is only slightly above the barrier, the system
is separable and all the modes evolve independently.

When the vibrational frequencies o‘ are close to integer ratio, the factor gjk
in eqn (7.31) can become very small for some combinations of j; k with
j1 ¼ k1. This causes divergence in the expa nsion series in the full NF transformation, especially when the ene rgy of the system is higher and the
anharmonic terms become more signiﬁcant. Physically, this means that the
extraction of conserved action varia bles in the vibrational modes is no longer
possible due to the breakdown of the regular dynamical structure by the
resonance among the vibrational modes. Convergence can be obtained when
we include in the generating function only the terms with j

With this form of Hamiltonian, the bath mode actions are no longer
constants of motion, but the reaction mode action /C22I1 can be shown to be
constant in the same way as eqn (7.35). We can therefore identify the
phase space objects, both the transition state and the reactivity boundary, in the
same way as the previous cases. The form of eqn (7.41) is termed partial
NF here.
Figure 7.6(b) depicts the phase space ﬂow in the case where the partial NF is
applicable. The projection of trajectories on the bath mode coordinates can
exhibit any complicated motions because we allow any coupling terms in eqn
(7.41). The projection on the NF reaction coordinates /C22q
1; /C22p1ðÞ , however, shows
regular structure given by the constancy of /C22I1, whereby the phase space objects
are readily identiﬁable.
When the energy gets higher and the anharmonicity becomes yet more
signiﬁcant, even the partial NF may fail to converge. In some cases, it is
still possible to obtain convergence by further relaxing the extent of the
normalization.
52,53 Here we choose the following form as the ﬁnal
Hamiltonian:

variant set: If the system is in this set at a certain time, it remains in the set

is also an invariant manifold and divides the phase

termed minimal NF. Figure 7.6(a) depicts the phase space ﬂow in the case
where the minimal NF is applicable. The constancy of the action is broken for
all the degrees of freedom due to the strong couplings. However, the reactivity
boundaries still exist robustly.
In summary, we can ﬁnd the hierarchy of reaction dynamics as shown
schematically in Figure 7.6. As the energy gets higher and the anharmonicity
becomes more signiﬁcant, the structure of the phase space ﬂow changes its
character from fully regular to partially structured ﬂow.
7.4 Calculation of Reaction Probabilities
7.4.1 Semi-Classical Reaction Rate Formula
In TST, the reaction rate constant k is given by dividing the ﬂux through the
transition state by the reactant partition function QR:
kTðÞ ¼ 1
2p/C22hQR
Z
NEðÞ exp /C0 E
kBT
/C18/C19
dE ð7:44Þ
where /C22h is Planck’s constant, E is the energy of the system, kB is Boltzmann’s
constant and T the temperature. The term NEðÞ is called the cumulative
reaction probability and corresponds to the ﬂux through the transition state
dividing surface at the energy E. Note that in section 7.3 we introduced the NF
transformation that enabled the identiﬁcation of the true transition state
dividing surface which is, at least locally, free from recrossings. Therefore, once
the trajectory has crossed the transition state, we can conclude ‘the reaction has
occurred’. This enables the calculation of NEðÞ by evaluating the amount of
trajectories that cross the transition state.
More precisely, the cumulative reaction probability is calculated by summing
up the reaction probability for all possible states of the vibrational modes with

is then expressed as the sum of the reaction probability over all the possible

and eqn (7.47). Hernandez and Miller 13 showed almost perfect quantitative

2 ! H2 þ H.
Here we look at some details of the cumulative reaction probability of
the reaction of H þ H2 and discuss what picture can be obtained from the
analytical expressions of the current theory. Figure 7.7 shows the cumulative
reaction probability NðEÞ as a function of the energy E both in linear and log
scales. The result of the harmonic approximation (dotted curve) and that of the
NF theory (solid curve) are compared. The calculation uses the potential
energy surface of Mielke et al.
57 The total angular momentum is ﬁxed to zero
for simplicity. This allows us to take the zeroth order approximation as in eqn
(7.11), i.e. a collection of harmonic oscillators and a parabolic barrier. Readers
interested in the extension of the theory to include the rotational motions
should refer to the literature.

Two features can be observed from the comparison shown in Figure 7.7. One
is that the harmonic approximation signiﬁcantly overestimates the reaction
probability in the low energy region (Figure 7.7(a)). The other is that the
cumulative reaction probability after the anharmonicity correction shows more
step-like behavior in higher energy.
To analyze these eﬀects, we present the explicit formula for the NF
Hamiltonian obtained after the calculation presented in section 7.3:

## Page 192

NðEÞ signiﬁcantly (Figure 7.7(a)). Since the energy is low, the dominant contribution comes from the lowest vibrational state ðn2 ¼ n3 ¼ 0Þ in the summation of eqn (7.48). The dominant terms in eqn (7.50) are:
0:42 þ 0:19 /C22I1 þ 0:021 /C22I2
1 ð7:51Þ
where the ﬁrst two terms comes from the harmonic approximation and the last
term denotes the anharmonic correction. Note that the sign of the correction
term ðþ0:021 /C22I2
1 Þ is in the direction to lower the value of /C22I1 for a given energy
below the barrier height. From this consideration it is seen that the reduction of
the reaction probability by the anharmonicity in the low energy region comes
from the one-dimensional anharmonicity along the reactive mode rather than
the couplings. As schematically depicted in Figure 7.8(a), the deviation from
the harmonic (parabolic) approximation along the reactive mode comes mainly
from the fact that the potential energy becomes ﬂat in the asymptotic regions
rather than decreasing inﬁnitely. This eﬀect reduces the tunneling probability
due to the increased length of the tunneling path, which is correctly reﬂected in
mathematical formula (eqn (7.51)) as well as the calculation result
(Figure 7.7(a)). Note that we have reduced the couplings by the method of NF,
which allowed us to discuss the reaction process essentially in one dimension,
separated from the bath modes.
The next feature in the cumulative reaction probability is the step-like behavior emphasized by the eﬀect of anharmonicity as shown in Figure 7.7(b). As
the energy is increased, more vibrational states become ‘open’ in the summation
of eqn (7.48), resulting in the increase of NðEÞ. Let us examine how this
(a)

mode and aﬀects the sharpness of the reaction probability.

## Page 193

vibrational excitation aﬀects the reactive mode. Corresponding terms in eqn
(7.50) are
0:42 þ 0:19 /C22I1 þ 0:25 /C22I2 /C0 0:085 /C22I1 /C22I2 ð7:52Þ
which can be rearranged into:
0:42 þ 0:19 /C0 0:085 /C22I2ðÞ /C22I1 þ 0:25 /C22I2 ð7:53Þ
The term in the parenthesis can be regarded as the ‘eﬀective curvature’ along
the reactive mode, corresponding to the l in the harmonic approximation (eqn
(7.11)). This eﬀective curvature decreases when the vibrational mode is excited
as seen from eqn (7.53). The decrease of curvature ﬂattens the eﬀective onedimensional potential along the reactive mode, resulting in a decrease in the
tunneling probability because the tunneling path becomes longer, as shown
schematically in Figure 7.8(b). The reason for the ﬂattening of the reaction
mode potential by the vibrational excitation can in turn be understood from the
dependence of the vibrational frequency on the reaction coordinate: In general
the vibrational frequency decreases in the saddle region due to the weakened
chemical bond. Thus the eﬀective potential energy ‘dressed’ by the vibrational
energy with a ﬁxed action variable ð /C22I
2 ¼ n2 þ 1=2ðÞ /C22hÞ goes higher in the
asymptotic region than in the saddle region, making the one-dimensional potential ﬂatter.
Note we have reduced the coupling terms by the method of NF so that the
action variables determined by the NF are constants of motion. This enabled us
to discuss the eﬀect of vibrational excitation by simply ﬁxing the bath mode
actions and looking at the change of the one-dimensional motion along the
reaction mode.
7.4.2 Instanton Trajectories
In this section we investigate the instanton trajectory which plays an important
role in the calculation and analyses of tunneling phenomena.
60–62 The instanton
is deﬁned as a periodic orbit on the inverted potential energy surface, or
equivalently, a periodic orbit in imaginary time. It serves as an important basis
for the calculations of tunneling reaction probabilities and tunneling splitting
of quantum states. In the case of one-dimensional parabolic barrier:

Having obtained the instanton solution in a simple form as eqn (7.58) in the NF
coordinates, we can readily convert it to the original coordinates by using the
backward coordinate transformation /C22q; /C22pðÞ 7! q; pðÞ .
Figure 7.9 plots several instanton trajectories with diﬀering values of /C22I1
obtained by the NF method for the reaction of H þ H2 ! H2 þ H. Note that
putting zero in the bath mode coordinates in eqn (7.58) conﬁnes the instanton
trajectories to the collinear conﬁgurations. We note that the saddle point of this
system has a collinear shape and that the Hamiltonian is symmetric with respect
to the inversion along the bending normal mode coordinate ( q
37!/C0 q3 and
p37!/C0 p3, if we take mode 3 as the bending mode). Since the generating
174 Chapter 7
 01/12/2013 15:16:24.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00154
View Online

## Page 195

function of the NF transformation is determined according to the full Hamiltonian, it possesses the same symmetric property with the Hamiltonian.
Therefore, if q3 is an antisymmetric coordinate, the transformed coordinate /C22q3
is also antisymmetic, although diﬀerent in value. Putting /C22q3 ¼ /C22p3 ¼ 0 thus
corresponds to collinear conﬁgurations.
The dotted curve in Figure 7.9 shows the intrinsic reaction coordinate (IRC)
of this system. In comparing the instanton trajectories with the IRC, it is seen
that the tunneling does not occur exactly along the IRC but connects the two
sides of the IRC in a shorter path. This shortcut in the tunneling phenomena
for a curved reaction path has been pointed out by several authors.
63–65 This is
because the probability of tunneling is increased by reducing the length of the
tunneling pathway, and the curved shape of the IRC enables the existence of
such shortcut pathways. Note that these shortcut tunneling paths cannot be
obtained by the normal mode approximation. The instanton solution in the
normal mode approximation runs along the normal mode q
1-axis, which is a
straight line through the saddle point. The curved shape of the IRC is
incorporated only when the higher order terms in the Taylor expansion are
taken into consideration. The results here, which reproduce the well-known
shortcut eﬀect of tunneling paths, show that this eﬀect is correctly incorporated
by the NF calculations.
7.5 Conclusions
Many atoms are usually involved in a chemical reaction. These atoms move
simultaneously during the process of chemical reaction to form and break
chemical bonds. As such, it is often hard to obtain an intuitive picture of what is
taking place in the process of chemical reaction. The concept of reaction path,
Figure 7.9 Instanton trajectories (solid) with several diﬀerent energies calculated by
the normal form theory are shown in the skewed coordinate system. The
dotted curve shows the IRC.
Dynamics of Chemical Reaction around a Saddle Point 175
 01/12/2013 15:16:24.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00154
View Online

## Page 196

or intrinsic reaction coordinate, 66 assigns a one-dimensional path embedded in
the multi-dimensional conﬁguration space and therefore enables us to imagine
a chemical reaction as a one-dimensional motion along the reaction path rather
than a collection of the motions of many particles. Although this is a signiﬁcant
advance in understanding the process of chemical reaction, the reaction path
Hamiltonian
67 tells that the reaction mode thus assigned is subject to coupling
with the vibrational modes through the curvature of the reaction path. The
normal form (NF) theory explained in this chapter enables the extraction of a
single phase space reaction coordinate that is decoupled from other degrees of
freedom. We can think of this latter coordinate as ‘what is essentially the
chemical reaction’, since the motion along this coordinate is independent of the
other modes and the reactivity can be told by referring solely to this coordinate.
The crux there is the inclusion of momenta as well as the position coordinates in
the coordinate transformation, to take the dynamics into account.
In this chapter we have focused on Hamiltonian dynamics, which describe
the dynamics of molecules in the gas phase. To analyze the chemical reaction in
the condensed phase, the NF chemical reaction theory has been recently extended to the Langevin equation
53,68–73 and the generalized Langevin equation,74–76 and the formula for the rate calculation has been given. 77 Quantum
eﬀects, which have been treated semi-classically in section 7.4, can also be incorporated exactly by using the Weyl representation.
78,79 The NF theory has
also been extended to time-dependent Hamiltonian systems to analyze the
chemical reactions in the laser ﬁeld 80,81 and to design the pulse shape to control
the reactions. 82 It is thus a promising tool for fundamental analyses and
applications.
However, the current NF chemical reaction theory has also some limitations.
Since it is based on the Taylor expansion around a stationary point, the region
where the NF can be successfully applied is limited by the convergence property
of the expansion. Thus the extraction of the separable reaction coordinate is
almost always limited in the vicinity of the saddle point. Outside this range, it is
not clear whether such separable mode exists or not. Analyses of the global
dynamics of chemical reactions will be a challenging future issue. Moreover, it
is known from the NF analysis that the structure of the phase space breaks
down through bifurcation in high energy regions.
45,46 It is probably necessary
to devise diﬀerent formulae to calculate the chemical reaction rates in such high
energy regions.

## Page 200

CHAPTER 8
Derivation of Rate Constants
from Accurate Quantum Wave
Packet Theory for Nonadiabatic
and Adiabatic Chemical
Reactions
TIANSHU CHU a,b AND KELI HAN* a
a State Key Laboratory of Molecular Reaction Dynamics, Dalian Institute
of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Dalian 116023, China;
b Institute for Computational Sciences and Engineering, Laboratory of
New Fiber Materials and Modern Textile, the Growing Base for State Key
Laboratory, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China
*Email: klhan@dicp.ac.cn
8.1 Introduction
Rate constant, the statistical dynamical quantity of most interest, is
indispensable to our understanding and controlling of chemical reactions at the
molecular level. In particular, derivation of the rate constant for an elementary
chemical reaction is of essential signiﬁcance since a complex reactive process
may ﬁnally have a relationship to an individual elementary reaction step. There
are various theoretical methods for computing rate constants for an elementary
chemical reaction, and the development of the time-dependent quantum wave
packet (TDQWP) method during the past two decades has enabled this
RSC Theoretical and Computational Chemistry Series No. 6
Reaction Rate Constant Computations: Theories and Applications
Edited by Keli Han and Tianshu Chu
r The Royal Society of Chemistry 2014
Published by the Royal Society of Chemistry, www.rsc.org
180
 01/12/2013 15:16:37.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00180

## Page 201

quantum method to make signiﬁcant contributions to the accurate computation of rate constants for elementary reactions.
However, the Born–Oppenheimer (BO) approximation, 1,2 which separates
the motion of nuclei from the motion of electrons due to their diﬀerent timescales, is of fundamental importance in understanding a reaction process. Thus
considerable quantum wave packet exploration of tri-atomic, tetra-atomic and
polyatomic reactions has been carried out based on the BO approximation,
which is the so-called adiabatic investigation with single (ground) electronic
state involved. Nevertheless, there are many situations where the BO
approximation is invalid. A few such examples include intersystem crossing
(ISC) in spin-forbidden reactions, the charge transfer process in ion–atom and
ion–molecule reactions, and geometric phase eﬀects in systems that exhibit
conical intersection (CI) . In all these examples, multiple electronic states are
involved. Comparable timescales of nucleic and electronic motions resulting
from a strong coupling between nuclei and electrons are responsible for the
breakdown of the BO approximation in describing the nonadiabatic reaction
dynamics occurring on multiple coupled electronic states. Because nonadiabatic couplings are, in by their nature, nuclei coupled to electrons, it is
proposed that nonadiabatic dynamics are inherently quantum dynamics
3 and
that quantum theory provides the basis for a description of nonadiabatic
reaction processes in atomic and molecular physics and chemistry.
Quantum description of a nonadiabatic reaction system requires good
treatment of nonadiabatic couplings arising from separating the electron motion from nuclear motion within the Born–Huang (BH) treatment.
2 The BH
treatment includes an arbitrary number of electronic states in the expansion of
the total wave packet of a reaction system and therefore replaces the BO
approximation assuming the nuclear motion on a single electronic state.
4
However, due to various reasons such as a limitation in computational
capacity, lack of appropriate numerical algorithm, lack of coupled potential
energy surfaces, etc., an accurate and realistic quantum dynamics description of
a nonadiabatic reaction system, even of a tri-atomic A þ BC one, has only been
achieved and reported in the last 15 years or so. For a tetra-atomic AB þ CD
reaction system, it is still extremely diﬃcult to carry out accurate quantum
calculations. With the interest in nonadiabatic chemical processes and
polyatomic reactive systems intensifying, the need to explore nonadiabatic
reactions with more than three atoms, including derivations of their rate
constants, is also increasing. In this chapter, we describe the accurate
quantum wave packet methods with an emphasis on our recently developed
nonadiabatic accurate quantum theories, and their applications in computing
rate constants for tri-atomic A þ BC, tetra-atomic AB þ CD and polyatomic
reactions.
The following theoretical part of the chapter (see section 8.2) presents our
developed quantum theories, which are capable of computing reaction rate
constants for electronically nonadiabatic reaction dynamics. These recently
developed time-dependent quantum wave packet theories mainly focused
on solving the Schro ¨dinger equation for nuclei motion, leaving the more
Derivation of Rate Constants from Accurate Quantum Wave Packet Theory 181
 01/12/2013 15:16:37.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00180
View Online

## Page 202

complicated treatment of electron motion to ab initio calculations. That is to
say, information on electron motion is ﬁrst implicated and embodied in
the coupled potential energy surfaces calibrated from the ab initio energy
points, and then employed in the numerical solution of Schro¨dinger equation of
nuclei motion.
Both time-dependent and time-independent quantum theories
5–14 can be
employed to study three-atom, four-atom and polyatomic reactions with
respect to nuclei motion. Clever features of the time-dependent wave packet
theory are the use of absorbing potentials
15 and the grid basis representations
(the fast Fourier transformation 6 and the discrete variable representation). 16,17
The time-dependent methods are particularly eﬃcient in computing initialstate-resolved (both the total and the state-to-state) reaction probabilities,
with these data being generated over a range of collision energy (that is,
determined by the width of the initial wave packet) in a single propagation.
Interested readers should please refer to the reviews provided in refs. 5, 8 and
11, and the references therein for the advantages and disadvantages of the two
kinds of quantum methods illustrated during their treatment of adiabatic
reactions.
Albeit that quantum treatment of nonadiabatic reactions is not as mature as
that of adiabatic ones, there are many theoretical eﬀorts aiming at extending
the adiabatic quantum theories to scattering problems with multiple electronic
states involved. In the past decades, approximate and accurate nonadiabatic
quantum theories were borne, being realistically applied to nonadiabatic
processes in tri-atomic and tetra-atomic reactions. For example, Schatz and
co-workers
18 developed a quantum wave packet approach to investigate role of
excited state in O( 1D) þ H2. Balint-Kurti and co-workers 19 applied a real wave
packet approach to explore the contribution of the excited state to reaction
dynamics underlying O(
1D) þ H2. Aquilanti and co-workers 20 extended their
adiabatic hyperquantization algorithm to electronically nonadiabatic reactions
such as F( 2P1/2, 2P3/2) þ H2. Nakamura and co-workers 21 applied a timeindependent quantum method to produce the cumulative reaction probabilities
in DH
2
1. Recently, combined with accurate ab initio based diabatic potential
energy surfaces and couplings among them, Alexander and co-workers 22–25
and our group 26–28 extended the time-independent quantum method and the
time-dependent wave packet approach, respectively, to F( 2P1/2, 2P3/2) þ H2,
Cl(2P1/2, 2P3/2) þ H2,O ( 1D, 3P) þ H2, etc., with both groups obtaining the
accurate quantum integral cross-sections, rate constants and product
rotational–vibrational (rovibrational) state distributions for a variety of triatomic reactions. The most studied nonadiabatic issues in tri-atomic reaction
systems by quantum theories to date can be ﬁnd in our recent review article.
27
For tetra-atomic reaction systems, to the best of our knowledge, there is no
previous (exact) quantum nonadiabatic investigations except for our recent
published works on O
2 þ O2
29 and OH þ H2.30 There have been numerous
review articles 5–14 that describe accurate adiabatic quantum theories and
their applications in quantum scattering and in rate computation over the
past 20 years; review articles of accurate nonadiabatic quantum scattering
182 Chapter 8
 01/12/2013 15:16:37.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00180
View Online

## Page 203

dynamics and their applications in rate computations are, however, relatively
sparse.27
Section 8.3 reports the applications of our developed nonadiabatic
quantum wave packet methods and the adiabatic quantum wave packet
method
7 to the computation of rate constants of tri-atomic, tetra-atomic and
polyatomic reactions. These include: the rate constant computations for the
nonadaibatic tri-atomic F(P 3/2,P 1/2) þ D2 (v ¼ 0, j ¼ 0) reactions and for the
nonadiabatic tetra-atomic nonadiabatic O 2(a1n) þ O2(a1n) quenching process;
and the rate constant computations for the adiabatic tri-atomic
N(
2D) þ H2(v ¼ 0, j ¼ 0–5) reaction and for the adiabatic polyatomic F þ CH4
reaction.
8.2 Accurate Nonadiabatic Quantum Dynamics
Methods
The following part of this section describes our recent advances in applying
accurate quantum wave packet methods to compute rate constants and to
understand nonadiabatic eﬀects in tri-atomic and tetra-atomic molecular
reactions. The quantum nonadiabatic approaches that we present here are
based on solving the time-dependent Schro ¨dinger equation formulated within
an electronically diabatic representation.
Development of diabatic potential energy surfaces is essential for the
nonadiabatic investigations we carried out. Due to a general lack of diabatic
potential matrix for tetra-atomic reactions, here we investigate the development
of diabatic potential matrix using the tetra-atomic reaction system of
O
2 þ O2.29 The fact that the potential energy operator is non-diagonal in a
diabatic representation leads to the matrix form of Schro ¨dinger equation for
nuclear motion, in which the nuclear wave function is described by a column
vector consisting of expansion coeﬃcients of the total wave function in the
electronic diabatic basis set. In other words, the expansion coeﬃcient (or the
component of the column vector) represents the nuclear motion on each
corresponding electronic state and is coupled to each other through the oﬀdiagonal elements of the potential matrix. In order to deal with ‘matrix feature’
of Schro ¨dinger equation for nonadiabatic reactive scattering, appropriate
propagation methods have been developed by us and then incorporated
into the time evolution of the wave packets. We note that the adiabatic timedependent wave packet methods
31–34 laid the cornerstone for the present
nonadiabatic treatment.
8.2.1 Nonadiabatic State-to-State Real Wave Packet Theory for
Tri-Atomic Reaction of A þ BC- AB þ C
The nonadiabatic real wave packet state-to-state approach introduced
here started by numerically solving the mapped Schro ¨dinger equation for
nuclei motion, formulated in terms of the product Jacobi coordinates

8.2.1.1 Preparation of Initial Wave Packet
To solve the time-dependent Schro ¨dinger equation, we ﬁrst need to prepare
an initial wave packet and put this initial wave packet on the starting
electronic state. Then the initial wave packet propagates on the multiple
electronic states of a reaction system (see Figure 8.2). This means that we only
need to prepare one non-zero component c

in the ﬁrst iteration to obtain q

where Aˆ is a damping factor to absorb the wave packet near the edges of

Through the iteration steps, wave packet evolution can be realized on
multiple electronic states. This process is represented schematically
in Figure 8.2, where part of the wave packet initially in state 1 occurs in
state 2 when moving near the crossing area of the potential energy
surfaces under the diabatic representation. Thus, the ﬁnal analysis using
the time-independent part of the propagated wave packet can be implemented on each individual electronic state involved to obtain dynamical
quantities.

The operation of the radial part ( R and r) of the kinetic operator can be
carried out eﬃciently using the fast Fourier transform method 16,35 to transform
from coordinate to momentum space where the kinetic operator is diagonal.
In turn, action of the potential operator f ^Vij gð i ¼ 1 /C0 n; j ¼ 1 /C0 nÞ is implemented in coordinate space (grid basis) where each element of the potential
operator is diagonal, resulting in matrix multiplication of the wave packet
(n column matrix) at each grid with n /C2 nV matrix. Here, deﬁnition of the
angular discrete-variable representation (DVR) is followed by Corey and
Lemoine.
36 And with this, the transformation matrix between grid and angular
ﬁnite basis representation (FBR) is given by:

8.2.2.3 Wave Packet Expansion and Evaluation of Hamiltonian
Operator
Evaluation of the Hamiltonian onto the wave packet is needed during the
propagation. As usual, actions of the kinetic T and the potential V operators
are carried out in their respective local representations.
In propagation, the wave packet is expanded in terms of a body-ﬁxed
translational–vibrational–rotational basis functions as follows:

8.2.2.4 Calculation Quantities with Analysis of the Propagated
Wave Packets
Final analysis is carried out using the time-independent part of the propagated
quantum wave packet, obtaining through the half-Fourier
transformation:
27,31,32

Before moving into the next section, we would like to brieﬂy cover the subject
of diabatic potential energy surfaces (or diabatic potential matrix), since they
are important and indispensable when carrying out nonadiabatic quantum
calculations for tri-atomic and tetra-atomic reactions.
