## Page 217

8.2.3 Diabatic Potential Energy Surfaces for Nonadiabatic
Tri-Atomic and Tetra-Atomic Reactions
Although nonadiabatic dynamics calculations for tri-atomic and tetra-atomic
reaction systems can be performed within either an adiabatic or a diabatic
representation or even within both representations, the diabatic representation
is preferred for carrying out nonadiabatic quantum scattering calculations.
Within the diabatic representation, nonadiabatic couplings appear as the oﬀdiagonal elements of the potential matrix. These elements are smooth functions
of the nuclear coordinates, which avoids the singularity of nonadiabatic
coupling terms and paves the way for a numerical solution of the Schro ¨dinger
equation. The development of diabatic potential energy surfaces ( i.e. eigenvalues of the electronic Hamiltonian in a diabatic electronic representation for
a large numbers of ﬁxed nuclear geometries) is therefore essential for carrying
out nonadiabatic scattering investigation.
Diabatic potential energy surfaces (or diabatic potential matrix) were constructed (interpolated or ﬁtted) from ab initio energy data at high calculation
levels with treatment of electron correlation. In the indirect diabatization
scheme, ab initio energy data were ﬁrst calculated within a basis set of adiabatic
electronic wave functions to generate adiabatic potential energy surfaces of
electronic state by ﬁtting or interpolating procedures. Then, diabatic potential
matrix can be obtained from the adiabatic potential surfaces by adiabaticto-diabatic transformation. In practice, one of the commonly adopted procedures in recent years for such a transformation is carried out using the
diabatization angle g, for example, for two electronic states:
V
dia
11 Vdia
12
Vdia

22

/C20/C21
ð8:54Þ
This procedure has been employed in the construction of the diabatic potential
matrix for the 1 2A0 and 22A0 electronic states of H 3 with conical intersection by
Abrol and Kuppermann, 41 in which the diabatization angle g (a function of
internal nuclear coordinates) was calculated by solving the three-dimensional
Poisson equation (with an optimal set of boundary conditions) for the entire U
domain of nuclear conﬁguration space bearing important signiﬁcance for reactive scattering. The procedure was also employed by Mota and Varandas
42 in
their construction of the double many-body expansion (DMBE) diabatic potential matrix for the 1
2A0 and 2 2A0 states of the HN 2 system, with a newly
proposed diabatization scheme where the diabatization angle is represented by
some speciﬁc functions. Earlier construction of the DK (Dobbyn and Knowles)
Derivation of Rate Constants from Accurate Quantum Wave Packet Theory 197
 01/12/2013 15:16:37.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00180

## Page 218

diabatic potential matrix43 for the 11A0 and 21A0 states of the O(1D) þ H2 system
also employed this procedure. Further, for systems in which spin–orbit couplings
between electronic states are not zero at asymptotic regions
44 and therefore
cannot be ignored, the diabatic potential matrix should include the spin–orbit
matrix. Truhlar and co-workers
44 applied an improved version of their previously proposed method called fourth fold way for spin-free systems to construct
the spin-valence diabatic potential matrix for even-electron spin-coupled systems.
Li and Werner
45 computed the spin–orbit matrix and included it in the accurate
diabatic potential matrix they constructed for the lowest three electronic states of
FH
2 by a diabatization procedure where the diabatization angle was obtained by
either integration of the relevant nonadiabatic coupling elements or from the
coeﬃcients of the multiconﬁguration self-consistent ﬁeld (MCSCF) conﬁgurations. Schatz and co-workers
46–48 constructed the spin–orbit coupling matrix
from the computed ab initio data of spin–orbit couplings and incorporated it into
the diabatic potential matrix for spin-coupled systems of O( 3P,1D) þ H2 and
S(3P,1D) þ H2, etc.
In contrast to the indirect diabatization procedure which involves a
diabatization angle, the direct diabatization procedure constructs the diabatic
potential matrix directly from ab initio energy data or from diabatic electronic
wave functions without any interme diate step. Some examples of the
potential energy matrix obtaining with the direct diabatization procedure are: the
diabatic potential matrix for the lowest three electronic states of the H
3
1 system
constructed by Nakamura and co-workers 21 using the diatomics-in-molecules
(DIM) PES with three-body correction terms, and by Varandas and co-workers49
using the dressed-DIM formalism; and th e diabatic potential matrix for the two
lowest electronic states of the C 2H system constructed by Halvick and co-workers50 using several speciﬁc function forms to r epresent the diabatic potential surfaces of electronic states and the diabatic couplings. Interested readers, please refer
to refs. 42, 51–56 for details of the variety of diabatization methods as well as the
many diabatic potential energy matrices constructed using from these methods.

one is the method using the calculated total cross-section s

8.3 Application to Nonadiabatic and Adiabatic

8.3.1 Rate Constant Computations for Nonadiabatic Reactions
8.3.1.1 Rate Constants Computations for the Nonadiabatic
Tri-atomic F(P 3/2,P 1/2) þ D2 (v ¼ 0, j ¼ 0) Reactions
The elementary reaction of F with H 2/D2 is a case example for textbooks on
molecular reaction dynamics which has subjected to many theoretical and experimental studies. 59–63 It is interesting that the reaction is initiated from two
spin states, i.e. the ground spin P 3/2 state and the excited spin P 1/2 state (with a
spin–orbit split of 1.15 kcal mol /C0 1 between the two states). This non-zero
feature of the asymptotic spin–orbit coupling suggests spin–orbit couplings
should not be ignored in the treatment of the F(P
3/2,P 1/2) þ D2 reactions. As
known, there are two electronic states of 1 2A0 (2S1/2) and 1 2A00(2P3/2) correlating with the F(P 3/2) þ D2 reagents and one electronic state of 2 2A0(2P1/2)t o
the F(P1/2) þ D2 reagents; only the 1 2A0 (2S1/2) state correlates with the groundstate FD( 1S) þ D(2S) products while the other two states correlate with the
excited-state products. Thus, with the consideration of the two electronic spin
states, a 6 /C2 6 diabatic potential matrix is necessary for carrying out nonadiabatic quantum scattering calculations of the F(P
3/2) þ D2 (v ¼ 0, j ¼ 0) and
F(P1/2) þ D2 (v ¼ 0, j ¼ 0) reactions. The Alexander–Stark–Werner (ASW)
diabatic potential matrix 60 shown in Figure 8.4, which includes the spin–orbit
coupling matrix, was therefore employed in the quantum calculation for
computing rate constants of the nonadiabatic F(P 3/2,P 1/2) þ D2 reaction.

While for the product arrangement, we did not transform back to the coupled basis set because the energy of the excited state products is much higher
than that of the ground state products and thus the excited state products are

nonadiabatic quantum scattering calculation does not include Coriolis coupling terms because it has been previously proved that the eﬀect on the reaction
dynamics of Coriolis couplings is relatively minor.

In Table 8.1, we list our computed rate constants of K3/2(T), K1/2(T) and

8.3.1.2 Rate Constant Computations for the Nonadiabatic
Tetra-atomic O 2 þ O2 Reactions
The electronic energy pooling (or the self-quenching) process
O2(a1n) þ O2(a1n)-O2(b1S) þ O2(x3S) has received a number of experimental
and theoretical treatments, 29,65–69 due to the important role it plays in the
chemical oxygen iodine laser. Howe ver, to the best of our knowledge, no
quantum dynamics study and no rate constant computation for this selfquenching process of the tetra-atomic O
2(a1n) þ O2(a1n) system have previously appeared. It is known that thi s energy pooling process is driven by
the spin–orbit couplings between the si n g l e ta n dt r i p l e ts t a t e so ft h et e t r a -
atomic system with at least six (four sing let and two triplet) electronic states
involved. Due to a lack of potential energy surfaces suitable for the nonadiabatic quantum calculation, we ﬁr st developed the potential energy and
spin–orbit coupling surfa ces which constitute a 6 /C2 6 diabatic potential
matrix. 29 The six adiabatic potential energy surfaces for the four singlet
states were constructed at the CA SSCF/CASPT2 level with the MOLPRO
package. 70 Here S 1,S 2,S 3 and S 4 correspond to the O 2(a1n) þ O2(a1n)r e -
agents, and the two triplet states T 6 and T 7 correspond to the
O2(b1S) þ O2(x3S) products. The spin–orbit couplings between singlet–
triplet states were calculated using the full Breit–Pauli spin-orbital

In Figure 8.5, we depict the three-dimensional (3D) perspective 29 of our
developed potential energy surfaces of S 1,S 2,T 6 and T 7 together with the

(right panel) in the PES contours of the singlet states. The potential energy
surfaces of S 3 and S 4 are not shown here due to the high crossing energies
between these two singlet states and the triplet states. In total, the
products formation in this energy pooling reaction can be via the following
nonadiabatic channels: S
1-T6,S 1-T7,S 2-T6 and S 2-T7,S 3-T6,S 3T7,S 4-T6 and S 4-T7. However, the calculated transition probabilities
from S 3 and S 4 to T 6 and T 7 are very small due to the high crossing energies;
therefore, the calculated results asso ciated with these two singlet states are
not shown.

6/C2 6 diabatic matrix, along with the S
1-T7 transition rate constant, are

202 Chapter 8
 01/12/2013 15:16:37.

## Page 223

presented together with the experimental measurements 65–67 in Figure 8.6. The
nonadiabatic quantum calculation was scattered from the initial ground rovibrational state of the reactants, while the wave packet is initiated on each of the
four singlet states, respectively. As can be seen, good agreement has been
achieved between the nonadiabatic quantum calculation and the experimental
results described in refs. 65 and 66 except at a temperature of 200 K, where the
quantum calculation result is smaller than the corresponding experimental one.
Over the range 320–1600 K, the rate constant for the S
1-T7 transition is also
in good agreement with the experimental measurement, albeit smaller in a low
temperature range 200–320 K. Thus, we can say that at temperatures higher
than 320 K, a reduced two-coupled electronic state model consisting of S
1 and
T7 is fairly good at predicting the thermally averaged dynamical quantities in
the self-quenching system of O 2 þ O2.
A detailed explanation into the role of the nonadiabatic transitions in ISC is
given as follows. More than 63% of the total cross-section of the intersystem
transitions comes from the S
1-T7 transition with a maximum value of 85%;
the S 2-T6 transition cross-section is only B1–4% of the total cross-section,
and the S 1-T6 and S2-T7 transitions each make a contribution of B4–18%.
This is in accordance with the energetic feature of the four electronic states,
that is, S
1-T7 has the smallest energy gap, S 2-T6 has the largest energy gap,
and the energy gaps for S 1-T6 and S 2-T7 are in the middle. Therefore, it
can be inferred that the energetic feature of the system mainly determines the
reactivity of the nonadiabatic transition during the tetra-atomic self-quenching
process.

solid circle and solid triangle represent the experimental measurements

8.3.2 Rate Constant Computations for Adiabatic Reactions
8.3.2.1 Rate Constants for the Adiabatic N( 2D) þ H2 Reaction
on the Ground State Potential Energy Surface
The reaction of N( 2D) with H 2 on the ground electronic state of 1 2A00 belongs
to the family of insertion reactions, which has received substantial attention in
the literature.
74–79 At present, the ground potential energy surface versions of
Ho et al.,75 Qu et al.,76 Varandas and Poveda, 77 etc. all have dynamic accuracy
and thus can be used in the time-dependent quantum wave packet scattering
calculations. Here, in our quantum calculation to derive the rate constants, we
employed the DMBE version of Varandas and Poveda,
77 which was calibrated
from 1498 MRCI/aug-cc-pVQZ calculated energy points together with some
MRCI/aug-cc-pV5Z energy points. The quantum wave packet scattering calculations were performed for the initial rovibrational states of v ¼ 0, j ¼ 0–5.
78
First, for the initial ground rovibrational state of v ¼ 0, j ¼ 0, both the total
reaction probabilities and the total cross-sections are calculated for a collision
energy range of 0.01–1.0 eV. Then, based on these dynamical quantities, the
rate constants are computed using two schemes, that is, the J-shifting scheme of
eqn (8.59) and the scheme using the total cross-sections of eqn (8.55). In the
J-shifting scheme, we used the total reaction probabilities of the three J (J
represents the total angular momentum) values of 0, 10 and 30. Since we have
examined and found that Coriolis coupling has negligible eﬀect on the present
dynamics,
78 the Coriolis coupling terms were excluded from our quantum
scattering calculations and thus saved computational resources. Table 8.2 lists
the computed results for rate constants in the temperature range of 200–1000 K
for the N(
2D) þ H2 (v ¼ 0, j ¼ 0) reaction obtained from the two schemes. Note
here that a multiplicative factor of 1/5 has been included in all the rate constant
calculations to account for the electronic degeneracy because there are ten
electronic doublet states in N( 2D) þ H2 but only two such states are sampled
when studying the dynamics on the 1 2A00 potential energy surface.
Table 8.2 Rate constants for the N( 2D) þ H2 (v ¼ 0, j ¼ 0) reaction computed
using the J-shifting scheme and using the total cross-section. The units
for T and for rate constant are K and cm 3mol/C0 1s/C0 1, respectively.

From Table 8.2 we can see that the rate constants computed by the two
schemes are very close to each other. Thus to save computational resources, we
only applied the J-shifting scheme to compute the rate constants for the cases of
j ¼ 1–5, using the calculated total reaction probabilities at J ¼ 0, 10 and 30. The
calculated rate constants, together with the J-shifting data for j ¼ 0, are shown
in Table 8.3. Overall, there is a slight increase in the rate constants with the
increasing initial rotational quantum number j. This suggests that the rotational excitation of H
2 does not have a very signiﬁcant inﬂuence in reactivity.
Moreover, we computed the rate constants that are statistically averaged
over the rotational states j ¼ 0–5 using the following formula: 78

Here B is the rotational constant of H 2, h is Planck’s constant, c is the velocity
of light in vacuum, and Qk is the rotational partition function, which is

The averaged rate constants over the rotational states are presented in
Figure 8.7, along with the experimental measurements 79 and other theoretical
values.74,75 There is comparatively good agreement between the experimental
measurements and our calculated rate constants on the DMBE surface. The
present values are slightly smaller than Ho’s quasi-classical trajectory (QCT)
rate constants on Ho’s potential energy surface
75 where the barrier heights for
hydrogen abstraction and insertion are about 0.3 kcal mol /C0 1 smaller than the
DMBE potential energy surface we employed. The present quantum results are
also larger than the QCT rate constants
74 calculated, respectively, on a single
12A0 surface (GS) and on the two Renner–Teller surfaces 1 2A0 and 1 2A0 (ES).
Two possible reasons can account for the observed diﬀerences compared with

## Page 226

previous theoretical calculations. First is the diﬀerence in the C 2v barrier height
in the potential energy surface employed in these theoretical studies and second
is some quantum eﬀects lacking in the previous QCT calculations. However,
the good agreement between the experimental measurement and the present
quantum calculation, which was performed only on the ground state adiabatic
potential energy surface, has nonetheless implied that nonadiabatic eﬀects
contribute insigniﬁcantly to the rate constants. In addition, this agrees well with
the ﬁndings from the nonadiabatic studies of Schatz and co-workers
74 carried
out on the two coupled surfaces of the 1 2A00 and 1 2A0 electronic states.
8.3.2.2 Rate Constants for the Adiabatic Polyatomic F þ CH4
Reaction
A fascinating but challenging issue in molecular reaction dynamics is the
characterization of reactive resonances in elementary chemical reactions. Since
Liu and co-workers experimentally demonstrated the existence of the reactive
resonances in the polyatomic reactions of F þ CH
4/CHD3/CD4,80–82 research
interest on the polyatomic reaction of F þ CH4 and its isotope variants has
continued to grow. On the theoretical side and for understanding the reaction
mechanism, some attention is focused on the construction of a 12-dimensional
ground potential energy surface of the polyatomic system while some is on
implementation of dynamical (both QCT and quantum) calculations.
83–88

## Page 227

The eﬀorts on the construction of the potential energy have successfully produced several versions of an analytical potential energy surface that can be used
in dynamical studies—to name a few, the recent potential energy surface of
Bowman and co-workers,
85 and the recent PES-2006 of Espinosa-Garcia
et al. 84 In our quantum dynamical study and rate constant computation, 88 we
employed the PES-2006 version. The ZPE-corrected PES of this version has
a negative barrier of /C0 0.07 kcal mol
/C0 1 with respect to the ZPE-corrected
reactants.84,88
Turning to the quantum scattering calculations, since it is generally
impractical to carry out a full 12-dimensional calculation for the polyatomic
reaction at present, we therefore carried out a reduced ﬁve-dimensional (5D)
quantum calculations based on the semi-rigid vibrating rotor target (SVRT)
model of Zhang and co-workers.
89,90 Here, we ﬁrst brieﬂy describe the 5DSVRT quantum method. In this model, the target CH 4 molecule is treated as a
semi-rigid rotor with the H–CH 3 complex maintaining a C 3v symmetry. The 5D
model describes the three-dimensional spatial motion of the semi-rigid rotor,
the one-dimensional (H
3)C–H pseudo stretching vibration and the onedimensional umbrella motion as well. Thus, the corresponding Hamiltonian for
the polyatomic reactive system can be written as: 90

respectively. The wave packet propagation is performed through the following

More information about the two equations and the 5D-SVRT method can be

By applying the 5D SVRT model and propagating the wave packet, we have
obtained the quantum total cross-sections over a collision energy range of 0.0–
0.35 eV for the polyatomic F þ CH
4 reaction that is initiated from the ground
rovibrational quantum state of j ¼ 0, vr ¼ 0, va ¼ 0.88 Here we use vr and va to
denote the initial quantum number of the pseudo vibration and the umbrella
vibration, respectively, and we note that the umbrella motion is explicitly deﬁned by the r
a coordinate and thus is straightforward in the 5D quantum
calculation. In the real system, however, there is no unique umbrella mode as
described here, and rigorous modeling of the bending modes in reagents of the
F þ CH
4 reaction in the real system requires calculations be carried out
beyond 5D.
Table 8.4 shows our computed rate constants from the calculated total crosssections over the temperature range of 180–400 K for the polyatomic system, in
conjunction with the experimental
84 and the previous improved canonical
variational transition (ICVT) theoretical data. 84 A multiplication factor of 4
was included in the present 5D rate constants to make a reasonable comparison. In total, there is a qualitatively agreement of our computed rate
constants with these existing data, that is, the present results show a reasonable
agreement, slightly underestimating and overestimating the experimental rate
constants at low and high temperature by factors of 1.17 and 1.21, respectively.
The diﬀerence may be attributed to the present reduced quantum scattering
calculations and it is our hope to see a more than 5D quantum scattering
calculation for this polyatomic reaction at the near future.
8.4 Conclusions and Prospects
In this chapter we present the time-dependent quantum wave packet approaches that can be used to compute rate constants for both nonadiabatic and
adiabatic chemical reactions. The emphasis is placed on our recently developed
time-dependent quantum wave packet methods for dealing with nonadiabatic
processes in tri-atomic and tetra-atomic reaction systems. Quantum wave
packet studies and rate constants computations of nonadiabatic reaction processes have been dynamically achieved by implementing nuclear wave packet
propagation on multiple electronic states, in combination with the coupled
diabatic PESs constructed from ab initio calculations. To this end, newly developed propagators are incorporated into the solution of the time-dependent
Schro¨dinger equation in matrix formulism. Applications of the nonadiabatic
time-dependent wave packet approaches and the adiabatic ones to the rate
constant computations of the nonadiabatic tri-atomic F (P
3/2,P 1/2) þ D2 (v ¼ 0,
208 Chapter 8
 01/12/2013 15:16:37.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00180
View Online

## Page 229

j ¼ 0) and to the nonadiabatic self-quenching process of tetra-atomic O 2 þ O2
have provided informative quantities with clear pictures of the contributions to
the reactivity of these systems from the involved electronic states and have
deepened our understanding of nonadiabatic eﬀects. Moreover, based on the
high level ab initio data, we have ﬁtted the potential matrix required in the
nonadiabatic studies of the O
2 þ O2 tetra-atomic system.
Understanding both nonadiabatic and adiabatic features of reaction processes is of great signiﬁcance in molecular physics and chemistry, and quantum
dynamics simulation of nonadiabatic transitions and adiabatic reactive processes can provide the most precise information. However, such a task remains
a continuing challenge to theoreticians. We are beginning to implement
quantum simulations of nonadiabatic transitions and adiabatic reactive processes in relatively small molecular systems, such as tri-atomic and tetra-atomic
systems, of which our understanding of nonadiabatic dynamics processes is still
relatively rare at a quantum state-to-state level. Work to extend to more tetraatomic systems and on deepening to state-to-state level is underway. At present,
however, there is a bottleneck in extending quantum nonadiabatic investigation
to polyatomic systems with high dimensionality due to the computational
diﬃculty. Consequently, quantitative and semi-quantitative modeling of nonadiabatic transitions in polyatomic cases can turn to developing further semiclassical methods. Within this regard, dynamical quantities from accurate
nonadiabatic quantum scattering calculations can serve as benchmark results
for researchers to testify and validate their developed modeling methods for
nonadiabatic dynamics in polyatomic reaction systems.

## Page 233

CHAPTER 9
Understanding Reactivity with
Reduced Potential Energy
Landscapes: Recent Advances
and New Directions
BRYAN R. GOLDSMITH, a ANTHONY FONG a AND
BARON PETERS* a,b
a Department of Chemical Engineering, University of California, Santa
Barbara, CA 93106-5080, USA; b Department of Chemistry and
Biochemistry, University of California, Santa Barbara, CA 93106-9510, USA
*Email: baronp@engineering.ucsb.edu
9.1 Introduction
The ability to navigate the potential energy surface (PES) eﬀectively is
important in order to study reactions in the gas phase, in solution or at gas–
solid interfaces using ab initio methods. Over the past few decades, the
development of novel algorithms for exploration of the PES has greatly
enhanced our understanding of reaction mechanisms. However, for some
systems like large biomolecules or catalysts on amorphous supports, our
capabilities remain limited. The remaining diﬃculties can stem from either the
high dimensionality of the PES and/or the lack of representative models. For
example, proteins often have a high-dimensional and rugged potential energy
landscape consisting of many local minima and maxima. While there are many
powerful algorithms for ﬁnding transition states, they often ﬁnd chemically
RSC Theoretical and Computational Chemistry Series No. 6
Reaction Rate Constant Computations: Theories and Applications
Edited by Keli Han and Tianshu Chu
r The Royal Society of Chemistry 2014
Published by the Royal Society of Chemistry, www.rsc.org
213
 01/12/2013 15:16:48.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00213

## Page 234

unimportant saddle points or relocate already known transition states. The
ability to navigate the PES eﬃciently and to ﬁnd new and chemically interesting
transition states on demand is still an active area of research. However, for
reactions occurring on amorphous supports like silica or silica–alumina, the
lack of structural information makes it diﬃcult to construct representative
cluster models.
Fortunately, there have been some recent developments utilizing the concept
of the reduced potential energy surface (RPES) that have the potential to help
resolve some of these diﬃculties for both exploring the PES of complex systems
and for studying catalytic sites on amorphous supports. In this chapter, we
ﬁrst brieﬂy overview ab initio landscape search algorithms and mention some
current algorithmic limitations. We then review the RPES and past research
eﬀorts to utilize this powerful framework to explore potential energy landscapes. In particular, we review the Boﬁll–Anglada RPES transition state
search algorithm, which allows for targeted transition state searches.
1 In this
algorithm the RPES retains degrees of freedom associated with the reaction
coordinate and ‘bath’ degrees of freedom adiabatically follow the reaction
coordinates. We then reverse the Boﬁll–Anglada perspective and show how
a RPES retaining only the peripheral degrees of freedom, with the reaction
coordinates adiabatically relaxed, can help model catalysts on amorphous
supports. The latter involves a new sequential nonlinear programming
algorithm which we demonstrate on a two-dimensional (2D) model energy
surface, on a simpliﬁed ‘chemical’ model and ﬁnally for ethene polymerization
on the Phillips catalyst (Cr/SiO
2).
9.2 Background
Reactions are of critical importance for both biological and chemical processes.
For example, all biochemical processes are comprised of solution phase
reactions, and heterogeneous catalysis facilitates the conversion of natural
resources into fuels and numerous commercial products. A molecular level
understanding of a reaction mechanism and the computation of rates requires
knowledge of the stable structures and the corresponding transition states that
connect them. On the PES, stable reactants, products and intermediates are
characterized by a zero gradient vector and a positive deﬁnite Hessian.
Newton–Raphson minimization
2–4 or quasi-Newton–Raphson 2,5 algorithms
are often used to ﬁnd minima on the PES. A more diﬃcult problem than energy
minimization is to locate relevant transition state (TS) structures. TS structures
correspond to ﬁrst-order saddle points, i.e. they maximize the potential along
one unstable direction and minimize the potential in all other directions.
Unstable and stable directions are typically identiﬁed by diagonalizing the
‘Hessian’, a matrix of second derivatives of the potential energy. Once a saddle
point has been located, walking downhill in energy in both directions from the
TS usually leads to two minima (exceptions occur where a pathway down from
the saddle bifurcates
6). In this article, we will refer to the minima on either side
214 Chapter 9
 01/12/2013 15:16:48.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00213
View Online

## Page 235

of the TS as the reactant and product states associated with the TS, even
though they may be intermediates in a multistep reaction pathway.
Over the past few decades many reliable ab initio TS search algorithms have
been developed, which has enabled a revolution in the level at which reaction
mechanisms can be understood. Some of these TS search algorithms are the
partitioned rational function optimization algorithm,
7,8 the Cerjan–Miller
algorithm9 (based on insight by Poppinger, 10 and afterward improved by
Simons and co-workers 11–14), the dimer methods, 8,15–18 the growing string
method,19 the force-reversed method, 20 and the nudged elastic band (NEB)
algorithm.21–24 Utilizing these TS search algorithms in conjunction with
ab initio calculations25–29 has enabled literally thousands of computational
mechanistic analyses at an atomistic level of detail, for example, computational
modeling in catalysis,
30–37 in enzymes, 38–43 in inorganic chemistry 44–47 and in
organic chemistry. 48–50
Even with the variety of TS search algorithms available, some challenges
remain for ﬁnding TS structures. Double-ended methods ( i.e. both the initial
and ﬁnal positions for the corresponding TS are speciﬁed) like the NEB
algorithm are robust and reliable if a good initial pathway is provided,
51–53 but
these require the computational chemist to input the initial and ﬁnal positions
of each atom in the system, which requires some a priori mechanistic
understanding. If one wishes to discover truly unexpected reaction mechanisms,
then double-ended methods are not the best strategy. Eigenvector-following
and dimer type algorithms converge rapidly when initiated near a saddle point,
and thus they are often used to reﬁne rough estimates of the transition state
after a few iterations of the more robust but slowly converging NEB type
methods.
22 Algorithms like the eigenvector-following methods 9,11–14,54,55 and
the dimer method 8,15–17 are also useful for their ability to reveal unanticipated
transition states. These algorithms can be initiated near the reactant minimum,
from which they can systematically climb up valleys on the PES to unexpected
saddle point regions leading to unforeseen intermediates or products. However,
high dimensional conﬁguration spaces tend to present many chemically
uninteresting saddle points, for example, the inconsequential 120 1 rotation of
a methyl group typically passes through a saddle point. When eigenvectorfollowing or dimer searches are not monitored carefully, it is possible to waste
enormous amounts of computational time discovering such uninteresting
transition states.
Additionally, the eigenvector-following and dimer methods tend to
rediscover the same transition states over and over again. For example, a study
pertaining to island diﬀusion using the dimer method illustrates that among 50
total searches only seven transition states were found, i.e. nearly 90% of search
trajectories rediscovered transition states that had previously been found.
56
By eliminating rediscovery in the previous example one could accelerate the
discovery of all seven transition states by nearly an order of magnitude.
Although the speciﬁc example pertains to the dimer method, the rediscovery
problem hampers nearly all intuition-free methods. Saddle point searches
initiated from many diﬀerent positions within a stable basin tend to locate only
Understanding Reactivity with Reduced Potential Energy Landscapes 215
 01/12/2013 15:16:48.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00213
View Online

## Page 236

a few saddle points. Peters and co-workers developed a method that uses
memory of the previously discovered saddle points to bias a TS search and
eliminate rediscovery.
57 Unfortunately, this method is diﬃcult to implement
and so computationally expensive that it may be preferable to tolerate
rediscovery.
57
Reduced potential energy surface (RPES) search algorithms, while no longer
completely intuition free, can potentially ﬁx the rediscovery problem. RPES
ideas were formulated in the 1980s by Rothman and Scharfenberg.
58–60 RPES
transition state search algorithms focus on ﬁnding transition states that involve
speciﬁc degrees of freedom, i.e. coordinates involved in bond breaking and
bond making. One RPES method is coordinate driving,
59,60 where a single
coordinate (often a linear combination of several internal coordinates) is used
to scan the PES. During the scan, the driving coordinate is constrained and the
remaining coordinates are relaxed. If the coordinate is chosen carefully, the
result of coordinate driving is a continuous potential energy proﬁle that
identiﬁes the activation barrier and the transition state. The disadvantage is
that coordinate driving requires a priori knowledge not only of which degrees of
freedom are important in the reaction coordinate, but also how they should be
combined in the driving coordinate.
Quapp and co-workers developed the reduced gradient following
method,
61–63 which attempts to ﬁnd saddle points by following curves having a
non-zero gradient in only one coordinate. The reduced gradient following
method is also useful for ﬁnding sets of valley ridge inﬂections and branching
points.
61,62 Boﬁll, Anglada and co-workers proposed a local quadratic
approximation method for following reaction paths on a RPES. 64 Ayers and
co-workers used the RPES with a generalized fast marching method to locate
the minimum energy path by spreading points on a grid from the reactant
conﬁguration toward the product conﬁguration.
65–68 Their method could be
applied to ﬁnd the minimum energy path with knowledge of only the reactant
state. Interestingly, some RPES TS search methods put forward by Ayers and
co-workers do not require an initial Hessian evaluation. 69 In one method,
Ayers and co-workers ﬁt the reduced Hessian via Shepard interpolation 70,71
before each step 69—an important advantage for implementation with electronic structure programs like VASP 72,73 that do not provide a Hessian matrix
except by expensive ﬁnite diﬀerence procedures.
Boﬁll and Anglada developed a particularly interesting RPES TS search
algorithm.1 The Boﬁll–Anglada algorithm focuses on ﬁnding transition states
that involve a few speciﬁc degrees of freedom, e.g. the coordinates involved in
bond breaking and bond making. The algorithm retains in the RPES a small
subspace of ‘reduced’ internal coordinates, q
r, with the remaining ‘bath’
coordinates, qb, adopting positions that minimize the energy subject to the
reduced coordinates. They used a Schur transformation 74 to locally expand the
RPES, and then formulated a Cerjan–Miller type saddle search on the RPES.
The algorithm eﬀectively walks up a valley on the RPES using a set of reduced
coordinates while simultaneously optimizing the unimportant bath degrees of
freedom.
216 Chapter 9
 01/12/2013 15:16:48.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00213
View Online

## Page 237

Surprisingly, Boﬁll and Anglada do not emphasize what may be the most
important advantage of their RPES transition state search algorithm. By
simply changing the q
r subspace to include diﬀerent bond lengths, bond angles
or torsion angles, their saddle search algorithm could be directed to a diﬀerent
TS with each new search trajectory. The Boﬁll–Anglada RPES algorithm
therefore provides a practical solution to the rediscovery problem without
requiring detailed a priori knowledge of the reaction mechanisms. Using
subspaces that never include degrees of freedom like methyl rotations can also
eliminate discovery of these chemically uninteresting saddle points.
Although the Boﬁll–Anglada algorithm has great potential, it has not been
widely used because of its brief history and complicated implementation.
Some challenges with the Boﬁll–Anglada algorithm are: (1) it uses internal but
non-redundant coordinates; (2) it involves diﬀerent mathematical operations
from more typical saddle search algorithms; and (3) it requires evaluation and
diagonalization of the Hessian and/or Hessian updates for a matrix that is not
positive deﬁnite. Nonetheless, the algorithm remains a useful tool for targeted
TS searches, and with further development, similar algorithms could become
the state-of-the-art tool.
The approaches mentioned above retain reaction coordinates in the RPES
and use the RPES framework to ﬁnd transition states. In the following sections,
we use the RPES in a very diﬀerent manner to analyze isolated active sites on
amorphous catalyst supports. This approach retains the peripheral coordinates
(corresponding to atoms away from the active site) in the RPES to represent
diﬀerent catalyst sites by models with diﬀerent peripheral atom positions. We
then use the RPES with sequential nonlinear programming to systematically
generate models of amorphous catalyst sites with diﬀerent reactivities. In this
fashion, active and inactive catalyst site structures with low (and therefore
favorable) formation energies are systematically generated. The comparison
between active and inactive sites could elucidate structure–property
relationships—a challenge that has long evaded computational studies of
amorphous catalysts.
In this chapter, we derive and illustrate the RPES method for modeling
catalyst sites on amorphous supports. The examples include a 2D model energy
surface, an ‘over-simpliﬁed’ chemical model to illustrate our view of active sites
in amorphous supports, and a cluster model of the propagation step on a
Phillips catalyst (Cr/SiO
2) for ethene polymerization. Before formulating the
sequential nonlinear programming algorithm to study catalysis on amorphous
supports, we ﬁrst provide an overview of the mathematical basis of algorithms
for navigating the RPES.
9.3 Reduced Potential Energy Surface
Here we review the mathematical formulation of the RPES. We use qr to
denote the X reduced ( i.e. the retained) coordinates and qb to denote the
remaining 3 N-6-X bath coordinates, where N is the number of atoms. In most
RPES applications, the coordinates qr are essentially those degrees of freedom

We denote the energy, gradient and Hessian matrix represented on the

b ¼ 0b, which simpliﬁes the expressions above considerably. The energy at any point on the RPES then depends only on the reduced
coordinates (see eqn (9.6)), as originally deﬁned in eqn (9.1). The reduced
Hessian matrix, H
red, is the Schur complement 74 of the full Hessian matrix.
Remarkably, the number of negative eigenvalues from the full Hessian matrix
is equal to the number of negative eigenvalues from the reduced Hessian
matrix.
75,76 Consequently, a minimum or TS on the RPES corresponds to a
minimum or TS on the full PES. 58,64 Now that the mathematical framework of
the RPES has been described, we will proceed to explain the sequential nonlinear programming algorithm to study catalytic sites on amorphous supports.
9.4 RPES Algorithm to Study Catalysis on Amorphous
Supports
The design of new, high-performance heterogeneous catalysts continues to
play an important role in the global economy. However, the structures of the
active sites on many supported catalysts are often not well-deﬁned, which
presents a formidable challenge for computational modeling. In particular,
new ab initio modeling strategies are needed to study catalytic sites on
amorphous supports. A typical strategy is to use cluster models optimized with
constraints on the peripheral atoms to mimic embedding in a solid matrix.
Figure 9.1 Every time the reduced coordinates are changed, the bath coordinates take
on positions that re-optimize the system energy.
Understanding Reactivity with Reduced Potential Energy Landscapes 219
 01/12/2013 15:16:48.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00213
View Online

## Page 240

However, the lack of structural information about amorphous materials
requires arbitrary placement of the constrained peripheral atoms and diﬀerent
choices lead to model sites with diﬀerent activities. Another strategy is to allow
the peripheral atoms of the cluster model to move during the course of a
reaction, but models of this type are unrealistically ﬂexible for sites embedded
in a solid matrix.
To correctly model a solid structure one must impose geometric constraints
on the peripheral atoms of the cluster model.
33,77–81 There is an established
procedure for crystalline materials, as one can constrain the peripheral atoms to
be in their crystallographic positions. However, in amorphous materials, the
positions to constrain the peripheral atoms are not obvious. In actuality,
amorphous materials have an unknown distribution of site environments and
thus there is no single conﬁguration to constrain the peripheral atoms!
Unfortunately, sampling the full distribution of site environments, and therefore the spectrum of sites according to reactivity, e.g. using Monte Carlo, is too
expensive using ab initio techniques.
To enable previously impossible ab initio studies of catalysis on amorphous
supports we exploit the RPES framework and constrained optimization, which
ﬁnds the lowest energy catalyst site structure at each value of the activation
barrier (see Figure 9.2). This knowledge allows one to distinguish between
highly active and ‘dead’ sites. In this section, we ﬁrst outline the mathematical
framework of the algorithm and then illustrate it on a 2D model energy surface.
Afterwards, the algorithm is applied to a simple cluster model. (Although it
could represent proton transfer from an amine and an oxo ligand on
amorphous carbon, the model system is primarily intended as an illustration of
the method.) Then we illustrate the method for a more realistic cluster model
of the Phillips catalyst (Cr/SiO
2) undergoing ethene insertion during
propagation.
Figure 9.2 The sequential nonlinear programming algorithm generates the lowest
energy catalyst site structure for a speciﬁed activation energy, DEz.

9.4.2 Model Energy Landscape
We now apply the proposed algorithm to a 2D model energy surface shown in
Figure 9.3(a). 61,82,83 Here there are only two coordinates: the peripheral (reduced) coordinate can be thought of as the amorphous catalyst environment
and the interior (bath) coordinate as the reaction coordinate. As the algorithm
generates ‘sites’ with DE
z decreasing toward zero, the transition state and
reactant state begin to merge (toward a less negative peripheral coordinate,
Figure 9.3(a)). Eventually, the reactant state loses metastability relative to the
product state. The algorithm cannot proceed beyond this point because the
reactant and product states cease to be separated by a transition state. This can
be observed in Figure 9.3(b), which tracks the smallest interior block eigenvalue
of the reactant state and transition state as the peripheral coordinate changes.
The interior block of the reactant state Hessian ( H
bb
R) must remain positive
deﬁnite and the interior block of the transition state ( Hbb
z) must have one
negative eigenvalue.
It is possible to probe structural and chemical changes as the system
moves along a path in the peripheral space ( i.e. Figure 9.3A(a)). Regardless
of the system dimensionality, this can readily be accomplished using site
energy vs. activation energy curves, as shown in Figure 9.3(c). These generated
Figure 9.3 (a) A model energy landscape, similar to work used to study enzyme
kinetics,84 displaying a path of reactant states and a path of transition
states at diﬀerent values of the peripheral coordinate (diﬀerent values of
DEz). (b) The lowest interior block eigenvalue of the transition state and
reactant state at diﬀerent values of the peripheral coordinate. (c) Catalyst
site energy vs. activation energy curves can be generated from which key
structural diﬀerences between active sites, typical, and inactive sites can be
identiﬁed.

## Page 245

curves have the potential to yield great chemical insight as they can reveal
structural diﬀerences between low energy catalyst sites that are active and
inactive.
9.4.3 Cluster Model of Proton Transfer between Anchored
Amine and Oxo Ligand
We now illustrate the algorithm on a higher dimensional model system
undergoing a single proton transfer (Figure 9.4). This was modeled at the HF/
3-21g level of theory. Although this could represent a simpliﬁed model of
proton transfer from an amine to an oxo group on amorphous carbon, we
emphasize that the primary purpose is to illustrate the algorithm for a simple
and easily reproduced chemical example.
85 Therefore we are not concerned
with accuracy of the model chemistry. The small cluster model contains 10
atoms. The NH
2 functional group and the oxygen make up the interior space,
i.e. the atoms which most directly participate in the proton transfer. The remaining atoms, i.e. those marked immobilized in Figure 9.4, represent atoms
which are anchored in a solid amorphous matrix. These ﬁxed atoms make up
the peripheral space.
The step size in activation energy, DDEz; was set to 0.0001 Ha per iteration.
The 3-aminoacrylaldehyde site energy, DEsite, is reported relative to the lowest
site energy. As shown in Figure 9.5, the nonlinear programming algorithm
generated 3-aminoacrylaldehyde sites with an activation energy range between
0 and 180 kJ mol
/C0 1 and site energy changes up to 120 kJ mol /C0 1. Recalling eqn
(9.19), it is emphasized that the curve in Figure 9.5 represents the most favorable site structure for any given activation energy. With this information,
one can begin to investigate structure–property relationships between active
and inactive sites. Here we examine the distance between the oxo ligand and the
proton being transferred (see r
H–O in Figure 9.4) as a function of activation
energy (Figure 9.6). From chemical intuition in this simple example, it is no
surprise that the algorithm is generating sites with decreasing r
H–O to lower the
activation barrier for proton transfer. Although this example is extremely
Figure 9.4 A simple cluster model of an amine and an oxo group grafted to an
amorphous carbon support. The reaction modeled is the proton transfer
from the amine to the oxo ligand. The NH 2 functional group and the
oxygen make up the interior space while those marked immobilized
represent atoms which make up the peripheral space.
Understanding Reactivity with Reduced Potential Energy Landscapes 225
 01/12/2013 15:16:48.

View Online

## Page 246

simple, this is a promising ﬁrst step towards ab initio modeling of sites on
amorphous supports. Having now demonstrated the algorithm on a 2D model
energy surface and a simple molecular model, we turn to an industrially relevant catalytic system to demonstrate the algorithm.
9.4.4 Ethene Insertion During Polymerization on the
Phillips Catalyst
The Phillips catalyst (Cr/amorphous SiO 2) is involved in the manufacture of
million tons of polyethylene every year, 86 but the origin of its reactivity remains
unclear. Here we apply the algorithm to study ethene insertion during polymerization by a model for the Phillips catalyst. The model is shown in
Figure 9.7, and all energies and structures in this example have quadruplet spin
multiplicity. The cluster model was optimized at the B3LYP/LANL2DZ level
for all atoms except the ﬂuorine atoms, which instead had a 6-31g basis. Basis
deﬁcient ﬂuorine atoms were chosen as the capping atoms to mimic the size of
Figure 9.6 Distance in the reactant state between the oxo ligand and the proton of
3-aminoacrylaldehyde (see Figure 9.4) shown as a function of activation
energy.
Figure 9.5 Catalyst site energy vs. activation energy curve for the proton transfer
from an amine to an oxo ligand on amorphous carbon.
226 Chapter 9
 01/12/2013 15:16:48.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00213
View Online

## Page 247

oxygen and the electron withdrawing strength of an extended SiO 2
framework.87,88
For the cluster model shown in Figure 9.7, the six ﬂuorine atoms were chosen
as the peripheral coordinates (to mimic the extended solid matrix). The step size
in activation energy was set to 0.0001 Ha per iteration. The sequential nonlinear
program generated low energy catalyst sites with activation energies for ethene
insertion between 7 and 19 kJ mol
/C0 1 as shown in Figure 9.8. The minimum
energy site has activation energy near 14 kJ mol /C0 1. Catalyst site models with
formation energies up to 10 kJ mol /C0 1 higher than the minimum energy site were
generated. Assuming a Boltzmann distribution, the catalyst sites that contribute the most to the rate are where the slope on Figure 9.8 is equal to –1. This
corresponds to sites with activation energy near 11.5 kJ mol
/C0 1. This ﬁnding
emphasizes that the catalyst sites with the most favorable formation energy are
not necessary the sites that contribute the most to the rate. As the site energy vs.
activation energy curve widens (for more ﬂexible systems), the sites that contribute the most to the rate are expected to shift further from the minimum
energy site.
We now turn our attention again to look for simple structure–property
relationships. We chose to look at the non-bonded Si–Si atom distance,
r
Si–Si, as a function of activation energy for ethene insertion (Figure 9.9).
Figure 9.7 Phillips catalyst cluster model used to study ethene insertion during
propagation.
Figure 9.8 Catalyst site energy vs. activation energy curve for ethene insertion during
propagation on the Phillips catalyst.
Understanding Reactivity with Reduced Potential Energy Landscapes 227
 01/12/2013 15:16:48.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00213
View Online

## Page 248

The r Si–Si distance could indicate a measure of compression in the catalyst site
due to embedding in the solid matrix. The RPES framework reveals that sites
with larger r Si–Si distances have larger activation barriers for ethene insertion
than sites with shorter r Si–Si distances.
9.5 Conclusions
In this chapter we discussed the recent development of ab initio reduced
potential energy surface (RPES) algorithms for ﬁnding transition states and
optimizing reaction paths. In particular, we emphasized promising characteristics of the Boﬁll–Anglada transition state search algorithm. The Boﬁll–
Anglada algorithm can focus the search on ﬁnding transition states that involve
certain important degrees of freedom. This capability suggests that the Boﬁll–
Anglada algorithm might, with some further developments, help eliminate the
wasted and costly searches that simply rediscover already known transition
states.
We have also shown how an RPES framework, in combination with sequential nonlinear programming, can help identify structure–property relationships for active sites on amorphous catalyst supports. The algorithm
systematically generates a family of low energy sites with varying reactivity and
thereby reveals structural diﬀerences between highly active and ‘dead’ sites on
the catalyst surface. We demonstrated the RPES-based algorithm for understanding amorphous catalysts on three examples including a likely limiting step
for ethene polymerization. This new application to amorphous catalysts illustrates how the powerful RPES framework can enable calculations that were
previously intractable.

## Page 253

CHAPTER 10
Quantum-Classical Liouville
Dynamics of Condensed Phase
Quantum Processes
GABRIEL HANNA* a AND RAYMOND KAPRAL b
a Department of Chemistry, University of Alberta, Edmonton, Alberta,
Canada; b Chemical Physics Theory Group, Department of Chemistry,
University of Toronto, Toronto, Ontario M5S 3H6, Canada
*Email: gabriel.hanna@ualberta.ca
10.1 Introduction
It is diﬃcult to simulate quantum rate and relaxation processes in condensed
phase chemical and biological systems . The large and often complex nature of
these systems necessitates the ability to follow the quantum dynamics of the
entire system, often for long periods of time. In many instances the dynamical
process of interest can be described by a relevant subset of the degrees of
freedom (DOF) of the entire system, but the coupling of this subsystem to its
environment or bath cannot be neglect ed. This partitioning into subsystem
and bath has spawned a number of approaches for the computation of
quantum rate processes. These incl ude a variety of quantum master and
other equations for the density matrix of the subsystem where the bath DOF
appear only implicitly through memory k ernels or transition rates. In some
circumstances, when the bath DOF ev olve much more slowly than those of
the subsystem, adiabatic dynamics may provide a useful description of the
evolution. In adiabatic dynamics, one makes the Born–Oppenheimer
RSC Theoretical and Computational Chemistry Series No. 6
Reaction Rate Constant Computations: Theories and Applications
Edited by Keli Han and Tianshu Chu
r The Royal Society of Chemistry 2014
Published by the Royal Society of Chemistry, www.rsc.org
233
 01/12/2013 15:16:56.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00233

## Page 254

approximation and ﬁrst solves the Schro ¨dinger equation for the subsystem
variables in the ﬁeld of the ﬁxed bat h coordinates. The resulting energy
eigenvalues depend parametrically on the bath coordinates and serve as
potential energy functions for the evolution of the bath. While often
tractable, adiabatic dynamics do not al ways provide an adequate description
of the system. In such circumstances one must account for nonadiabatic
eﬀects.
Nonadiabatic dynamics is a quantum phenomenon which occurs in systems
that interact suﬃciently strongly with their environments to cause a breakdown
of the Born–Oppenheimer approximation. Nonadiabatic transitions play signiﬁcant roles in many chemical processes such as proton and electron transfer
events in solution and biological systems, and in the response of molecules to
radiation ﬁelds and their subsequent relaxation. Since the bath in which the
quantum dynamics of interest occurs often consists of relatively heavy molecules, its evolution can be modeled by classical mechanics to a high degree of
accuracy. This observation has led to the development of mixed quantum–
classical methods for nonadiabatic processes.
In mixed quantum–classical approaches one is faced with the issue of how
to combine quantum and classical dy namics. Adiabatic dynamics as discussed above is perhaps the simplest of such methods. To go beyond adiabatic dynamics, mean ﬁeld and surface-hopping schemes have been
constructed.
1–4 In mean ﬁeld descriptions, the total wave function is
decomposed into a fast part corresponding to the light (quantum) particles
and a slow part corresponding to the he avy (classical) particles. The timedependent Schro¨dinger equation for the light particles is solved and the resulting wave function is used to compute the force which determines the
evolution of the classical DOF. In surface-hopping schemes,
2 the wave
function of the quantum subsystem is again propagated using the timedependent Schro¨dinger equation. However, the classical DOF now evolve on
single adiabatic potential energy surfaces and hopping between these surfaces
occurs according to probabilistic rul es. The nonadiabatic approach we consider in this chapter is based on the qu antum–classical Liouville equation,
5–14
which speciﬁes the evolution of the de nsity matrix for a quantum mechanical
subsystem coupled to a classical environment. This approach retains quantum coherence in a more rigorous way th an the surface hopping or mean ﬁeld
approaches.
In the following sections we show how the quantum–classical Liouville
equation and quantum–classical expressions for reaction rates can be deduced
from the full quantum expressions. The formalism is then applied to the investigation of nonadiabatic proton transfer reactions in condensed phase polar
solvents. A quantum–classical Liouville-based method for calculating linear
and nonlinear vibrational spectra is then described, which involves nonequilibrium dynamics on multiple adiabatic potential energy surfaces. This
method is then used to investigate the linear and third-order vibrational
spectroscopy of a proton stretching mode in a solvated hydrogen-bonded
complex.

10.2 Nonadiabatic Dynamics
In the description of nonadiabatic processes it is often convenient to partition
the entire quantum system into a quantum subsystem whose properties are of
primary interest and its quantum environment or bath. The Hamiltonian,
^H ¼ ^P2=2M þ ^p2=2m þ ^Vð^q; ^QÞ, consists of kinetic energy operators of the
bath and subsystem, respectively, and the total potential energy. In writing this
Hamiltonian we have used lower case symbols to refer to the quantum subsystem and upper case symbols to refer to its quantum environment. If the
quantum subsystem is prepared in a speciﬁc state, its subsequent evolution may
involve transitions among several potential energy surfaces which will determine how the populations and oﬀ-diagonal elements of the density matrix will
evolve in time.
The evolution of the density matrix, ^rðtÞ, that describes such processes is
given by the quantum Liouville equation:

and quantum bath by taking the partial Wigner transform over the bath DOF:

Similar considerations apply to the study of quantum reaction rates. In most
instances one is interested in a rate process involving a subset of the DOF of the
system characterized by some reaction coordinate operator (or set of reaction
coordinate operators) ^x. This is the case, for example, for electron and proton
transfers from a reactant state A to a product state B taking place in a
condensed phase chemical or biochemical system. The free energy W along a
reaction coordinate often has the typical double-well form shown in

In these examples the dynamics is not conﬁned to a single adiabatic potential
energy surface so that the full quantum dynamics of the entire system must be
followed in order to obtain the observable of interest. For large systems, typical
of condensed phase applications, this is a computationally diﬃcult, if not impossible, task. For this reason, we focus our attention on quantum–classical
descriptions where such limitations are much less severe. In particular, the
formulation based on the quantum–classical Liouville equation is the topic of
the remainder of this chapter.
10.3 Quantum–Classical Liouville Dynamics
The quantum–classical Liouville equation (QCLE) provides an approximate
but accurate description of a quantum subsystem coupled in an arbitrary
manner to a bath that can be described by classical dynamics in the absence of
coupling to the quantum subsystem. The QCLE describes the time evolution of
the partially Wigner transformed density matrix of the system ^r
W ðR; P; tÞ
discussed above, and is given by:

variables, is the QCLE. We note that the reduction to a quantum–classical
system can also be justiﬁed by arguments based on decoherence.
15 This equation has been shown to provide an accurate description of the quantum dynamics of the subsystem. Given this quantum–classical description of the
dynamics, the observables of interest can be computed. The subsystem density
matrix elements ^raa0
s ðtÞ can be computed as described above by integrating over
the bath phase space variables in order to determine the quantum–classical
values of the populations and coherences of the subsystem.

by taking the quantum-classical limit of the dynamics while retaining the full
quantum equilibrium structure in the correlation function expression in eqn

the evaluation of the spectral density function which contains equilibrium
structural information about the system.
10.4 Simulation of Quantum–Classical Dynamics
Several algorithms have been constructed to simulate the solution of the
QCLE. The simulation methods usually utilize particular representations of the
quantum subsystem. Surface-hopping schemes that make use of the adiabatic
basis have been constructed;
12,16–19 density matrix evolution has been carried
out in the diabatic basis using trajectory-based methods, 20 some of which make
use of a mapping representation of the diabatic states. 21,22 A representation of
the dynamics in the force basis has been implemented to simulate the dynamics
using the multithreads algorithm. 23,24
Here we outline the solution of the QCLE in an adiabatic basis based on a
decomposition of the propagator into short-time segments. 16,18 The quantumclassical evolution of Naa0
B ðR; P; tÞ in the adiabatic basis can be written as:

The momentum jump prescribed by this equation corresponds to the energy
transfer in a transition and satisﬁes energy conservation. If there is insuﬃcient
kinetic energy available in the bath for the quantum subsystem to make the
transition, the transition is not allowed.
This algorithm corresponds to a surface-hopping trajectory picture of the
dynamics. The short-time segments involve evolution along the surface ( aa
0),
which may be adiabatic (when a ¼ a0) or the arithmetic mean of two adiabatic
surfaces (when aaa0), governed by the propagator e/C0iðoaa0 þLaa0 Þt. These trajectory segments are interrupted by nonadiabatic transitions due to the operator
J , which causes the system to undergo a transition to a new surface (or mean
surface), followed by subsequent evolution on this surface.
10.5 Proton Transfer Reactions
Proton transfer reactions occur often in chemical and biological systems and an
understanding of their mechanisms and a knowledge of their rates are essential
for a description of such systems.
25 Consider a prototypical proton transfer of
the form AH /C0BÐA /C0HB occurring in a condensed phase polar environment.
As a result of electrostatic interactions between the proton and polar solvent
molecules, solvent dynamics will aﬀect the proton transfer in a hydrogen bond.
Hydrogen bonds can be classiﬁed as weak or strong. In weak hydrogen bonds
the proton acceptor and donor groups are typically far apart and a high energy
barrier separates minima corresponding to metastable states of the hydrogenbonded complex. In strong hydrogen bonds the proton acceptor and donor
groups are typically close together and a low (or no) barrier separates metastable minima.
A model for this type of reaction was constructed by Azzouz and Borgis
26 and
further details concerning the interaction potentials can be found in ref. 27. The
AHB complex is linear and the position of the proton is described by a onedimensional (1D) coordinate operator ^q corresponding to the distance between
the center of mass of the complex and the proton. The potential model incorporates the variation of the charge distribution with the proton position
within the complex. The potential energy function describing hydrogen bonding
within the complex was chosen to model a slightly strongly hydrogen-bonded
phenol ( A) trimethylamine ( B)c o m p l e x .T h eAHB complex is dissolved in a
solvent composed of polar, nonpolarizable model methyl chloride molecules.
Although the covalent form of the complex, AH /C0B, is favored in the absence of
solvent, the ionic form, A
/C0– HB1, is the more stable conﬁguration in the dipolar
solvent. This model has been studied using a number of methods. 27–33
A time-dependent rate coeﬃcient for the proton transfer reaction AÐB can
be deﬁned by setting t/C3 in eqn (10.12) to a general time t. Making use of the
symmetry properties of Wa0a
A the rate coeﬃcient can be written as:

The angular brackets h/C1 /C1 /C1iare deﬁned as h/C1 /C1 /C1i /C17R dX /C1/C1/C1e/C0bH1 = R dX e /C0bH1 ,

i P2
i =2Mi þ E1ðRÞ.
The full QCL dynamics is replaced by adiabatic dynamics on the ground state
surface in eqn (10.29). Information about the mechanism for the proton transfer
can be obtained from an examination of the adiabatic dynamics of a proton
transition and its coupling to the solvent polarization. Simulations show that a
rapid solvent polarization takes place in about 200 fs. This induces a very rapid
shift of the protonic charge that takes the complex from the ionic to covalent
forms in about 40 fs and is followed by a rapid solvent rearrangement occurring
in about 100 fs in response to this change in character of the complex. The shift in
protonic charge occurs only in a very narrow range of solvent polarization values.
The plateau value of the time-dependent rate coeﬃcient k(t) ¼ k
AB
(t) þ kBA(t), which is the sum of the forward and reverse rate constants, determines the overall chemical relaxation time, tchem ¼ k/C01 for the proton
transfer. The nonadiabatic time-dependent rate coeﬃcient k(t) is shown in
Figure 10.2. The rate constant extracted from the plateau value of this plot is
k ¼ 0.163 ps /C01. Up to six nonadiabatic transitions were required to obtain
converged results. An examination of the trajectories in the ensemble revealed
that the major nonadiabatic correction to the rate comes from two quantum
transitions: ground state - coherent state - ground state. This picture of how
nonadiabatic transitions inﬂuence the reaction rate is quite diﬀerent from that
in standard surface-hopping methods.
This result reveals a crucial aspect of QCL dynamics in determining the rate
constant, namely the coherent dynamics on the mean of two adiabatic surfaces.
Figure 10.2 Nonadiabatic rate coeﬃcient as a function of time.
Quantum-Classical Liouville Dynamics of Condensed Phase Quantum Processes 243
 01/12/2013 15:16:56.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00233
View Online

## Page 264

When a nonadiabatic transition takes the system from the ground-state surface
to the average of the ground and ﬁrst excited state surfaces, the solvent polarization tends to ﬂuctuate around its barrier-top value. This enhanced barrier
recrossing, which occurs while the system is on the mean surface, lowers the
reaction rate. The next transition, which takes the system either back to
the ground state or to the ﬁrst excited state, destroys the coherence created in
the ﬁrst transition.
The rate constant result is obtained using an approximate ( i.e. high temperature) expression for the spectral density function that describes the quantum equilibrium structure. However, in some circumstances, especially for low
temperatures, eﬀects arising from the quantum equilibrium structure can lead
to signiﬁcant modiﬁcations of the reaction rate. To deal with such cases, one
could resort to numerical schemes for computing the equilibrium structure,
similar to those based on the initial value representation
32,36,37 and linearization techniques. 38–40 Furthermore, diﬀerent formulas for the rate constant can
be derived within this framework using other choices of the reaction coordinate
and chemical species variables. This ﬂexibility in choice, along with a more
general treatment of the quantum equilibrium structure, should allow one to
eﬀectively capture quantum eﬀects in a variety of condensed phase rate processes occurring over a wide range of temperatures.
10.6 Linear and Nonlinear Vibrational Spectra
Linear and nonlinear infrared spectroscopy are powerful tools for probing the
structure and vibrational dynamics of molecular systems.
41–46 In order to take
full advantage of them, however, accurate models and methods are required for
simulating and interpreting spectra. A common approach for modeling spectra
is based on computing optical response functions (ORFs).
41 Unfortunately,
exact calculations of quantum–mechanical ORFs are not feasible for most
systems of practical interest due to the large number of DOF. Instead, mixed
quantum-classical methods can provide suitable alternatives.
41,47–49
The standard mixed quantum-classical approach to computing ORFs for
complex condensed phase systems involves molecular dynamics simulations of
the photo-inactive DOF on the adiabatic surface corresponding to the chromophore’s ground state, which is based on an ad hoc choice of the ground state
potential in the reference Hamiltonian used to arrive at a classical limit of the
quantum ORF.
41 However, other equally legitimate choices of the reference
potential give rise to diﬀerent expressions of the classical limit. In order to
circumvent such ad hoc choices and to arrive at more rigorous expressions,
several semi-classical approaches for calculating linear and nonlinear ORFs
have been proposed, including the cellular dynamics method,
41,50 the mixedstate propagation method, 51,52 linearization schemes, 53–56 and methods based
on the forward–backward initial value representation. 55,56
The standard approach has the advantage that ORFs can be eﬃciently calculated from a single equilibrium molecular dynamics trajectory on the ground
244 Chapter 10
 01/12/2013 15:16:56.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00233
View Online

## Page 265

state adiabatic surface, as opposed to many nonequilibrium surface-hopping
trajectories. This is due to the fact that the time origin can be set anywhere
along an equilibrium trajectory, making it possible to average over a large
number of trajectory segments and thereby enhancing the computational ease
and eﬃciency. In the case of weak coupling between the chromophore and its
environment, the standard approach is expected to be valid since the surface
that dictates the dynamics of the photo-inactive DOF is rather insensitive to the
quantum state of the chromophore. However, this is not true when the chromophore is strongly coupled to its environment, in which case there can be
signiﬁcant diﬀerences between the adiabatic surfaces corresponding to the
various states of the chromophore. Consequently, when the state of the chromophore switches, the photo-inactive DOF experience sudden changes in their
positions, which can in turn lead to unique spectral signatures.
In this section, we present a rigorous route based on QCL dynamics for
deriving quantum–classical expressions for linear and third-order ORFs, which
reﬂect nonequilibrium dynamics on multiple adiabatic surfaces as opposed to
equilibrium ground state dynamics.
57 As will be shown, these ORFs consist of
contributions from several Liouville pathways that diﬀer with respect to the
surfaces on which the dynamics of the photo-inactive DOF takes place between
light–matter interactions.
We begin by assuming that the quantum–mechanical system under study may
be well-approximated by a mixed quantum–classical system driven by a classical
radiation ﬁeld, in which the photo-active DOF ( i.e. chromophore) are treated
quantum mechanically and the photo-inactive DOF ( i.e. environment) are
treated classically. The time-dependent Hamiltonian for this system is given by:

10.6.1 Linear Optical Response
In order to simulate a chromophore’s linear optical response as measured in
one-dimensional IR (1D-IR) spectroscopy, we must consider its interaction
with a single impulsive pulse at time t ¼ 0 fs. Prior to the interaction with the
pulse, the chromophore is assumed to be in its ground state and the photoinactive DOF are in the corresponding thermal equilibrium state:

Equation (10.47) is diﬀerent from the standard mixed quantum–classical
expression for the linear ORF, since the dynamics takes place on the average
surface rather than on the ground-state surface. However, the diﬀerence between the two expressions becomes negligible in the limit of inhomogeneous
broadening, where the linear ORF decays to zero on timescales faster than
those associated with the motions of the photo-inactive DOF, or when the
ground and excited surfaces are similar ( e.g. in the case of weak coupling between the photo-active and photo-inactive DOF). However, in systems where
this is not the case, eqn (10.47) is expected to give rise to pronounced signatures
of the nonequilibrium dynamics on the average surface on the spectrum.
10.6.2 Third Order Optical Response
In order to simulate the third-order optical response of a chromophore as
measured by two-dimensional IR (2D-IR) spectroscopy, we must consider its
interaction with three subsequent laser pulses with wave vectors k
a, kb and kc
(yielding phases fa, fb and fc, respectively), and time delays t1 (between
pulses a and b)a n d t2 (between pulses b and c). This interaction generates a
third-order polarization in the sample, which gives rise to a signal ﬁeld that is
heterodyne detected after a time interval t3 following pulse c,i nt h e
background-free directions kr ¼/C0ka þ kb þ kc and knr ¼ ka /C0kb þ kc, corresponding to the rephasing and nonrephasing signals, respectively. Therefore, we

three impulsive square pulses at times 0, t
1 and t1 þ t2, followed by the detection
of the signal ﬁeld at time t1 þ t2 þ t3. As mentioned above, the ﬁeld-free dynamics between pulses is dictated by eqn (10.37) with ~Wab ¼ 0.

Equation (10.49) diﬀers from the standard mixed quantum–classical expressions for the rephasing and nonrephasing signals, 58,59 since the dynamics

approach.
10.6.3 1D- and 2D-IR Spectroscopy of a Solvated
Hydrogen-Bonded Complex
The QCL ORF approach is demonstrated in the case of the 1D-IR and 2D-IR
spectroscopy of the Azzouz–Borgis model (described in section 10.5) for a
moderately strong hydrogen-bonded complex dissolved in a polar solvent. This
model has been previously studied using the standard equilibrium ground state
dynamics approach.
59 Both 1D-IR and 2D-IR spectroscopy have been used
extensively to probe hydrogen-bonded systems. 46,60–73 These experiments were
accompanied by substantial theoretical and computational work, 41,58,65,74–121
but relatively little attention has been given to the signatures of nonequilibrium
processes on the spectra. Therefore, the use of this general approach can shed
light on these relatively unexplored spectral signatures, which are expected to
be manifested when chromophores are strongly coupled to their environments.
Within the context of the QCL approach, the photo-active quantum DOF
is the high-frequency proton stretch of the complex, and only the 0 2 1 and
1 2 2 vibrational transitions are considered. The corresponding transition
dipole moments are m
abðRÞ/h a; R j ^r j b; Ri, where ^r ¼ ^quAB is the position
operator of the proton stretch and uAB is the unit vector between A and B. The
remaining DOF in the environment are photo-inactive and are treated
classically.
Since the 1D-IR and 2D-IR spectra reported below directly probe the 0 2 1
and 1 2 2 transitions of the proton stretch, it is convenient to examine the
coordinate dependence of the vibrational energy levels in terms of the ground
250 Chapter 10
 01/12/2013 15:16:56.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00233
View Online

## Page 271

(00), ﬁrst-excited (11) and second-excited (22) free energy surfaces (FESs) as a
function of a collective solvent coordinate such as the solvent polarization, DE.
Figure 10.3 shows the 00, 11 and 22 FESs, as well as the averages of the 00 and
11 FESs (01) and the 11 and 22 FESs (12), as a function of DE. The fact that the
FESs are very diﬀerent suggests that the corresponding adiabatic surfaces are
also very diﬀerent, and that the coupling between the proton stretch and its
environment is strong.
The marked diﬀerences between the various FESs suggest that any transition
between adiabatic surfaces will be accompanied by pronounced solvent rearrangements. Therefore, one expects signiﬁcant signatures of nonequilibrium
dynamics on the spectroscopy of the proton stretch, thereby making this a
suitable model for investigating these eﬀects. This is demonstrated in
Figure 10.4, which shows the averaged fundamental transition frequency, o

along the 00 - 01- 00 - 01 and 00 - 01- 11 - 01 Liouville pathways. As
can be seen, these two spectral trajectories are very diﬀerent from one another,
one drifting upwards and the other downwards, rather than ﬂuctuating about a
ﬁxed equilibrium value. In addition, the oscillations (caused by the coupling to
the low frequency intramolecular A–B stretch) are diﬀerent, suggesting that the
vibrational motion of the A–B stretching mode depends strongly on the
chromophore’s quantum state.
The 1D-IR spectra of the proton stretch calculated using the standard and
QCL approaches are shown in Figure 10.5. Both spectra contain three major
bands centered at B200 cm
/C01, B2150–2250 cm/C01 and B2500 cm/C01, which can
be assigned to the transition state, covalent and ionic conﬁgurations, respectively.
59 However, the ionic and covalent bands in the QCL spectrum are
red-shifted and broader, and the transition state band is more intense in
comparison with the standard spectrum. These diﬀerences are due to solvation
during t1 on the mean (01) surface, which tends to destabilize the ionic and
covalent bath conﬁgurations and stabilize the transition state conﬁgurations,
thereby red-shifting and broadening the ionic and covalent bands, while
sharpening and intensifying the transition state band.
The 2D-IR spectra of the proton stretch for three diﬀerent values of t
2 calculated using the standard (top panels) and QCL (bottom panels) approaches
are shown in Figure 10.6. The diﬀerences between the spectra calculated via the
two approaches are due to signatures of nonequilibrium processes taken into
account in the QCL approach but not in the standard approach. At t
2 ¼ 0 fs,
both approaches yield spectra which contain three positive diagonal peaks that
correspond to the ionic, covalent and transition state bands in the 1D-IR
spectrum (see Figure 10.5), and negative oﬀ-diagonal peaks which arise from
the 00 - 01- 11 - 12 Liouville pathway (see eqn (10.49)). The spectra at
t
2 ¼ 0 fs are relatively similar since the extent of solvation on the average 01 and

12 surfaces during the coherence periods t1 and t3 is limited by the short
dephasing time ( B150 fs). Nonetheless, nonequilibrium dynamics on the
average 01 surface intensiﬁes the diagonal transition-state peak at the expense
of the ionic and covalent peaks. The ionic and covalent peaks also broaden and
red-shift since the 01 surface drives the system away from the ionic and covalent
conﬁgurations and toward the transition-state conﬁgurations. The negative
feature in the lower right corner of the spectrum also broadens, red-shifting
along the o
1 axis and blue-shifting along the o3 axis. This is due to the fact that
o10 decreases during solvation on the 01 surface during t1, while o21 increases
during solvation on the 12 surface during t3 (see Figure 10.3). Finally, the
negative peak at ( o1, o3,) B (200 cm /C01, 1500 cm /C01), which only appears in
the QCL spectrum, arises since solvation on the 01 surface during t1 drives the
system toward transition state conﬁgurations, leading to absorption at the
overtone frequency of 1500 cm /C01 during t3.
The diﬀerences between the two sets of spectra become more pronounced
with increasing t2.A t t1, the second pulse either returns the system to the 00
surface or puts it on the 11 surface. If the system is returned to the ground state,
the ensuing nonequilibrium dynamics during t
2 will drive the system away from
transition-state conﬁgurations toward ionic and covalent conﬁgurations.
However, if the system is promoted to the ﬁrst-excited state, the ensuing
nonequilibrium dynamics during t
2 drives the system away from the ionic and
covalent conﬁgurations and toward the transition-state conﬁgurations. Since
the intermediate spectral range between the ionic/covalent and transition-state
bands is signiﬁcantly less photo-active (see Figure 10.5), solvation dampens the
signal at intermediate times (see t
2 ¼ 125 fs in Figure 10.6).

and 250 fs.
