## Page 274

A revival of the signal occurs when the system reaches either the ionic/
covalent regions in the case of ground state solvation, or the transition-state
region in the case of ﬁrst-excited state solvation. At this point, the QCL spectrum
(see t
2 ¼ 250 fs in Figure 10.6) looks quite diﬀerent from the standard one, as it
starts taking on its post-solvation shape which consists of three spectral features:
(1) a positive diagonal peak centered around o1 ¼ o3 ¼ 2500 cm /C01,w h i c hi s
similar to the positive diagonal peak in the standard spectrum at t2 ¼ 250 fs; (2)
an elongated positive spectral feature parallel to the o1 axis and centered around
o3 ¼ 200 cm/C01; and (3) an elongated negative spectral feature parallel to the o1
axis and centered around o3 ¼ 1500 cm/C01. Excited state solvation ends with the
system in a region where nonradiative nonadiabatic transitions are likely to
occur. The eﬀects of these transitions on the spectra are not incorporated into
this formalism, but have been taken into account in ref. 122.
10.7 Concluding Remarks and Perspectives
Reaction rates and optical spectra associated with speciﬁc quantum DOF in
condensed phase environments are diﬃcult to compute due to the many-body
nature of these systems. One practical solution to this problem is to take a
mixed quantum–classical approach, in which the environment of the quantum
DOF of interest is treated classically. Interactions between the quantum subsystem and the classical environment or a radiation ﬁeld can induce nonadiabatic transitions between the adiabatic states of the subsystem, leading to a
breakdown of the adiabatic approximation.
In this chapter, we discussed the nonadiabatic dynamics prescribed by the
solution of the QCLE. The nature of the resulting trajectories is quite diﬀerent
from that in standard surface-hopping schemes,
2,123,124 where transitions occur
between adiabatic states and classical evolution takes place only on single
adiabatic surfaces. In the QCL approach, trajectories are generated by a
classical-like evolution, either on single adiabatic surfaces or on the mean of
two coherently coupled surfaces. Moreover, during the coherent evolution
segments the observable of interest acquires a phase, which leads to the creation
or destruction of coherence when the observable is averaged over an ensemble
of trajectories. In addition, the QCL propagator ensures that energy is exactly
conserved along a trajectory even if the momentum-jump approximation is
invoked.
Starting from its quantum mechanical origins, we showed how one can arrive
at a computationally tractable expression for the rate constant of a condensed
phase quantum process. The calculation of this reactive-ﬂux correlation function expression involves QCL dynamics of the species variables with the initial
conditions sampled from the quantum equilibrium distribution. This approach
was demonstrated for the calculation of the rate constant of a proton transfer
reaction in a hydrogen-bonded complex dissolved in a polar solvent.
The QCL approach has also been used to rigorously derive mixed quantum–
classical expressions for linear and third-order ORFs, which reﬂect the nonequilibrium dynamics of the photo-inactive DOF on multiple adiabatic surfaces
254 Chapter 10
 01/12/2013 15:16:56.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00233
View Online

## Page 275

due to interactions with a sequence of laser pulses. This QCL ORF approach
should be contrasted with the standard approach, which is based on calculating
ORFs that reﬂect equilibrium dynamics of the photo-inactive DOF on the
ground-state surface. The nonequilibrium nature of the underlying dynamics is
expected to have a pronounced eﬀect on the spectra of chromophores that are
strongly coupled to their environments. In general, this approach will be useful
for simulating the optical response of many-body systems, such as liquids and
biomolecules, for which the excited state adiabatic surfaces are signiﬁcantly
diﬀerent from the ground state surface.
The studies of intermolecular proton transfer in a polar solvent constitute the
ﬁrst applications of QCL dynamics to the calculation of transport properties
and optical spectra in a realistic, large-scale system. The QCL approach for
computing rate constants may be widely applied to chemical and biological
processes involving proton transfer, electron transfer and proton-coupled
electron transfer, since a quantum–mechanical description of the transferring
protons and/or electrons is absolutely required. To date, the QCL ORF approach has been used to treat linear and third-order vibrational spectroscopies
such as 1D-IR and 2D-IR, but it can also be used to treat second-order spectroscopies such as sum frequency generation and other third-order spectroscopies such as three-pulse photon echo and pump-probe. In addition, the QCL
ORF approach may be generalized to take into account vibronic transitions in
order to simulate electronic spectra. In any case, methodological improvements
and the development of new algorithms for executing QCL dynamics remain
worthwhile endeavors due to the need for understanding increasingly complex
systems.

## Page 280

CHAPTER 11
Free Energetics and Kinetics
of Charge Transfer and Shift
Reactions in Room-Temperature
Ionic Liquids
YOUNGSEON SHIM a AND HYUNG J. KIM* b
a Samsung Advanced Institute of Technology, SEC, Yongin 446-712, Korea;
b Department of Chemistry, Carnegie Mellon University, Pittsburgh,
PA 15213, USA and School of Computational Sciences,
Korea Institute for Advanced Study, Korea
*Email: hjkim@cmu.edu
11.1 Introduction
It is well-known that solvation plays a critical role in the free energetics and
dynamics of chemical processes involving charge shift and transfer, such as S
N1
and S N2 reactions and electron and proton transfers. 1,2 Because of strong
interactions of the reaction system ( i.e. solute) with surrounding solvent molecules, the structure and dynamics of the latter exert a strong inﬂuence on and
thus signiﬁcantly alter the reaction thermodynamics and kinetics in solution
compared with the gas-phase. There have been extensive theoretical and
computational eﬀorts to understand solvation eﬀects at the molecular level in
conventional dipolar solvents, such as water and alcohols.
2,3 It is well established that coupling between the charge shift/transfer of the solute and the
RSC Theoretical and Computational Chemistry Series No. 6
Reaction Rate Constant Computations: Theories and Applications
Edited by Keli Han and Tianshu Chu
r The Royal Society of Chemistry 2014
Published by the Royal Society of Chemistry, www.rsc.org
260
 17/11/2013 09:29:09.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00260

## Page 281

rearrangement of solvent dipole moments is a key factor that governs reaction
free energetics and dynamics.
Another important class of solvents that can modulate chemical processes
strongly through solvation is room-temperature ionic liquids (RTILs). These are
purely ionic systems, consisting of bulky organic cations paired with various
organic or inorganic anions. Because of their unique properties, including nonvolatility, non-ﬂammability, low melting points, and good thermal and chemical
stability, they have a wide range of applications in science and technology
4,5 such
as chemical synthesis,6 catalysis,7–10 separation11–13 as well as energy storage and
conversion.14,15 Many solvatochromic measurements 16–25 and molecular dynamics (MD) simulations 26–28 indicate that the eﬀective polarity of RTILs,
measuring their capability of solvating dipolar solutes, is comparable with that of
highly polar solvents ( e.g. acetonitrile and methanol) even though their bulk
dielectric constant
29,30 is considerably lower than the polar solvents. This is due
to ion monopolar charges that contribute little to the dielectric constant but yield
strong electrostatic interactions with the solute charge distribution. Therefore,
RTIL ions are expected to have pronounced eﬀects on charge shift and transfer
processes. Considerable experimental attention has been paid to understanding
how solvation in RTILs inﬂuences chemical reactions.
31–46 The kinetics of many
bimolecular reactions were found to be considerably faster than the diﬀusion
limit.
32–38,40–42 In addition, the reaction rate of unimolecular systems is often
higher than the predictions based on RTIL viscosity. 45,46 The latter feature was
attributed to the rapid crossing of the reaction barrier in RTILs. 47,48
In this chapter, we give an account of our recent MD and theoretical analysis of
electron transfer48 (ET) and SN1 ionization49 reactions in RTILs. Speciﬁcally, we
consider the unimolecular ET of a model diatomic reaction complex in 1-butyl-3methyldicyanamide (BMI
1DCA/C0 ) and ionization of 2-chloro-2-methylpropane
in 1-ethyl-3-methylimidazolium hexaﬂuorophosphateðEMIþPE/C0
6 Þ. The inﬂuence
of the RTIL environment on free energetics and dynamics of these reactions is
described with attention paid to its similarities to and diﬀerences from the conventional polar solvents. The MD results for barrier crossing dynamics on reaction kinetics are analyzed via the Grote–Hynes (GH) theory
50 and compared
with the transition state theory (TST) 51 and Kramers theory52 predictions.
11.2 Formulation of Unimolecular ET Reactions
Here we give a brief review of the theoretical formulation of unimolecular ET in
solution employed in this chapter.
11.2.1 ET Free Energetics
We employ a two-state electronic description widely used for ET, 53 based on
the vacuum electronic Hamiltonian:

11.3 Electron–Exchange Reaction
In this section, we consider electron exchange reaction:
D/C0 0:5 /C1/C1/C1Aþ0:5 Ð Dþ0:5 /C1/C1/C1A/C0 0:5; ð11:15Þ
in the ionic liquid BMI 1DCA/C0 and in the aprotic polar solvent acetonitrile
using a model diatomic reaction complex. 48 Kinetics for charge separation and
recombination:
D /C1/C1/C1A Ð Dþ /C1/C1/C1A/C0 ; ð11:16Þ
analyzed in ref. 47 will not be considered here. The interested reader is
referred to the original article.
11.3.1 Simulation Models and Methods
The simulation cell comprises a single diatomic ET complex immersed either in
BMI
1DCA/C0 consisting of 156 pairs of cations and anions or in CH 3CN
composed of 216 acetonitrile molecules. Flexible all-atom descriptions were
used for both BMI 1DCA/C0 (ref. 63) and acetonitrile (ref. 64). The donor (D)
and acceptor (A) atoms of the ET complex, each of mass m ¼ 100 amu, are
separated by 6.0 A˚ and interact with the solvent through Lennard-Jones (LJ)
and Coulomb potentials. Two diﬀerent sets of the LJ parameters, s ¼ 4A˚ and
8A˚ , both with e/kB ¼ 100 K, were considered for D and A. For solvation dynamics, we considered two diﬀerent solute charge distributions, a neutral pair
(NP) with the diatom charge q ¼ 0 and a charge-separated pair with q ¼/C6 0.5e
(HIP), where e is the elementary charge. In the calculation of diabatic free
264 Chapter 11
 17/11/2013 09:29:09.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00260
View Online

## Page 285

energy curves, only HIP relevant to R and P states was employed and the free
energy perturbation method was used. 59 In barrier crossing simulations, the
two-state solute description in eqn (11.2), hereafter referred to as ‘2P’, was
employed to account for rapid variations of the solute charge distribution in the
barrier region. For convenience, we will denote diﬀerent solute descriptions by
their charge distribution and s value hereafter. For example, NP8 and 2P4 refer
to the neutral pair ( q ¼ 0) with s ¼ 8A˚ and the two-state solute with s ¼ 4A˚ ,
respectively.
The simulations were conducted in the canonical ensemble at T ¼ 300 K
using the DL
POLY program. 65 The trajectories were integrated via the Verlet
leapfrog algorithm using a time step of 1 fs. For HIP and NP solutes
in BMI
1DCA/C0 , equilibrium simulations were carried out with 4 ns equilibration, followed by a 10 ns trajectory from which averages were computed.
For CH 3CN, the corresponding production runs were 5 ns long after 1 ns
equilibration.
In barrier crossing simulations, 800 distinct conﬁgurations with DEE0,
separated at least by 10 ps, were ﬁrst collected from the 10 ns equilibrium
trajectory of NP in BMI
1DCA/C0 . For each of these RTIL conﬁgurations, we
performed MD for additional 100 fs in the presence of the 2P solute by imposing the constraints DE ¼ 0 and DE˙¼ 0.
66,67 The 800 conﬁgurations thus
constructed were then used as initial conﬁgurations to simulate barrier crossing
dynamics. Speciﬁcally, at t ¼ 0, the constraints were lifted and solvent velocities
were reassigned according to the Maxwell–Boltzmann distribution. The subsequent dynamics were monitored via MD for 2 ps both forward and backward
in time. For acetonitrile, 600 distinct initial conﬁgurations were used. The
transmission coeﬃcient for barrier crossing was calculated via:

The MD results for diabatic free energies for symmetric ET, eqn (11.15) with
DF
rxn ¼ 0, are presented in Figure 11.1 and Table 11.1. The solvent reorganization free energy l and nonadiabatic barrier height DFz
NA in BMI1DCA/C0 are
comparable to and often larger than those in acetonitrile. This indicates that
BMI1DCA/C0 is eﬀectively more polar than acetonitrile, in concert with many
solvatochromic studies. 16–28 We notice that the Marcus theory prediction 68 for
the nonadiabatic barrier height, DFz
Marcusð¼ l=4Þ, for HIP4 is generally in good
accord with the MD result DFz
NA in BMI 1DCA/C0 . Similar observations were
made in recent MD studies in other RTILs. 47,69–71 However, the larger HIP8

11.3.3 Barrier Crossing
Turning to barrier crossing in adiabatic ET, we ﬁrst consider a few sample
trajectories in order to gain insight into its dynamic characteristics. The three
trajectories in BMI
1DCA/C0 displayed in Figure 11.2 are characterized by
crossing of the transition state located at DE ¼ 0 from the R to P side at t ¼ 0.
Thus they would be all ‘reactive’ in the TST description. Full propagation of
dynamics shows that the trajectories in Figure 11.2(a) and (b) are indeed reactive, i.e. their process starts from the R state and ends in P. The former is a

BMI1DCA/C0 is not signiﬁcant. We attribute this intriguing ﬁnding to fast ET
dynamics in the barrier region we noted above. Since barrier crossing occurs
very rapidly, it is governed not by the long-time ‘hydrodynamic’ friction related
to viscosity, but by the short-time friction evaluated at the reactive frequency.
Also noteworthy is that k
ðbÞ
MD decreases with the increasing magnitude of electronic coupling Vel, consonant with an earlier MD work in a model polar
solvent.67 Since the barrier becomes broader with growing Vel, barrier
recrossings before the ET system falls down the P well become easier and thus
occur more frequently.
For additional insight, we consider the GH and Kramers theory predictions
for the transmission coeﬃcient, using the solvent friction ~z

The results for activation and deactivation kinetics for symmetric ET are
summarized in Table 11.2. k(w) was calculated using eqn (11.10) with kact,i
obtained from eqn (11.13) with Ci(t) in Figure 11.3(b). The eﬀective time scale

tw for activation/deactivation was analyzed by employing the well-known expression for activation rate: 61,62,73–76
kact ¼ 1

For a Debye solvent, tw is given by the solvation time ts, deﬁned as the integrated area of Ci(t). We notice that tw is much shorter than ts in BMI1DCA/C0 ,
whereas they are nearly the same in acetonitrile. Also tw increases signiﬁcantly
as the ET barrier height decreases in the former, while it varies little in the
latter. We understand these results as follows.
As shown in Figure 11.3(b), solvation dynamics in acetonitrile are extremely
fast; their relaxation is completed in less than 2 ps in the presence of HIP4.
Therefore the entire relaxation of C
i(t) contributes to activation and deactivation and hence twEts in acetonitrile. In contrast, solvation dynamics in
BMI1DCA/C0 have a distinct biphasic character, viz. ultrafast initial relaxation
in the ﬁrst B200 fs, followed by an extremely slow decay with characteristic
time longer than B100 ps. According to many prior MD studies 26,28,77–85 and
spectroscopic measurements, 22–24,86–96 solvation dynamics in RTILs are characterized by multiple relaxation times, spanning a broad range from subpicosecond to approximately nanosecond. For rapid activation and
deactivation, primarily the ultrafast component of C
i(t) contributes to tw,s o
that tw /C28 ts. In other words, it is the characteristic time of short-time solvation
dynamics that is largely responsible for tw in the case of fast activation/deactivation. This also indicates that as activation/deactivation becomes slower,
the long-time component of solvation dynamics will begin to make a contribution and, as a consequence, t
w in BMI 1DCA/C0 will become longer.

## Page 290

To pursue the last point further, we have analyzed variations of tw associated
with kact,R and k(w) in eqn (11.10) as a function of the barrier height by varying
the free energy of reaction DFrxn. Before we discuss our results, we ﬁrst note
from eqn (11.18) that as DFz decreases, the eﬀective time scale for activation/
deactivation dynamics in the R well, measured as e /C0 bDFz
k/C0 1
act (i.e. the inverse of
the deactivation rate), increases even for Debye solvents with constant tw.W e
can understand this trend as follows. As DFz decreases, so does the deterministic force on the ET system near the transition state, given by the slope of the
diabatic R curve there. This decrease in the force slows the deactivation process, which involves a reduction of the energy of the excited ET system by a
couple of k
BT from that of the transition state. This in turn increases the time
window for non-deterministic forces to exert an inﬂuence on the ET system and
as a result, further decelerates the deactivation.
With this in mind, we consider the results for t
w and k(w) in BMI1DCA/C0 and
in CH 3CN in Figure 11.4. We make a cautionary remark at the outset that
because of the approximate nature of eqn (11.13), viz. the neglect of boundary
conditions,61 these results may not be reliable for low barriers. Despite this
caveat, we notice that tw in acetonitrile varies little with DFz and the corresponding k(w) shows a weak DFz dependence. In contrast, BMI 1DCA/C0 shows a
marked increase in tw with decreasing DFz for bDFzr10. This tw trend in RTIL
is attributed to the growing contribution from the slow component of solvation
dynamics as the ET barrier decreases and the well relaxation via deterministic
forces decelerates. As a result, k
(w) in BMI 1DCA/C0 decreases much more rapidly with diminishing DFz than in acetonitrile. This indicates that activation
dynamics in the R well would play an important role in ET with relatively low

11.3.5 Overall ET Kinetics
Results for overall reaction rate kET (eqn (11.8)) and related transmission coeﬃcient kET for symmetric ET are presented in Table 11.3. In their calculations
we employed kðbÞ
MD determined directly from MD in the presence of the 2P
solutes and k(w) obtained via eqn (11.13) with the aid of the simulation results
for HIP solvation dynamics. Hence the notation 2P/HIP in Table 11.3. For
comparison, kKR
ET determined with kðbÞ
KR instead of kðbÞ
MD is also presented. As
expected from the analysis above, kET and kKR
ET are in good agreement in
acetonitrile. However, kKR
ET underestimates the overall ET rate by 1–2 orders of
magnitude in BMI 1DCA/C0 due to the failure of Kramers theory in describing
barrier crossing in RTILs. We now consider the two diﬀerent solute models in
more detail.
For the 2P8/HIP8 model with s ¼ 8A˚ , DFz and kTST are essentially the same
in BMI 1DCA/C0 and acetonitrile. Thus the diﬀerence in their kET arises almost
exclusively from dynamics. In the case of acetonitrile, both barrier crossing
(kðbÞ
MD ¼ 0:44) and well relaxation ( k(w) ¼ 0.47) dynamics make a similar contribution to kET. By contrast, k(w)(¼ 0.035) in BMI 1DCA/C0 is smaller than
kðbÞ
MDð¼ 0:25Þ by almost a factor of 10. Therefore, the activation and deactivation in the R and P wells are rate-limiting in ET for 2P8/HIP8 in
BMI1DCA/C0 . It is also activation and deactivation dynamics that is mainly
responsible for the diﬀerence in kET between BMI 1DCA/C0 and acetonitrile.
Even though their barrier heights are the same, kET in the latter is about eight
times higher than that in the former. It is interesting that a kET diﬀerence of this
magnitude between acetonitrile and imidazolium-based RTIL was observed in
a recent experimental study.
39
In the case of the smaller 2P4/HIP4 solute, BMI 1DCA/C0 and acetonitrile are
characterized by similar values of the transmission coeﬃcient for both barrier

## Page 292

crossing and well relaxation. Thus their diﬀerence in kET by two decades arises
almost entirely from the 2.5 kcal mol /C0 1 diﬀerence in the ET barrier height. We
also notice that kðbÞ
MD is 3–4 times smaller than k(w) in both BMI 1DCA/C0 and
acetonitrile. This indicates that activation and deactivation dynamics do not
play a signiﬁcant role and ET kinetics are mainly controlled by barrier crossing.
Nevertheless, since kðbÞ
MD is not that small compared to unity, the TST captures
overall ET kinetics within a factor 3–5.
Finally, we note that roles played by activation/deactivation in ET kinetics of
2P4/HIP4 in BMI 1DCA/C0 would vary substantially with DFz. In the thermoneutral case, ET is mainly governed by barrier crossing dynamics. Due to rapid
barrier crossing, however, the resulting departure from the TST rate constant is
moderate as discussed above. As DF
rxn decreases with respect to the thermoneutral case, relaxation dynamics in the reactant well would play an increasingly more important role and the deviation from the TST rate would increase
accordingly. For bDF
zr7, ET for 2P4/HIP4 in BMI 1DCA/C0 would become
activation-controlled and TST would fail to describe its reaction rate. Analogously, for endothermic ET, its kinetics would be controlled mainly by deactivation in the product state if the barrier height for the reverse reaction
isr7k
BT. This suggests that rate-limiting factors of adiabatic ET in RTILs
would vary from barrier crossing when the activation barriers for the forward
and backward reactions are both high, to activation/deactivation when one or
both of the barriers are low. Since t
w depends on the timescale of solvation
dynamics, the actual onset of the activation/deactivation-controlled ET regime
will vary with RTILs. We expect that as ts becomes shorter, i.e. solvation dynamics become faster, the value of bDFz at which activation becomes ratedetermining would become smaller.
11.4 S N1 Ionization Reaction
In this section, we consider the S N1 ionization reaction 97 RX-R1 þ X/C0 of
t-butyl chloride ( t-BuCl), also referred to as 2-chloro-2-methylpropane, in
EMIþPF/C0
6 as a prototype of a charge shift process in an RTIL. For comparison, two conventional dipolar solvents, acetonitrile and water, are also
considered.
11.4.1 Models and Methods
Analogous to ET considered above, we employ a two-state description 98–102
based on a covalent and an ionic valence-bond (VB) state:

central carbon atom and three methyl groups in a planar geometry with C3

6 and at 298 K for acetonitrile and

carried out with 2 ns equilibration, followed by a 2 ns trajectory from
which averages were computed. For EMI þPF/C0
6 , we computed averages
using two diﬀerent 1 ns production runs, each obtained with 2 ns equilibration.

## Page 294

The adiabatic free energy curve for the t-BuCl ionization was calculated as a
function of r using the thermodynamic integration method. 111
11.4.2 Ionization Free Energy and Pathway
The adiabatic free energy A(r) for S N1 ionization in EMI þPF/C0
6 is compared
with the ground-state electronic curve in the gas phase in Figure 11.5(a). Both
curves exhibit a global minimum at r ¼ 1.8 A˚ , which corresponds to the reactant
state of t-BuCl. We also notice a dramatic stabilization of t-BuCl in EMI þPF/C0
6
with respect to the gas phase for r43A˚ . The dissociation free energy DArxn
decreases from B85 kcal mol/C0 1 in the gas phase to B40 kcal mol/C0 1 in
EMIþPF/C0
6 . This stabilization arises from strong electrostatic interactions that
develop between the RTIL and t-BuCl as the latter dissociates. The evolution of
partial charges of t-BuCl with r in Figure 11.5(b) shows that it becomes nearly
completely ionic, viz. t-Bu1 /C1/C1/C1Cl/C0 , for rZ2.5 A˚ in EMI þPF/C0
6 . We also notice
that the reactant state has a small dipole moment, i.e. it is not purely covalent
because of the mixing of the covalent and ionic VB states via electronic
coupling.
Returning to Figure 11.5(a), A(r)i nE M I þPF/C0
6 has a second minimum
around r ¼ 10 A˚ , which is about 3.5 kcal mol /C0 1 more stable than two local
maxima located at rE6.5 and 14 A˚ . We identify this minimum as the ‘solventseparated ion pair’ (SSIP) state, originally introduced by Winstein and coworkers to explain salt eﬀects on solvolysis.
112 A detailed analysis of solvent
ions in the region between the t-Bu and Cl moieties indicates that, on average,
one pair of EMI 1 and PF/C0
6 ions separate the reaction couple at SSIP. The free

energy results suggest that the t-BuCl dissociation in EMI þPF/C0
6 proceeds in a
stepwise manner, RX -SSIP-R1 þ X/C0 , i.e. formation of a solvent-separated
ion pair, followed by dissociation to ions. Nonetheless, the large free energy of
reaction ( B40 kcal mol /C0 1) and barrier height ( B44 kcal mol /C0 1)i nE M IþPF/C0
6
seem to lend support to the experimental interpretation 31 that RTILs are
ionizing but not dissociating solvents.
In Figure 11.6, the free energy proﬁles for t-BuCl ionization in acetonitrile
and in water are compared with those in EMI þPF/C0
6 . Water exhibits a diﬀerent
behavior from two other solvents. Its A(r) is characterized by a second
local minimum around r ¼ 2.9 A˚ , which we identify as a contact ion pair (CIP)
state.112 The transition state for the formation of CIP from RX is located at
rE2.25 A˚ and its free energy is 21.5 kcal mol /C0 1 higher than the RX state.
A further dissociation of CIP is an activated process with a barrier of height
B5 kcal mol
/C0 1 located at rE5A˚ . These results suggest that the dissociation
mechanism of t-BuCl is RX -CIP-R1 þ X/C0 in water.
Turning to acetonitrile, we notice that its A(r) is characterized by a broad
second local minimum around r ¼ 9A˚ as well as a global minimum at r ¼ 1.8 A˚ ,
analogous to the EMI þPF/C0
6 case. However, the barrier between the second
minimum and dissociated ions ( i.e. r ¼ N) is only B0.5 kcal mol /C0 1 high. Since
this value is less than the thermal energy at 298 K, we do not think the local
minimum at rE9A˚ will be a reaction intermediate. Therefore the S
N1i o n -
ization pathway in acetonitrile would be RX -R1 þ X/C0 . The overall activation

free energy is about Z4 kcal mol/C0 1 higher in acetonitrile at 298 K than in

with decreasing T,20 the activation free energy diﬀerence between the two
solvents would grow as we lower the temperature of the latter. Thus the TST
rate for the S
N1 ionization of t-BuCl at room temperature would be considerably higher in EMI þPF/C0
6 than in acetonitrile.
11.4.3 Barrier Crossing Dynamics
We proceed to barrier crossing and its inﬂuence on S N1 ionization kinetics.
Since it is not easy to obtain via MD the random force along the solute r coordinate in solution, we instead employ a deterministic force:

and CH 3CN, we found obE6p s /C0 1. For both solvents, the kGH values are

## Page 297

for the former also means that analogous to the ET case, barrier crossing
dynamics do not play a major role in the ionization of t-BuCl in
EMIþPF/C0
6 even though its viscosity is very high. Since the characteristic time
associated with barrier crossing is short due to the relatively high barrier frequency, the long-time component of friction contributes little to barrier
crossing dynamics.
11.5 Conclusions
In this chapter we present an account of our recent work on unimolecular ET of
a model diatomic complex and S
N1 ionization of t-BuCl in RTILs. Despite
their relatively low dielectric constant, RTILs have a pronounced eﬀect on
reaction free energetics and thermodynamics of these processes because of
strong electrostatic coupling of their ions with the solute charge transfer and
shift. This ﬁnding is in concert with large solvatochromic shifts and thus high
empirical solvent polarity of RTILs observed in many experimental and
simulation studies.
For both ET and S
N1 ionization, barrier crossing dynamics in RTILs and
acetonitrile yield a similar kinetics eﬀect despite a huge diﬀerence in their viscosity. This interesting result is attributed to rapid crossing dynamics caused by
a high barrier frequency. The total friction (and thus total viscosity) is not a
relevant quantity for barrier crossing kinetics since the timescale for barrier
crossing is considerably shorter than the time needed to reach the hydrodynamic regime for friction. This also provides a theoretical explanation for
rapid isomerizations
45,46 observed recently in RTILs.
The roles played by RTILs in ET activation/deactivation can vary rather
signiﬁcantly with the barrier height, which sets the timescale relevant for deactivation via deterministic dynamics ( i.e. inertial relaxation). Speciﬁcally, as
the barrier height for the forward (backward) ET reaction decreases, the
timescale for inertial relaxation in the reactant (product) well increases. This in
turn allows the contribution of the slow component of solvation dynamics and
thus further decelerates activation/deactivation kinetics. RTIL solvation dynamics typically show a non-exponential decay with multiple relaxation times
ranging from subpicosecond to nanosecond and longer. We thus expect that the

CHAPTER 12
Semi-Classical Treatments of
Electron Transfer Rate from

YI ZHAO
State Key Laboratory for Physical Chemistry of Solid Surfaces,

12.1 Introduction
The electron transfer (ET) rate is a fundamental parameter with which to
characterize ET processes in physical, chemical, material and biological sciences.
It is aﬀected by a number of quantum phenomena, such as nuclear tunneling,
curve crossing, quantum interference and the coupling to the environment. It is
thus a challenge to accurately evaluate the ET rate since one has to simultaneously incorporate the quantum eﬀects and dissipation. In spite of the
diﬃculty, ET in certain limiting cases is well understood with extensive studies. In
general, ET is classiﬁed into two types of reactions, nonadiabatic and adiabatic,
in terms of the weak and strong electronic coupling strengths between the donor
and acceptor states. As the environmental ﬂuctuation is assumed to be faster
RSC Theoretical and Computational Chemistry Series No. 6
Reaction Rate Constant Computations: Theories and Applications
Edited by Keli Han and Tianshu Chu
r The Royal Society of Chemistry 2014
Published by the Royal Society of Chemistry, www.rsc.org
283
 01/12/2013 15:20:23.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00283

## Page 304

than the ET dynamics, the thermal equilibrium distribution may be maintained
in the donor state during the ET process. In this case, the rate theories for the two
types of ET have been established. In the weak electronic coupling regime, for
instance, the reactions can be treated by the Fermi Golden rule (FGR)
1,2 or the
well-known semi-classical Marcus formula. 3,4 In the strong electronic coupling
limit, however, transition state theory (TST) 4,5 is applicable. To cover the weak
and strong coupling limits, Marcus and Hush 4,6 used the thermally averaged
Landau–Zener (LZ) transmission probability 7–9 to perform the Marcus–Hush
formula. The nuclear tunneling eﬀect is heuristically incorporated independently
from the electronic transition.
Although those approaches have been successfully applied to explain the
ET in many systems, their limitations are also obvious especially in the intermediate coupling regime. For instance, the Marcus–Hush formula becomes
invalid to explain ET at low temperatures because the electronic transition and
nuclear tunneling are coupled together and the simple multiplication form
cannot correctly represent the eﬀects;
10,11 in addition, the tunneling corrections
are frequently used from a parabolic approximation 12,13 which is divergent
at low temperatures. Furthermore, the LZ formula does not work at all at
energies close to and lower than the potential crossing.
As solvent dynamics begin to play a role in ET, the above approaches
starting from the thermal equilibrium distribution cannot be used. Zusman
14 and
Burshtein and co-workers15 were the ﬁrst to independently investigate the ET in
this case. Since then, a large number of theoretical approaches have been proposed
to consider the competition between solv ent relaxation and electronic transition
(see, for instance, ref. 16 and references therein). In Zusman’s original work, ET is
considered as a one-dimensional reaction along the solvent coordinate. When the
intramolecular vibrational modes are in corporated in ET, one has to deal with
multi-dimensional diﬀusive equations because the motions of these modes may be
also damped by the surroundings. Under the assumption that the ﬂuctuations of
the intramolecular vibrational modes are much faster than that of the solvent
mode, the multi-dimensional equations could be reduced to a one-dimensional
equation with a sink function, as described by the Sumi–Marcus (SM) theory.
17 In
t h eS Mt h e o r y ,17 the sink function is dealt with the classical limit of the local
FGR. In order to incorporate the quantum eﬀect, Bixon and Jortner (BJ) 18
proposed an alternative approach to treat the high-frequency vibrational modes.
Barbara and co-workers
19 subsequently combined the ideas of SM and BJ. This
hybrid model is a minimal model which envisages ET to occur on a threedimensional surface with the coordinates of a solvent polarization, a low frequency classical vibrational mode and a high frequency quantum vibrational one.
There are alternative studies for ET 20–36 based on the real-time dynamic
approaches. These approaches are very important for dynamic investigation
such as electronic population relaxation and its transient non-equilibrium
properties. However, the numerical convergence for the rate calculation is still a
dilemma
37 because of the multi-dimensional problem in ET.
In this chapter, we present an overview of our work on the ET rate. We start
from the assumption that the donor population has a thermal equilibrium
284 Chapter 12
 01/12/2013 15:20:23.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00283
View Online

## Page 305

distribution and propose formulae for the ET rate 34,38–42 to overcome the
deﬁciency of available approaches. Then, we extend the approaches to ET in
the solvent,
35,43–47 where we have proposed two kinds of approaches: quantum
Kramer-like theory and an extended SM approach. The former is suitable for
low to intermediate solvent friction limits while the latter is useful for the case
of very strong friction. In both cases, our approaches cover weak to strong
electronic couplings. They are simple yet accurate enough to be directly
applicable to explain experimental data, e.g. the localized-to-delocalized ET in
mixed-valence molecules.
48,49
To deal with ET in organic semiconductors, one has to incorporate the coherent motion of electron in the multi-states. The single two-state rate model
developed for the donor–acceptor system may not be used straightforwardly.
Here, we display a time-dependent wavepacket diﬀusion (TDWPD) approach
50
for the charge carrier dynamics. In the approach, the nuclear vibrational motions
are dealt with the semi-classical ﬂuctuations on the electronic energies of molecules. In this way, we can apply the approach to the nanoscale organic crystals.
The content of the chapter is arranged as follows. Section 12.2 describes the
semi-classical approximation of thermal rate constants for ET in a fast solvent
relaxation limit. Section 12.3 discusses solvent-controlled ET theories. Section
12.4 presents the TDWPD approach and section 12.5 shows several applications. Concluding remarks are given in section 12.6.
12.2 Nonadiabatic Transition State Theory for Electron
Transfer
When the solvent dynamics are faster than those of ET, the population on the
donor state has enough time to maintain the thermal distribution during ET.
Thus, TST can be used to calculate the ET rate. This rate can be also used as the
sink function
19,51,52 to describe the fast vibrational modes in the treatment of
solvent-controlled ET reactions.
To deal with the ET rate in such a case, our strategy is to combine the
generalized nonadiabatic transition state theory (NA-TST) 38 and the Zhu–
Nakamura (ZN) nonadiabatic transition probability. 10,11,53–55 The generalized
NA-TST is formulated based on Miller’s reactive ﬂux–ﬂux correlation function
approach.
56 The ZN theory, on the other hand, is practically free from the
drawbacks of the LZ theory mentioned above. Numerical tests 38 have also
conﬁrmed that it is essential for accurate evaluation of the thermal rate constant to take into account the multi-dimensional topography of the seam surface and to treat the nonadiabatic electronic transition and nuclear tunneling
eﬀects properly.
The starting point is to consider the ET reactions as a special case of nonadiabatic chemical reactions on two electronic states. The potential for these
states in the diabatic representation is written as:
V ¼j AiV
AAhA jþj BiVBBhB jþj AiHABhB jþj BiHBAhA j; ð12:1Þ

With the above simple background, we consider ET in complex systems. In
this case, eqn (12.4) cannot be used straightforwardly because the nonadiabatic
transition probability is explicitly dependent on the reaction coordinate and
one does not know how to select this one-dimensional reaction coordinate from
the multi-dimensional systems. Therefore, our strategy is to start from a
generalized quantum rate expression. Miller et al.

neglected, i.e. k(t) /C171. In such a case, kTST itself should predict a reasonable ET
rate. However, when the transferred electron in the acceptor state has not
enough time to relax its energy to the environment, the electron can recross
to the donor state. In this energy diﬀusion limit, the dynamic transmission

account and a set of reaction coordinates is along instantaneous normal mode
direction with an imaginary frequency at the transition point, i.e. at the
crossing seam surface x
0 ¼ S(Q) of the donor and acceptor potentials. Along
each direction of the reaction coordinates, we can safely use the ZN formulae to
obtain the nonadiabatic transition probability because the reaction in this
direction has the maximum mean free path. The idea is the same as the
mechanism in Tully’s surface hopping.

In eqn (12.19), the eﬀects of nonadiabatic transition including the nuclear
tunneling are properly taken into account by Z and naturally the main task is to
evaluate the thermally averaged transition probability /C22Pðb; xÞ, which has to be
evaluated using the Monte Carlo technique for multi-dimensional systems. It is
easily shown that the Marcus–Hush formula in adiabatic and nonadiabatic
limits can be recovered from eqn (12.19) and eqn (12.20) within the hightemperature approximation.
To demonstrate the accuracy of eqn (12.17), Figure 12.2 shows the ET rates
vs. electronic couplings for a simple one-dimensional model system obtained
from diﬀerent approaches. It is seen that the present approach with the ZN
probability correctly predicts the rigorous quantum result from the weak to
strong electronic coupling regime.
Figure 12.3 shows the ET rates vs. electronic couplings for a multidimensional system in the Marcus inverted regime.
42 It is shown that the rate
initially increases quadratically in the weak coupling regime, as predicted by the
perturbation theory. As the coupling increases, the perturbation theory always
overestimates the rate. This is diﬀerent from the Marcus normal case where the
perturbation theory does not necessarily provides the larger rate than the real
one.
41 The rate predicted by the present approach reaches maximum at a
certain value of coupling strength and decreases with further increase of the
Figure 12.2 ET rate via electronic coupling for a simple one-dimensional model.
290 Chapter 12
 01/12/2013 15:20:23.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00283
View Online

## Page 311

coupling. This behavior of the adiabatic suppression is the property peculiar to
the inverted case, since the large electronic coupling makes adiabatic potentials
separate far from each other and the nonadiabatic transition probability
becomes small as a result. Compared with the prediction from the LZ formula,
the ZN formulae give a much larger rate especially at low temperatures. With
decreasing the temperature, the coupling strength at the maximal rate predicted
by the LZ formula shifts toward a smaller value, while it stays nearly
unchanged in the case of the ZN formulae. This interesting behavior can be
understood from the energy dependence of the nonadiabatic transition probability. In the case of the ZN formulae, we ﬁnd that the probability reaches a
maximum at a certain intermediate coupling strength at energies in the region
around the crossing point.
12.3 Electron Transfer Rate Theory Incorporated
Solvent Dynamic Eﬀect
As the solvent dynamics begin to play a role in ET, the thermal equilibrium
distribution of the donor population may be broken down during ET. The case
is similar to adiabatic chemical reactions in solvent. Kramers proposed a
method to study the thermal rate of escape from a metastable state coupled to a
dissipative environment (see the review article Ref. 67). Later, Pollak et al .
64
formulated a uniﬁed theory–Kramers turnover theory, which covers the whole
range of friction strength and is applicable to an arbitrary memory friction. The
quantum tunneling was further incorporated by Rips and Pollak
65 with use of
parabolic approximation. Our strategy for ET is to extend the quantum
(a)
(b)
(c)

Ea represents the minimum energy on the seam surface. Solid line:
present result; dashed line: the results predicted from the LZ formula;
dotted line: results from perturbation theory.

 01/12/2013 15:20:23.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00283

## Page 312

Kramers theory65–67 to the two PESs. The Kramers theory can be considered as
the special case of studying nonadiabatic chemical reactions where the nonadiabatic coupling is so strong that the reaction is controlled by the lower
adiabatic PES. Similar to nonadiabatic TST, the inﬂuence of the upper PES to
the ET rate is involved in nonadiabatic transition probability.
If the solvent dynamic motion is much slower than intramolecular vibrational motions, the motions of solvent and intramolecular modes can be separately considered, as in the SM theory. As mentioned above, the original SM
theory is limited to the weak electronic coupling. We extend the SM theory by
introducing two approaches to investigate ET reactions from the weak to
strong electronic coupling regime. One is the quantum R-matrix theory, useful
for dealing with the intramolecular vibrational motions in the whole electronic
coupling domain. The other is the split operator approach which is employed to
solve the reaction–diﬀusion equation.
12.3.1 Quantum Kramer-Like Theory
We start from the reaction coordinate model of ET. Its Hamiltonian in the
diabatic representation has a conventional form:

68
For the nonadiabatic ET reactions, the upper adiabatic PES cannot
be neglected. However, recent work 34,38,41 shows that such reactions
themselves may still be described by the adiabatic Hamiltonian eqn (12.24),
although the nonadiabatic tunneling eﬀects should be properly incorporated.
The mechanism is from the surface hopping proposed by Tully and Preston.
63
One, therefore, may extend the quantum Kramers theory to the ET process.
To do so, we make the normal mode analysis in the vicinity of the barrier of
the potential in eqn (12.24). The standard procedure reads:

## Page 315

The value of P(e|e0) dominates the biased distribution of n(e) from the
thermal one. It is explicitly needed only when the unstable mode and stable
modes are weakly coupled in the well region, because otherwise the mode energy is thermalized and n(e) satisﬁes the Boltzmann distribution. When the
stable modes are thermally distributed, the condition probability in the classical
limit satisﬁes a Gaussian distribution whose width is decided by the average
energy loss of the unstable-mode motion.
67 In the present work, we focus on the
strong friction region. This implies that the mode energy is thermalized (the
energy loss parameter is much larger than 1).
To demonstrate the accuracy of eqn (12.26) together with eqn (12.30) and its

## Page 317

We have calculated the electronic coupling dependence of rate from the
quantum Kramers-like theory as well as from the above mentioned methods. In
the calculations, we set the parameters l ¼ 1, o
c ¼ 0. In addition, T ¼ 1 to keep
the relatively high barrier in the lower adiabatic PES. The results are shown in
Figure 12.4. 35 The present approach predicts correctly the rate in the nonadiabatic limit ( HABo0.006) and the rate indeed changes quadratically with
HAB as predicted by the FGR. It recovers the Kramers theory in the adiabatic
limit (HAB40.03) and becomes consistent with the Zusman theory in between.
As expected, Zusman theory cannot describe the ET rate in the adiabatic limit.
It predicts a much larger rate than the Kramers theory and tends to TST.
12.3.2 Extended Sumi–Marcus Theory 44,45
For organic molecules in solution, both the molecular vibrational motions and
the solvent relaxation play important roles in ET. Usually, the vibrational
motions are much faster than the solvent polarization motion because the vibrational motions have much higher frequencies. Thus, the thermal equilibrium
may be maintained for those vibrational modes during the course of ET. In this
case, one may separately consider the intramolecular vibrational motions and
solvent motions. Along the directions of the high-frequency modes, ET can still
be described by NA-TST, but the solvent motion has to be treated with the
dynamic equations. Sumi and Marcus
17 proposed a method to describe such a
kind of ET, where the solvent motion satisﬁes the diﬀusion–reaction equation
and intramolecular ET is incorporated with a sink function in the diﬀusion
equation. In the original SM theory, the sink function is obtained from the
Figure 12.4 ET rate evaluated from diﬀerent theories as a function of the coupling
strength for a spin-boson model ( l ¼ 1 and oc ¼ 0.1) and the temperature
T ¼ 1. Thick solid line: present approach; dashed line: he Zusman theory;
dash-dotted line: FGR; thin solid line: interpolation; thick dotted line:
adiabatic Kramers theory; thin dotted line: adiabatic limit of Zusman
theory.
Semi-Classical Treatments of Electron Transfer Rate 297
 01/12/2013 15:20:23.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00283
View Online

## Page 318

semi-classical local FGR for the weak ET, and the conventional TST for the
adiabatic ET. Here, we combine the SM theory and our NA-TST of ET to
propose a method which is suitable in the crossover regime from the nonadiabatic to the adiabatic limit.
For the ET aﬀected by the motions of the high-frequency vibrational modes
and the solvent polarization ﬂuctuation, the PESs of the donor and the
acceptor can be respectively modeled as:
V
1ðq; xÞ¼ V1ðqÞþ V1ðxÞ; ð12:47Þ
V2ðq; xÞ¼ V2ðqÞþ V2ðxÞþ DG: ð12:48Þ
Here q represents the collective coordinate of the intramolecular vibrational
modes. x is the solvent coordinate that is deﬁned either by the mean square
ﬂuctuation of the excess solvent polarization or by the energy gap of the
solvation energy upon moving an electron from the donor to the acceptor.
Generally, one has to consider two-dimensional diﬀusion equations to incorporate the relaxation dynamics from both slow x and fast q coordinates. In
the case where the ﬂuctuations of the intramolecular vibrational modes are very
fast compared with the solvent relaxation, the adiabatic elimination procedure
21,25,27 can lead the two-dimensional diﬀusive equations to onedimensional ones. Denoting P1(x,t)a n d P2(x,t) as the population distributions
of the donor and the acceptor states at a given x and time t, respectively, one
obtains the one-dimensional coupled diﬀusion-reaction equations: 17
@P1ðx; tÞ=@t ¼½ L1 /C0 k1ðxÞ/C138P1ðx; tÞþ k2ðxÞP2ðx; tÞ; ð12:49Þ
@P2ðx; tÞ=@t ¼½ L2 /C0 k2ðxÞ/C138P2ðx; tÞþ k1ðxÞP1ðx; tÞ: ð12:50Þ
Here ki(x) is the sink function that invokes the contribution from the fast
modes. The generalized Smoluchowski operator Li is given by:
Li ¼ DðtÞf@2=@x2 þ b@=@ x½dViðxÞ=dx/C138g ð 12:51Þ

Note that the sink function represents the local reaction rate along the

be obtained from eqn (12.4) or eqn (12.19) together with the ZN formula or
R-matrix approach for wide electronic coupling strengths.

from the real-time one for solving Schro ¨dinger equations.

The ET rate is unambiguously deﬁned only from a single exponential decay
of the population at the donor state. However, the reaction–diﬀusion equation
eqn (12.53) does not always predict population decay exponentially. Alternatively, two kinds of average survival times are introduced as:
t

Here SðtÞ¼
R
Pðx; tÞdx represents the total population left in the reactive regime at time t. The quantity ta monitors the short-time dynamics of the
population and is equivalent to the mean ﬁrst passage time. tb detects information of a time region later than ta. Thus the correspondence rates are deﬁned
as 1/ ta and 1/ tb, respectively. Both rates become equal only when the population decays single-exponentially.
To investigate the single exponential decay, one can deﬁne a timedependent rate:

If the rate constant is meaningful, kf(t) will reach a plateau after long time. This
plateau value corresponds to the true rate constant.
300 Chapter 12
 01/12/2013 15:20:23.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00283

## Page 321

As a numerical example, we investigate ET in the Marcus inverted regime to
reveal the solvent eﬀect. The potentials in the fast ( q) and slow ( x) coordinates
are modeled by two shifted harmonic oscillators, although the approaches can
be straightforwardly applied to anharmonic systems. The parameters,
o
q ¼ 500 cm/C0 1, lq ¼ 24 oq and DG ¼/C0 40 oq, are used to make the reaction
occur in the inverted regime. Here, lq and lx represent reorganization energies
in the fast and slow coordinates, respectively.
Figure 12.5 displays the relaxation time ts (¼ 1/bD(t)) dependence of the
rates obtained from the FGR and the R-matrix approaches. 44 Again, the
perturbation theory predicted rates that were too large in the whole range of
the relaxation time in the case of the strong electronic coupling, although the
tendencies with respect to t
s obtained from both approaches were similar.
At the small value of ts, the three kinds of rates have the same values, illustrating
that the population of the reactants decays single-exponentially. In this case, we
expect that the population should satisfy the thermal equilibrium distribution
and NA-TST can be useful. When t
s becomes very large, the ET rates become
independent of ts. In this case, the solvent ﬂuctuation is so slow that the
population distribution along x is not distorted by the diﬀusion in the course of
the reaction. Thus the rates depend only on the sink function and initial
population distribution. This regime has been named the non-diﬀusion limit.
17
12.4 Time-Dependent Wavepacket Diﬀusion Approach
The charge transport in organic crystals is conventionally described either as a
coherent or an incoherent process with respect to the interaction strength of the
charge carrier with lattice vibration. In the weak electron–phonon interaction
Figure 12.5 ET rate as a function of the relaxation time ts with T ¼ 300 K
and HAB ¼ 800 cm /C0 1. Solid line, dashed line and dotted line correspond
to 1/ta,1 /tb and long time rates, respectively, obtained from the R-matrix
theory as the sink function. Filled circles with dotted line, ﬁlled triangles
with dotted line and ﬁlled squares with dotted line are those obtained
from the local FGR as the sink function.
Semi-Classical Treatments of Electron Transfer Rate 301
 01/12/2013 15:20:23.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00283
View Online

## Page 322

limit, the coherent motion of the charge carrier is maintained and the wellestablished band-like theory for inorganic semiconductors 75–78 is applicable.
For the case of strong interaction, the charge carrier acts as a polaron because
of the strong bondage by the lattice polarization cloud, and its motion can be
described by a hopping-type model.
5,79–82 In this case, several groups have
investigated charge transport by using a Marcus hopping model to successfully
describe organic semiconductors.83–86 These results not only help to understand
the structure–transport relationship at a molecular level but also indicate that
the hopping mechanism is a reasonable model with which to describe charge
transport in organic materials, at least at room temperature. To overcome the
limitation of the weak electronic coupling, the present approaches for the
donor–acceptor system are applicable; for instance, Nan et al.
87 have applied
our NA-TST 38 to investigate the mobility in quaterthiophene and sexithiophene under a hopping mechanism.
However, the charge carrier motions in many organic semiconductors are
between these two limits. It is thus expected that more sophisticated microscopic charge transport theories need to be developed to unify the concepts of
the band-like and hopping transport. Indeed, many work along this line have
been performed.
46,78,88–105 It is known, however, that most of those rigorous
quantum approaches are limited to tens of sites because of the numerical
convergence problem and computer memory limitations.
As an alternative, one may consider the eﬀect of the electron–phonon
interactions as the classical stochastic ﬁelds which act upon the site energies and
electronic couplings between sites.
106–111 Subsequently, the carrier evolution,
governed by a stochastic Liouville or Schro ¨dinger equation, is stochastically
averaged.108–110,112–116 Following the stochastic ﬁeld strategy, we consider the
total Hamiltonian in the interaction representation of the phonon so that the
electron–phonon interactions become time-dependent and can be modeled by
stochastic functions. However, we do not assume any special statistical properties for the stochastic functions. Alternatively, these time-dependent stochastic functions are generated straightforwardly from the spectral density. It is
known that the spectral density solely determines the phonon properties and it
can be obtained from ab initio calculations or molecular dynamics (MD)
simulations for realistic systems.
117,118 Once these time-dependent matrix
elements are known, the charge carrier dynamics can be revealed by solving the
time-dependent Schro¨dinger equation.
We consider a charge carrier moving along a chain of identical molecules
with equal distances. The thermal motion of the molecules in the chain is taken
into account by allowing the site energies e
ii(t) and the electronic coupling
elements eij(t)(iaj) to ﬂuctuate in time. The corresponding Hamiltonian is:

109,110,119,120 we describe the diagonal ﬂuctuation and the nondiagonal ﬂuctuation as stochastic processes. Their correlation functions are

109,110,120 Although several works have extended the white noise to

78,98,99 Essentially, these ﬂuctuations should satisfy the ﬂuctuation–
dissipation theorem 123 to correctly describe the eﬀect of the molecular vibrational motions on the carrier dynamics. In addition, the diagonal ﬂuctuation
generated by the vibrational motion should be related to the reorganization

Fox et al. 132 have proposed a fast, accurate algorithm for the numerical

which are uniformly distributed over the interval (0, 2 p). It is noted that eqn

The ﬂuctuation deij(t) generated from eqn (12.80) only incorporates the real
part of the corresponding correlation function, despite the quantum eﬀect of
the phonon motions being partially incorporated by the modiﬁed correlation
function compared with the classical MD simulations. The charge carrier dynamics would be more accurate if the imaginary ﬂuctuation is added to satisfy
the detailed balance principle. The Hamiltonian of the system, however, becomes non-Hermitian in this case and one has to pay an attention to the numerical convergence of the ensemble average. Here, we consider the real
ﬂuctuation to investigate the validity of neglecting the imaginary ﬂuctuation.

## Page 327

12.5 Applications
12.5.1 Electron Transfer within Charge-Localized
Dinitroaromatic Radical Anions
Nelsen and co-workers 145 measured the ET rates within the 2,7-dinitronaphthalene anion radical in diﬀerent solvents and noted that the solvent
dynamic eﬀect was not important. They thus tried to use the Marcus formula or
the BJ theory
1 in the perturbation limit to explain their experimental results.
However, both cannot explain the experimental results correctly because the
electronic coupling is not weak enough. Since the solvent dynamics are fast,
here we can use the rate expression (eqn (12.17)) with ZN transition probability. Indeed, the predicted rates are in excellent agreement with the experiment. The results are shown in Figure 12.6. In the calculations, the
reorganization energy and electronic coupling are estimated by Nelsen’s
measurement.
Nelsen and co-workers also measured the ET rates within charge-localized
dinitroaromatic radical anions in several solvents.
146 The optical data reveal
that this system has a very large electronic coupling (3000–4000 cm /C0 1). In such
a case, solvent dynamics may play a signiﬁcant role. Therefore, the quantum
Kramers-like theory is used to estimate the rate. We use the temperature
dependence of solvent relaxation time t:
ln t ¼ ln b þ H=RT; ð12:92Þ
where b and H are two parameters whose values have been recommended by
Grampp and Jaenicke.
147 Figure 12.7 shows the calculated and experimental
Figure 12.6 ET rates within 2,7-dinitronaphthalene as a function of temperature in
the diﬀerent solvents. Dots and lines correspond to the results from the
experiment of Nelsen et al. 145 and the theoretical simulations, respectively. Solid: acetonitrile (CH 3CN); dashed line: dimethylformamide
(DMF); dotted-dash: propionitrile (PrCN).
Semi-Classical Treatments of Electron Transfer Rate 307

## Page 328

rates. It is found that both of them agree very well and even the slope tendency
of the experimental rates with temperature can be correctly predicted.
12.5.2 Ab Initio Calculations on the Intramolecular Electron
Transfer Rates of a Bis(hydrazine) Radical Cation
It is more interesting to pure-theoretically predict the rates without using any
parameters by optical measurements. Here, we combine electronic structure
calculations to predict the reorganization energies and electronic couplings
together with the present rate approaches to obtain the ET rates. A charge
localized intervalance radical cation of a bis(hydrazine) ([22/hex/22]
1), shown
in Figure 12.8, is taken as an example. 47
There are several approaches proposed to calculate the parameters of controlling ET: reorganization energy and electronic coupling. We adopt two
methods to calculate the reorganization energy. One is the four-point technique
introduced by Nelsen et al .,
148 which can directly and easily predict the total
reorganization energy. The other is based on the summarization of the
reorganization energy of each mode; this approach can tell one which mode
plays an important role in ET. For the calculation of the electronic coupling,
several diﬀerent approaches have been proposed. For instance, the energy
diﬀerence of two molecular orbitals (MOs) based on Koopmans’ theorem
(KT),
149–151 directly calculates the coupling of two diabatic states (two-state
model, TM) that represent left- and right-localized states, 152,153 and the energy
gap between two adiabatic states using spin–ﬂip (SF) strategy. 154,155 All these
approaches have been used in our calculations for the purpose of comparison.
Figure 12.7 Comparison of ESR rates with those from the quantum Kramers-like
theory for a dinitroaromatic radical anion in acetonitrile (MeCN) and
benzonitrile (PhCN).
308 Chapter 12
 01/12/2013 15:20:23.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00283
View Online

## Page 329

With use of obtained parameters, the extended SM theory is employed to
predict the ET rates because the experimental rates are measured in a strong
friction solvent.
The present system has diﬀerent isomerized geometries (Figure 12.8). The
following four reactions dominate ET:
oi
þ½22=hex=22/C138þ ! oþi½22=hex=22/C138þ; ð12:93Þ
ooþ½22=hex=22/C138þ ! oþo½22=hex=22/C138þ; ð12:94Þ
iiþ½22=hex=22/C138þ ! iþi½22=hex=22/C138þ; ð12:95Þ
ioþ½22=hex=22/C138þ ! iþo½22=hex=22/C138þ: ð12:96Þ
The calculations reveal that the ET rates are heavily dependent on the isomer
geometries. Compared with the experimental data (1.3 /C2 108 s/C0 1), the rate of the
reaction eqn (12.95) is about 1000 times larger, and the rates from other three
isomers are consistent with the experiment well (the thermal average rate of the
three ET processes is 2.14 /C2 10
8 s/C0 1). It manifests that the electron spin resonance (ESR) measurement may correspond to these three isomers of [22/hex/
22]1 components. The rate within ii 1[22/hex/22]1is about 1000 times larger
than that in oo 1[22/hex/22]1. Comparing with diﬀerent electronic structure
approaches, we have conﬁrmed that density functional theory (DFT) with a 5050 functional is a reasonable approach for the present system to predict both
the reorganization energy and electronic coupling. For the reorganization energy, both the four-point approach and summarizing the reorganization energy
of each mode work well, but one has to be cautious when applying the fourpoint method to isomers. In the calculation of the electronic coupling, the twostate model is still a good choice compared with SF and Koopmanns’ theorem
for the present systems.

## Page 330

12.5.3 Electron Mobilities of N-type Organic Semiconductors
from Time-Dependent Wavepacket Diﬀusion Method:
Pentacenequinone Derivatives
Recent experiments have measured the electron mobilities in two n-type
pentacenequinone organic semiconductor isomers: 5,7,12,14-tetraaza-6,13pentacenequinone (TAPQ5, shown in Figure 12.9) and 1,4,8,11-tetraaza6,13-pentacenequinone(TAPQ7, shown in Figure 12.10). The mobilities
of TAPQ5 and TAPQ7 are in the range of 0.05–0.12 cm
2 (Vs)/C0 1 156 and
2–6/C2 10/C0 5 cm2 (Vs)/C0 1,157 respectively, and the former is three orders larger than
the latter. However, the molecular packing of the two crystals has a similar pstacking and quadruple weak H-bonds. The possible reason for such a large
diﬀerence has been suggested from their diﬀerent morphologies experimentally.
157 In spite of this explanation, it is not clear what are the possible
ultimate values that the experiments can reach. We therefore focus on this
problem and understand how their isomer structures inﬂuence the electron
mobilities.
158
The electronic structure calculations reveal that the charge carriers essentially
display one-dimensional transport properties within the TAPQ5 and TAPQ7
crystals. The mobilities are then calculated by using the TDWPD approach, in
which the dynamic ﬂuctuations of the electronic couplings are incorporated via
their correlation functions obtained from MD simulations. Figure 12.11 displays the temperature dependencies of the mobilities for the TAPQ5 and
TAPQ7 crystals. For a purpose of comparison, we also show the mobilities
with use of thermal averaged electronic couplings, i.e. not considering the
ﬂuctuation of electronic couplings. It is seen that the mobilities with the
Figure 12.9 (a) Chemical structure of TAPQ5. (b) Crystal structure in the bc plane.
(c) Crystal structure in the ab plane (a ¼ 3.89 A˚ , b ¼ 9.07 A˚ , c ¼ 18.43 A˚ ,
b ¼ 92.31 for unit cell). The Arabic numerals correspond to the types of
molecular pairs used in the calculations of electronic couplings.
310 Chapter 12
 01/12/2013 15:20:23.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00283
View Online

## Page 331

ﬂuctuations of the electronic couplings become small compared with those
from the thermal averaged electronic couplings for both TAPQ5 and TAPQ7,
which are consistent with previous investigations. 99,159
From Figure 12.11, we further ﬁnd that the two systems exhibit band-like
charge transport properties, which generally directs to the coherent motion of
electron among several sites. However, it has been shown
160,161 that the nuclear
tunneling eﬀect can also predict a band-like property of the mobility in the
hopping model, where the electron is localized on one site rather than
coherently delocalized among several sites. To judge whether this kind of bandlike property is coming from the coherent motion or nuclear tunneling eﬀect,
we also calculate the mobilities from the hopping model with the ET rate based
on the perturbation theory. In this model, the mobility is still given by Einstein
relation, but the diﬀusion coeﬃcient is related to the ET rate by D(T) ¼ l
2k(T)/2,
where k(T) is the ET rate. The results are also displayed in Figure 12.11.
Surprisingly, the mobilities from the FGR have band-like properties for both
systems and are essentially the same as those from TDWPD method with use
of thermal averaged electronic couplings. Since the mobility from the FGR
represents the hopping-type mechanism, we thus conclude that the band-like
property is not from the coherent motion of electron, but from the nuclear
tunneling eﬀect. Furthermore, the mobility predicted by the Marcus formula, in
which the nuclear tunneling eﬀect is neglected, is much smaller than that from
that with FGR, and the Marcus formula even changes the band-like transport
property into a thermally activated one.
Figure 12.10 (a) Chemical structure of TAPQ7. (b) Crystal structure in the ac
plane. (c) Crystal structure in the ab plane ( a ¼ 10.59 A˚ , b ¼ 16.04 A˚ ,
c ¼ 3.80 A˚ , b ¼ 95.61 of unit cell). The labels correspond to the types of
molecular pairs used in the calculations of electronic couplings.
Semi-Classical Treatments of Electron Transfer Rate 311
 01/12/2013 15:20:23.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00283
View Online

## Page 332

Although the mobilities from the FGR and TDWPD methods are very close
to each other, we ﬁnd 4 VDA/l41 (for instance, the ratio is about 4 for TAPQ7).
Such a large ratio should lead to the delocalized property of electron and the
FGR should explicitly be invalid, which is inconsistent with our numerical
result. To analyze why the FGR is still applicable to the present systems, we
compare the ET rate with the period of vibrational motions. For instance, the
vibrational period is 23 fs and the inverse of the ET rate is 10 fs for TAPQ7.
Although the nuclei move slower than the electron, they are in the same order.
Therefore, one expects that the vibrational motions will play an important role
in the ET process. As a result, the site energy ﬂuctuation caused by the
vibrational motions is fast enough to break down the electronic coherence
between two adjacent sites, in other words, as the electron moves from the
donor to the acceptor, it will be trapped in the acceptor state because of the fast
Figure 12.11 Temperature dependence of electron mobilities ( m) in TAPQ5 (above)
and TAPQ7 (below) crystals, respectively. TDWPD and TDWPD-f
correspond to the mobilities from the time-dependent wavepacket
diﬀusion approach without and with the ﬂuctuations for electronic
couplings. ‘expt.’ represents the measured mobility at 100 1C.
312 Chapter 12
 01/12/2013 15:20:23.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00283
View Online

## Page 333

ﬂuctuations of site energies and cannot transfer back to the donor state to
perform the coherent motion. In this situation, the ET process becomes a
single-step process and without coherence, which may be described well by the
perturbation theory.
Finally, we propose that the theoretical mobilities at 400 K for TAPQ5 and
TAPQ7 crystals are 1.54 cm
2 (Vs)/C0 1 and 8.75 cm 2 (Vs)/C0 1, respectively. The
predicted mobility of TAPQ7 is about six times larger than that of TAPQ5.
Therefore, one expects that the isomerized structure has an explicit eﬀect on the
mobility. Furthermore, comparing the predicted mobilities with the experimental values,
156 the predicted mobility for TAPQ5 is about ten times larger;
the diﬀerence comes from the fact that the theoretical mobility is for ideal
monocrystalline whereas the measured one is for polycrystalline. For TAPQ7,
however, the predicted mobility is much larger than the experimental value.
Therefore, one expects that once the monocrystalline ﬁlm of TAPQ7 is
synthesized, there should be a major improvement in the mobility, and TAPQ7
should have a higher mobility than that of the TAPQ5 crystal.
12.6 Conclusions
We have presented several approaches to calculate the rate constants of electron transfer occurring in solvent from the weak to strong electronic couplings.
In the fast solvent relaxation limit, the approach based on the nonadiabatic
transition state theory can be adopted. It is related to the Marcus formula by a
prefactor and referred as a modiﬁed Marcus formula. When the solvent dynamics begin to play a role, the quantum Kramers-like theory is applicable. For
the case where the intramolecular vibrational motions are much faster than the
solvent motion, the extended Sumi–Marcus theory is a better choice. As the
coherent motion of electron is incorporated, such as in the organic semiconductors, the time-dependent wavepacket diﬀusion approach is proposed.
Several applications show that the proposed approaches, together with electronic structure calculations for the factors controlling electron transfer, can be
used to theoretically predict electron transfer rates correctly.

## Page 339

CHAPTER 13
Modiﬁed Zusman Equation for
Quantum Solvation Dynamics
and Rate Processes
HOU-DAO ZHANG, a JIAN XU, a RUI-XUE XU b AND
YIJING YAN* a,b
a Department of Chemistry, Hong Kong University of Science and
Technology, Kowloon, Hong Kong; b Hefei National Laboratory for
Physical Sciences at the Microscale, University of Science and Technology of
China, Hefei, Anhui 230026, China
*Email: yyan@ust.hk
13.1 Introduction
Quantum transfer dynamics such as electron or proton or excitation-energy
transfer play pivotal roles in many chemical and biological systems. Along with
quantum transfer, surrounding bath environment is subject to reorganization,
which in turn inﬂuences back to the quantum transfer system, resulting in
dynamical Stokes shift and other interesting phenomena. The interplay
between quantum system anharmonicity and coherent transfer coupling and
entanglement with surrounding bath reorganization often results in rich
dynamical behaviors of quantum transfer. Taking electron transfer as an
example, when increasing the solvent (bath) relaxation time, reactions change
from the nonadibatic regime to the solvent-controlled adiabatic regime.
1–10
RSC Theoretical and Computational Chemistry Series No. 6
Reaction Rate Constant Computations: Theories and Applications
Edited by Keli Han and Tianshu Chu
r The Royal Society of Chemistry 2014
Published by the Royal Society of Chemistry, www.rsc.org
319
 17/11/2013 09:29:13.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00319

## Page 340

The Zusman equation (ZE), 1–4 due mainly to its physically insightful picture
on solvation dynamics, is (at least used to be) one of the most commonly used
approaches in the study of quantum transfer processes. In this approach, the
electronic system degrees of freedom are coupled to a collective bath coordinate
that is assumed to be diﬀusive. The only approximation involved is the classical
high temperature treatment of bath. To account for the dynamic Stokes shift,
the standard ZE includes also the imaginary part of bath correlation function.
This part does not depend on temperature and is therefore exact in the diﬀusion
regime.
However, the ZE was often found to give unphysical results such as
negative rates or populations,
11–14 even in the case of high temperature and
small electronic coupling. The positivity issue of the ZE has been extensively
exploited, with the aid of the analytical results on steady-state solutions to twolevel systems.
15 In particular Figure 1 in ref. 15 reports the positivity ‘phase’
diagrams for the asymptotic long time properties of the ZE in the fullparameters space of model two-level systems. The ZE does result in negative
rates/populations in many regions of high temperature and small electronic
coupling. These observations suggest that the original ZE is largely an
uncontrollable classical high temperature theory and has thus very limited
practical use.
This chapter accounts our recent eﬀort on the revival of this physically
appealing theory, but in context of a modiﬁed ZE theory.
15 Without numerical
penalty, the modiﬁed ZE remarkably extends the range of applicability in
parameters space, including moderately low temperature regime. Moreover, the
modiﬁed ZE is a well controllable theory, supported by a simple accuracy
control measure.
15
We also discuss the hierarchical equations of motion (HEOM) theory, 16–20 a
rather general theory for quantum dissipative dynamics on which the
development of the modiﬁed ZE was based.
15 HEOM is mathematically
equivalent to Feymann–Vernon’s inﬂuence functional theory, 21,22 but has great
advantage in numerical eﬃciency and universality for various applications.
Recently advancements include the optimized HEOM theory,
23–27 eﬃcient
propagator methods, 28,29 and graphics processing unit and parallel programming techniques. 30,31 While the ZE approach describes the correlated
non-Markovian system-bath dynamics through an explicit inclusion of both
system’s degrees of freedom and solvation bath coordinate, HEOM
achieves the same goal but via auxiliary density operators (ADOs) in system
space only. The established relation between them thus highlights the physical
contents of ADOs in HEOM, an alternative correlated system-bath coherence
theory.
To complete this theoretical account we review also the celebrated Caldeira–
Leggett quantum master equation
32 and its relation to ZE. Moreover, we
propose, for the ﬁrst time, a physically transparent protocol to obtain the
conventional ZE from the master equation in the diﬀusion limit. This novel
protocol is universal ( cf. section 13.2.2), constructed based purely on the
thermal dynamics consideration, validated by the central limiting theorem.

13.2 Zusman Equation via the Conventional Approach
13.2.1 Caldeira-Leggett’s Master Equation
Condensed phase dynamics such as electron and energy transfer are an important subject in quantum dissipative mechanics. It treats an arbitrary system
(Hs) embedded in bath that assumes to be harmonic, hB ¼ 1

oscillators, ^FB ¼ P

The key quantity in quantum dissipative dynamics is the reduced system
density operator, rS(t) /C17trBrT(t), the bath-subspace trace over the total composite density operator. It is worth mentioning here that the harmonic bath
described above assumes rather Gaussian statistics for thermal bath inﬂuence. 18
Realistic anharmonic environments usually do obey Gaussian statistics in the
thermodynamic mean ﬁeld limit. For general treatment of nonperturbative and
non-Markovian quantum dissipation systems, HEOM formalism
16–19 has now
emerged as a standard theory. It is discussed in the next section.
The conventional quantum master equation approach remains of great value
for its transparent physical implications. This approach can be exact for harmonic system with arbitrary non-Markovian dissipation,
34,35 but for anharmonic systems it is only valid in the Markovian limit, together with high

However, direct substituting it for eqn (13.9), followed by integrating out the

The new protocol, proposed here, is a generic high-friction map for every
momentum related operator appearing in eqn (13.9) and eqn (13.11) to be replaced by its correspondence in coordinate space. This protocol is rather
physically transparent, as it is closely related to a thermodynamic equilibrium

13.3 Modiﬁed Zusman Equation versus HEOM
13.3.1 The HEOM Formalism
We turn now to the HEOM formalism. 16–19 We will demonstrate that it not just
recovers the ZE, but also leads to a formally simple but physically signiﬁcant
modiﬁcation.
15,29 To proceed we focus on the following extended singleexponential form of bath correlation function (see eqn (13.2) for the
parameters):

right-hand-side of eqn (13.8), as speciﬁed explicitly there. Including the white
noise component as eqn (13.23) leads to some remarkable features for the resulting modiﬁed ZE theory, as can be witnessed in the following.

(13.23), the so-called the white noise residue ansatz: dCB(t) /C17CB(t) /C0
Ze/C0 gDtE2Dd(t). Its validation is therefore just that of the modiﬁed ZE.

13.4 Kinetic Rates via Hierarchy Green’s Functions
13.4.1 Kinetic Rate Equation versus Quantum Dissipative
Dynamics
In the study of kinetic processes one is often interested in the rates of population transfer among speciﬁed system states. Considered here would be certain
rate equations, involving only populations, { P
a(t) ¼ raa(t)}, as dynamics variables. These are diagonal elements of reduced system density matrix, { r(t)},
under the speciﬁed representation. The rate equation can apparently be formulated from quantum dissipative dynamics on r(t), followed by eliminating
those oﬀ-diagonal elements.
Let us start with the memory form of quantum master equation:
_rðtÞ¼/C0 iL
eff rðtÞ/C0
Zt
0
dt ^Pðt /C0 tÞrðtÞ: ð13:47Þ
This form could be rather general, provided the initial system–bath factorization ansatz, rTð0Þ¼ rð0Þreq
B , is applicable and the system Hamiltonian is
time-independent. In contact with the HEOM formalism of eqn (13.27), we
show in section 13.4.2 that the memory dissipation kernel in eqn (13.47), ^PðtÞ,
is the time-domain counterpart of a self-energy in the hierarchical
Liouville space.
To obtain the rate equations, we write eqn (13.47) in the Laplace domain:
s~rðsÞ/C0 rð0Þ¼/C0 iL
eff þ PðsÞ½/C138 ~rðsÞ; ð13:48Þ
and rearrange the r-matrix into the form of (column) vector, r ¼ (P, C), with
P ¼{raa} and C ¼ {rab; aab} being the population and coherence components,

(KPP), coherence-to-population K(PC), population-to-coherence ( KCP) and

we can now recast eqn (13.48) as:
s ePðsÞ/C0 Pð0Þ¼/C0 KPPðsÞ ePðsÞ/C0 KPCðsÞ eCðsÞ;
s eCðsÞ¼/C0 KCPðsÞ ePðsÞ/C0 KCCðsÞ eCðsÞ:
ð13:50Þ
Using the second equation to eliminate the coherence in the ﬁrst equation, we
