s ePðsÞ/C0 ePð0Þ¼ KðsÞ ePðsÞ; ð13:51Þ
with:
KðsÞ/C17/C0 KPPðsÞþ KPCðsÞ½s þ KCCðsÞ/C138/C0 1KCPðsÞ: ð13:52Þ
This is the Laplace s-frequency dependent rate matrix. The time-domain
counterpart of eqn (13.51) is:

Note that the Markovian dissipative dynamical process is governed by a

by the constant rate matrix, K(s) ¼ K(0). Equation (13.52) would indicate nonMarkovian rates in general, even with Markovian dissipative dynamics.
However, kinetic rates are physically concerned with post-coherence events, in
which the coherence-to-coherence dynamics timescale, the magnitude of
|K
CC |/C0 1, is short compared with the relevant s/C0 1 of interest. Therefore, the
kinetic rate matrix of eqn (13.52) in the kinetics regime is often of

In concluding this subsection, let us examine eqn (13.60) more closely.
Consider ﬁrst its frequency dependence. At each inward step for Q (n), the
frequency variable ( sZ0) shifts down by one quanta of gD. Thus, to specifyQ (0)(s) at a given s, knowledge on Q (M)(s0) at the quantized distance,
s0 ¼ s þ MgD, is required. The hierarchy self-energy, Q (n) in eqn (13.60), also
goes with the | Z|. The above observations may imply HEOM has the picture of
dissipaton, with parameters of ( gD,| Z|) from the exponential component of
bath correlation function in eqn (13.23). Each individual hierarchy self-energy
takes one dissipaton into account. Therefore, HEOM at the level of Mth-tier
truncation considers M-dissipaton eﬀect on the reduced system dynamics.
13.4.3 Analytical Solutions to Kinetics Rate Between Two
States
For entangled N-state transfer processes, the hierarchy Green’s functions formalism involves tensors each having N4 elements. As far as the kinetics regime
is concerned, however, many interesting rate processes proceed practically in a
step-wise manner. In this case the kinetics rate matrix could be determined by
the individual rates between two states. Remarkably, the hierarchy Green’s
functions for the modiﬁed ZE/HEOM is analytically solvable for two-state
systems.
9 The resulting analytical expression of rate resolution, Kba(s), for individual elementary | ai-hb| rate process can therefore be used to construct
the N/C2 N kinetics rate matrix.
The ﬁnal results of the modiﬁed ZE via HEOM on two-state systems are
summarized as follows. 9,15 The system Hamiltonian for given | ai-|bi rate
process assumes:
HS ¼ð Eba þ lÞj bihb jþ Vðj aihb jþj biha jÞ: ð13:63Þ
While V is the transfer coupling (oﬀ-diagonal) parameter, Eba assumes often the
value of reaction equilibrium free Gibbs energy ðDG/C14
r Þ, with the energy of
reactant set to be zero. The solvent bath is initially at thermal equilibrium with
the reactant (| ai) state, for thermal rate problems. Thus the on-site energy for
product (| bi) in eqn (13.63) also includes the solvent reorganization contribution. We also assume the case of on-site diagonal ﬂuctuation. Thus, the
dissipative mode reads:
^QS ¼j bihb j : ð13:64Þ

Having the analytical solutions to the hierarchy Green’s functions, the
comparison between the original ZE and the modiﬁed ZE can be readily explored over the entire parameter space. Evidently, the proposed modiﬁcation is
remarkably signiﬁcant.15 The protocol of accuracy control presented in section
13.3.3 has also been veriﬁed extensively.
13.5 Conclusions
We have presented a comprehensive account of the theoretical background of
the Zusman equation and its recently development. We have discussed in detail,
both the physical picture and mathematical derivation, of how the Zusman
equation is related to Caldeira–Leggett’s master equation and to HEOM formalism, and from the latter, how it is optimally modiﬁed.
The Caldeira–Leggett–Zusman method takes the solvation modes explicitly
into account, while the HEOM approach treats the same dynamics, but implicitly, via a set of ADOs in the reduced system space. The established relation
between these two approaches to solvation dynamics has also led to a fundamentally important modiﬁcation of the Zusman equation on the basis of the
recent development of optimal HEOM theory. In turn, it also oﬀers a clear
physical picture on the ADOs in the HEOM formalism. Thus it suggests direct
use of the HEOM formalism to study the solvation dynamics, due to its great
ﬂexibility for various systems, with pre-determined accuracy control. Recent
advancements in the HEOM artillery also include its implementation with
graphics processing unit and parallel programming techniques.
30,31
Finally, we would like to elaborate the proposed protocol of the high-friction
map, eqn (13.17). Its construction is based purely on the thermodynamic
consideration, eqn (13.15), validated by the central limit theorem. Therefore it
may oﬀer a general rule to obtain the Smoluchowski limit to any phase-space
dynamics under study. The protocol proposed in this chapter is based on the
fact that the map is universal at formal level and is therefore obtainable with
thermodynamic consideration. It means the Smoluchowski dynamics can be
taken care of by the related Fokker–Planck equation, upon the universal map
being carried out. It is worth pointing out that the resultant diﬀusion
operator in eqn (13.18) clearly originates from only the Hamiltonian part of the
334 Chapter 13
 17/11/2013 09:29:13.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00319
View Online

## Page 355

Fokker–Planck operator, the ﬁrst term in eqn (13.11). This result is in line with
the common sense view that the Hamiltonian description at microscopic level
can be replaced by the free energy and entropy description in non-equilibrium
statistical dynamics.

CHAPTER 14
Time-Dependent Treatment
of SVRT Model for
Polyatom–Polyatom Reaction
JOHN Z. H. ZHANG a,b
a State Key Lab of Precision Spectroscopy, Institute of Theoretical and

200062, China;

14.1 Introduction
A major challenge in quantum dynamics is to develop quantitatively accurate
methods for practical computational study of chemical reactions involving
polyatomic molecules. Currently, rigorous quantum dynamics calculations are
limited to those systems involving no more than four atoms.
1 In order to
perform a quantitatively accurate quantum dynamics study for the vast majority of chemical reactions that are of chemical or biological interest, it is
necessary to develop practical computational methods to treat the reaction
dynamics of polyatomic molecules. To this end, some reduced dimensionality
methods have been proposed to treat polyatomic systems (tetra-atomic systems
in particular) by reducing the dynamical degrees of freedom from six to three.
2,3
By proper counting and weighting, some useful results have been reported of
these reduced dimensionality calculations for the benchmark H 2 þ OH
RSC Theoretical and Computational Chemistry Series No. 6
Reaction Rate Constant Computations: Theories and Applications
Edited by Keli Han and Tianshu Chu
r The Royal Society of Chemistry 2014
Published by the Royal Society of Chemistry, www.rsc.org
337
 01/12/2013 15:17:30.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00337

## Page 358

system.4–7 However, it is desirable to develop a more systematic model to treat
reaction dynamics for more general polyatomic molecules.
In previous papers, we proposed a semi-rigid vibrating rotor target (SVRT)
model for general computational study of quantum dynamics between an atom
and a polyatomic or complex target (reacting) molecule.
8,9 As shown in refs. 8
and 9, the SVRT model for an atom–polyatom reaction is a natural generalization of the simplest atom–diatom reaction model and it permits realistic
quantum dynamics study for a general polyatomic reaction with an atomic
projectile in four mathematical dimensions.
The basic SVRT model has been recently extended to studying reactive
collision dynamics between two polyatomic molecules.
10 In this chapter, we
describe the detailed development of the SVRT model for a general polyatom–
polyatom reactive collision and its implementation for numerical computation
using the time-dependent quantum wavepacket method. Extension of the basic
SVRT model to include adiabatic correction due to internal vibrational motions of the molecules is also presented. In the SVRT model for a polyatom–
polyatom reaction,
10 the target (reacting) molecule T is treated by the same
SVRT model as in refs. 8 and 9, and is described by seven coordinates. The
reactant molecule R is treated as a rigid asymmetric rotor described by six
coordinates. As a result, in the SVRT model for a polyatom–polyatom reaction
we are dealing with two three-dimensional asymmetric rotors compared with
two linear rotors in the exact diatom–diatom reactive collision.
1,11 Thus it is
natural to think of the polyatom–polyatom SVRT model for a reaction as a
generalization of the exact diatom–diatom reaction model, just as the atom–
polyatom SVRT model can be considered a generalization of the exact atom–
diatom reaction model.
8,9
In the basic SVRT model, the internal structures of the molecules are ﬁxed,
although this may not be reasonable for molecules whose structure changes
from the reactant to the product. A simple remedy to overcome this rigidity
constraint without adding more degrees of freedom to the dynamical model is
to employ adiabatic correction for the neglected internal vibrational motions of
the molecules as described in ref. 9 for an atom–polyatom reaction. The implementation of this adiabatic correction for the current polyatom–polyatom
SVRT model is provided. In the following, we present a detailed mathematical
treatment of SVRT model for polyatom-polyatom reactive collision in section
14.2 and its implementation using the time-dependent (TD) wavepacket
method in section 14.3. Section 14.4 generalizes the basic SVRT model to
introduce an adiabatic SVRT model (ASVRT) to accommodate the structural
change of the molecules.
14.2 The SVRT Model for a Polyatom–Polyatom
Reaction
To avoid confusion, we use the letter T to denote the target (reacting) molecule
and its associated quantities, and the letter R to denote the reactant molecule.
338 Chapter 14
 01/12/2013 15:17:30.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00337
View Online

## Page 359

In the SVRT model for a polyatom–polyatom reaction, 10 the target molecule T
is composed of two rigid fragments B and C while the reactant molecule R is
treated as a rigid rotor as depicted in Figure 14.1. The reactive collision between
molecules T and R can be expressed as:
R þ T ! R /C0 B þ C; R /C0 C þ B ð14:1Þ
with T ¼ B–C.
14.2.1 Hamiltonian for the Target Molecule T
In the SVRT model, 8,9 the target molecule T is treated as a semi-rigid
vibrating rotor composed of two rigid parts B and C whose centers of mass
are connected by the reactive bond coordinate r as shown in Figure 14.1.
Since the only allowed relative motion between B and C is the translational
motion along the vector r, there is no vibrational angular momentum and
the quantum Hamiltonian for molecule T (without the center-of-mass kinetic
energy) is given by: 8,9

mass are connected by the reactive coordinate r which describes the
generalized vibrational motion responsible for breaking the B–C bond.
The reactant molecule R is treated as a rigid asymmetric rotor.

In eqn (14.17), the CMS motion of the collision system has been taken out and
the standard scaling of the wavefunction by 1/R is assumed.
It is important to note that the interaction potential V in eqn (14.17) depends
on seven internal coordinates. For convenience, we choose these coordinates to
be (R, r, y
T, wT, yR, wR, f). Here the angle f is deﬁned as f ¼ jT – jR where jT
and jR are, respectively, the azimuthal angles deﬁned in the Euler angles
OT ¼ (jT, yT, wT) for molecule T and OR ¼ (jR, yT, wR) for molecule R as illustrated in Figure 14.1. Thus the SVRT dynamical model for the polyatom–
polyatom reaction deﬁned by the Hamiltonian of eqn (14.17) involves seven
degrees of freedom.
It is useful to note that theoretical treatment of molecular system consisting
of two rigid rotors in six degrees-of-freedom has been presented before for
bound state problem.
12,13 In particular, benchmark six-dimensional bound
state calculations for water dimer (treated as two rigid rotors) have recently
been reported by Leforestier et al .
14a,b and Chen et al .15 These state-of-the-art
quantum dynamics calculations for the water dimer are very encouraging because they demonstrate the practicality of carrying out rigorous quantum
calculations for systems consisting of two polyatomic molecules. Many of the
mathematical equations used in these bound state calculations are quite similar
to what we employ to treat reactive scattering problems discussed below.
However, due to the inclusion of the extra bond coordinate r in the SVRT
Hamiltonian and the nature of reactive scattering problem, the mathematical
and computational aspect of the current dynamical model for the polyatom–
polyatom reaction is not trivial extension of that for bound state problem. In
the following, we present the general TD mathematical treatment of the SVRT
model for a polyatom–polyatom reaction in seven degrees of freedom.

For the target molecule T, we choose the molecular z axis to be along the r

If both T and R are linear molecules, the collision Hamiltonian of eqn (14.17)
becomes identical to the diatom-diatom Hamiltonian in ref. 11 with the replacement of mr2 by the moment of inertia I. Thus the current SVRT model for
polyatom-polyatom reaction can be viewed as a generalization of the exact
treatment for diatom-diatom reaction.
14.3 TD Wavepacket Treatment
14.3.1 Basis Set Expansion
The numerical calculation for the SVRT model Hamiltonian of eqn (14.17) for
a polyatom–polyatom reaction can be implemented using the time-dependent
(TD) wavepacket approach for reaction.
23 In the TD approach, one solves the
TD Schro¨dinger equation:

Thus the K-states of the BF total angular momentum eigenfunction are
coupled through the centrifugal potential (or orbital angular momentum) for
total angular momentum quantum number J40. Consequently, the exponential operator of eqn (14.47) couples only the K states and the coupling matrix
can be diagonalized to yield the result of exponential operation.
If the centrifugal sudden (CS) approximation is employed for total angular
momentum J40, the oﬀ-diagonal terms in eqn (14.50) are simply neglected to
yield the CS result:

which signiﬁcantly simpliﬁes the numerical computation by treating K as a
conserved quantum number. The interaction potential operator V is always
diagonal in K states because it is independent of Euler angles, which is the main
reason to employ BF angular momentum eigenfunction in mathematical
treatment.
For the rest of TD wavepacket calculation, we refer the reader to ref. 11 for
details and do not repeat them here.

## Page 368

14.4 The ASVRT Model for a Polyatom–Polyatom
Reaction
In many practical situations, the internal structure of molecule T or R changes
during the reaction process. For example, a particular bond angle may change
from an initial value in the parent molecule T to a ﬁnal value in fragment B or C.
In such cases, it may be necessary to accommodate the internal structural
change in the dynamical model in order to describe the energetics of the reaction correctly. Since the basic SVRT model for a polyatom–polyatom reaction already involves coupled equation with seven degrees of freedom, it is
not computationally prudent to introduce more coordinates into the dynamics
model explicitly. To a good approximation, we may treat the change of the
internal structure as a function of all seven coordinates adiabatically. By employing the adiabatic approximation, we avoid the explicit coupling of internal
vibrational motions to that of the scattering coordinates. For computational
reasons, however, we wish to minimize the number of coordinates that the
internal structure depends on. The simplest approach is to make the internal
structure depends adiabatically on only one coordinate which is characteristic
of the evolution of the reaction process such as the reaction coordinate s. In the
following we simply use s to denote this special coordinate which is in general a
function of all seven internal coordinates.
The exact classical Hamiltonian for a vibrating rotor (molecule T or R) can
be written:
25

given by Watson.26 In our approach, however, we simply neglect the vibrational
angular momentum Pi in eqn (14.53) to obtain simpliﬁed classical Hamiltonian:

The dynamical computation for the ASVRT model given in eqn (14.59) is
essentially identical to that for the basic SVRT model of eqn (14.17). Thus the
TD wavepacket treatment described in the previous section for the SVRT model
can be applied directly to the ASVRT model with minor modiﬁcations. The
ASVRT reaction model allows the internal vibrational motions of molecules to
adjust adiabatically to the change of the special coordinate s and thus can describe the change of internal structure of both the target and reactant molecules.
The simplest choice of s would be the reactive coordinate r. However, it is better
to choose something like s ¼ r/R which is closer to the reaction coordinate.
14.5 Conclusions
The current SVRT model for a polyatom–polyatom reaction is a generalization
of the exact diatom–diatom reaction model. It provides a general and realistic
approach for practical quantum dynamics computation of chemical reactions
involving two polyatomic molecules. Since the SVRT model for a polyatom–
polyatom reaction involves only seven mathematical degrees of freedom regardless of the number of atoms the molecules may contain, it is subject to
accurate numerical calculation. Thus the SVRT model opens the door to carry
out quantitatively accurate quantum dynamics calculations for general
polyatom–polyatom reactions that are of practical interest in chemistry and
biology. Since the SVRT model describes the rotational motion of the complex
molecule correctly, the stereo dynamics of the reaction is correctly described,
which is of fundamental importance for studying reaction dynamics involving
large complex molecules.
The improved ASVRT model gives a more realistic description than the basic
SVRT model by allowing the internal structure of the molecules to adjust to the
change of the reaction coordinate. Thus the ASVRT model gives a more accurate representation of the transition state and reaction barrier. The dynamical calculation of the ASVRT model for a polyatom–polyatom reaction also
involves seven degrees of freedom and is subject to rigorous numerical computation as well. It is useful to mention that the SVRT model has been adapted
to treat the reaction dynamics of a polyatomic molecule with a surface which
also involves seven dynamical degrees of freedom.
27

CHAPTER 15
Role of Water in Radical
Reactions: Molecular Simulation
and Modelling
DOROTA SWIATLA-WOJCIK
Institute of Applied Radiation Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, Lodz
University of Technology, Zeromskiego 116, 90-924 Lodz, Poland
Email: swiatlad@p.lodz.pl
15.1 Introduction
Atoms, molecules and ions with an open shell conﬁguration are called free
radicals, or radicals, for short. Due to the presence of unpaired electrons,
radicals are highly chemically reactive. Reactions of free radicals in aqueous
systems are extensively studied due to both scientiﬁc and engineering interest.
Important areas of application include biological systems,
1–4 green chemistry
technologies,5–8 nuclear power engineering 9,10 and atmospheric chemistry. 11,12
In the troposphere hydroxyl radicals, which are generated constantly during
the daytime, remove volatile organic compounds from the air. The /C15 OH radical
appears to be one of the most important processors of greenhouse gases, oxidizing ca. 83% of annual methane emissions. Since
/C15 OH is known to form
hydrated complexes, the presence of water molecules in the troposphere may
aﬀect atmospheric chemistry. This possible eﬀect of water on the reactivity of
/C15 OH in the troposphere has motivated many experimental and theoretical
studies on the /C15 OH–H2O dimer and higher order clusters. 13–21
RSC Theoretical and Computational Chemistry Series No. 6
Reaction Rate Constant Computations: Theories and Applications
Edited by Keli Han and Tianshu Chu
r The Royal Society of Chemistry 2014
Published by the Royal Society of Chemistry, www.rsc.org
352
 01/12/2013 15:17:39.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00352

## Page 373

In biological systems free radicals may contribute to DNA damage, mutations or degenerative disorder of cell cycle leading to cancer, stroke, Parkinson’s and Alzheimer’s disease or schizophrenia,
1,2 but they are also involved
in processes that are necessary for life such as intracellular killing of bacteria or
the cell signalling process.
4
Radical reactions in high-temperature aqueous solutions are receiving
continuously increasing attention due to a number of industrial and environmental applications, including advanced oxidation processes (AOPs),
materials synthesis, waste destruction, wastewater treatment and biomass
processing.
5–8
In electric power generation, however, radical-driven corrosion poses severe
problems for material integrity. In water-cooled nuclear power reactors, exposure of the coolant to fast neutrons and gamma rays generates transient
primary radicals: the hydrated electrons (e
aq
/C0 ),/C15 OH and H/C15 . Due to secondary
reactions these species are converted into secondary radicals, such as perhydroxyl radical HO
2, oxide and superoxide radical anions, and ﬁnally to stable
molecular products, H 2, and corrosive H 2O2 and O2.22 The stability and safety
of light water nuclear power reactors require controlling radiation-generated
radical chemistry in compressed water at about 573 K.
The unique property of the water substance is that each of the H
2O molecules
is able to accept two hydrogen bonds and, at the same time, to donate two
hydrogen bonds. Because of this, water appears as a highly associated solvent
with a consequent eﬀect on the rates and mechanisms of chemical reactions.
The application areas outlined above require an understanding of the role of
water under conditions ranging from near ambient, which are of interest for
biology, biochemistry and medicine, to near critical and supercritical, important for green chemistry technologies and nuclear power engineering.
In the ﬁrst part of this chapter we provide an overview of the physical and
solvent properties of water that may inﬂuence the mechanism and kinetics of
radical reactions. In many applications the role of hydrogen bonding is often
ignored because of experimental diﬃculties in characterising the aqueous solution on a microscopic level. Computational tools are less limited. Comparative molecular simulations have proved to be useful for the generation of a
comprehensive view of solvent structure and dynamics depending on the conditions of temperature and pressure. In the second part of this chapter the
methodology used in simulations to analyse hydrogen bonding is presented.
Then we demonstrate how insight into the hydrogen bonding may improve our
understanding of the free radical behaviour, providing a guide for experiment
and modelling kinetics of radical reactions in water. In the last part we focus on
the Noyes relationship and its application to the assessment of rate constants at
temperatures that are important for green chemistry technologies and nuclear
power engineering. Use of the Noyes equation is advantageous because one can
explicitly model solvent eﬀects on diﬀusion and chemical transformation of
reactants. We conclude with a reference to reactions involving water as a
reactant. These reactions may signiﬁcantly aﬀect the chemistry of aqueous
systems at high temperatures.

15.2 Physical and Solvent Properties of Water
The physical and solvent properties of water depend strongly on temperature
and pressure.7,8,23,24 Near the critical point ( Tc¼ 647.1 K, Pc¼ 22.06 MPa), the
isothermal compressibility of water may be 10 4 times higher than that of the
saturated liquid at 25 1C, and isobaric speciﬁc heat capacity may increase to
1/C2 103 kJ K/C0 1 kg/C0 1 whereas thermal conductivity can be as high as 0.8 W m/C0 1 K/C0 1.
The transport properties of hot compressed water fall between those of a gas
and a liquid. At densities of ca. 700 kg m
/C0 3 and lower, the diﬀusion coeﬃcient
D is proportional to the inverse of the density, like in gases. 25,26 A change in
transport properties has consequent eﬀect on radical reactions, which are
diﬀusion-controlled or partially diﬀusion-controlled (see section 15.4).
By setting the appropriate pressure and temperature conditions one can tune
solvation properties to aﬀect reaction rates and chemical equilibria. For example, as illustrated in Figure 15.1, by changing state parameters one can
reduce the static dielectric constant of water to a value characteristic for lowpolar solvents. Supercritical water (SW) may substitute toxic organic solvents,
such as acetone ( e
25¼ 20.7) or benzene ( e25¼ 2.3). In contrast to ambient water,
supercritical ﬂuid is a poor solvent for ionic species but is well miscible with
hydrocarbons and gases.
Another quantity that is likely to aﬀect the kinetics and mechanisms of
chemical reactions is the ionic product, Kw. The eﬀect of temperature and
Figure 15.1 Static dielectric constant of water as function of temperature calculated
for the isobar P¼ 25 MPa using the empirical equation proposed to
correlate the measured values with temperature and density of water. 27
Inset shows ambient values of the static dielectric constant of selected
organic solvents.

 01/12/2013 15:17:39.

## Page 375

pressure on Kw is even more pronounced. Using the empirical equation proposed Marshall and Franck 28 one can calculate p Kw¼ –logKw as a function of
temperature and pressure. Exemplary dependencies, depicted in Figure 15.2,
show that the ionic product of subcritical water or dense supercritical ﬂuid is a
few orders of magnitude higher than ambient water ( Kw¼ 10/C0 14). A high
concentration of H 3O1 and OH/C0 ions facilitates acid- and base-catalysed reactions, and favours ionic mechanisms. However, Kw for supercritical water at
low density can be tens of orders of magnitude lower than for ambient water.
Under these conditions the radical mechanism is favoured.
29
It is generally accepted that many macroscopic properties of water are determined by hydrogen bonding.30–32 Thus it has been argued that an alternation
in the character of water as a solvent with increasing temperature and decreasing density is the consequence of the structural transformations in the
hydrogen-bonded network.
6,7,26,33–35 These transformations also inﬂuence the
hydration and diﬀusion of solutes. Due to the central role hydrogen bonding
plays in aqueous systems it is a key to understanding the role of water as both a
participant and medium where radical reactions occur.
15.3 Hydrogen Bonds in Water Solvent
Hydrogen bonding in liquid water continuously attracts attention of both
theoreticians and experimentalists. Hydrogen, or H-bonds, are directional.
With the enthalpy of formation of –21 kJ mol
/C0 1 for the water dimer31 exceeding
Figure 15.2 Negative logarithm of the ionic product of water calculated as a function
of temperature and pressure following the empirical formula proposed
by Marshall and Franck. 28
Role of Water in Radical Reactions: Molecular Simulation and Modelling 355
 01/12/2013 15:17:39.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00352
View Online

## Page 376

the energy of thermal ﬂuctuations, 2.5 kJ mol /C0 1 at 298 K, they are stable
enough to establish a well-developed H-bond network under ambient conditions. The existence of a three-dimensional network with tetrahedral coordination of molecules has been conﬁrmed by X-ray and neutron scattering
studies, and numerous simulations.
30,33,34
The present view is that ambient water shows a gel-like structure, where
rotations cause individual hydrogen bonds to break and re-form in new conﬁgurations on a timescale of picoseconds.
36 Stanley and co-workers suggested
that this gel-like structure is far from being homogenous because of the
co-existence of two phases, a low-density ice-like and a more dense liquid phase
with distorted tetrahedral arrangement of the molecules.
37 This structural inhomogeneity has been observed in small-angle X-ray scattering (SAXS) and
small-angle neutron scattering (SANS) experiments. SAXS and SANS techniques provide the most direct probes of density ﬂuctuations on a large scale. These
experiments provided evidence of the presence of density ﬂuctuations existing in
ambient water on a length-scale of 1 nm.
38 Molecular dynamics (MD) simulation
studies of the connectivity of water molecules via H-bonds26,33,39 support the
view that, in agreement with early continuum models, 30,40,41 ambient water appears as a space-ﬁlling network of molecules held together by H-bonds.
The connectivity shows how a local hydrogen bonding interaction leads to
global macroscopic network, but is not suﬃcient to give information about the
local density ﬂuctuations. To deal with this problem Blumberg and co-workers
used MD simulation to analyse the connectivity of four-bonded molecules. 39
They found supramolecular structures formed by continuously connected fourbonded molecules. These more ordered and less dense structures, called patches, are embedded in the continues H-bond network of ambient water.
26,39 To
demonstrate a diﬀerence between H-bond net and H-bond patch we compare
the net of size k
net¼ 2 and the patch of size k4¼ 2. The former is equivalent to
H-bond dimer, whereas the later represents a collection of H-bond structures
made up of 8–20 molecules, two of which are four-bonded. An important
consequence of the existence of the H-bond network, although not yet fully
understood, is the fast transfer of protons, hydroxyl ions and hydrogen atoms.
Diﬀusion coeﬃcients of these species at 298 K, 9.3 /C2 10
/C0 9,5 . 3/C2 10/C0 9 and
7/C2 10/C0 9 m2s/C0 1, respectively, 42,43 are signiﬁcantly higher than the self-diﬀusion
coeﬃcient of H 2O molecules of 2.3/C2 10/C0 9 m2 s/C0 1.44
The high mobility and solvation of H 3O1 and OH- in liquid water have been
the subject of intense research and debate. 45 Models explaining the high mobility of these ionic species are intimately connected with their solvation in liquid water and the surrounding H-bond pattern. A piece of experimental work
conﬁrming the role of H-bond network and, at the same time, indicating
structural transformations in the H-bond network with increasing temperature
is illustrated in Figure 15.3. The D-values for the hydrated proton and OH
/C0
ion, calculated from the limiting conductances, 42 are compared with the selfdiﬀusion coeﬃcient of water. 44,46 Both ions diﬀuse faster than water molecules
at ambient and elevated temperatures but approach the self-diﬀusion rates near
623 K (350 1C).

 01/12/2013 15:17:39.

View Online

## Page 377

The introduction of temperature as a variable provides a critical test for our
understanding of the role of hydrogen bonding interactions in aqueous media.
Knowledge of the eﬀect of temperature and density on the formation of
supramolecular structures, the collective behaviour of molecules and H-bond
connectivity patterns is a prerequisite for development of applications of hot
water indicated in section 15.1. With respect to experiment, theoretical methods
including computer simulation and quantum chemical calculations play an
equal—and sometimes pivotal role—in the quantitative characterization of Hbond complexes. In a molecular simulation most of relevant structural, kinetic
and thermodynamic observables can be computed at one time providing a link
with macroscopic properties.
47,48 In classical simulations use of ﬂexible models,
which better reproduce distortions and persistence of the H-bond network, is
better for systems at elevated or high temperature.
49 Classical MD and Monte
Carlo (MC) simulations are computationally much less demanding than
density functional theory (DFT) based ab initio molecular dynamics (AIMD)
50
or quantum treatments of electronic structure. 51,52 AIMD adopts the main
techniques from classical simulations but avoids limitations connected with
empirical potentials. In AIMD, often referred to as Car–Parrinello molecular
dynamics (CPMD), electronic degrees of freedom are introduced as dynamical
variables in a set of coupled equations of motion and forces are evaluated from
electronic structure calculations for ﬁxed nuclei, usually employing DFT. Since
the time required for DFT calculations nearly scales as cube of the number of
basis functions or plane waves per atom, a reasonable system size and the
timescale of ﬁrst generation CPMD applications have been limited to the
Figure 15.3 Temperature dependences for the diﬀusion coeﬃcient of H 3O1 (J) and
OH/C0 (K) in water, calculated from limiting equivalent conductances, 42
compared with the self-diﬀusion coeﬃcient of water ( n).44,45

Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00352

## Page 378

simulation of about 100 atoms over a period of 10 ps. The numerical approximations to extend the attainable size and length scales of the simulation
showed signiﬁcant dependence on numerical details.
53–55 The second generation of CPMD removes these drawbacks, enabling longer runs ( B250 ps) on
larger systems (128 molecules). 56,57
15.3.1 Deﬁnitions of H-bond in Molecular Simulations
To proceed with hydrogen bonding in a molecular simulation one must deﬁne
the H-bond. Deﬁnitions based on potential energy,
41,58–60 geometry,61–64 electronic charge density, 65–67 electrostatic potential 68,69 or topological relations70–72 have been used to decide whether water molecules are H-bonded.
A conventional procedure is to assume cut-oﬀ values for an interaction energy
or for selected molecular site–site separations. A threshold energy is often assumed according to the statistical distribution of a pair interaction energy.
As depicted in Figure 15.4, the energy distribution function calculated for
liquid water shows a low-energy part with a maximum at ca. –19 kJ mol
/C0 1.
Commonly used energy cut-oﬀs for H-bonds in water, /C0 10 or /C0 8 kJ mol/C0 1,
correspond to a broad minimum of the distribution function. The two molecules are regarded as H-bonded if the pair interaction energy is more negative
than the chosen cut-oﬀ.
More convenient in applications are conventional geometric deﬁnitions.
These are based on distance–angle relations between H-donor and H-acceptor
molecules. The angles and distances usually involved
61–64 are illustrated in
Figure 15.4 Normalized statistical distribution of a pair interaction energy calculated
from MD simulation of ambient water. 64
358 Chapter 15
 01/12/2013 15:17:39.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00352
View Online

## Page 379

Figure 15.5. The simplest geometrical criterion for the H-bond controls either
the ROO or RHO distances, and the respective radial distribution functions are
used to assess the allowable length of the H-bond. Commonly the position of
the ﬁrst minimum is assumed. More complex geometric deﬁnitions include
additional terms describing the deviation of the H-bond from linearity.
Energetic and geometric deﬁnitions seem to be consistent since the potential
energy of H-bonded molecules depends on geometric parameters. However,
they are not equivalent because of ﬁxed cut-oﬀ parameters, which are assumed
more or less arbitrarily. A certain amount of pairs identiﬁed as H-bonded by
geometric deﬁnition show interaction energies exceeding the energy cut-oﬀ.
64
The number of molecular pairs showing weak attraction or even repulsive
interaction increases for the subcritical and supercritical water.
64,73 However,
the energetic deﬁnition may identify as H-bonded pairs having the wrong
relative orientation. A fundamental feature that diﬀerentiates the H-bond from
van der Waals interaction is its directionality. The average H-bond in ice
measured by X-ray diﬀraction is linear within 15 1,
31 whereas neutron scattering
experiments suggest disruption of the H-bond in liquid water if a deviation
from the linearity exceeds 30 1.
74
Figure 15.5 presents the distribution of the H-bond orientation for pairs
satisfying the energetic criterion Er–8 kJ mol/C0 1. The contribution of pairs with
improper orientation of the H-donating molecule is noticeable even if the

## Page 380

hybrid, energy-distance criterion is used. In comparison with the three-term
deﬁnition, which controls the interaction energy, ROH distance and the H-bond
directionality simple geometric and energetic deﬁnitions overestimate the
number of H-bonds, their connectivity and lifetime. An overestimation of the
degree of H-bonding occurs to be substantial for subcritical and supercritical
water,
64,73 whereas more signiﬁcant diﬀerences in the dynamics of hydrogen
bonds occur for ambient conditions. 64
15.3.2 H-Bonds from Ambient to Supercritical Conditions
Application of hot compressed water in environmentally friendly technologies
has inspired intense fundamental research to understand the eﬀect of temperature and density on H-bonding. In contrast to much stronger ionic or
covalent bonds, H-bonds are inﬂuenced by temperature and pressure/density.
As water is heated, the average number of H-bonds per water molecule decreases. For example, at 573 K and 720 kg m
/C0 3, individual water molecules
retain about 50% of the H-bonds established under ambient conditions
(Figure 15.6), whereas at 673 K and 167 kg m
/C0 3, less than 15% of these bonds is
retained.
Although the trend that H-bonding in water becomes weaker with increasing
temperature and decreasing density has been determined with a variety of experimental and computational techniques, thermodynamic conditions for the
Figure 15.6 Histograms for the number of H-bonds established by water molecules at
temperatures and densities speciﬁed in the ﬁgure. Determined on the
basis of MD simulations. 26
360 Chapter 15
 01/12/2013 15:17:39.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00352
View Online

## Page 381

persistence of H-bonds in supercritical water have been the subject of discussion.33–35 Finally, a presence of H-bonds in water has been evidenced at
supercritical temperatures as high as 800 K and densities as low as
100 kg m
/C0 3.7,33,35
Ultrafast infrared (IR) spectroscopy measurements for 278–345 K suggest
that the H-bond rearrangement in water is collective. 75 According to MD
simulations, breakage of the inﬁnite H-bond network into a variety of
H-bonded clusters can be expected for the temperature 573 K and density
B700 kg m
/C0 3.26,33 Under these conditions the average number of H-bonds per
molecule coincides with the percolation threshold predicted by the random
bond theory.
76,77 Above the percolation threshold, the H-bond network exists
in the form of separated nets, whose size decreases with increasing temperature
and decreasing density.
26,33,78
Breakage of the continuous gel-like H-bond network is associated with the
diminution of patches. 26 A picture of patches embedded into the continuous
H-bond network is consistent with two structurally diﬀerent H-bonding
environments that interconvert on a temperature change.
79 This ﬂuctuating
inhomogeneity may explain the anomalous behaviour of isothermal compressibility and heat capacity of water compared with typical liquids. The
diminution of patches coincides with broad minimum shown by p K
w
(Figure 15.2). It can be argued that decay of less dense patches increases a
local density of water, shifting Kw to higher values.
15.3.3 H-Bonding Eﬀect on Solutes
Alteration of the degree of H-bonding and structural transformations occurring in the H-bond network upon heating aﬀects the solvation properties of
water. Depending on the temperature and pressure conditions, water can either
retard or accelerate the progress of chemical reactions.
35 Through stabilization
of reactants, transition states and products, solvation may change the activation energy in the chemical step or lead to reaction selectivity.
80 Reactions
between reactants that are less polar than the formed transition state are facilitated by increasing dielectric constant. However, better stabilization of
reactants due to increasing polarity or via hydrogen bonding will decrease the
reaction rate.
If solute–water and water–water interactions are comparable, diminution of
the extended H-bonding network may change the behaviour of hydrated radicals. Understanding the H-bonding contribution to solvation is particularly
important for the hydroxyl radical because of its role in biological, chemical
and environmental systems (section 15.1). Experimental probing of the structural properties of
/C15 OH in aqueous media is still challenging because of its high
reactivity and short lifetime. Computational methods are much less limited,
providing a direct insight not only into the structure and dynamics of hydration
shells, but also into energetic and transport properties. Simulations performed
Role of Water in Radical Reactions: Molecular Simulation and Modelling 361
 01/12/2013 15:17:39.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00352
View Online

## Page 382

for/C15 OH in aqueous media involved MC, MD and CPMD methods and mostly
concerned ambient or near ambient conditions. 80–88 Only a few studies have
focused on high-temperature systems. 89–93
Credibility of simulation depends on potentials employed to describe
interactions in the molecular system. Flexible models for solute and solvent
molecules, although computationally more demanding, are more adequate for
the study of solvation at high temperatures. 89,93 Flexible models, the Bopp–
Jancso–Heinzinger (BJH) model for the water molecule and the compatible
DFT-derived model for
/C15 OH,87 have been recently used for comparative MD
simulation studies of solvated /C15 OH under ambient, elevated and supercritical
temperatures.93 These simulations showed that the behaviour of /C15 OH in diluted
aqueous solution depends strongly on density and correlates with the topological transformations in the H-bond network. Figures 15.7 and 15.8 show that,
although the computed hydration number and the internal energy of hydration
D
hU decrease linearly with decreasing density r, a degree of radical–water
H-bonding exhibits a non-monotonic dependence on r.
At near ambient temperatures /C15 OH establishes mostly one H-donor bond.
The increasing contribution of H-acceptor bonds (Figure 15.9) is driven by
the connectivity of solvent molecules and coincides with diminution of
patches.

that size of solvation shell is not se nsitive to the H-bond connectivity

w a t e ra sw e l la sf o rt h eh y d r a t e dr a d i c a lin the supercritical ﬂuid at densities
higher than the critical density (the reduced density 41). The cavities
established by H-bonded water molecules can hinder the diﬀusion of
solutes. A neutral radical may experience backward scattering, connected with
the presence of H-bond network. Results of MD simulations suggest similar
behaviour of the self-diﬀusion coeﬃcients of water and
/C15 O Hr a d i c a lu pt ot h e
percolation threshold.87,91 Under supercritical conditions the eﬀect of H-bond
clusters strongly depends on density. A concept of a solvent cage around the
reactants is often invoked to explain the eﬀect of density on rates of dissociation
or decomposition at supercritical temperatures. For example, the rate constant for
H
2O2 dissociation decreases with the increasing density in dense supercritical
water because the two /C15 OH radicals recombine within a cage regenerating
H2O2.89 The opposite trend observed for the reduced density o1s u g g e s t st h e
disappearance of the solvent cage eﬀect under these conditions. Below the critical
density the structure of supercritical water is highly non-homogenous, demonstrating the coexistence of empty regions and molecular aggregates in the form of
branched H-bonded chains.
26,73,78 MD simulation of /C15 OHaq has demonstrated
that, in the presence of/C15 OH, the structural inhomogeneity is noticeably reduced.93
An increase of the gOrOw peak, seen in Figure 15.10 for supercritical water below
the critical density, indicates an accumulation of H 2O molecules around
the radical. This feature and associated strong H-donor bonding interaction
have been prescribed to the radical self-trapping in low-density supercritical
water.

## Page 385

Numerous computational tools provided by molecular simulations enable the
determination of the structure and lifetime of solvation shells, an insight into the
translations and vibrations of molecules, evaluation of activation volumes and
assessment of thermodynamic properties of the reactants. These data are required
to properly choose the input parameters for kinetic models describing the eﬀects
of density and temperature on reaction rate constants in aqueous solutions.
15.4 Applications
The application of high-temperature water in green chemistry technologies and
the electric power industry initiated experimental and computational studies designed to understand the eﬀect of temperature on the rate of chemical reactions.
The governing mechanism for supercritical water oxidation (SCWO) technology, hydrothermal combustion or wet oxidation biomass processing is the
formation of highly reactive radical intermediates.
5–8 The formation and destruction of oxidants contribute signiﬁcantly to the overall kinetics during
SCWO of organic compounds.
94–97 Understanding the kinetic mechanism is a
key component in designing, controlling and optimizing SCWO reactors.
Conversely, the formation of oxidising agents, so desirable in AOP technology, poses serious problems in nuclear power engineering. Mutual reactions
of radiation-generated short-living radicals result in corrosive environments.
The austenitic steels and nickel alloys used in the currently operating light water
reactors (LWRs) are susceptible to intergranular stress corrosion cracking
(IGSCC).
10 To maintain the integrity of the materials it is necessary to select
conditions under which the radiolytic decomposition of the coolant is suppressed. Pulse radiolysis measurements combined with modelling have provided information on the kinetics of the principal reactions involving radical
intermediates in aqueous solutions.
10,22,98
In order to estimate high-temperature rate constants, it is necessary to establish the kinetic behaviour of reactions over as wide a temperature range as
possible. Modelling is very important to show what physics or chemistry is
missing, to test experimental uncertainties, and to provide a view outside the
window of experimental observation. Alteration of the physical and chemical
properties of water upon heating (see section 15.2) makes kinetic analysis of
reacting systems rather complex. A convenient method for extrapolation of the
kinetic data is provided by the Noyes relationship.
99
15.4.1 Modelling Rate Constants by the Noyes Relationship
A reaction occurring in the condensed phase requires three steps to be completed: (i) diﬀusion of the reactants to encounter each other; (ii) the chemical
transformation into products; and (iii) diﬀusion of the products from the reaction volume to avoid restoration of the reactants. Involving two of these
consecutive steps, the Noyes eqn (15.1) expresses the reciprocal observed rate
constant k by the sum of the reciprocal diﬀusion-controlled rate constant k
d
and the reciprocal rate constant for the chemical step kr:
1
k¼ 1
kd
þ 1
kr
ð15:1Þ

## Page 386

If kd{kr the reaction is diﬀusion controlled. This means the rate limiting step is
diﬀusion of the reactants towards each other to the encounter distance, RAB,a t
which they react. The diﬀusion controlled rate constant for a bimolecular reaction, Aþ B - Products, takes the form of the Smoluchowski eqn (15.2):
k
d¼ 4pN/C1ð DAþ DBÞ/C1ð RAþ RBÞ/C1 bspin ð15:2Þ
where N is Avogadro’s number, DA and DB, are diﬀusion coeﬃcients, the encounter distance RAB is represented by the sum of reaction radii of the reactants, and bspin is the spin factor.
Estimation of reaction radii is diﬃcult and various approaches are used. For
addition and abstraction reactions of H /C15 and/C15 OH involving molecules, 0.19
and 0.22 nm values derived from gas viscosity data have been assumed. 100–102
For ions, the crystallographic radius is usually used. Reaction radii are also
calculated from interatomic distances and the van der Waals radii of the external atoms or assumed on the basis RDF of hydrated species (section 15.3.3).
For a reaction between two radicals, modiﬁcation of the Smoluchowski
equation by the spin factor may be required. Diﬀusion-controlled encounters
between two radicals can produce either a reactive singlet or non-reactive triplet
state. The factor b
spin in eqn (15.2) accounts for the fraction of encounters that
lead to a singlet state. Statistically, the singlet state is produced in a quarter of
encounters. However, reactants that do not react at their ﬁrst encounter have a
ﬁnite probability to re-encounter before diﬀusing out of the reaction volume.
Spin relaxation of a triplet pair to the singlet state occurring within a reencounter time makes b
spin40.25. In the limiting case, bspin¼ 1 can be assumed
for radicals having short spin-lattice relaxation time.
An implicit assumption that the reaction radii and the spin factor do not
change with temperature is usually made. This assumption implies that a
change of kd with temperature is mainly due to DA and DB. An approximate
dependence for the diﬀusion coeﬃcient D in liquids results from the Stokes–
Einstein eqn (15.3):
D¼ kBT
a/C1 p/C1 Z/C1 rh
ð15:3Þ
where kB is the Boltzmann constant, T is the absolute temperature, Z is the
solvent viscosity, and rh is the hydrodynamic radius of the diﬀusing species. The
coeﬃcient a varies from 4, if rh is comparable with the size of the solvent
molecule, to 6, if the diﬀusing entity is much larger. The hydrodynamic radius
of a solute depends on solvation and can be assessed by molecular simulation of
a diluted solution (section 15.3.3). In the absence of relevant data, D can be
scaled with the solvent temperature T by eqn (15.4), which results from eqn
(15.3) if one assumes that r

Parameters that simulate the kinetic data for radical reactions are listed in
Table 15.1. Two exemplary ﬁts are presented in Figures 15.12 and 15.13.
The self-reaction of the hydrogen radical, H /C15 þ H/C15 - H2, is supposed to be
truly diﬀusion-controlled. The accepted reaction radius of 0.19 nm (Table 15.1)
is, by a factor of 3.5, greater than the hydrodynamic radius rh obtained from
eqn (15.3) for DH(25)¼ 7/C2 10/C0 9 m2 s/C0 1. The fast diﬀusion of H /C15 and the long
time of spin relaxation, B1.3/C2 10/C0 5 s, make spin relaxation before diﬀusing out
of the encounter volume rather improbable. Figure 15.12 shows that the kinetic
data are well reproduced by the Smoluchowski equation assuming the temperature dependence for D
H from eqn (15.5) and the spin factor bspin¼ 0.25.
Figure 15.13 presents plots for the reaction between the hydrogen and hydroxyl radicals. For the radical–radical reactions involving/C15 OH, the spin factor
equal to unity can be assumed because of an abnormally fast spin relaxation of
/C15 OH, occurring within 1 ns. 104 At room temperature the reaction H/C15 þ/C15 OH -
H2O, like most of the radical–radical reactions and some of the radical–
molecule reactions, occurs at rates limited by diﬀusion. 105 However, one cannot
assume that these reactions remain diﬀusion-controlled at high temperatures.
As illustrated in Figure 15.13, if diﬀusion coeﬃcients of the reactants increase
with temperature faster than k
r, the reaction rate can be limited by the chemical
step.

## Page 391

that kr increases more slowly with temperature than kd and, at elevated temperatures, the overall rate of these reactions is limited by kr. The revealed
activationless behaviour of kr is consistent with the localization of /C15 OH in
cavities existing in the H-bond network (see section 15.3.3). Being localized in a
cavity the radical is loosely bound to the surrounding water molecules, establishing mostly one short lived H-bond. This suggests that the reaction potential
surface does not present a barrier to the motion along the reaction coordinate.

reactions and has been invoked to explain the non-linear Arrhenius behaviour
observed for reactions of

Applying the Noyes equation for near and supercritical conditions one
should account for a possible alteration of reaction and hydrodynamic radii, a
spin factor, activation energy, and transmission coeﬃcient, needed to evaluate

estimated by means of molecular simulations.
15.4.2 Radical Reactions Involving Water as a Reactant
The radical reactions in which water participates as one of the reactant often
lead to the formation of highly reactive radical intermediates. These reactions
are usually written in the form of equilibria, assuming that forward and
backward steps require the same transition state. Examples are given below.
H
2Oþ H/C15 Ð/C15 OHþ H2 ð15:19Þ
H2Oþ O/C15 Ð/C15 OHþ/C15 OH ð15:20Þ
H2Oþ HO/C15
2Ð/C15 OHþ H2O2 ð15:21Þ
Reactions with water involve the energy of activation much higher than that
required for diﬀusion. They are too slow to be observed at room temperature,
but may signiﬁcantly contribute to the overall oxidation kinetics at nuclear
reactor and SCWO operating temperatures. Reactions (15.19) to (15.21) have
been invoked as a partial explanation for the experimentally observed water
density eﬀects on SCWO reaction rates.
108,109 The importance of reaction

## Page 393

(15.19) in so-called ‘hydrogen water chemistry’ in nuclear reactors, where H 2 is
added to the coolant to suppress radiolysis, has been also indicated. 110
There are no direct measurements of the rates of reactions (15.19) to (15.21)
in the condensed phase. The rate constant k19¼ 10/C6 2M/C0 1 s/C0 1 has been derived for reaction (15.19) from a photolysis study of H 2O2 solutions at ambient
temperature.111 This estimate is six orders of magnitude higher than
k19¼ 7.2/C2 10/C0 5 M/C0 1 s/C0 1,112 calculated from the thermodynamic data assuming
equilibrium (15.19) and taking k-19¼ 4.3/C2 107 M/C0 1 s/C0 1 measured directly. 113
Such a large discrepancy may indicate that either the photolysis estimate is
incorrect or that the assumption on the equilibrium does not hold, suggesting
that the forward or backward reaction involves intermediates.
For near and supercritical conditions, combustion gas-phase data are often
used as the point of reference to assess solvent eﬀects. The gas-phase values of
k
19, available for temperatures 800–2500 K, show the activation energy B90 kJ
mol/C0 1.114 In condensed phase, stabilization of H 2O molecules via H-bonding
may increase the activation barrier, but on the other hand the reaction can be
promoted by the solvent cage eﬀect. Diﬀusion-kinetic modelling
115 and stochastic simulation116 of chemical reactions in radiation tracks have shown that
the occurrence of reaction (15.19) is consistent with the anomalous increase in
H
2 yield observed in water radiolysis at temperatures above 523 K, if k19 is of
the order of 1–2 /C2 104 M/C0 1 s/C0 1 (4–8/C2 105 s/C0 1) at 573 K. Considering the two
estimates for ambient temperature, this result may suggest B40 or 100 kJ mol/C0 1
for the activation energy in liquid water. Since the barrier height can be sensitive to the degree of water H-bonding, the activation energy in supercritical
water may depend on density and temperature.
The radical reactions with water should appear in chemical kinetic models
to assure a correct analysis of kinetic data for processes in hot compressed
water. The example of reaction (15.19) shows that the high temperature
rate constants reported from pulse radiolysis measurements may require reevaluation.
15.5 Conclusions
The role of water in radical reactions is largely determined by the hydrogenbonding interactions. Because radical reactions can be controlled by the
chemical step or by diﬀusion, understanding the H-bonding eﬀect on the reaction rates in aqueous solutions of importance for green chemistry technologies and nuclear power engineering is rather complex. The Noyes
equation combined with molecular simulations provides a computational tool
to replace empirical expressions by a more comprehensive and general
description.
Theoretical and experimental studies are needed to specify the kinetics of
radical reactions involving H
2O molecules. These reactions, involving high
activation energy, are insigniﬁcant at ambient and elevated temperatures, but
can contribute signiﬁcantly to the chemistry in near and supercritical water.

CHAPTER 16
Molecular Modelling of Proton
Transfer Kinetics in Biological
Systems
PATRICK BERTRAND
Bioe´nerge´tique et Inge´nierie des Prote´ines, UMR 7281 CNRS-Universite ´Aix
Marseille, 31, Chemin Joseph Aiguier, 13402 Marseille Cedex 20, France
Email: bertrand@imm.cnrs.fr
16.1 Introduction
Long-range proton transfers play an essential role in biological cells, particularly in enzymes and bioenergetic systems. The determination of the threedimensional structure of a number of physiologically important proteins and
the impressive advances of simulation techniques have stimulated over about
20 years a number of theoretical studies devoted to biological proton transfers.
Simulations based on current realistic models provide eﬃcient tools for
studying their mechanism at the molecular level, so this chapter may interest
theoreticians as well as biochemists and biophysicists working on enzymatic
and bioenergetic processes.
In this chapter, we ﬁrst introduce biological proton transfers and then present
the main concepts used in theoretical models. The power of these models is
exempliﬁed by studies carried out on relatively simple and experimentally well
documented systems: a channel spanning the membrane (gramicidin); a nonredox enzyme (carbonic anhydrase); and a proton pump driven by light
RSC Theoretical and Computational Chemistry Series No. 6
Reaction Rate Constant Computations: Theories and Applications
Edited by Keli Han and Tianshu Chu
r The Royal Society of Chemistry 2014
Published by the Royal Society of Chemistry, www.rsc.org
379
 01/12/2013 15:17:49.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00379

## Page 400

(bacteriorhodopsin). Theoretical studies of the much more complex respiratory
and photosynthetic systems are just beginning and only an introduction is given.
16.2 Biological Proton Transfers
Biological proton transfers take place in proteins. These are either soluble
enzymes or membrane-bound protein complexes, whose function requires
proton exchanges with the solvent. Protons cannot diﬀuse freely in the protein
medium but fast proton transfers, compatible with the millisecond timescale of
most biological processes, can occur along chains of well-deﬁned protonatable
groups. These groups are either water molecules or ionisable residues like Asp,
Glu, Ser and His. Their proton aﬃnity is quantiﬁed by their p K
a
* whose value
may diﬀer from that measured in water.
16.2.1 Biological Systems Involving Proton Transfers
16.2.1.1 Soluble Enzymes
Nearly all chemical reactions that take place in living cells are selectively catalysed
by enzymes. The free-energy change of the reaction drives a sequence of enzyme
modiﬁcations (the catalytic cycle) at the end of which the initial state is recovered.
During catalysis, the substrate diﬀuses toward the active site where it interacts with
speciﬁc groups, which facilitates the breaking and forming of chemical bonds. This
process very often produces or consumes protons, which must be exchanged with
the solvent through proton transfer chains so that the initial state of the active site
is restored. When the catalysed reactio n is an oxidoreduction, electrons are exchanged with the physiological partner through a chain of redox centres.
16.2.1.2 Membrane-Bound Bioenergetic Complexes
Living organisms use various energy sources to satisfy their energy requirements, but the mechanism whereby this energy is converted into a usable form
in the cell is universal: the proton concentration gradient generated by the
translocation of protons across a membrane is used for adenosine triphosphate
(ATP) synthesis. Since translocation takes place against an electrochemical
gradient, it must be driven by another process:
1
– In some archaebacteria, the driving force is provided by conformational
changes induced by light energy. The most studied system is a protein called
bacteriorhodopsin, in which photon absorption by a chromophore called
retinal leads to a series of structural changes which drive proton transfers.
– In many microorganisms and in the mitochondrion of eukaryotic cells, the
driving force is provided by electron transfers generated by the oxidation
of a substrate in a process called respiration. For example, the mitochondrial respiratory system is made of four membrane-bound complexes
*pKa is a standard notation for – log 10 (Ka) is the acid dissociation constant.
380 Chapter 16
 01/12/2013 15:17:49.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00379
View Online

## Page 401

which operate sequentially. Electrons injected in complexes I and II in the
course of the Krebs cycle are used to reduce membrane quinones. The
quinones reduce complex III ( bc
1 complex) which reduces in turn a soluble
protein, cytochrome c, and electrons are ﬁnally transmitted to complex IV
(cytochrome c oxidase) where the terminal electron acceptor O 2 is reduced
into two H 2O molecules. Electron transfers are coupled to proton translocation in complexes I, III and IV.
– In plants, algae and a number of bacteria, light energy is collected and
conveyed to membrane-bound complexes called ‘reaction centres’ which
contain a pigment and redox centres. Upon absorption of a photon, the
pigment is excited into a strongly reducing state, which triggers a sequence
of electron transfers, some of which are coupled to proton translocation.
The proton concentration gradient produced by all these processes is used in
F
0.F1 ATP synthase complexes. The proton ﬂow generated by the proton motive
force rotates the F 0 subunit, which drives ATP synthesis in the F 1 subunit.2
16.2.2 Kinetic Data
16.2.2.1 Rate Constant
Kinetic information about biological proton transfers can be obtained only in
favourable cases. In some systems, it is possible to trigger the transfer and
monitor the time evolution of the protonation state of peculiar groups with
time-resolved spectroscopic techniques such as ultraviolet–visible (UV-vis)
absorption and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR). In enzymes, rate constants
can sometimes be deduced from the catalytic constant (or turnover number) k
cat
measured in steady-state conditions. This parameter, expressed in s /C0 1,i st h e
maximum number of catalytic cycles per second that is achieved when the
concentration of all reactants is saturating. When a proton transfer step is
known to be rate-limiting, k
cat is equated to the rate constant of this step. This is
expected when kcat varies signiﬁcantly when this step is converted into a deuteron
transfer step, either by deuterating the substrate or by measuring kcat in D 2O.
16.2.2.2 Temperature Dependence
Biological proton transfers models are generally based on the expression of the
rate constant given by the classical formulation of transition state theory (TST):3
kTST ¼ k kBT=hðÞ expð/C0 DGz=RTÞð 16:1Þ
kB is the Boltzmann constant, h is the Planck constant, R is the gas constant
and T the absolute temperature. The transmission coeﬃcient k accounts for
recrossing eﬀects. The exponential factor is strongly dependent on the activation free-energy DG
z.y With k ¼ 1 and T ¼ 300 K, kTST is about 2 /C2 106 s/C0 1 for
yThroughout this chapter, kcal mol /C0 1 is used as the energy unit. 1 kcal mol /C0 1 ¼ 4.18 kJ mol /C0 1.
Molecular Modelling of Proton Transfer Kinetics in Biological Systems 381
 01/12/2013 15:17:49.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00379
View Online

## Page 402

DGz ¼ 9 kcal mol/C0 1; increasing DGz by 1.4 kcal mol /C0 1 decreases kTST by a factor
of 10. Since quantum–mechanical eﬀects can modify the transmission coeﬃcient (see section 16.3.4), it is preferable whenever possible to determine DG
z
independently from the temperature dependence of the rate constant.
16.2.2.3 Kinetic Isotope Eﬀect
As already mentioned, it is often possible to convert a proton transfer step into
a deuteron transfer step. The magnitude of the kinetic isotope eﬀect (KIE) and
its temperature dependence provide strong restraints to theoretical models, but
their interpretation often requires the consideration of quantum–mechanical
eﬀects (see section 16.3.4).
16.2.2.4 Dependence on DpKa
Let us consider a proton transfer reaction involving the A and B groups in a
protein:
AH þ B ! A/C0 þ BHþ ð16:2Þ
The standard free-energy (Gibbs energy) change of the reaction can be
written:
DG/C14 ¼/C0 2:3 RT pKa BðÞ AH/C0 pKa AðÞ BHþ
/C2/C3
¼/C0 2:3 RT pKa BðÞ A/C0 /C0 pKa AðÞ B
/C2/C3
ð16:3Þ
where p Ka(A)BH1 and p Ka(A)B are the p Kas of A when B is protonated and
deprotonated, respectively. The p Kas of ionisable groups of small proteins are
conveniently measured by nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR), 4 but this is not
possible for large proteins like enzymes. Although indirect methods can
sometimes be devised (see section 16.5.1.2), p Ka values must often be calculated
with microscopic models in which electrostatic interactions are properly described.
5,6 When the DpKa of a reaction can be altered by modifying ionizable
groups or their environment, the proﬁle of kH as a function of DpKa places an
additional constraint on theoretical models (see section 16.5).
16.3 Modelling Biological Proton Transfers: Basic
Ingredients
The aim of this section is to introduce methods used in the applications described in the following sections and, more generally, to help the non-specialist
reader to understand the literature about biological proton transfer models.
16.3.1 Introduction
Let us ﬁrst recall the meaning of the rate constant of the reaction described by
eqn 16.2. In a protein solution at room temperature, incessant and random

protonation and deprotonation of group A in a given molecule. This interval
is a random quantity and the average of its inverse taken over a very long
time, which is the same for all molecules, is the rate constant k
A-B of the
reaction:7,8
kA!B ¼ 1

/C18/C19
ð16:4Þ
where N is the number of deprotonation events during the long time period.
These events are rare at the timescale of the protein dynamics; a rate constant
of 10
6 s/C0 1 is 10 7 times smaller than the typical frequency of structural ﬂuctuations which is about kBT/hE1013 s/C0 1 at room temperature. The rate constant
kB-A is deﬁned in the same way by considering the deprotonation time tB-A
of B. The two rate constants are linked by:
kA!B=kB!A ¼ expð/C0 DG/C14 =RTÞð 16:5Þ
where DG1 is the standard free-energy change of the reaction (eqn (16.3)).
Proton transfers are induced by energy ﬂuctuations produced by motions
of the {protein þ solvent} system. These motions are generally described in
the framework of the Born–Oppenheimer approximation which enables us
to deﬁne the potential energy surface (PES) E
g(q) of the ground state, where
{q} represents the coordinates of all atoms of the system. This surface
exhibits two minima at q ¼ qeq
a and q ¼ qeq
b corresponding to the equilibrium
geometries of the {A–H, B} and {A /C0 ,H 1–B} states, and it goes through a
maximum at the transition state qEqz. If A is initially protonated, most motions make q vary around qeq
a . However, from time to time, some rare ‘productive’ ﬂuctuations allow q to reach qz and the {A–H, B} to {A /C0 ,H 1–B}
transition occurs.
According to eqn (16.4), the rate constant kA-B should be calculated by
simulating the motion of the system and performing a statistical study of the
protonation/deprotonation events. Owing to the extreme rarity of the transitions, this direct method would require prohibitively long simulations. Thus, it
is preferable to replace the time average for a single system by an instantaneous
ensemble average. In an ensemble of systems in equilibrium at temperature T,
there is a certain fraction of systems such that qEq
z, and the rate constant is
obtained by multiplying this fraction by the average velocity of crossing the
transition region during productive ﬂuctuations. When the barrier energy is
larger than k
BT, which is usually the case for biological proton transfers, this
method leads to eqn (16.1). 9,10 Since the transmission coeﬃcient is generally
close to unity, 3 determining the rate constant comes down to calculating the
activation free-energy DGz. Free-energy calculations are usually based on
molecular dynamics simulations but they can be accelerated by eﬃcient procedures (see section 16.3.3).

## Page 404

16.3.2 Molecular Dynamics: Quantum Mechanics/Molecular
Mechanics Methods
To initiate a molecular dynamics simulation, the PES E(q(0)) corresponding to
an initial structure q(0) is calculated and the forces experienced by all atoms are
deduced. From this force-ﬁeld and a set of initial velocities compatible with the
temperature, the displacement of all atoms is calculated by Newtonian mechanics for a very short time Dt, typically 0.1 to 1 fs. The energy E(q(Dt)) corresponding to the new set of coordinates is calculated, and in this way a
classical trajectory q(t) is generated step by step. In order to propagate long
time trajectories, the energy E(q) must be calculated rapidly and accurately.
This is not possible by treating quantum mechanically the whole system, which
is therefore partitioned into two parts:
11
– A ‘quantum system’ containing the reacting part of the protein, whose
energy is calculated by semi-empirical quantum chemical methods.
– A ‘classical system’ made of other atoms of the protein, the solvent and
possibly the membrane, whose energy is calculated by molecular mechanical force-ﬁelds.
A crucial point in the implementation of these quantum mechanics (QM)/
molecular mechanics (MM) methods is the treatment of the boundary region
between the two systems.
11
16.3.2.1 Modelling the Quantum System
Methods particularly devoted to biomolecular processes are more frequently
used than multipurpose semi-empirical molecular orbital methods like AM1
and PM3. Self-consistent-charge density functional theory based tight-binding
(SCC-DFTB), an eﬃcient method derived from density functional theory, is
well adapted for QM/MM studies of biomolecular systems.
12
In the empirical valence bond (EVB) model developed by Warshel’s group,
the protonation states of the system are described by valence bond type functions and the matrix representing the Hamiltonian is calculated with force-ﬁeld
like functions accounting for the interactions with the protein and the solvent.
These functions are calibrated to reproduce experimental or ab initio data. The
smallest eigenvalue of the matrix gives the ground state PES E
g(q).13
The multi-state empirical valence bond (MS-EVB) model elaborated by Voth
and his group is more ﬂexible since the valence bond functions are optimized at
each step of the molecular dynamics calculation. 14 This model is well adapted
for the study of diﬀusion processes and proton transport chains. 15
16.3.2.2 Modelling the Protein and the Solvent
The protein medium is electrostatically very heterogeneous. Aliphatic and
aromatic residues are apolar; peptide bonds, internal water molecules and some
384 Chapter 16
 01/12/2013 15:17:49.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00379
View Online

## Page 405

residues possess a dipole moment and other residues carry a negative (Asp,
Glu) or positive (Lys, Arg) charge at pH 7. Moreover, proteins contain numerous induced dipoles and redox centres are often charged. Electrostatic
energy calculations based on microscopic models are therefore very time-consuming.
16 In practice, specialised force-ﬁelds like CHARMM22 and AMBER
for proteins 17,18 and TIP3 for the solvent 19 are used. The protein dipoles–
Langevin dipoles (PDLD) model of the protein–solvent system, or the more
realistic surface constraint all atom solvent (SCAAS) model for the solvent, is
used with the EVB model.
13
16.3.3 Free-Energy Calculations
16.3.3.1 Potential of Mean Force
As mentioned in section 16.3.1, rate constant calculations often rest on the
determination of the activation free-energy DGz which is usually deduced from
the free-energy proﬁle of the system as a function of the reaction coordinate.
The reaction coordinate w is a function w(q) of the coordinates of the atoms of
the system chosen so as to follow the reaction advancement. In an ensemble of
{protein þ solvent} systems in equilibrium at temperature T, the w value distribution is characterized by the probability density r(w) given by:
rðw
0Þ¼
R
expð/C0 EgðqÞ=kBTÞ dðwðqÞ/C0 w0ÞdqR
expð/C0 EgðqÞ=kBTÞdq ð16:6Þ
where Eg(q) is the ground state PES and integration is performed over all
possible q values. In the numerator, the Dirac function selects those conﬁgurations for which w(q) ¼ w0 and the denominator is the partition function of the
system. To simplify, this equation is written as:
rðw0Þ¼h dðw qðÞ /C0 w0ÞiEg ð16:7Þ
The free-energy variation of the system along the reaction coordinate is then
given by:
gðwÞ¼/C0 kBT ln rðwÞð 16:8Þ
The function g(w), called the potential of mean force (PMF), is the key
quantity of proton transfer models. Indeed, the standard free-energy change is
given by:
DG
/C14 ¼ gðwbÞ/C0 gðwaÞð 16:9Þ
Here wa ¼ w(qeq
a )a n d wb ¼ w(qeq
b ), where qeq
a and qeq
b are the coordinates of the
Eg(q) minima corresponding to the {A–H, B} and {A /C0 ,H 1–B} states (see
section 16.3.1). Likewise, the activation free-energy is given by:
DGz ¼ gðwzÞ/C0 gðwaÞð 16:10Þ
Here wz ¼ w(qz). To determine the PMF, the density r(w) deﬁned by eqn (16.6)
must be numerically calculated by sampling conﬁgurations generated by all

## Page 406

possible q values. This can be done with Monte Carlo methods, but it is generally preferable to replace the ensemble average of eqn (16.6) by a time average
achieved by molecular dynamics (MD) simulations. Although trajectories
generated with the ground state PES E
g(q) very rarely reach the high energy
transition region, the PES can be biased to force the system to explore this
region. The PMF is reconstituted from the information gained from trajectories
generated with a family of biased PES. In the following, two frequently used
methods are brieﬂy described.
16.3.3.2 Umbrella Sampling
The term ‘umbrella sampling’ (US) refers to a series of potential wells centred
on w
n values positioned along the [ wa, wb] interval. A potential like vn(w) ¼ 1
2 c (w –
wn)2 is added to Eg(q), giving the biased PES:
En qðÞ ¼ Eg qðÞ þ vnðw qðÞ Þ ð 16:11Þ
Since this PES exhibits a deep well for wEwn, this value of the reaction coordinate is very often taken during trajectories generated with En(q). The
probability density rn(w) corresponding to En(q), which is given by eqn (16.6) in
which Eg(q) is replaced by En(q), is therefore well deﬁned in the vicinity of wn.
The PMF g(w) is reconstituted in the vicinity of wn through the following
equation:20
gðwnÞ¼/C0 vnðwnÞ/C0 kBT ln rnðwnÞþ Cn ð16:12Þ
where Cn is an undetermined constant. In practice, the [ wa, wb] interval is decomposed into a series of overlapping ‘windows’ centred on various wn values,
and the functions g(w) obtained in adjacent windows are matched by a simple
vertical shift. More elaborate methods can be used such as the iterative
weighted histogram analysis method (WHAM) algorithm.
21
16.3.3.3 Free-Energy Perturbation/Umbrella Sampling
This is an adaptation of the umbrella sampling philosophy to the EVB model.
In this model, a natural reaction coordinate is the energy gap w(q) ¼ E
b(q)–
Ea(q), where Ea(q) and Eb(q) are the diagonal matrix elements corresponding to
a ¼ {A–H, B} and b ¼ {A/C0 ,H 1–B} valence states (see section 16.3.2.1). It varies
from wa ¼ Eb(qeq
a )– Ea(qeq
a )t o wb ¼ Eb(qeq
b )– Ea(qeq
b ). A family of biased PESs
called ‘mapping potentials’ is deﬁned by:
El qðÞ ¼ ð 1 /C0 lÞ Ea qðÞ þ lEb qðÞ ; 0 /C20 l /C20 1 ð16:13Þ
