## Page 408

16.3.4 Nuclear Quantum–Mechanical Eﬀects
In molecular dynamics simulations, quantum mechanics is used to calculate the
energy of the quantum system but the motion of the whole system is treated
classically. Taking account of the quantum character of the motion does not
invalidate eqn (16.1), but it gives rise to nuclear quantum mechanical (NQM)
eﬀects which change DG
z and the transmission coeﬃcient k. Zero point energy
eﬀects contribute to DGz, while ‘dynamical eﬀects’ such as tunnelling and
transitions to excited vibrational states contribute to k. Various approaches
have been proposed to evaluate these contributions.
16.3.4.1 Ensemble-Average Variational Transition State Theory
with Multidimensional Tunnelling
The DGz value deduced from the PMF is corrected by replacing classical vibrational partition functions by their quantum homolog. Recrossing, tunnelling and non-classical reﬂection eﬀects can be included in the transmission
coeﬃcient by various procedures. This ensemble-average variational transition
state theory with multidimensional tunnelling (EA-VTST/MT) method was
applied to proton and hydride transfers in various enzymes such as yeast
enolase,
26 liver alcohol dehydrogenase 27 and triosephosphate isomerase. 28 For
a review, see ref. 3 and the chapter by J. Gao in this book.
16.3.4.2 Quantum Classical Path
In the quantum centroid TST theory ﬁrst proposed by Gillan and further
elaborated by Voth and co-workers,
29–31 each quantum particle is replaced by a
ring of harmonically interacting ‘quasi particles’ subjected to an eﬀective
quantum mechanical potential. The quantum–classical path (QCP) method
consists of propagating classical trajectories of the ring and using the position
of its centre of mass, called the centroid, to calculate quantum partition
functions.
32 This method was applied to the kinetics of the C–H bond cleavage
occurring in lipoxygenase, which is characterized by a very large KIE, 33,34 and
to other enzymes. 35
16.3.4.3 Hybrid Quantum/Classical Molecular Dynamics
The free-energy proﬁle is calculated by the FEP/US method (see section
16.3.3.3). However, at each step of the molecular dynamics simulation, the
vibrational energy and the wave function of the transferred proton are determined from a three-dimensional Schro ¨dinger equation and are included in the
FEP/US procedure. In addition, dynamical eﬀects due to transitions among
proton vibrational states are calculated with a ‘molecular dynamics with
quantum transition’ (MDQT) procedure in which the proton wave function
evolution is determined by a time-dependent Schro ¨dinger equation. This procedure is combined with a reactive ﬂux approach to calculate the transmission
388 Chapter 16
 01/12/2013 15:17:49.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00379
View Online

## Page 409

coeﬃcient. This method was used to calculate the rate constant and the KIE for
hydride transfer in liver alcohol dehydrogenase. 36,37
16.3.4.4 Wave Propagation
Classical trajectories q(t) are generated with the PES Ea(q) of the initial state
and the Ea(q(t)) surface is cut every 20 fs along the line connecting the donor
(A) and acceptor (B) atoms. The obtained sections V(d), where d is the A–H
distance, are used as one-dimensional potentials in the time-dependent onedimensional Schro¨dinger equation which determines the proton motion. The
decay of the population of the initial reactant well, obtained by averaging the
results of 100 independent trajectories, gives the rate constant. This realistic
approach encounters the same convergence problems as those mentioned in
section 16.3.1 and the calculations must be accelerated by using biasing potentials. It was applied to the hydrogen atom transfer in lipoxygenase.
38
In all the studies mentioned in this section, allowing for NQM eﬀects was
found to signiﬁcantly improve the agreement between calculated and experimental data. However, these studies concerned single proton transfer steps
occurring at the active site of enzymes. In simulations of proton transfer chains
kinetics which require very long calculations, NQM eﬀects are generally
ignored.
16.3.5 Simulation of Proton Transfer Chain Kinetics
Up to now, we have considered a single proton transfer step (eqn (16.2)).
However, proton exchanges through membrane proteins and between the active site of an enzyme and the solvent take place through proton transfer
chains. Proteins contain numerous protonatable residues that may participate
in these chains, but only some of them are functional. They can be identiﬁed by
comparing sequences of proteins of the same family and examining whether the
kinetics is altered when a ‘conserved’ residue is replaced by a non-protonatable
one. The analysis of such experiments is not always straightforward.
39 Another
complementary method consists of comparing the calculated ‘eﬃciency’ of
putative proton transfer pathways. Several search algorithms based on topological criteria have been proposed, but their results are reliable only if the
dynamics and the energetics of the chains are also considered.
40 The kinetics of
proton transfer chains are too slow to be calculated with molecular dynamics
simulations involving the whole {protein þ solvent} system. In practice, one
simulates the time evolution of the chain subjected to interactions with the
protein and the solvent. The most frequently used methods are outlined below.
16.3.5.1 Centre of Excess Charge
The progress of the proton along the chain is followed by the centre of excess
charge (CEC), a collective reaction coordinate whose exact deﬁnition varies
with the authors.
41 The PMF corresponding to the CEC is calculated by
Molecular Modelling of Proton Transfer Kinetics in Biological Systems 389
 01/12/2013 15:17:49.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00379
View Online

## Page 410

generating molecular dynamics simulations with extensive umbrella sampling
(see section 16.3.3.2). This requires computationally eﬃcient methods like SCCDFT or MS-EVB. The free-energy proﬁle of the chain generally displays a
succession of barriers and the highest barrier is taken as the activation freeenergy DG
z of the rate limiting step. Introducing this quantity in a TST type
equation yields a rate constant that can be compared with experimental data.
16.3.5.2 Langevin Equation
In this method, one considers that the interactions of the proton transfer chain
with the rest of the protein and the solvent generate friction and random forces.
These processes are characterized by phenomenological parameters evaluated
by generating molecular dynamics trajectories, which are also used to build the
PMF. The PMF and the phenomenological parameters are then introduced
into the Langevin equation to simulate the time evolution of the protonation
state of the chain.
42 A ‘transit time’ can be deﬁned and compared with the
experimental data. 22
16.3.5.3 Kinetic Model
A number of discrete states, diﬀering by the protonation state and the geometry
of the protonatable sites, are deﬁned for the chain. Transitions among these
states are characterised by rate constants which are taken to be of the TST type,
with Dg
z values evaluated from electrostatic calculations and/or kinetic data.
The time course of the state’s populations is determined by a set of a large
number of coupled diﬀerential equations (master equation), which is generally
solved with stochastic methods ﬁrst introduced by Gillepsie.
43
16.4 The Gramicidin Channel: a Paradigm for Water
Wires in Proteins?
Gramicidin A (gA) is a highly hydrophobic pentadecapeptide which can be
inserted in phospholipid monolayers. Under some conditions, two peptides can
associate head-to-head to form a b-helical dimer constituting a B4A˚ width and
B25 A˚ length channel spanning the lipid bilayer of a membrane. This channel
contains a single-ﬁle chain of 8–10 water molecules stabilized by hydrogen
bonding with carbonyls groups of the peptide backbone (see Figure 16.2). It is
permeable to monovalent cations such as Na
1 and K 1, a property which
confers its antibiotic activity to gramicidin, and also to protons. Water chains
are often found in proteins and the gA channel has long been considered as a
prototype for their study. These ‘water wires’ are commonly believed to ensure
fast proton transfer by reference to the Grotthuss mechanism which is responsible for the fast diﬀusion of protons in bulk water. We brieﬂy describe this
mechanism, review data about the proton conductance of the gA channel and
describe two recent models of proton transport in gA.
390 Chapter 16
 01/12/2013 15:17:49.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00379
View Online

## Page 411

16.4.1 Proton Diﬀusion in Water: the Grotthuss Mechanism
About 100 years ago, it was inferred that proton diﬀusion in water occurs
through breaking/forming hydrogen bonds along networks of water molecules,
a mechanism reminiscent of that postulated by Theodor von Grotthuss to
explain water electrolysis in 1806.
44,45 Yet, the details of this so-called ‘Grotthuss mechanism’ were elucidated only recently. 46,47 A hydrated proton H 3OA
1
centred on O A forms ‘strong’ hydrogen bonds with three H 2O molecules
(H9O3OA
1, Eigen complex), which form ‘normal’ hydrogen bonds with their
neighbours. When one of these normal bonds is broken by a ﬂuctuation of the
hydrogen-bonded network, a transient symmetrical H 5OAOB
1 Zundel complex
is formed, which evolves rapidly to an H 9O3OB
1 Eigen complex centred on O B
when a new, normal bond is formed (see Figure 1 in ref. 48). The validity of this
mechanism was subsequently conﬁrmed by spectroscopic studies and molecular
dynamics simulations,
48 and by the fact that it accounts for the diﬀusion
constant of protons in water. 49,50 This process requires very little activation
energy in bulk water where structural reorganization is delocalized over a great
number of water molecules.
50 The situation is diﬀerent in proteins, where
protons follow well-deﬁned pathways along which they interact electrostatically with a relatively rigid structure.
16.4.2 Proton Conductance of the Gramicidin Channel
Up to now, no proton transfer rate constant has been measured in gramicidin.
The results of theoretical studies were therefore confronted to a stationary
proton ﬂux produced by applying a voltage across a membrane soaked in a HCl
solution. Single channel currents could be measured by voltage clamping the
membrane. These experiments showed that the channel operates in the ohmic
regime provided the voltage is not too high ( Vr100 mV) and the HCl concentration is not too small ( C
HClZ1M). As an example, with the ‘GMO’
membrane, a current of 33 pA was measured at 19 1C for V ¼ 50 mV and
Figure 16.2 Structure of the gramicidin channel. Water molecules are depicted as red
spheres.

Elsevier.

## Page 412

CHCl ¼ 1M, giving the conductance g ¼ 650 pS. 51 The conductance is roughly
proportional to CHCl and it depends on the nature of the membrane. 52 Fitting
the temperature dependence of the conductance to eqn (16.1) between 5 1C and
441C gave DGz ¼ 6.4 kcal mol /C0 1.51
16.4.3 Modelling Proton Transfer in Gramicidin
The existence of detailed structural data and the small size of the gA channel
have encouraged numerous theoretical studies. Earlier works were strongly
inﬂuenced by the Grotthuss mechanism and focused on the dynamics of the
isolated water chain.
53 In more recent studies, attempts were made to simulate
proton transport by considering the electrostatic interactions with the peptide,
the membrane and the solvent.
A study 22 based on the EVB model and the Langevin equation was carried
out in which 13 valence bond states corresponding to all possible locations of a
proton in the water chain were deﬁned and the EVB matrix was constructed
(see section 16.3.2.1). Solvent molecules close to the channel ends were described by the Langevin dipole model and more distant ones were treated as a
continuum. The membrane was modelled by a grid of induced dipoles. Freeenergy proﬁles for stepwise proton transfers were determined by the FEP/US
method (see section 16.3.3.3). Joining these proﬁles gave a free-energy barrier
with a maximum at 6 kcal mol
/C0 1 at the centre of the channel, which reﬂects the
reduction of the proton solvation energy as it moves away from the channel
ends. These results were used to build an eﬀective potential, which was introduced in the Langevin equation to simulate proton transport in the channel.
The simulations revealed that, during the transport, the proton is often delocalized over several water molecules forming, for example, H
5O2
1 Zundel
complexes. The calculated values of DGz and the transit time were found to be
consistent with those estimated from the conductance data. However, it should
be noted that the solution pH, which is a key parameter according to conductance experiments, does not appear explicitly in this model.
Proton transport in the gA channel was also simulated with a kinetic model
(see section 16.3.5.3).
54 Each H2O molecule was allowed to take six orientations
and each H 3O1 molecule, four orientations, so that the chain of eleven H 2O
molecules could take 10 11 states. Three types of transitions were allowed: rotation of H 2O and H 3O1; proton transfer from H 3O1 to a neighbouring H 2O
molecule when they form a hydrogen bond; and proton uptake and release for
water molecules located at the channel ends. The rate constants were taken of
the TST type, with Dg
z values calculated by continuum electrostatics or deduced from data about proton transfer in water. For the proton uptake and
release steps, the Dg
z value depended explicitly on the pH. The master equation
was solved by a sequential dynamical Monte Carlo algorithm and the PMF was
deduced from the probability of occupancy of the various sites. When no
voltage was applied, the PMF was a symmetrical barrier with a maximum at 3.4
kcal mol
/C0 1. Stationary proton ﬂuxes calculated for various pH and voltages
values were in reasonable agreement with the conductance data. Despite the
simpliﬁed description of electrostatic interactions and the questionable
392 Chapter 16
 01/12/2013 15:17:49.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00379
View Online

## Page 413

parametrization of the rate constants, this model is an interesting attempt to
link microscopic simulations to macroscopic observables.
Although gramicidin A has long been considered as a paradigm for water
wires in proteins, its interest for testing proton transfer models is limited because theoretical calculations can be confronted only to stationary proton ﬂux.
A promising method for measuring proton transfer rates in gA, based on the
study of the time-resolved two-dimensional IR spectrum of the channel, has
been proposed.
55 In any case, all recent theoretical studies demonstrate that the
proton conductivity of the gA channel is not due to the Grotthuss mechanism
and that the proton transfer capability of water wires in proteins is determined
by the proton electrostatic energy in diﬀerent sites along the chain. This is well
illustrated by the case of aquaporin, a protein forming a 25 A ˚ channel which
ensures fast water transport across biological membranes. This channel contains a chain of water molecules similar to that of gramicidin, but it is fully
impermeable to protons. In earlier works inspired by the Grotthuss mechanism,
this remarkable property was ascribed to the peculiar arrangement of the H
2O
molecules in the channel. More recent studies have shown that proton transport is actually blocked by a high energy barrier of about 25 kcal mol
/C0 1 due to
electrostatic interactions. 56–58
16.5 Intramolecular Proton Transfer in Carbonic
Anhydrase
Mammalian carbonic anhydrase II (CA II) is a small enzyme (29 kDa) which
catalyses CO 2 hydration and the inverse reaction, HCO 3
/C0 dehydration, in a
number of physiological processes:
CO2 þ H2O $ HCO/C0
3 þ Hþ ð16:17Þ
With a maximum turnover rate of about 10 6 s/C0 1 at 25 1C in both directions,
this reaction is one of fastest catalysed reactions. The active site is a Zn 21 centre
coordinated by three His nitrogen and one OH /C0 . The catalytic cycle comprises
a chemical sequence:
CO2 þ Zn OH /C0 þ H2O $ Zn H 2O þ HCO/C0
3 ð16:18Þ
followed by a proton transfer sequence which restores the initial state of the
enzyme:
Zn H 2O þ A $
kB

proposed to account for the pH dependence of the catalytic constants kcat
h

16.5.1 Kinetic Data Obtained with CAII
Carbonic anhydrase is one of the rare enzymes in which the chemical step of
catalysis is so fast that intramolecular proton transfer is rate limiting, which
allows this transfer to be studied in detail.
16.5.1.1 Rate Constants
According to Steiner’s model, the proton transfer rate constants of the reaction
described by eqn (16.19) are the maximum catalytic constants (k
cat
h )max ¼ 106 s/C0 1
and (kcat
d )max ¼ 6/C2 105 s/C0 1 measured at high and low pH, respectively. Both rate
constants decrease four-fold when the reaction is studied in D 2O.59 The temperature dependence of ( kcat
h )max yields DGz ¼ 9.0 kcal mol /C0 1 at 25 1C.60 Carbonic anhydrase also catalyses the 18O exchange between HCO 3
/C0 and H 2O,
which involves several steps whose kinetics can be followed by mass spectrometry. Simulating the pH dependence of their rates yields p K
a(ZnH2O) ¼ 6.8,
pKa(His64) ¼ 7.2 together with the rate constant of the reaction:
Zn 18OH/C0 þ AHþ ! Zn H 2
18O þ A ð16:18Þ
which is nothing else than the rate constant kB deﬁned in eqn (16.19). The
value kB ¼ 8/C2 105 s/C0 1 obtained in this way is in excellent agreement with
(kcat
d )max.61
16.5.1.2 Dependence on DpKa
When the enzyme is modiﬁed by site-directed mutagenesis, the values of
pKa(ZnH2O), p Ka(His64) and kB are altered, and the variation of kB as a
function of DpKa ¼ pKa(ZnH2O) – pKa(His64) can be studied by simulating the
pH proﬁle of the 18O exchange reaction rates. Replacing His64 by alanine
decreases kB 20-fold, but the activity is restored when proton donors like
imidazole and pyridine are added in the solution. The variation of log( kB)a sa
function of DpKa ¼ pKa(ZnH2O) – p Ka(donor) is quasi parabolic, with a
maximum close to DpKa ¼ 0.62 In CAIII, an isoenzyme of CAII where position
64 residue is a lysine, proton transfers are much slower, with ( kcat
h)max ¼ 104 s/C0 1
and kB ¼ 3/C2 103 s/C0 1. Replacing Lys64 by His increases kB to 2 /C2 104 s/C0 1, which
conﬁrms the importance of the position 64 residue for proton transfers in this
family of enzymes. Double mutations can still increase kB and 18O exchange
experiments showed that this increase is correlated with DpKa.63
16.5.2 Modelling Proton Transfers in Carbonic Anhydrase
The crystallographic structure of CAII shows that Zn and His64 are about 7 A ˚
apart and that a network of hydrogen-bonded H 2O molecules starting from the

OH/C0 ligand of Zn 21 ends very close to His64. 64 The structure also reveals that
the orientation of His64 can change easily from inward (toward the active site)
to outward (toward the solvent), suggesting a role of mobile proton shuttle (see
Figure 16.3). These peculiarities have motivated a number of theoretical studies
among which three are brieﬂy described below.
Proton transfer from His64 to ZnOH
/C0 was modelled in a series of mutated
forms of the CAIII enzyme in which Lys64 was replaced by His (see section
16.5.1.2). The geometry of the modiﬁed proteins needed for the simulations
was obtained from molecular mechanics calculations based on the CAIII
crystal structure. A detailed study was ﬁrst carried out on the Lys64His/
Phe198Asp double mutant in which proton transfer is particularly fast, with
k
B ¼ 2/C2 105 s/C0 1.63 The results of free-energy calculations based on the EVB
model, the semi-microscopic version of the protein dipole Langevin dipoles
(PDLD/S) method and the linear response approximation (LRA), were introduced in the Langevin equation to generate long time trajectories. The transit
time 7 /C2 10
/C0 7 s agrees reasonably with 1/ kB ¼ 50/C2 10/C0 7 s.65 The log( kB) versus
DpKa variation observed in this series of mutants was then tackled with a
simpliﬁed proton transfer chain made of His64, H 2O and ZnOH /C0 .25 For each
mutated form, the activation free-energies for stepwise proton transfer were
evaluated with eqn (16.16) using calculated or estimated values of the various
parameters and an approximate free-energy proﬁle was constructed. The
highest energy barrier along this proﬁle was taken as the activation free-energy
DG
z for proton transfer along the chain and the rate constant kTST was calculated with eqn (16.1). The variation of kTST as a function of the diﬀerence
DpKa ¼ pKa(ZnH2O) – p Ka(His64) deduced from 18O exchange experiments
was found to reproduce the experimental data nicely. The authors highlighted
the diﬀerence between this procedure based on calculations carried out on the
real system, and simple ﬁtting to the Marcus equation which gives phenomenological parameters with unphysical values.
63
Proton transfer from Zn 21H2O to solvent in CAII was modelled with the
MS-EVB method. 66 The parameters of the model were determined by ﬁtting a

reference PES generated by a hybrid B3LYP/AMBER method, and the PMF
was calculated with extensive umbrella sampling using the WHAM algorithm
and the CEC as reaction coordinate (see sections 16.3.3.2 and 16.3.5.1, respectively). To specify the role of His64 in proton transfer, calculations were
carried out for three conﬁgurations of the chain: without His64; with His64 in
the inward orientation; and with His64 in the outward orientation (see
Figure 16.3). The values p K
a(His64 outward) EpKa(ZnH2O) ¼ 7.1 deduced
from the PMF are in good agreement with experiment. The barrier height of
the limiting step was 15 kcal mol
/C0 1 without His64 and 11.4 kcal mol /C0 1 when

10 kcal mol
/C0 1, in good agreement with the experimental value DGz ¼ 9 kcal
mol/C0 1. The same model was used to analyse the eﬀects of His64 to Ala mutation and the rescue by imidazole in CAII. 67 A similar study based on the
SCC-DFTB/CHARMM model gave rather diﬀerent results. 68 Indeed, the
barrier height due to electrostatic interactions was found to be about 11 kcal
mol
/C0 1 whatever the orientation of His64 and the number of water molecules
included in the chain, and the analysis of the CEC migration suggested a
mechanism in which OH
/C0 is sequentially propagated.
All these studies underscore the importance of energetic factors like the p Kas
of His64, of water molecules, and particularly of ZnH 2O which must be close to
7 to ensure fast catalysis in both directions (eqn (16.17)).
16.6 Bacteriorhodopsin: A Light-Driven Proton Pump
16.6.1 Introduction
Some archaebacteria like Halobacterium salinarum possess a bioenergetic
system capable of converting directly li ght energy into a proton gradient. It is
made of a single small protein (24 kD a) called bacteriorhodopsin in which
seven transmembrane a-helices surround a channel containing protonatable
groups and water molecules. A chromophor e called retinal, attached to the
side chain of Lys216 via a Schiﬀ base linkage, is positioned transversally,
approximately midway in the channel (see Figure 16.4). Absorption of a
570 nm photon initiates a complex se quence of events which leaves after a
few ps the retinal in a ground state with a distorted geometry. In the course of
the relaxation to the ‘resting’ geome try which lasts about 10 ms, structural
changes drive proton transfers in the ch annel. Spectroscopic data obtained in
intermediate states of the relaxatio n have enabled the identiﬁcation of
the groups that exchange protons.
69 In the resting state, all C ¼C bonds of
the retinal are in the trans geometry and the Schiﬀ base (SB) is protonated.
Absorption of a photon results in isomerization of the retinal in the 13 cis,15
anti geometry, which triggers the proto n transfer sequence (see Figure 16.4):
PT1 from SB to Asp85; PT2 from proton release group (PRG) to the extracellular medium; PT3 from Asp96 to SB; PT4 from the cytoplasm to Asp96
with a return of the retinal to the all trans geometry; and PT5 from Asp85
to PRG.
396 Chapter 16
 01/12/2013 15:17:49.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00379
View Online

## Page 417

The result is the net transfer of a proton from the cytoplasmic side of the
membrane to the extracellular medium. Bacteriorhodopsin is therefore a lightdriven proton pump. PRG was soon identiﬁed with a group located close to the
extracellular side of the membrane, made of Glu194, Glu204 and a network of
water molecules, but the precise protonation site has long been controversial.
The results of a time-resolved IR study favoured water molecules,
70 but more
detailed experiments and free-energy calculations have shown that the proton is
actually delocalised over the two Glu residues.
71
16.6.2 Rate Constants
The formation and disappearance of intermediate states can be followed by
spectroscopic techniques and their ﬁtting to a kinetic model yields a set of rate
constants. The consensus model is based on the photocycle shown in
Figure 16.5 in which all proton transfer steps are reversible except the O -bR
transition, which accounts for the fact that bR is the only stable state in the
dark. A ﬁrst set of rate constants was deduced from visible absorption experiments carried out in the 5–30 1C range between pH 4.5 and 9 (see
Table 16.1).
72 More recent time-resolved FTIR experiments were designed to

The retinal is linked to the side chain of Lys216 by a Schiﬀ base. The ﬁve
proton transfer steps are indicated.

## Page 418

follow simultaneously the evolution of the retinal, the protein, protonatable
residues and water molecules at 20 1C, pH 7. 73 The kinetics were analysed with
the photocycle of Figure 16.5, and several rate constants were found to diﬀer
markedly from those obtained previously (see Table 16.1). This is a serious
point because the former data were often used in theoretical studies.
16.6.3 Modelling Proton Transfers in Bacteriorhodopsin
Proton pumping results from the coupling of various phenomena occurring
during the relaxation, that is, conformational changes of the protein and of the
retinal, motion of water molecules and proton transfers. Although a detailed
description of this complex process is not yet possible, interesting theoretical
studies based on structural models of photocycle intermediates have already
appeared. These structural models were built from high resolution crystal
studies carried out on wild-type and mutated forms of the protein, trapped in
certain conditions.
74
Figure 16.5 Bacteriorhodopsin photocycle. Absorption of a photon leaves, after a
few ps, the protein in a ground state K with a distorted geometry.
Further reversible conformational changes lead to the L state, which
initiates the proton transfer sequence.

## Page 419

16.6.3.1 Thermodynamics of the Proton Pump
The equilibrium protonation states of the retinal and of ionisable residues were
calculated for all intermediate states by applying the Poisson–Boltzmann
equation to a macroscopic dielectric model of the (protein, membrane, solvent)
system based on the structural models geometry.
75,76 It turns out that the
geometrical variations of the structural models account for the protonation
states changes that occur along the photocycle (see Figure 16.5). This is consistent with the kinetic data obtained in ref. 73 (see Table 16.1), since the freeenergy changes deduced from the forward and backward rate constants and
eqn (16.5) indicate that all proton transfer reactions are weakly exergonic with
7DG17r3 kcal mol
/C0 1, except for PT3 which is slightly endergonic. This exergonic character explains the directionality of the proton pump.
16.6.3.2 Proton Transfer Kinetics
Various models have been proposed to describe the coupling of proton transfers to structural rearrangements so as to reproduce the data of Table 16.1. The
eﬀects of retinal twisting and water molecules motion on the kinetics of SBH
1
to Asp85/C0 proton transfer (PT1) were examined by using a structural model of
the L state where the retinal is twisted with the Schiﬀ base oriented toward the
cytoplasm.77 A number of minimum energy reaction pathways were calculated
by QM/MM methods with SCC-DFT/CHARMM and TIP3 to describe the
water molecules. A low-energy conformer in which SBH
1 and Asp85 /C0 are
bridged by a water molecule wB was found, and PMF calculations suggested
that a weakly exergonic concerted proton transfer (wB -Asp85
/C0 , BSH1-wB)
takes place in this conformer with a low free-energy barrier of about 7 kcal
mol
/C0 1. However, this value is distinctly smaller than DGzE10.5 kcal mol /C0 1
orE12 kcal mol /C0 1 calculated with eqn (16.1) from the L -M1 rate constants
reported in Table 16.1.
A diﬀerent view of this proton transfer step was proposed by Warshel and
co-workers.78 The PMFs of the ﬁrst steps of the photocycle were calculated
with the FEP/US method by molecular dynamics simulations based on structural models of the bR, K, L, M
1 and M2 states. The PESs were determined by
combining the EVB approach and a model of the retinal given by the quantum–
mechanical consistent force ﬁeld for pi electrons (QCFF/PI) method. Finally,
free-energy changes between intermediate states were calculated and all PMFs
were collected on an energetic diagram showing the coupling of proton transfer
to protein structural changes. Detailed analysis revealed that the structural
changes occurring between the bR and M
2 states destabilise the (BSH 1,
Asp85/C0 ) ion pair, thus driving the PT1 transfer. The barrier height for this
transfer was estimated at 10 /C6 2 kcal mol /C0 1, a value consistent with the rate
constant of the L -M1 transition. However, the calculated PMFs indicate that
the ﬁrst exergonic transition is M 2-N, which is not consistent with the data
(see Table 16.1). The PT2, PT3 and PT5 proton transfer steps were also studied
by free energy or minimum energy pathways calculations. 79–81
Molecular Modelling of Proton Transfer Kinetics in Biological Systems 399
 01/12/2013 15:17:49.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00379

## Page 420

An attempt was made to simulate the pumping mechanism during the whole
relaxation process with a kinetic model. 82 A set of microstates diﬀering by their
conformation (M, N, O, bR) and/or the protonation state of Asp96, SB, Asp 85
and PRG was deﬁned. Transitions between microstates induced by conformational changes or proton transfers were characterised by their rate constants. The ratio of forward and backward rate constants is determined by DG1
(see eqn (16.5)), which was deduced from the kinetic data of ref. 72 and from
dielectric macroscopic calculations.
76 Their sum, which has the signiﬁcance of a
relaxation rate, was considered as adjustable. Three relaxation rates were used:
k
f and ks for fast and slow proton transfers and kc for conformational changes.
Solving the ‘master equation’ gives the time course of the microstates populations. The equilibrium populations reached at the end of the relaxation are
entirely determined by the DG1 values. In contrast, the pumping eﬃciency,
deﬁned as the fractional number of proton release from PRG over the course of
the relaxation, depends on the relaxation rates. The pumping eﬃciency was
found to be larger than 0.7 for k
s/kcZ1 and kf/ksZ5. The authors also deﬁned
groups of microstates expected to give the same visible absorption spectrum
and they showed that k
f, ks and kc can be chosen so that the time evolutions of
the microstate populations reproduce the data reported in ref. 72. This model
accounts for the main experimental features of the system by using only three
adjustable parameters. However, it was based on the former kinetic data and it
would be interesting to know whether it can reproduce the more reliable data
reported in ref. 73.
16.7 Electron Coupled Proton Transfers
In bioenergetic systems, proton translocation across membranes is generally
driven by electron transfers generated by redox reactions (respiratory systems)
or light energy (photosynthetic systems). Owing to their complexity and to the
lack of detailed kinetic data, modelling these processes is currently a
diﬃcult task.
16.7.1 The Bacterial Photosynthetic Reaction Centre
In this complex, absorption of a photon triggers ultrafast charge separation
between a bacteriochlorophyll pigment and Q
A, a ubiquinone molecule tightly
bound to the protein which transfers electrons to another, loosely bound ubiquinone QB. The consecutive transfers of two electrons to Q B are coupled to the
uptake of two protons from the cytoplasm, giving the Q BH2 quinol molecule
which leaves the reaction centre and diﬀuses in the membrane. Electrons are
ﬁnally given to the bc
1 complex and protons are released into the periplasm.
The crystal structure shows that the distance between the Q B site and the
cytoplasm is about 15 A˚ and suggests that proton uptake might take place via a
complex network of protonatable residues, including a well-positioned glutamic acid (GluL212 in Rhodobacter sphaeroides ). Time-resolved UV-vis absorption studies of native and mutated forms of the complex have shown that

A diﬀerent scheme, involving a redox intermediate between Q A and QB, was
proposed for the ﬁrst electron transfer (see eqn (16.22)) on the basis of timeresolved FTIR experiment,86 but it was recently ruled out. 87 The nature and the
order of the events involved in the second electron transfer (see eqn (16.23)) is
also a matter of debate.
88 These uncertainties and the absence of kinetic data
about the proton transfer steps have not encouraged theoretical calculations.
A preliminary study was based on a kinetic model in which activation free
energies were evaluated with eqn (16.16).
89 More recently, proton transfers
involved in the second electron transfer were studied by minimum energy path
calculations carried out with B3LYP/6-31G(d,p) and the Amber99 forceﬁeld.
90 However, the complexity of the hydrogen-bond network connecting Q B
with the cytoplasmic region (see ref. 91) suggests that detailed kinetic information is required in order to obtain reliable results.
16.7.2 Cytochrome c Oxidase
Cytochrome c oxidase (C cO) is the last complex of the mitochondrial respiratory chain, where the four electron reduction of dioxygen to two water
molecules is coupled to the translocation of four protons from the negative to
the positive side of the membrane. Electrons given by reduced cytochrome c are
transmitted through Cu
A and heme a to the active site, a binuclear heme a3-CuB
centre. Structural and spectroscopic experiments have provided a detailed description of internal electron transfers and of the chemical catalytic cycle of O 2
reduction, but the mechanism whereby these processes are coupled to proton
transfers is not yet understood. The crystal structures of bovine C cO and of the
very similar bacterial CcO complexes show that two hydrophilic channels called
K and D may transport protons from the negative side of the membrane to the
active site. Convincing arguments indicate that the K channel delivers 2 ‘substrate’ protons required for H
2O formation and that the D channel transports
the additional two substrate protons and the four ‘pumped’ protons. The D
channel contains about 10 water molecules. It starts near Asp132 and ends at
Glu286 ( R. sphaeroides numbering), where substrate protons are given to the
Molecular Modelling of Proton Transfer Kinetics in Biological Systems 401
 01/12/2013 15:17:49.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00379
View Online

## Page 422

active site, while pumped protons are transferred to a still unidentiﬁed ‘protonloading site’ (PLS) (see Figure 16.6).
A number of mutations were introduced in the D channel of bacterial C cO
complexes and their eﬀects were interpreted by theoretical calculations based
on the protein structure. A ﬁrst series of mutations was devised to study factors
that inﬂuence the p K
a of Glu286 and to specify the role of a cluster of water
molecules located near Ser200. 92 Their eﬀect on the kinetics of various phases
of the catalytic cycle was studied by stopped ﬂow and ﬂow-ﬂash techniques,
together with catalytic and pumping activities measurements. The results were
analysed with proton transport simulations carried out with the MS-EVB
method, with AMBER99 force-ﬁeld for the protein, the TIP3P model for water
molecules and the CEC as reaction coordinate. It was concluded that these
phases of the catalytic cycle are not rate limited by proton transport through
the D channel. The authors underscore that the eﬀects of the mutations on the
proton transfer kinetics can be predicted only by performing a complete theoretical simulation.
The Asn139 to Asp mutation is especially intriguing since it shifts the apparent pK
a of Glu286, located approximately 18 A˚ apart, from 9.4 to about 11
and blocks proton pumping without aﬀecting proton transfer to the active site.
An attempt was made to reproduce these eﬀects with a model in which the PLS
is identiﬁed with the D propionate of heme a
3 (see Figure 16.6) and Glu286
takes two conformations characterized by diﬀerent p Ka.93 The time evolution
of the system was simulated with a kinetic model in which some rate constants
were deduced from computed free-energy barriers and p Ka values, while others
Figure 16.6 Structure of cytochrome c oxidase from R. sphaeroides and the D channel
(PDB ﬁle 1M56). The D propionate of heme a3 and some amino acids
discussed in the text are identiﬁed.
402 Chapter 16

## Page 423

were considered as adjustable parameters. Free-energy calculations based on
the wild-type and mutated protein structures were carried out with a hybrid of
the FEP/US and PDLD/S-LRA methods, and the eﬀects of the mutation could
be qualitatively reproduced.
These studies illustrate the eﬀort that is currently needed to approach some
aspects of proton transfers in C cO complexes. There is still a long way until the
pumping machinery is fully understood.
16.8 Conclusions
The kinetics of biological proton transfers are determined by the proton electrostatic energy in diﬀerent sites along a given pathway, and evaluation of rate
constants requires free-energy calculations based on the structure of the system
and a reliable description of the electrostatic interactions. These calculations,
which involve propagating numerous trajectories with a series of biased PES,
are much more demanding in terms of computational cost than potential energy calculations. Eﬃcient models worked out to simulate ‘simple’ biological
proton transfers like those occurring in enzymes give reliable results when
correctly calibrated, for example, to reproduce apparent p K
a values in proteins.
The situation is diﬀerent in the case of bioenergetic systems where proton
transfers are coupled to conformational changes and/or electron transfers.
Detailed kinetic data about proton transfers are rare, contrary to protoncoupled electron transfers in small molecules which are conveniently studied
with electrochemical techniques.
94,95 The case of bacteriorhodopsin is relatively
simple because no electron transfer is involved and much information about
intermediate states is available, but simulating the proton pumping machinery
of more complex systems like cytochrome c oxidase remains currently a challenge for theoreticians.
96 Moreover, the modelling of proton transfers kinetics
could be complicated by the existence of parallel proton transfer pathways, as
suggested in the case of enzymes,
40 the bacterial reaction centre 91 and cytochrome c oxidase.97

## Page 428

CHAPTER 17
Putting Together the Pieces: A
Global Description of Valence
and Long-Range Forces via
Combined Hyperbolic Inverse
Power Representation of the
Potential Energy Surface
A. J. C. VARANDAS
Departmento de Quı ´ mica, Universidade de Coimbra, 3004-535 Coimbra,
Portugal
Email: varandas@qtvs1.qui.uc.pt
17.1 Introduction
Regarded as a plot of the molecular energy vs. molecular geometry (or the
equation of the potential energy as a function of the nuclear coordinates), the
concept of potential energy surface (PES) takes its meaning on the masterly
paper of 1927 where the Born–Oppenheimer (BO) approximation 1 was set,
even if the general idea of a PES owes 2 its justiﬁcation to work published 24
years later. 3,4 In the same year, Heitler and London 5 explained the covalent
bond by writing a wave equation for the hydrogen molecule and, shortly after,
London
6 gave the ﬁrst explanation of the attractive dispersion forces that carry
his name. About the same time, Eyring and Polanyi 7 performed the ﬁrst
RSC Theoretical and Computational Chemistry Series No. 6
Reaction Rate Constant Computations: Theories and Applications
Edited by Keli Han and Tianshu Chu
r The Royal Society of Chemistry 2014
Published by the Royal Society of Chemistry, www.rsc.org
408
 01/12/2013 15:17:58.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00408

## Page 429

quantum mechanical calculation of the PES for the reaction H þ H2-H2 þ H,
still a benchmark for theoretical chemists. Indeed, this turned out to be a
formidable problem at the time that could only be solved by introducing clever
and intuitive approximations commonly known as the London–
Eyring–Polanyi (LEP) method.
7,8 In 1955, the LEP approach saw a reﬁnement
by Sato 9,10 who added a convenient parameter to yield the so-called LEPS 7–9
method. The LEPS form may then contain parameters that can be optimized to
ﬁt a speciﬁc reaction, even if diverse, of H 3. For example, if high-level ab initio
information is available for, say, the barrier height, such parameters can be
adjusted to optimize agreement with this information. Otherwise, they can be
adjusted empirically to obtain agreement with experiment. Such a scheme had a
tremendous impact on the ﬁeld of reaction dynamics, with extensions embedding further empiricism being reported even today to approximate the full PES
of species with ﬁve or more atoms.
11
Two other important semi-empirical methods emerged in the 1960s. The
bond–energy–bond–order (BEBO) method of Johnston and Parr 12,13 is a
procedure formulated for calculating the activation energies of hydrogen
transfer reactions from bond energies which, somewhat extended,
14 can be used
to map the full three-dimensional conﬁguration space of a triatomic. The other,
Ellison’s
15 diatomics-in-molecules (DIM) theory, is a general and useful procedure for obtaining PESs for small polyatomic systems using, as the only
input, information on the diatomic curves of the fragments. A diﬃculty with
this lies, however, on how to best account for the overlap between the polyatomic basis functions, a problem that arises in DIM because only the spin and
spatial transformation properties of the product basis functions are utilized.
Because it is necessary to have a complete analytic or numeric description to
determine the overlap between these basis functions, such a lack of information
opens the DIM prescriptions of overlap to some arbitrariness, though of no
signiﬁcant concern for the semi-empirical modeling of the PES.
16–22
Other approaches for predicting attributes of the PES or getting it in global
form have been devised and many reviews on methods along with applications
are available for details.
23–28 However, it is fair to say that progress was relatively meager until the 1980s, mostly due to the enormous popularity of the
LEPS method and the diﬃculty in going beyond triatomic systems.
A signiﬁcant breakthrough in the representation of polyatomic PESs occurred
with the proposition by Murrell and co-workers
29–31 of the so-called manybody expansion (MBE) method. By borrowing the familiar idea of a cluster
development from statistical mechanics and the well-established spin-spatial
Wigner–Witmer32,33 correlation rules (for building the electronic states of a
molecule from those of the constituent fragments), the MBE method warrants,
by built-in construction all proper asymptotic limits, a key request for use in
reaction dynamics. Another important requirement of the PES is to reﬂect the
permutational symmetry of the molecule. Although this was promptly recognized and even used
34–36 to build a PES of the MBE type for systems with up to
four-atoms by using the fully symmetrized integrity basis, 37 only recently have
Braams and Bowman28,38 made its use eﬃcient for application to medium-sized
Putting Together the Pieces: A Global Description of Valence and Long-Range Forces 409
 01/12/2013 15:17:58.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00408
View Online

## Page 430

molecules based on permutational symmetry invariant polynomials. With appealing simplicity, the MBE method is still today one of the most employed
ﬁtting techniques, either in its original form or as a starting point for hybrid
approaches.
39,40
Although our understanding of long-range interactions dates back to the
early days of quantum mechanics, 5 their general use in the ﬁeld of reaction
dynamics is much more recent despite the fact that the electrostatic interaction
was utilized in 1938 by Gorin
41 to predict the rate constant. Indeed, the regions
of the PES dominated by van der Waals (vdW) interactions should play a key
role in rationalizing low-temperature phenomena since the process occurs
suﬃciently slowly for the collision to follow the most attractive path. Thus, a
proper description of the long-range forces is essential when targeting reactive
collisions such as S(
1D) þ H2 at translational energies as low as 0.46 meV. 42 As
expected, quantal eﬀects such as tunneling are key in chemical reactivity at low
energy regimes. Indeed, the large de Broglie wavelength of ultra-cold molecules
entirely changes the nature of reaction dynamics,
43 with energy barriers on the
PES playing a diﬀerent role in this regime as quantum tunneling becomes the
dominant reaction pathway.
44 Note that cold chemistry 45 made it possible to
test theoretical predictions involving barriers down to o15 K.46 Despite its
impact on the observables, it appears that no attempt was made to include
dispersion interactions into the representation of the PES until 1984.
47 The
same applies to the Coulomb (non-pairwise additive in the presence of charge
overlap) and induction interactions which were never explicitly considered until
then onto the description of a global polyatomic PES. In fact, to the best of our
knowledge, the ﬁrst scheme to attempt such an endeavor arose with the double
many-body expansion (DMBE) method,
23,27,47 where the total energy is ﬁrst
split into long- and short-range components, and both subsequently developed
in MBE form.
Often, a PES describes some key region of conﬁguration space better than
others due to the accuracy of the distinct input data sets used for its calibration.
Schemes for switching from one form to another according to convenience have
therefore been devised. However, the selection of the switching form in conﬁguration space can be intricate since for a N-atom system such a space has
t ¼ 3N–6 dimensions. This led to the proposition of switching in the energy
coordinate, the only one common to all potentials irrespective of dimensionality.
48 Known as the energy-switching (ES) approach, the method is general
and easy to use whatever the nature of the PES. 49–54 A compound PES ‘revealing the best of multiple worlds’ can therefore be obtained by merging two
or more functions. Indeed, the ES form should in principle reveal all the
convenient characteristics of the ones participating on its construction.
48,53
The numerical calculation of a ground-state PES by solving the electronic
Schro¨ dinger equation is currently a routine problem in its basics. Yet, this by
no means implies that the electronic structure problem is a solved one. Suﬃce it
to note that the solution of the dynamics problem requires solving the electronic one for any arbitrary arrangement of the nuclei, with the expected results
necessarily depending on the eﬀort (one-electron basis set and electronic
410 Chapter 17
 01/12/2013 15:17:58.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00408
View Online

## Page 431

structure method, i.e. n-electron wave function) put onto its solution. Two ways
forward then emerge. 55 One is to use an aﬀordable basis and theory for calculating the energy and gradient of it by solving the electronic Schro ¨ dinger
equation at any geometry of the nuclei dictated from the integration of the
nuclear equations of motion, this is the direct dynamics approach, also known
as ‘on-the-ﬂy’ dynamics. Despite its attractiveness, use of direct dynamics
suﬀers from well-known limitations including the high level of ab initio theory
required for sound dynamics and the number of propagation time steps .
Conversely, the conventional approach to dynamics consists of ﬁrst solving the
electronic problem at the highest possible level of theory (thus for a limited
number of geometries), ﬁtting the calculated data to some convenient form, and
ﬁnally using the PES so obtained to solve any nuclear dynamics problem. The
focus here will be on the global representation of PESs of high dimensionality,
even though for reasons already made clear the approach is tested only for
systems with up to three atoms.
Because potential modeling can be viewed as a strictly numerical step, splines
as well as semi-numerical techniques became increasingly adopted for ﬁtting
purposes. In fact, numerical (cubic splines
56,57), semi-numerical (like the
Reproducing–Kernel–Hilbert space 39) and interpolation (such as Shepard’s
interpolation) methods39,58,59 have all been much employed for representing the
PES. Additionally, interpolation methods based on the product representation
of PESs have been advocated. Although the latter are by their own nature
multi-dimensional and non-separable, such representations can be valuable for
eﬃciently carrying out the dynamics on polyatomics.
60,61 Because the above
citations are but a small sample of the work in this ﬁeld, suﬃce it to add that
spline ﬁts have not reached beyond the triatomic case due to the threat of
dimensionality: if X points are required to represent adequately a 1 D (onedimensional) cut, a fair representation of the full conﬁguration space will require
an eﬀortpX
3N–6, the so-called X3N–6 explosion. Indeed, only recently has some
promising progress62 been made to overcome such a limitation, although the
required computational eﬀort remains untested as yet. Hence, if high accuracy is
on demand, the approach of choice at present may rely on calculating a bunch of
accurate high-level ab initio points suitably distributed over the conﬁguration
space which then serve to calibrate some kind of global analytic model.
Often the rule rather than the exception, one must go beyond
4 the strict BO
approximation as the molecule involves multiple electronic states with the same
spatial and spin symmetries that may intersect each other along (3 N–8) dimensional seams. Near the degeneracy locus, the nuclear wave functions of
such electronic states will be strongly coupled as reﬂected by the so-called derivative coupling terms. It so happens that the derivative coupling between two
electronic states varies inversely proportional to the diﬀerence of their electronic energies.
63 Accordingly, if the energies of the electronic states are well
separated, the derivative coupling can be neglected and the PES treated as
single-sheeted. Conversely, as the nuclear coordinates approach the locus of
electronic degeneracy, the derivative coupling becomes singular
27,63 and the
PES can only be treated in matrix form. Methods for describing single-sheeted
Putting Together the Pieces: A Global Description of Valence and Long-Range Forces 411
 01/12/2013 15:17:58.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00408
View Online

## Page 432

forms should not then be utilized. In fact, although the ground state of a multisheeted manifold can occasionally be represented with a single-sheeted
formalism using special coordinates,
30 splines and interpolation schemes are
unable to describe the characteristic conical shape of the PESs near the degeneracy seam. Naturally, one can resort to appropriate multi-sheeted formalisms.
27,31 However, it is also possible to transform the electronic basis so
that the derivative couplings are removed to ﬁrst order and the diagonal
adiabatic PESs replaced by a matrix whose elements are smooth functions of
the nuclear displacements, so-called representation in the diabatic basis.
64 It
turns out that nuclear dynamics is more easily performed in this electronic basis
and that methods like the one here developed can easily be applied to model the
elements of the diabatic potential matrix.
65,66 For this, it becomes further advantageous to express the diabatic terms as MBE developments. 67–69
Once available, the reliability of an ab initio based global analytic form can
be enhanced to attain a cm /C0 1 accuracy through a direct ﬁt of spectroscopic
data.70–73 In this, it is used as the target function, with the values of the parameters obtained from the ﬁt to the ab initio data taken as initial estimates to
least-squares ﬁt the spectroscopic data. Such a procedure often requires only
some ﬁne-tuning, so that the PES ﬁts essentially within the respective error
bars both the ab initio and spectroscopic data. It should be recalled that the
form so obtained is strictly an eﬀective potential beyond the BO approximation, since the empirical information has embedded non-adiabatic and other
eﬀects in it.
Notwithstanding the spectacular progress on ab initio methods for calculating the PES, the accuracy achieved in the representation of a diatomic curve
with a compact functional form is far beyond what is typically achieved for a
polyatomic molecule even when this contains an extra atom too many.
Moreover, the involved least-squares work progresses disproportionately to
what one might expect, which makes this a mammoth task before the dynamics
studies can be performed. Because an accurate PES is the keystone for an exact
study of chemical reactivity, we review here some progress that shows promise
in alleviating such a burden.
A brief excursion onto reaction rate constant calculations is now justiﬁed due
to it being the primary focus of the present collection of articles. Because the
theme has been covered extensively both in books
74–77 and review articles, 78–81
of which we mention a few (see also references therein, and the recent report on
a quantum method for atom–diatom reactive scattering in hyperspherical coordinates
82), only a brief address to the point is mandatory.
As Figure 17.1 shows, the concept of PES is key to the ﬁeld of theoretical
physical chemistry in a transverse manner, with applications to reaction dynamics and kinetics being by far the most demanding in that they require
coverage of the entire conﬁguration space of the supermolecule. Thus, the term
‘global PES’ is here meant to imply that the latter describes as accurately as
possible any fragmentation of the supermolecule. Once such a form is available,
it can be utilized for classical, quantum or hybrid classical–quantum calculations of reaction dynamics.
412 Chapter 17
 01/12/2013 15:17:58.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00408
View Online

## Page 433

A summary of the details involved in obtaining the thermalized rate coeﬃcient (rate constant) from the PES is given in Figure 17.2. Hopefully, this
shows in an almost self-explanatory manner the hierarchy of the various dynamics and kinetic attributes as one evolves from the microscopic reaction
probability to the macroscopic rate constant. Also indicated by the dashed line
is the fact that at least steps I and II will be performed simultaneously if a direct
dynamics approach is employed. Thus, when the dynamics is carried out
classically, the unspeciﬁed reactive cross-section is given by s
r ¼ pb2
maxPr, where
bmax is the maximum impact parameter that leads to reaction, and Pr ¼ Nr/N
the reaction probability obtained from Nr reactive trajectories out of a total of
N that were run. In turn, the corresponding standard deviation (68% error) is
given by Dsr ¼ sr½ðN /C0 NrÞ=ðNNrÞ/C1381=2. Similarly, the diﬀerential cross-section
(i.e. the product attribute scanned in the scattering angle d O ¼ 2p sin ydy) assumes the form:

Often, the reagent molecules are prepared such that the initial coordinates and

of the product molecules are assigned by determining the good actions that
determine the vibrational–rotational motions. Then, they are rounded to the

known as the quasi-classical trajectory (QCT) method, shows numerous difﬁculties when one goes beyond the triatom case both because the calculation of

## Page 435

and rotational actions are well deﬁned only if the molecular motion is quasiperiodic.89 Furthermore, the QCT method suﬀers from the neglect of tunneling
and of the so-called zero-point energy (ZPE) leakage problem. This stems from
the nature of the classical treatment itself which allows a continuous transfer of
energy between the various degrees of freedom, thus imposing no limitation to
the depletion of the vibrational energy below the minimum required by
quantum mechanics, i.e. ZPE (see elsewhere
79,90,91 and references therein for
further details). Fixes to the histogram boxing have been suggested with satisfactory success,
92,93 with the approach showing also promise in dealing with
the challenging computational problem of ZPE leakage, at least for the atom
plus diatom reactive case.
93
This chapter is organized as follows. The approach 94 highlighted here is
described in section 17.2. Due to the special class of basis functions employed
for the least-squares ﬁt, the method is named combined hyperbolic inverse
power representation 94 or CHIPR from its initials, and uses the MBE as the
starting point. Still in its infancy, the approach has been tested so far on triatomic systems that have been much studied by other schemes, namely H
3 and
HO2. Reasons for their choice are pinpointed in section 17.3, where the results
obtained are reviewed. Section 17.4 gathers the concluding remarks and prospects for future work.
17.2 The CHIPR Method
17.2.1 The Underlying Many-body Expansion
Let the potential be written in MBE 30,31 form:
VðRÞ¼
X
n
X
Rn2Rt
VðnÞ
S ðRnÞð 17:3Þ
where the summation implies all possible cluster terms in n-body space and S
implies that all terms are written such as to reﬂect the appropriate permutational symmetry of the full molecular aggregate. Thus, t speciﬁes the number
of independent degrees of freedom which, for a N-atom aggregate, does not
necessarily coincide with the number of atom pairs, t
2 ¼ N(N–1)/2. Recall now
that the Decius sets of independent coordinates are locally deﬁned since they
apply at diﬀerent regions of the molecule conﬁguration space. It will then be
convenient to identify t with t
2 in such cases, with no serious consequences 31,95
since transformations between any type of coordinates are straightforward and
both representations describe the same geometry without ambiguity. 96
A few observations are appropriate regarding the various terms in eqn (17.3):
(a) because point-charges interact pair-additively, poles due to the Coulomb
interaction between the nuclei must be contained in the two-body terms; (b)
many-body eﬀects are of relatively small importance at the highly repulsive
regions of the PES (where many electronic states should anyway play a role,
thus limiting the validity of the singled-sheeted representation); and (c) the

## Page 436

Witmer–Witmer spin-spatial correlation rules can in principle be satisﬁed via
pseudo-one-body energy terms. 97,98 Thus, eqn (17.3) is best written as:
VðRÞ¼ Vð1Þ
S ðRÞþ Vð2Þ
S ðRÞþ
X
n42
X
Rn2R
VðnÞ
S ðRnÞð 17:4Þ
where terms with physical peculiarities are separated from the ones ( nZ3)
where a more general treatment is anticipated. Obviously, the permutational
symmetry in the two-body term is satisﬁed by summing over all pairs, while in
the pseudo-one-body terms it must be imposed through adequate constraints in
the switching-function formalism (if required, this is expected to transform the
multi-sheeted molecular potential into a single-sheeted one,
98,99 the only case
considered here—although a generalization to multi-sheeted PESs is straightforward, as discussed later).
Depending on the range of validity, the terms in the MBE can be classiﬁed
into short- and long-range types.
23 The former behave inverse-exponentially
like on the bond distances and thus similarly to overlap integrals. The longrange terms instead have an inverse-power dependence and traditionally appear
when describing the long-range interaction potential via perturbation theory.
100
Suﬃce it to add that such a partition is mimicked by modern ab initio molecular
electronic structure calculations where the total energy is commonly split into a
complete active space self-consistent ﬁeld (CASSCF) part which includes the
non-dynamical correlation arising from quasi-degeneracies and the dynamical
correlation that behaves asymptotically with an inverse-power form.
27,101
Because electronic charge distributions distort when overlapping each other,
the expansion in eqn (17.4) should contain both short- and long-ranged contributions of arbitrary order in the expansion, nZ3. Indeed, only the nuclear
repulsion is expected to be pair-wise additive and hence contained on the twobody energies. This led to our proposition of the DMBE
23,27 method in 1984.
Unfortunately, there is no simple way of extracting the n-body long-range
energies from molecular orbital calculations, which often give the energy as one
or at most two numbers (one referring to the CASSCF energy which contains
the non-dynamical correlation, the other to the dynamical correlation). In fact,
although many of the n-body long-range terms pertain to the latter, they are
diﬃcult to extract as individual contributions.
102–106 Additionally, there will be
terms that can hardly be assigned to one of those two contributions as they may
show an hybrid character: inverse-exponential like for some degrees of freedom, inverse-power like relative to others.
Suppose now that the long-range energy is included only up to the two-body
level. Although the relevance of each contribution is likely to decrease with n if
the series is convergent,
30 one cannot tell a priori that the expansion will converge because the number of such n-body terms raises combinatorially as N!/
n!(N – n)!. Stating it diﬀerently, eqn (17.3) would miss a large number of ( nZ3)
long-range terms. As it turns out, such terms may cancel each other according
to the speciﬁc geometrical arrangement of the aggregate, and hence it may
come as no surprise that such a severely truncated expansion often gives a
416 Chapter 17
 01/12/2013 15:17:58.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00408
View Online

## Page 437

representation20 that is satisfactory enough for many purposes. For the above
reasons, it is appealing to avoid the individual speciﬁcation of the various
n-body terms by choosing instead a suﬃciently ﬂexible, yet formally reliable,
representation that can be calibrated via a global least-squares ﬁt to the ab initio
data. This is the essence of the CHIPR
94 method.
17.2.2 Coordinates and n-Body Representations
Fitting is a term that can involve either interpolation, where an exact ﬁt to the
data is envisaged, or smoothing, in which a smooth model function is constructed that approximately ﬁts the data. In ordinary least-squares, ﬁtting also
means trying to ﬁnd the model function that minimizes the vertical displacement (y-axis deviation of a point from a curve). Obviously, the concept can be
extended to many dimensions. To set the words, consider the 1 D case with the
following model function:
yðxÞ¼
X
M
m¼1
cmfmðxÞð 17:5Þ
where {fm} are given functions, so-called basis functions. If the number of data
points is larger than M, there is generally no exact solution. If fm(x) ¼ xm–1 and
M equals the number of points, one has a typical interpolation problem where
the points (constraints) can be exactly reproduced. One is then led to a system
of linear equations Ac ¼ y, where A is a Vandermonde matrix ða
ij ¼ xj/C0 1
i Þ, c the
vector of all coeﬃcients, and y the vector of constants on the right-hand side of
the linear equations. Explicit formulas for solving Vandermonde systems or the
inverse of such a matrix are well known, and we just note that Vandermonde
problems are usually ill-conditioned by failing to compute accurately the inverse matrix or the solution vector.
107 This may explain why high-order polynomial ﬁts tend to be lumpy. Although the above linear ﬁts generally give a
solution that is unique, increasing M decreases in principle the root mean
squared deviation (rmsd) of the ﬁt but not necessarily warrants an improved
model (a well-behaved form from the physics point of view over the whole
conﬁguration space). The diﬀerence between a mathematical solution and a
sound physical model can therefore be huge. In fact, the propensity for a solution to display unphysical behavior ( e.g. deep holes) tends to increase with
ﬂexibility. This trend is so popular that it is likely to be the major stumbling
block to traditional dynamics.
If the two-body terms are removed from eqn (17.4), one then expects that any
other cusped topological features will be absent by deﬁnition when ﬁtting a
single-sheeted form. Thus, the problem reduces to a polynomial-type ﬁtting as
above once appropriate (optimized) basis functions are chosen. Because extremely accurate two-body curves can nowadays be obtained for most diatomics, this appears indeed a step to take. Moreover, it is well established that a
single global representation of the total molecular potential does not rigorously
become separable in fragment coordinates, except in the case of atom plus
Putting Together the Pieces: A Global Description of Valence and Long-Range Forces 417
 01/12/2013 15:17:58.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00408
View Online

## Page 438

molecule fragments. Although such a single global representation may lead to
only a small artiﬁcial dependency, 28 the reasons given above may provide
compelling arguments for adopting a partial (up to two-body) MBE. Indeed,
such an artiﬁcial dependence could be reduced if the polynomial modeling were
used only for the terms beyond two-body rather than to ﬁt the energy of the full
molecular aggregate.
As a ﬁrst guess, one would expect the interparticle coordinates or their
corresponding displacements with respect to some reference to be the proper
independent variables. It turns out that the latter will not yield a physically
correct description in the limit of asymptotically large interparticle distances,
since they diverge as they go to inﬁnity. One then requires more convenient
variables which permit a correct physical description of the potential in those
fragmentation regions, an issue that is examined further below. Although a
damping function could be utilized to nullify a diverging polynomial form
written in terms of displacement coordinates,
31 an alternative is to smooth-out
the coordinates themselves by using transformed variables. Many propositions
have been advanced for this.
28 Speciﬁcally, we may use transformed coordinates that map the bond distances (or displacements) in such a way that once
transformed they assume some constant value in those regions, thus avoiding a
wrong behavior. Such transformed variables include
28 exponential functions,
Gaussian functions, reciprocal functions and scalar triple-product functions. 65
Reciprocal functions are frequently utilized to describe repulsive walls, but they
may be utilized to describe both this and the long-range parts of the PES.
23,27
Another relevant issue is the localization of the coordinates. While exponential
and reciprocal functions are typically localized at (or near) their origins and
their values are signiﬁcant over a wide range of geometries, Gaussian functions
are localized at speciﬁc origins and their values vanish quickly when moving
away from the origin. In CHIPR,
94 one utilizes hyperbolic functions. Note that
sech shares with exponentials the correct asymptotic behavior and with
Gaussians a localized-like behavior. The approach utilized in CHIPR to deﬁne
the basis functions therefore follows a similar idea but adding some underlying
physics.
It should be emphasized that one must ensure the correct permutational
symmetry of the molecule, which will be warranted by built-in construction via
use of symmetrized sums (here indicated by the prime) of monomials,
Y

p ¼ Q0t

introduce simpliﬁcations on ﬁtting polynomial forms and even in choosing the
points to ﬁt, 35 with substantial progress recently made on applications up to
medium-sized polyatomic systems. 38
Let the coordinates relative to some reference geometry be yp, with p ¼ 1,

17.2.3 Primitives versus Contracted Basis
The idea in CHIPR is to expand each degree of freedom in terms of a
distributed-origin contracted basis set:

p are two constants, and a is the integer in eqn (17.9). Thus, eqn
(17.12) helps on distributing the origins of the sech functions according to the
above two-parameter relation. Although this may resemble the even-tempered
algorithm for one-electron basis sets,
108 it has no other support but from our
recent work. 94,109 The functions fp,a have the quasi-localized character of a
Gaussian function but, unlike this, show the overlap-type dependence expected
for all n-body terms except for short-ranged ones which should display an
inverse-power dependence on the distance. Recall that sech( x) ¼ cosh
/C0 1(x) diesoﬀ exponentially as:
sechðaxÞ¼ 2
expð/C0 axÞþ expðaxÞ ð17:13Þ
Thus, for large values of x, the function sech behaves inverse-exponential
like. Such a behavior may also be advantageous when modeling the cusp-like
behavior that often occurs on potential energy surfaces due to conical
420 Chapter 17
 01/12/2013 15:17:58.

## Page 441

intersections, in particular if a suﬃcient number of such sech functions is included. Although this is applicable to any other complete set of functions, one
hopes that the number of required terms can be smaller in the present formulation due to the exponential built-in character of the basis functions
themselves.
The total number of parameters per degree of freedom will then be equal to
2M þ 2, where the last 2 accounts for the reference geometry and parameter z in
eqn (17.12); to avoid further non-linearities, it has been suggested to ﬁx at a
reasonable value.
94 For the following examples bM is taken as 1/5, a value that
appears to do a good job. 94,109 The location of the distributed origins could in
principle be treated independently and subject to optimization if so desired, a
strategy that has not been attempted thus far. To summarize, the functions in
eqn (17.9) to eqn (17.11) can be viewed as the basis for the representation of the
various degrees of freedom of the PES (assumed here as t
2), and with the
addition of a rotational component and appropriate symmetry projection be
used as a variational basis for rovibrational calculations.
One may expect enhanced diﬃculty when performing the ﬁt with the basis
of eqn (17.11) rather than eqn (17.9). In fact, a good strategy is to use
sech functions for all primitive basis functions but the last, which will employ the
product in eqn (17.11). Even this may be preceded by a ﬁt with a pure sech basis,
thus serving as try-out to optimize the M-th basis of the type in eqn (17.11). This
is actually the strategy adopted in the case studies considered in the next section.
We conclude this survey of CHIPR by emphasizing that the chosen primitives
provide a consistent set that may suitably mimic the proper inverse-power dependence on the atom-diatom distance at long-range while having a convenient
quasi-localized form for least-squares purposes.
17.3 Case Studies
As already noted, the method has thus far been applied only to systems with up
to three atoms.
94,109 Although this may be an handicap for the discussion, it
helps when debating systems that aﬀord a comparison with other methodologies while allowing us to pinpoint some avenues for future investigation.
Although the strategy is to start with accurate diatomic curves from the literature, it would be pitiful if the CHIPR formalism itself were not applicable to
diatomics. This is shown to be not the case in section 17.3.1, while applications
to triatomics
94,109 are surveyed in section 17.3.2.
17.3.1 Two-Body Systems
Although the contracted basis has at least one inverse-power term, this has
been targeted to describe the long-range region rather than the Coulomb cusp
that arises at the united-atom limit of the collapsed diatomic. On the other
hand, one would like to impose the Coulomb cusp in a way similar to what has
been done for the EHFACE2U model (extended Hartree–Fock approximate
correlation energy model including the united-atom limit
23,110). To avoid the

17.3.2.1 Trihydrogen: the Best Studied Neutral Triatomic
Ground-state trihydrogen has long been a benchmark in theoretical and
computational chemistry. 31 As the title suggests, several highly accurate global
ab initio based forms are available. 113–117 Yet, despite its apparent simplicity, it
is the prototype by excellence to challenge the CHIPR scheme here discussed or
any other single-sheeted formalism since it is the paradigm of a PES with a
conical intersection and the best studied Jahn–Teller triatomic system. 27,114
Indeed, because such topological features are more the rule than the exception
among reactive species, H
3 is possibly the best to quantitatively assess how
much they can aﬀect the performance of CHIPR in practical applications. For
convenience, the CHIPR form has been calibrated from a ﬁt-to-points generated from the popular double-sheeted DMBE form
114 for H 3.
First, the contracted basis has been obtained from a ﬁt-to-points along the
line of D 3h symmetry. For this, the two-body energies as predicted from the
most recent 48 EHFACE2U curve for ground-singlet H 2 (this diﬀers slightly
from the one employed in the H 3 DMBE form which stems from an early
HFACE version of the model 47) have been subtracted to obtain the three-body
energy. The aim was therefore to mimic the lowest sheet of the DMBE PES
even though using two-body curves of slightly enhanced accuracy. Figure 17.5
illustrates the results of such a ﬁt as a function of the side length R of the
equilateral triangle formed by the three atoms. Also shown is the result

## Page 445

obtained when the contraction coeﬃcients are determined from a non-linear
least-squares ﬁt (see later). The contraction containing six parameters (b6) is
shown to provide a good ﬁt inside the domain covered by the ﬁtted points, even
though the two larger basis show a somewhat better performance. Interestingly,
when the number of coeﬃcients equals the number of ﬁtted points and a solution exists, the ﬁt appears stable and capable of reproducing the data even
outside the ﬁtted domain.
Figure 17.6 shows the performance of the CHIPR ﬁts as a function of the size
of the polynomial expansion in the basis b8. The same initial guess has been
utilized for the vector of coeﬃcients (both linear and non-linear) in all ﬁts. The
ﬁrst observation refers to the slow decrease of the error with size of the polynomial expansion after an initial steep variation. This can be attributed to the
diﬃculty in eﬃciently describing the conical intersection at geometries with D
3h
symmetry. Indeed, the rmsd can hardly become smaller than a few tenths of a
kcal mol/C0 1. We further observe that the onset for chemical accuracy arises with
31 least-squares parameters, thus equivalent to a full polynomial expansion up
to the power 7. Remarkably, all solutions up to this point have been found
exempt of unphysical holes at very short distances while displaying an acceptable behavior at long range. However, except for the power 9, other highorder polynomials tend to yield a slight but expectedly unphysical hole at very
short distances, typically for structures with C
2v or higher symmetry. This is

## Page 446

likely due to extra ﬂexibility which is channeled to minimize the error in the
data at such regions (where the three-body energy tends to decrease in magnitude and eventually become rather negative as illustrated in Figure 17.5).
Because such artifacts can easily be eliminated without any serious implication,
94 they are not of concern here. We emphasize instead the usefulness of
the contracted basis to help explaining why such unphysical features
may occur.
Figure 17.7 shows a CHIPR ﬁt (I) to 9477 points with a total of 337 linear
parameters in the polynomial expansion, thus via a symmetrized sum of
monomials up to power 20! Note that rather than using the contracted basis
(and hence solving a linear problem), the optimization of the primitive functions has been allowed, yielding an rmsd of 0.425 kcal mol
/C0 1. Figure 17.5
compares the resulting fully optimized contracted basis b8 with the one obtained from the above ﬁt to the D3h cut. Interestingly, it keeps much of the
original shape but diﬀers as one might expect on the details. This suggests that
the use of the original contracted basis may itself not be exempt of some
ground, although likely to be less eﬃcient.
We may now ask how the method performs when only a small number of
points is aﬀordable since this is a critical issue for large polyatomic systems.
This has been investigated by comparing the CHIPR form (ﬁt I) obtained
above with another obtained from ﬁt II to a grid of 420 distinct points, also
with 345 ( ¼ 337 þ 8) parameters. Note that no points have been considered for
+HHH ¼ 601, with an extra 75 points added to avoid solving a non-linear
system of equations. Interestingly, ﬁt II shows no unphysical features and an
