## Page 447

unweighted rmsd of 0.287 kcal mol /C0 1. Visible diﬀerences were found only for
equilateral triangular geometries where the method is known to perform poorly
due to the conical intersection.
The rmsd for the ﬁt in Figure 17.7 takes therefore into account the relatively
larger errors that are obtained as the valence angle approaches 60 1, where the
crossing seam of the two lowest doublet PESs is known to occur. One then
wonders what to pursue for improvement. Tentatively, the suggestion might be
the addition of an extra three-body term to speciﬁcally ﬁt such regions of
conﬁguration space. The hope would then lie in that such a term would do the
job very much like a sum of Gaussian functions is often used to simulate the
cusp behavior of a Slater-type atomic orbital. Of course, the use of non-analytic
JT coordinates
30 could be another possibility. More adequate though would be
the (quasi-)diabatization of the PES manifold (preferably as suggested elsewhere67,69) followed by a ﬁt of the diabatic matrix elements. All such topics
justify investigation.
Table 17.3 compares the attributes of the CHIPR I and II forms with the
corresponding values for the lowest adiabatic sheet of the DMBE form. The
agreement is seen to be quite good, although enhanced results may be obtained
by redesigning the ﬁtted grid and weights. The location of such attributes is
shown in Figure 17.8, which shows the full PES as a relaxed triangular plot in
hyperspherical coordinates. Also indicated is the location of the conical intersection where the CHIPR ﬁt is the least accurate; a cut along the diameter
shows a smooth maximum as in a narrowly avoided crossing rather than the
sharp cusp that is expected in a true conical intersection.
17.3.2.2 The Paradigmatic Hydroperoxyl Radical
The hydroperoxyl radical is key in various research ﬁelds: in combustion,
H þ O
2 is the ‘single most important reaction’; 119–123 in atmospheric chemistry,
the reaction:
Oð3PÞþ OHðX2PÞÐ HO?
2 ! H þ O2ðX3S/C0
g Þð 17:15Þ
plays a crucial role on explaining the removal of stratospheric ‘odd-oxygen’ and
mesospheric high-vibrationally excited OH. 124,125 Moreover, eqn (17.15) is a
well-known prototype of a chemical reaction with signiﬁcant long-range forces.

## Page 448

Due to being highly exothermic and occurring with no intrinsic barrier, the
reaction in eqn (17.15) is also crucial as a source of molecular oxygen even at
very low temperatures,126 which has attracted much interest due to the so-called
interstellar ‘oxygen problem’; 127 measurements128 suggest an abundance of O 2
in the cores of the interstellar clouds that is orders of magnitude lower than
predicted by the latest astrochemical models.
129 A possible cause of such an
uncertainty is the low-temperature rate constant of eqn (17.15) which has not
yet been accurately determined.
129
The PES of HO 2(X2A00) has been studied at many levels of ab initio
theory.21,106,111,130–143 Three PESs have been most utilized for dynamics studies79,81,93,125,144 (a vast number of other references can be obtained through
cross-referencing): Melius–Blint; 130 double many-body expansion (DMBE
IV111); and diatomics-in-molecules (DIM) 21,145 (see elsewhere 22 for a dressedDIM form which partly shares the DMBE formalism). Although DMBE IV is
likely the most used due to being remarkably adequate

## Page 449

reaction rate constants, a spline interpolant to 17 341 high-level ab initio
points146 calculated at the internally contracted multi-reference conﬁguration
interaction101 level with the Davidson correction and an augmented quadruplezeta correlation consistent basis set 147–149 (MRCI þ Q/AVQZ) is also available.
In fact, many theoretical investigations 57,145,150–156 of the O þ OH rate constant
below 50 K have utilized the latter, so-called XXZLG, 157 or both this and
DMBE IV. Experimentally, the O þ OH reaction has also been much studied,129,158–160 and recommendations of rate constants available. 161,162 In particular, the most recent measurements by Carty et al. 129 in the temperature
range 39 rT/Kr143 have shown no variation with temperature, which led
them to conclude that it would largely remain constant between 39 and 10 K,
temperatures typical of cold interstellar clouds.
Figure 17.9 shows contractions b6 and b8 for the OO and OH degrees of
freedom. They were parameterized from ﬁts to uniform grids along cuts for
stretching at the equilibrium angle of +HOO ¼ 104.291 (the equilibrium bond
distances are R
OO ¼ 2.5143 a0 and ROH ¼ 1.8345 a0). Note that b6 extrapolates
nicely for short distances in the case of O–OH, as shown by the unﬁtted point at
1.0 a
0 and corroborated by others for even shorter distances. The situation is
more problematic for short H–OO where the points display an irregular behavior. However, such regions of conﬁguration space should be less relevant,
and hence the basis b6 for OO and b8 for OH or just b6 for both have been
utilized.
Two HO
2 ﬁts will be illustrated: one to points generated from the popular
single-sheeted DMBE IV and the other to energies previously utilized in the

## Page 450

XXZLG interpolant. To avoid contamination of the three-body energies with
two-body ones, the two-body EHFACE2U potential curves from DMBE IV
have been utilized for the ﬁrst ﬁt. In fact, the EHFACE2U model has also been
adopted for the second ﬁt, but calibrated from the corresponding two-body
energies.
The same grid of triatomic geometries has been used to perform both ﬁts. It
has been extracted from the points calculated
57 for the spline interpolant.
Providing a uniform 3 D grid for distances between 1 a 0 to 15 a 0, some energies
for very short distances may have to be guessed possibly due to lack of convergence of the MRCI method.
101 Some data selection was therefore unavoidable, with the actual points selected for the CHIPR ﬁts still amounting to
a total of 14 070.
109 Least-squares weights were then assigned, with the reader
being directed elsewhere 94,109 for details.
Given the large number of points, a polynomial expansion up to the power
15 in the contracted basis has been considered, which involved (including the 12
parameters in b6 for OO and OH) a total of 425 parameters and hence a ratio of
33 points per parameter. Table 17.4 gathers the accumulated rmsd of both ﬁts.
Remarkably, it is o1 kcal mol
/C0 1 for ﬁt II and nearly an order of magnitude
smaller for ﬁt I. No need to increase the number of parameters was felt to be
justiﬁed for ﬁt II, since HO
2 shows a conical intersection that can be only
roughly approximated as a narrowly avoided crossing via a single-sheeted
formalism like the present one.
A summary of all topographical features of the PES is shown in Figure 17.10,
while a numerical characterization is given for ﬁt II in Table 17.5. When
available, the CHIPR results for ﬁt II show good agreement with the XXZLG
ones. Unfortunately, it turned out to be impossible to characterize the forces of
the latter PES either due to numerical diﬃculties in calculating second derivatives and, for linear geometries, accurate energies and gradients themselves.
However, the general good agreement between the two forms can be seen from
Figure 17.11 which shows the calculated isotropic component for the H–O
2 and
O–OH interactions when the diatomic is at equilibrium. Note that diﬀerences
occur at short O–OH separations, but these sample even shorter atom–atom
ones during the averaging procedure. For example, at R
O–HO ¼ 3a 0, the calculation of Vn samples OH distances as short as 1.2 a 0 where both the energy of

the diatomic and triatomic are likely to be less accurate. Moreover, the O þ OH

disagreement. Also visible from Figure 17.11 is the diﬃculty met by the spline
interpolant in describing both short- and long-range regions of the PES, with
the latter being known to play a key role in dynamics at cold and ultra-cold
regimes.
145,156

## Page 452

dips have been located in the long range region of XXZLG, which may have
unpredictable consequences in the dynamics at ultralow temperatures.
It should be added that the H–O 2 vdW minimum is predicted to be along the
direction of rupture of the H–O bond in HO 2 rather than at linear geometries.109 This is visible from Figure 17.13 which compares the three PESs for H
moving around an equilibrium oxygen molecule. Note that CHIPR is slightly
more attractive than XXZLG at the H–O
2 vdW region but the diﬀerences in
topography are minor. The above implies the existence of a saddle point connecting the H /C1/C1/C1O
2 long-range minimum to the covalent one. Such a feature
has been known 22,135 except for the fact that the barrier is now predicted to
occur below the H þ O2 dissociation asymptote. The situation then parallels the
one encountered for O þ HO in that it is the result from of an avoided crossing
between the lowest and ﬁrst excited 2A00 PESs ( 2B1/2A2 in C2v; 2S /C0 /2p in
CNv).22 The eﬀect of electrostatic forces should not itself be ignored. 109
Of course, one may wish to go further and ask how CHIPR performs on
extracting three-body long-range energies from the ‘single-number’ MRCI results. Because CASSCF and dynamical correlation energies are unavailable to
us, this can only be done in a tentative manner by subtracting the sum of twobody energies from the total energy. The result is shown in Figure 17.14. It is

## Page 453

seen that the total three-body energy is more attractive when the O atom attacks the H end of OH as one might anticipate due to H-bond formation. Also
visible is a shallow dip at the onset of the repulsive three-body energy, with no
apparent special signiﬁcance. In turn, a similar analysis for H moving around
an equilibrium O
2 displays a three-body long-range energy term that is rather
small but repulsive, as shown in Figure 17.15.
The spectroscopic properties of ﬁt II can be assessed through vibrational
calculations.167 The only experimental values available are for the (0,0,1),
(0,1,0) and (1,0,0) fundamentals 168,169 and the (2,0,0) overtone. 170 Compared
with them, the results calculated from CHIPR show an rmsd of 5.33 cm /C0 1, with
corresponding values of 157 7.2 cm/C0 1 and171 123.7 cm/C0 1 for XXZLG and
DMBE IV.
The performance of the CHIPR form can now be examined through calculations of the rate constant for the O þ OH reaction, which were carried out by
running quasi-classical trajectories at low and very low temperatures, 109 where
experimental data is available129,159,160 as well as the results of calculations with
XXZLG.155 In fact, there is no reason to distrust a classical model as long as a
few partial waves are involved. 145 Note that the ZPE leakage problem 93 (and
references therein) of classical mechanics has been ignored as well as any possibility of a distribution of OH under non-local thermodynamic equilibrium.
144
Figure 17.16 shows that there is good agreement between the CHIPR and
XXZLG results from moderate temperatures upwards, although diﬀerences up
to B25% are visible at low temperatures, a result attributable to having

explicitly introduced long-range interactions into CHIPR. Because these should

Figure 17.16 also shows that the rate constants can be modeled with the
expression

where scap is the cross-sections for capture and siso for the H-bond-to-covalent
isomerization;109 ge(T) has the meaning in eqn (17.2). Thus, it may justify

Oð3PÞþ OHðX2PÞÐð O /C1/C1/C1HOÞ? Ð HO?

Moreover, siso is predicted to peak at hEtriB10 K while decaying exponentially
onwards,173 thus manifesting a non-capture behavior. Such a behavior has been
attributed109 to isomerization, since most trajectories form a H-bond complex
while transiting to the covalent minima at low energies 109 (see Figure 17.10). It
may also lead one to expect long-lived resonances, since some intramolecular

129,159,160 (open squares, diamonds, and hexagons). Also shown
are the recommended 161,162 (rec.) data (open up-pointing triangle and

triangles and open circle
144), as well as the ones reported 155 for XXZLG
(second curve from bottom in double-dotted style). When available, the
error bars are also indicated. For enhanced visibility, the results for
CHIPR and DMBE (solid circles and down-pointing triangles, respectively) are shown with 95% ( /C172Ds) error bars.

 01/12/2013 15:17:58.

## Page 458

energy transfer is required to promote a torque capable of ‘pushing’ the trajectory toward the saddle point. Also visible from Figure 17.16 is the fact that
the rate constant calculated from CHIPR attains a maximum at 50K due to
prevalence of the T
1/2 dependence of the averaged collision velocity; the decrease in velocity is not compensated by the increase in the cross section at low
temperatures. While the agreement with the most recent experimental data is as
good as for XXZLG, it is somewhat enhanced when compared with traditional
data.
129,159,160,162 In turn, the good agreement with DMBE IV may explain why
the latter remains amongst the reference potentials for HO 2.157 Thus, the results
from CHIPR support both theoretical 145 and experimental 129 conclusions in
that the rate constant is unlikely to vanish for Tr10 K.
17.4 Conclusions
While noting its relationship to other methods, we have shown in this review
that the CHIPR approach can oﬀer an accurate scheme to ﬁt a smooth function
of the many-body expansion type to a multidimensional large data set using an
optimized basis. Although the method is in its infancy and the applications
cover only up to triatomic systems, these provide challenging enough tests to
warrant promise on its use for larger polyatomics. One of the systems studied,
trihydrogen, is the best prototype of a Jahn–Teller system and hence oﬀers a
good test to potential errors that may occur when forcefully ﬁtting data to a
single-sheeted form, since cusp-like features are ubiquitous in most reactive
systems with practical interest. Indeed, the appearance of such a topological
feature in the paradigmatic hydroperoxyl radical may explain by itself the
disparity observed when comparing the rmsd of two ﬁts carried out for this
system: one mimicking the popular single-sheeted (cusp-free) DMBE IV form,
the other chosen to model energies calculated at a high-level level of ab initio
theory.
To summarize, the CHIPR method has proven to be: (1) robust, by systematically yielding enhanced results upon basis set improvement; (2) physically sound by inducing a successful ﬁt via use of a basis and distributed origins;
and (3) capable of ﬁtting all regions of the PES without manifesting unphysical
behavior either at short- or long-range distances. Such characteristics are
largely unprecedented. Advantageously, the method may be combined with a
pragmatic scheme like energy switching
48 if spectroscopic accuracy is demanded. The method can also be straightforwardly adapted to other choices of
primitive functions if need be. Regarding applications to dynamics, the CHIPR
form reported here is possibly the most reliable one available for HO 2, with the
results of quasi-classical trajectories run for the O þ OH reaction opening a
promising perspective for accurate quantum dynamics studies.
Acknowledgements

Competitividade – COMPETE’ and national funds under the auspices of

## Page 466

CHAPTER 18
Extension of Marcus Rate
Theory to Electron Transfer
Reactions with Large Solvation

18.1 Introduction
The Marcus theory of charge transfer reactions in solution has provided a very
simple two-chemical state picture, based on two intersecting parabolas, that has
made it possible to understand the experimental data, to interpret them
quantitatively and to make predictions.
1 In the early versions, the solvent was
modeled either by a dielectric continuum or a harmonic phonon bath. 1–4
Starting with the pioneering work of Warshel in the early 1980s, 5 the Marcus
theory has fostered the computational modeling and molecular interpretation
of those reactions using force ﬁeld molecular dynamics (MD) and, more
recently, ﬁrst principle molecular dynamics (FPMD).
6,7 One of Warshel’s main
contributions was to exhibit the so-called energy-gap coordinate, DE, as the
relevant microscopic reaction coordinate, as suggested originally by Marcus 8
and to show that, to a very good approximation, this quantity obeys a
RSC Theoretical and Computational Chemistry Series No. 6
Reaction Rate Constant Computations: Theories and Applications
Edited by Keli Han and Tianshu Chu
r The Royal Society of Chemistry 2014
Published by the Royal Society of Chemistry, www.rsc.org
446
 01/12/2013 15:18:07.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00446

## Page 467

Gaussian statistics. 9 When translated to free energies, this property gives raise
to the Marcus’s two parabola picture. Since the study of Kuharski et al. 10 for
ferrous–ferric ions exchange in water, this Gaussian property has been veriﬁed
many times, either for electron or proton transfers in solution or in complex
environment such as proteins,
6,11–18 and with force-ﬁeld or ﬁrst principle
MD.19–23 It has also been recognized that the strict Gaussian assumption is
equivalent to a linear response approximation. 24,25 It implies identical solvent
ﬂuctuations in the two states and thus identical free energy curvatures for those
states.
26 Departure from linear response can be estimated through free energy
integration methods using DE as the relevant reaction coordinate.
Although this fact is sometimes overlooked, it has been observed in the
literature that the Gaussian assumption (or linear response) is by essence
incompatible with the existence of diﬀerent solvation states in reactants and
products, and thus of diﬀerent curvatures of the free energy wells or two
diﬀerent Gaussian widths.
26 Such situation can occur when the species
solvation has a diﬀerent character in reactants and products, as postulated
theoretically by Kakitani and Mataga,
27–29 and illustrated by Carter and
Hynes30 for the MD simulation of a neutral to ionic pair internal conversion in
a polar solvent. The computed free energy curvature corresponding to solvent
ﬂuctuations around the neutral pair or the ion pair do diﬀer by a factor 1.6.
30
Small et al. 31 have shown that such a situation also occurs for light-induced
charge transfers involving a large electronic polarizability enhancement. More
recently, Blumberger encountered a similar deviation from the linear response
regime underlying the Marcus theory in the case of the Cu
21/Cu1 oxidoreduction reaction, which he studied using FPMD simulations. 32 This
nonlinear eﬀect was attributed to the chemical speciﬁcity of copper ions with
respect to water and a strong coordination change from Cu
1 to Cu 21, going
from a dihydrate to a ﬁve-fold distorted pyramidal structure. We have shown a
similar breakdown of Marcus oxidation theory for the Ag
1/Ag0 redox couple,
where the reaction goes from a neutral to a charged species, and involves a
drastic change in the solvation structure and dynamics. 33 In that case, the very
diﬀerent solvation regime for Ag 1/Ag0 was attributed to the neutral rather than
charged character of Ag 0 combined to its exceptional excitonic character
generating a very loose hydration structure.
Using the previous examples for illustration, we have recently proposed two
complementary theoretical extensions of Marcus theory. With the observation
that a clear distinction should be made between a chemical state (with given
electronic distribution) and a solvation state (with given solvent structural and
ﬂuctuations properties), the ﬁrst extension appears as a direct generalization of
Marcus theory based on a two chemical state/two solvation states picture
rather than the two-chemical-state/one-solvation-state picture underlying
Marcus theory. The second, separate, although complementary extension is a
non-Gaussian theory.
33
The purpose of this chapter is ﬁrst to review the basics of Marcus rate theory
in a modern statistical mechanics language involving the microscopic—
although collective—energy gap coordinate. In the same language, we also
Extension of Marcus Rate Theory to Electron Transfer Reactions 447
 01/12/2013 15:18:07.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00446
View Online

## Page 468

present our non-Gaussian extension to that theory which applies to the cases
described above, where the solvation properties are markedly diﬀerent in the
reactant and product chemical states. This extension provides a global and
coherent picture that reconciles the observation of two Gaussians of diﬀerent
widths in reactant and product states with Marcus theory. Note that several
extensions of Marcus theory have been proposed in the literature, either relying
on limiting cases of the harmonic bath Kubo–Toyozawa Hamiltonian
31,34 or
focused on corrections to the dielectric continuum solvent model. 35 We propose
here a statistical mechanics approach, involving a minimum number of relevant
physical parameters, which is not committed to a speciﬁc underlying
Hamiltonian or to a (generalized) dielectric solvent description. With respect to
ref. 33, we discuss the implications of the model and report the associated rate
formula and the rate–free energy relationships.
This chapter is organized as follows. Section 18.2 describes the theoretical
foundation of Marcus theory, formulated in a molecular rather than dielectric
continuum solvent framework. Its non-Gaussian extension is presented in
section 18.3 and its implications for rate calculations are discussed in section
18.4. The theory is then confronted to various molecular simulation results in
section 18.5 and a conclusion is proposed in section 18.6.
18.2 Marcus Theory: A Gaussian Solvation (GS) Model
The theoretical model discussed here can be applied to a general charge transfer
reaction of the form:
D
/C0 /C0 A ! D /C0 A/C0: ð18:1Þ
(D and A being possibly charged molecular entities). For notational
convenience, the reactant and product states are noted as 0 and 1, respectively.
In such notation, the reactant state 0 is evidenced as a reference, unperturbed
state. This also refers to a light-induced electron transfer where the reactant
state 0 is the ground state and the product state 1 is an excited one, so that the
notion of vertical transition energy has a clear meaning.
We start by recalling the basis of Marcus theory, formulated with the
microscopic energy gap variable DE instead of the macroscopic solvent polarization variable used originally by Marcus.
1 The derivation is standard but
makes it possible to introduce the various quantities of interest and for the
relationships to be generalized.
We ﬁrst introduce the energy gap coordinate, here also the vertical transition
energy, as the diﬀerence between the product and reactant (or excited and
ground state) energies for a given solvent conﬁguration, R
I (denoting the
position of all atoms):
DE fRI gðÞ ¼ E1 fRI gðÞ /C0 E0 fRI gðÞ : ð18:2Þ
In the ‘molecular’ Marcus theory presented here, it plays the crucial role of the
order parameter.

The logarithm of this probability distribution determines, upon a constant, the

WZðEÞ¼ AZ /C0 kBT ln pZðEÞ; ð18:4Þ

## Page 471

variable, and all the derivations above follow. Gaussian approximation or
linear response are thus equivalent qualiﬁers. 25
Note that extensions of the one-Gaussian approximation were proposed very
early by Marcus himself to incorporate molecular vibrations coupled to the
electron transfer (and the so-called inner sphere),
3,36 and later on for treating
proton transfer reactions, 4 reduction induced dissociation 37,38 or proton coupled electron transfer (PCET) reactions. 39–41 Those extensions amount to
introduce extra coordinates in addition to the solvent, generally Gaussian
variables, that are coupled to the charge transfer and lead to reorganization
energies that add to the solvent one. In this work we instead focus on the DE
collective coordinate itself and investigate how the (nonlinear) coupling to all
other coordinates results in a non-Gaussian behavior for DE.
18.3 Extension to a Non-Gaussian Solvation (NGS)
Model
An obvious extension of the Marcus approach consists of departing from the
Gaussian assumption of eqn (18.7) and eqn (18.8), and postulating from the
beginning a non-harmonic form for the Landau free energy in the reactant
state 0:
W

In Figure 18.1, we display the behaviors of DEZ for various sets of parameters

The explored parameter range will appear realistic in the light of the applications described in section 18.5. It can be seen that, apart from the straight line
obtained when Marcus theory is fulﬁlled, a variety of behaviors can be expected, going from very concave to very convex, or to sigmoidal shape. The
breakdown of linearity is here manifest.
It should be noted, however, that the quantity DE
Z is not an experimental
observable but it can be tested by molecular simulations; this is done in section
18.5. We turn below to possible experimental evidences of the breakdown
of Marcus Gaussian solvation theory, namely the study of reaction rate/free
energy relationships.

18.4 Incidence of the NGS Model on Marcus Rate
Theory
On experimental ground, Marcus theory is usually applied to interpret reaction
rate/free energy relationships. More precisely, a family of electron transfer
reactions is studied that are assumed to have the same degree of coupling to the
solvent but diﬀerent gas-phase excitation energies. In that framework, the
formulation above has to be slightly adapted; the instantaneous energy gap DE
has to be decomposed into its solvent-related part E (often reduced to the
electrostatic energy gap) and the solvent-independent, gas-phase vertical excitation energy E
0, i.e. DE ¼ E þ E0. All the considerations described above hold
for the properties of the solvent-related energy gap with the following
modiﬁcation of eqn (18.6):
W
1ðEÞ¼ W0ðEÞþ E þ E0 ð18:27Þ
The electron transfer rate is given by k / 1
bh exp /C0bDW #/C0/C1
, where DW # is the
activation free energy. The experimental reaction rate/free-energy relationship
involves to plot log k (or, up to a constant, /C00.43/C2bDW # ) as function of the
reaction free energy, DW. That quantity can be tuned by means of the intrinsic
reaction free-energy, E0. Obviously, since non-adiabaticity of the electron
transfer process is assumed, the transition state is determined by the condition
W1(E) ¼ W0(E), implying E ¼/C0 E0 and:
DW # ¼ W0ð/C0E0Þ/C0 W0ðDE0Þ

signiﬁcantly diﬀerent from that of an ideal inverted parabola.
18.5 Application to Realistic Electron Transfer Systems
A number of cases are reported in the experimental literature where substantial
deviations from the standard one-Gaussian Marcus theory are observed. They
certainly deserve re-examination in the light of the non-Gaussian theory proposed above. Instead we consider here a few cases that have been pointed out
recently in the computational literature and for which unambiguous quantitative comparisons can be drawn. These are the case of the Cu
1 to Cu 21 oxidation reaction studied by ﬁrst-principle molecular dynamics simulations
(FPMD) by Blumberger,
32 the Ag 0 to Ag 1 reaction studied recently in our
group,33 and the photo-reaction molecular model of Small et al. 31 involving
large electronic polarizability shifts.
We begin by discussing the case of the Ag 0-Ag1 þ e/C0 reaction for which we
could compute the Z-dependent energy gap, DEZ ¼hDE({RI}iZ, using FPMD
and the methodology described in refs. 19–23 and 32. See the simulation details
explained in ref. 33. Six simulations were generated, corresponding to
increasing values of Z (Z ¼ 0, 0.2, 0.35, 0.5, 0.8, 1.0), making it possible to estimate the reaction free energy, DA, through the computation of DE
Z in each
window and the integration formula, eqn (18.14). The free energy curves WZ (E)
and the associated probabilities pZ (E) can be reconstructed too, using weighted
histogram techniques.42 The DEZ vs Z curve obtained by simulation is displayed

## Page 475

in Figure 18.3. The estimated statistical errors bars are 0.03–0.1 eV, i.e. about
the size of the symbols in the ﬁgure. A highly nonlinear sigmoidal proﬁle is
obtained, which departs notably from the linear Marcus prediction, even more
so that the previous result by Blumberger
32 for Cu 21/Cu1 (see also
Figure 18.5). In the same ﬁgure we present the best ﬁt corresponding to the
NGS models. It can be seen that it is able to reproduce the nonlinear sigmoidal
behavior with very good accuracy. The corresponding parameters are given in
Table 18.1.
The necessity of the NGS model with respect to the straight Marcus theory is
corroborated by the fact that there are indeed diﬀerent structural and dynamic
properties of the solvent around a neutral atom such as Ag
0 (presenting furthermore a strong excitonic character 43) or around its charged cationic species
Ag1. This assertion is substantiated in Figure 18.4 where are displayed the Ag 0/
Hw and Ag 1/Hw radial distribution functions computed from the FPMD
simulations for Z ¼ 0 and Z ¼ 1, respectively. The water structure around Ag 1 is
typical of a positive ion, with pronounced ﬁrst and second shells around r ¼ 3A˚
and r ¼ 4.5 A˚ , respectively, whereas the structure around Ag 0 appears very
smooth and ﬂoppy, even more so than for a hydrated rare gas atom. This
ﬂoppiness can be attributed to the hyperpolarizability of Ag
0, which can be
regarded as a Ag 1/e/C0 system with a mobile electron interacting with the surrounding water. 43
Figure 18.5 presents similarly the DEZ results for the Cu 21/Cu1 redox couple
and the comparison with the NGS model (see the corresponding parameters in
Table 18.1). The simulation data are those of Blumberger.
32 They were interpreted by the author as the interplay between two Marcus linear curves
intersecting close to Z ¼ 0.5 and corresponding to the transition between two
solvent coordination regimes. In our interpretation suggested by application of
the NGS model, it is rather a two solvation-state picture that manifests itself by
a (slightly) sigmoidal curve rather than by the intersection of two linear

performing quantitatively better than the Q-model (see, for example, the predicted diabatic free energy curves at high energy gap).
18.6 Conclusions
In this chapter, we have exposed the Marcus theory of charge transfer reaction
in solution and rephrased it in a now standard, modern statistical mechanics
language in terms of the microscopic energy gap variable. The key assumption
in Marcus developments is to assume that this collective variable obeys an exact
Gaussian statistics. This was shown to be equivalent to a linear response
approximation.
We have also given attention to the fact that this straight Gaussian assumption can be violated. This is the case when solvation has a quite diﬀerent
character in the reactant and product states, so that the solvent ﬂuctuations are
diﬀerent. Such eﬀect was recently evidenced by FPMD simulations for the
elementary oxidation reaction Cu
1- Cu21 þ e/C0, and we found it ampliﬁed in
the case of Ag 1/Ag0, with a transition between a rather labile coordination
around a neutral, although extremely polarizable, atom to a more rigid

 01/12/2013 15:18:07.

## Page 480

four-fold coordination around its charged cation. Violations of Marcus theory
have also been pointed out on other diﬀerent occasions. 26,30,31
To account for such a situation, we introduced a non-Gaussian
solvation model which starts from a more phenomenological assumption,
irrespective of any underlying Hamiltonian. Given a non-harmonic, generic,
polynomial form for the reactant free energy curve, the peculiar statistical
mechanics of electron transfer reactions, formulated in the microscopic energy
gap coordinate, can be carried out and exploited. The theory relies on two extra
parameters with respect to Marcus theory, and was shown to be relevant for the
reactions studied in this work, with a high degree of precision. Such model is
expected to remain valid for a wide range of physicochemical situations where
linear response theory is possibly violated, leading to various nonlinear behaviors. In simulations, a clear manifestation is the nonlinear, possibly sigmoidal shape of the curve DEhi
Z vs Z; this is, unfortunately, not experimentally
observable. The most striking predicted experimental consequence is the
non-parabolic behavior of the activation free energy/reaction free energy relationship that has been reported several times in the past. Those experimental
results should be reinterpreted carefully in terms of the present nonGaussian model.

CHAPTER 19
Theoretical Studies on
Mechanism and Kinetics of
Atmospheric Chemical Reactions
L. SANDHIYA AND K. SENTHILKUMAR*
Department of Physics, Bharathiar University, Coimbatore-641 046, India
*Email: ksenthil@buc.edu.in
19.1 Introduction
The chemistry of molecules consists of three major modules: molecular architecture (structure); molecular dynamics (conformational analysis); and molecular transformation (chemical reactions). The molecular architecture
consists of the basic principles of molecular structure and it deals with the
atomic structure, orbitals, hybridization and bonding. Molecular dynamics
deals with the molecular motion involving rotation around chemical bonds,
steric interactions, torsional strain and properties associated with the conformational changes. Molecular transformation accounts for bond formation
and bond breaking within the molecule or between molecules, which is generally called the chemical reaction, and consists of two major aspects, reaction
mechanism and kinetics. The third module is one of the major areas of
chemistry. This aims to understand the reaction mechanism and its manipulation to reduce the reaction barrier, improve stereoselectivity, increase product
yield, or suppress undesirable side reactions.
Over the past decade, computational studies through theoretical techniques
have been identiﬁed as best tools to study the chemical reactions. The
RSC Theoretical and Computational Chemistry Series No. 6
Reaction Rate Constant Computations: Theories and Applications
Edited by Keli Han and Tianshu Chu
r The Royal Society of Chemistry 2014
Published by the Royal Society of Chemistry, www.rsc.org
462
 17/11/2013 09:29:44.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00462

## Page 483

theoretical methods provide a prime methodology to search for novel chemistry, to understand a complex, multi-step reaction sequence and to construct
reaction mechanism of unknown phenomena observed in laboratory studies.
1
In laboratory studies, the reaction mechanism and kinetics of chemical reactions are studied through the state-of-the-art techniques such as ﬂash photolysis, low-pressure discharge-fast-ﬂow systems and spectroscopic methods.
2
Though these methods provide accurate rate constants, there are still uncertainties due to diﬃculties associated with experimental techniques. Also, the
accurate prediction of reaction pathways through experimental techniques is
diﬃcult, since these methods do not isolate intermediates and transition states
due to their instability. However, theory complements these shortcomings by
providing methods to locate transition states and intermediates, thereby
modeling a reaction pathway.
3 Furthermore, the temperature and pressure
dependence of the kinetic parameters beyond the laboratory conditions is accessible through theoretical techniques. In recent years, understanding chemical
reactions in the atmosphere has been an important area of research.
Atmospheric chemistry is a subject which focuses on the chemical composition of the Earth’s atmosphere, and the underlying physical and chemical
phenomena which control the sources of chemical species from anthropogenic
and natural emissions. Atmospheric chemistry comprises a vast number of
reactions occurring through a variety of intermediates and transition states.
These reactions involve sequential or concerted reaction mechanisms and give
rise to new products which may be harmless, harmful or even hazardous to life
on Earth. The characteristics of such reactions are elucidated using experimental, theoretical and modeling approaches.
Most of the chemical reactions occur in the troposphere and the major reactive species are volatile organic compounds (VOCs)
2 and radicals present in
the troposphere. The major classes of VOCs are alkanes, alkenes, aromatic
hydrocarbons and oxygenated VOCs. These VOCs are transformed by the
chemical processes of photolysis, reaction with hydroxyl radical during daylight hours, reaction with nitrate radical during evening and night time hours,
and reaction with O
3 and reaction with Cl atoms in coastal and marine
areas.2,4,5 The subsequent reactions from the initial oxidation reactions are also
important in order to determine the fate of the emitted VOC. The chemistry of
these subsequent reactions is quite complex, such that the underlying chemical
processes are very diﬃcult to determine accurately using laboratory studies.
However, theoretical studies are able to identify novel chemistry associated
with possible primary and secondary reactions in the troposphere.
19.2 Potential Energy Hypersurfaces
The potential energy surface (PES) is a central concept in computational
chemistry, which forms the basis for all the three modules of chemistry discussed
in the previous section. The PES is a hypersurface which is deﬁned by the potential energy of a collection of atoms over all possible atomic arrangements.
6
For a molecule with N number of atoms, the PES has (3 N-6) coordinate
Theoretical Studies on Mechanism and Kinetics of Atmospheric Chemical Reactions 463
 17/11/2013 09:29:44.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00462
View Online

## Page 484

dimensions. Along the PES there are points where the ﬁrst derivative of energy is
zero. Such points are called stationary points. 7,8 A stationary point may be a
local minimum or maximum, or a saddle point. The local minima (reactant,
product or reaction intermediate) correspond to optimal molecular structure,
and a local maximum (transition state) is a point on the PES characterized
without slope in any direction. The nature of the stationary points is understood
by evaluating the (3 N-6)
2 second derivatives of energy (which correspond to the
Hessian components) at the stationary point. The eigenvalues of the Hessian
matrix are proportional to the squares of the vibrational frequencies, and the
Hessian has all positive eigenvalues at a local minimum and one negative
eigenvalue at a transition state. Thus, at a local minimum, all the vibrational
frequencies are real numbers, a ﬁrst-order saddle point (transition state) has one
imaginary vibrational frequency and a higher-order saddle point has more than
one imaginary frequency. That is, in transition state, the second derivative of
energy with respect to one coordinate is negative, while in higher order saddle
points, the second derivative of energy with respect to more than one coordinate
is negative.
7 The eigenvector corresponding to the imaginary frequency is known
as the transition vector because it corresponds to molecular displacement along
the reaction path through the transition state. A schematic representation of a
PES representing the minima and maxima is shown in Figure 19.1.
The path linking the two minima through a transition state is the intrinsic
reaction coordinate (IRC) path.
9 The reaction coordinate is the particular
coordinate corresponding to the transition state, at which bond making and
bond breaking processes take place. The IRC path is the lowest energy pathway
Figure 19.1 Schematic representation of a potential energy surface.
464 Chapter 19
 17/11/2013 09:29:44.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00462
View Online

## Page 485

followed by a molecule while going from one minimum to another passing
through a transition state, which should acquire enough energy to overcome
the activation barrier to reach the other minimum. It should be noted that not
all the conformations of reacting molecules follow IRC exactly: a molecule with
suﬃcient energy can stay outside the IRC to some extent. From Figure 19.1, it
is observed that the transition state represents a maximum energy point along
the direction of the IRC, but along all the other directions it is a minimum.
Even though both the minima and saddle point are stationary points, a minimum is a minimum in all directions, but a saddle point is maximum along the
reaction coordinate and minimum in all other directions. Reaction intermediates and transition states are short-lived species and it is very diﬃcult to determine their structures through spectroscopic studies. One of the main tasks of
computational chemistry is to determine the structure and energy of the minima
and the transition state along a PES.
Transition states are located using many approaches. One of the widely used
methods is synchronous transit guided quasi-Newton (STQN) method.
10 This
method uses a linear synchronous transit or quadratic synchronous transit
approach to get closer to the quadratic region around the transition state (TS).
Then, the optimization is completed using a quasi-Newton or eigenvectorfollowing algorithm. This method will converge eﬃciently to the actual transition state structure using an empirical estimate of the hessian. That is, the
transition state search begins with the identiﬁcation of the reaction mode and
maximizing energy along this mode while minimizing energy in all other directions. The search should start in a region where the reaction coordinate already has a negative curvature. Commonly, this method is used to identify a TS
through either a QST2 or QST3 technique. The QST2 option requires two input
speciﬁcations, reactant and product, and the QST3 option requires three input
speciﬁcations, reactant, product and guessed structure for transition state.
To verify that the transition state identiﬁed truly connects the designated
reactant and product, IRC calculations should be carried out. The IRC calculation starts from the transition state and then steps towards the reactant and
then towards the product. IRC uses mass-weighted Cartesian coordinates even
if the geometry is provided in terms of internal coordinates. It explicitly computes the Hessian along the reaction path. The IRC method is the most advanced method to trace the path of a chemical reaction.
19.3 Reaction Path and Reaction Phases
To understand the mechanism of a chemical reaction through computational
methods, three approaches should be followed:
(i) The energetics of the reaction should be calculated by locating the
stationary points on the PES of the reaction complex.
(ii) The connectivity of the transition state with the corresponding reactant
and product should be veriﬁed by following the IRC path, as explained by
Fukui,
9 which is the steepest descent path in mass-weighted coordinates.
Theoretical Studies on Mechanism and Kinetics of Atmospheric Chemical Reactions 465
 17/11/2013 09:29:44.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00462
View Online

## Page 486

(iii) The dynamics of a chemical reaction which provides a detailed account
of the changes of electronic structure and energy of the reaction complex
along the reaction path should be determined.
During a chemical reaction, one or two chemical processes can take place.
These processes are taking place within the conﬁned regions described by
speciﬁc reaction coordinate(s) of the reaction path and are called reaction
phases.
11,12 In the reactant phase, the reactant undergoes structural changes for
the actual chemical processes and, in the product phase, the products relax to a
more stable form. Each phase is associated with changes in the internal coordinates of reactive species. Since the geometrical changes involve many coordinates, it is very diﬃcult to identify which coordinate is more important in
determining the reaction mechanism. To overcome this diﬃculty, the changes
of the reaction complex can be described by two essential reaction path
properties: (i) reaction path direction;
13 and (ii) reaction path curvature. 13,14
These properties are described by investigating the dynamical behavior of the
reaction complex. This involves (3 N-7) vibrational motions orthogonal to the
reaction path and the translational motion along the path. During a chemical
reaction, at the reactant minimum, a vibrational mode initiates the reaction,
where the largest vibration converts into a translational motion along the reaction path. In a reaction path curvature, the translational and vibrational
motions combine and energy transfer between these motions takes place.
13–16
This energy transfer consequently enhances the reaction rate.
19.4 Thermodynamic Parameters
A transition state is a thermodynamic concept, which is an ensemble existing in
a kind of equilibrium with the reactants. The transition state is characterized as
a state of free energy maximum along the reaction coordinate, which is called as
activated complex. The transition state structure is a saddle point on the enthalpy surface. The total energy of a molecule is the sum of translational, vibrational, rotational and electronic energies. The molecular vibration energy is
not equal to zero at absolute zero temperature (0 K). The energy corresponding
to molecular vibrations at 0 K is known as zero point vibrational energy (ZPVE
or ZPE). To compare theoretical results with the experimental data, the ZPVE
must be added to the total electronic energy obtained from electronic structure
calculations; thus the electronic energy is converted into 0 K enthalpies. Then,
the other thermodynamic quantities involved in chemical reaction such as enthalpy and Gibb’s free energy are calculated. The enthalpy, DH
rxn of a reaction
is deﬁned by:
DHrxn ¼ DHf ðproductÞ/C0 DHf ðreac tan tÞð 19:1Þ
where DHf is the heat of formation, calculated relative to the elements in their
standard states. The Gibb’s free energy, DG, of a reaction is deﬁned by:
DG ¼ DEelec þ DZPVE þ DET /C0 TDS ð19:2Þ

Here DEelec, DZPVE, DET and DS are the diﬀerences in electronic energy, zeropoint vibrational energy, thermal energy and entropy between the products and
reactants, respectively. 17 To calculate these thermodynamic parameters, the
basic thermochemical quantity is the partition function q( V,T), which is the
corresponding quantity of the total partition function Q in volume V. The total
partition function Q is the product of partition functions for translation, rotation, vibration and electronic degrees of freedom.
Q ¼ qelecqvibqrotqtran ð19:3Þ
By using the total partition function, the entropy, S18 can be calculated from
the thermodynamic relationship:
S ¼ R þ R lnðQðV; TÞÞ þ RT @ ln Q

where R is the universal gas constant. The entropic contribution is of primary
importance in chemical reaction mechanisms. The internal energy 18 can also be
obtained from the partition function as:
E ¼ RT2 @ ln Q

The partition function is obtained from the frequency calculations. As stated
above, the thermodynamic parameters are derived from ideal-gas approximation and the contribution from vibrational motion is composed of the
product of all vibration modes. For the K
th vibrational mode, the corresponding partition function is expressed as:
qn;K ¼ 1

Here nK is the vibrational frequency of mode K. Thus thermodynamic parameters calculated from quantum chemical methods are a tool for the mechanical investigation of reactions.
19
19.5 Reaction Rate Theory
The calculation of theoretical rate constants for gas-phase chemical reactions
involved in atmospheric chemistry is a subject of great interest. Theoretical
kinetic methodologies utilize the quantum chemical characterization of the
stationary points along the PES of a reaction to calculate the rate constants and
product distributions. These methods allow for the elucidation of rate constants over the temperature and pressure range in the atmosphere. Various
theoretical methods are available for rate constant calculations. Here, we focus
on transition state theory (TST) and its variants to calculate the reaction rate
constants.
TST is a classical theory which describes a chemical reaction as a transformation between three regions in phase space: the reactant, the transition state
Theoretical Studies on Mechanism and Kinetics of Atmospheric Chemical Reactions 467
 17/11/2013 09:29:44.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00462
View Online

## Page 488

and the product. 20 The transition state is the dividing phase space separating
the reactant and product phase spaces. The TST is based on two assumptions:
(i) Thermodynamic equilibrium should exist throughout the entire reaction
system for all degrees of freedom.
(ii) A trajectory that crosses TS should continue till the product phase is
reached and any trajectory that crosses TS will not recross it.
Based on the ﬁrst assumption, it is observed that the net rate of forward reaction
at equilibrium is proportional to the total ﬂuxof classical trajectories from reactant
to product side of the dividing surface. If this ﬂux is calculated with the Maxwell–
Boltzmann thermal weighing function at a given temperature, then the TST corresponds to canonical TST. 21–25 However, if the ﬂux is calculated with a deltafunction weighing accounting only for th e trajectories of a given total energy E,
then the TST corresponds to microcanonical TST.26–30 According to assumptions
(i) and (ii), the microcanonical TST is exact only if the trajectory from the reactant
to product does not cross the dividing surface more than once. The canonical TST
is exact if the trajectory recrosses the dividing surface. Such recrossing trajectories
lead to an equilibrium ﬂux across the dividing surface and the net reaction rate
increases. This overestimation of the rate constant increases as the temperature
increases. Thus, classical TST is expected to fail in complex systems.
One way of improving the classical TST results is to choose a dividing surface
so as to minimize the ﬂux through it.
31–34 In this way, we can obtain the lowest
upper bound to the true classical rate within the space of variations of the
dividing surface. This dividing surface is then called as generalized transition
state (GT).
35 The dividing surface is chosen as a parameter perpendicular to the
reaction coordinate and this dividing surface parameter is variationally optimized for classical motion. This parameter is then used as a base to include
quantal eﬀects in classical motion. If the dividing surface is variationally moved
for the total energy corresponding to each reaction coordinate, it is called
microcanonical variational theory ( mVT).
31,36 For most of the reactions, the
variational dividing surface for each energy and angular momentum contributes signiﬁcantly to the reaction rate constant at each temperature. If the
dividing surface is variationally moved for both energy and angular momentum
corresponding to the reaction coordinates, then the corresponding TST is
called canonical variational theory (CVT).
31 The CVT method has been proved
to be a practical method for diatomic and polyatomic reactions. 31,37–39 If the
GT intersects the minimum energy path (MEP) at some non-zero value of the
reaction coordinate s, then the generalized TST rate constant, kGT
C ðT; sÞ at
temperature T for this dividing surface is given by: 36
kGT
C ðT; sÞ¼ s
bh
QGT
C ðT; sÞ
fR
C ðTÞ exp½/C0 bVMEPðsÞ/C138 ð 19:7Þ
Here b is ( ðkBTÞ/C0 1, where kB is Boltzmann’s constant, h is Planck’s constant,
VMEPðsÞ is the classical potential energy at point s on the MEP relative to the

overall zero of energy, QGT

generalized transition state dividing surface with its zero of energy at VMEPðsÞ,

C ðTÞ is the reactant classical partition function
per unit volume. The reaction path symmetry factor s accounts for the number
of equivalent reaction paths. The CVT rate constant is then obtained by
varying the location of generalized transition state in order to minimize

C ðT; sÞ, which occurs at

C ðTÞ corresponds to a maximum in DGGT;0
C ðT; sÞ, the generalized free energy
of activation. Hence, CVT rate is equivalent to the maximum free energy of
activation.36,40 While calculating the rate constant, both TST and CVT include
entropic as well as energetic eﬀects, but only CVT uses both the factors while
calculating the dividing surface. Thus, CVT is more accurate than TST.
Eqn (19.8) represents a hybrid quantal rate constant, in which the GT degrees of freedom orthogonal to the reaction path are treated quantum mechanically, while the motion along the reaction coordinate is treated classically.
This rate constant neglects tunneling and hence it underestimates the true
quantal rate constant especially at low temperatures.
41,42 The tunneling factor
is included in the CVT rate constant by using a multiplicative transmission
coeﬃcient kðTÞ in eqn (19.8):
kCVT ðTÞ¼ kCVT ðTÞkCVT
C ðTÞð 19:13Þ
Theoretical Studies on Mechanism and Kinetics of Atmospheric Chemical Reactions 469
 17/11/2013 09:29:44.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00462
View Online

## Page 490

This expression accounts for the dynamical quantum eﬀects of reaction–
coordinate tunneling and non-classical reﬂection. The tunneling correction is
deﬁned as the Boltzmann average of the ratio of the quantum and classical
probabilities. One of the approximations of reaction coordinate tunneling is
small curvature (SC) approximation.
37,43 In SC approximation, the reaction
path curvature (which couples reaction–coordinate motion with vibrational
motion) is treated by using an eﬀective mass for reaction–coordinate motion.
The inclusion of eﬀective mass in reaction–coordinate motion in SC approximation leads to the semi-classical adiabatic ground-state transmission coeﬃcient, k
CVT = SCSAGðTÞ. The CVT rate constant with this tunneling
approximation is more accurate for systems with small to intermediate
curvature.37,43
19.6 Tropospheric Chemistry
Volatile organic compounds (VOCs) are emitted as pollutants into the atmosphere from anthropogenic and biogenic sources.
2,44–46 These compounds
can undergo a series of chemical reactions, leading to their removal from the
troposphere or transformation into other compounds. Among the various atmospheric oxidants, OH radical reacts exclusively with a VOC and plays an
important role in determining the lifetime of the VOC in the troposphere.
47,48
The reactivity of OH radical towards VOCs has been well examined using
various theoretical 4,49–62 and experimental approaches, 2,60,63 and a large
database exists with measured rate coeﬃcients.
As stated earlier, the major classes of VOCs are alkanes, alkenes, aromatics
and oxygenated VOCs. The dominant reaction channel in the reaction between
alkenes and OH radical is the addition of OH radical on one of the carbons in
the double bond.
49,50 This reaction proceeds through a pre-reactive van der
Waals complex, where the hydrogen atom of the OH radical associates with the
double bond p-system. Studies on these reactions show that the initial complex
formation as well as the subsequent addition step inﬂuence the rate constant of
principle reactions in the atmospheric temperatures.
49,50 The rate constant for
these reactions is found to show negative dependence on temperature. The
reaction between alkynes and OH radical is also found to form an OH adduct
through a pre-reactive van der Waals complex. Most of these reactions undergo
fast equilibration between the free reactants and the complex, which cancels the
impact of the transition state on the reaction rate.
51 The reaction of oxygenated
VOCs with OH radical is found to proceed either by H-atom abstraction or
addition reactions. These reactions are strongly aﬀected by H-bonding, which
inﬂuences the overall rate constant and the product distribution.
64 The reaction
between aromatic compounds and OH radical proceeds either by H-atom abstraction by OH radical or electrophilic addition of OH radical to the aromatic
ring.
61,62 The competition between OH addition and H-abstraction leads to a
complex temperature-dependent OH consumption in the atmosphere by aromatic oxidation reactions. 62
470 Chapter 19
 17/11/2013 09:29:44.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00462
View Online

## Page 491

The alkyl radical formed in the initial oxidation reactions subsequently react
with O 2 resulting in the formation of alkylperoxy radical. The alkylperoxy
radical is the key intermediate in the oxidation of VOCs by OH radical and
further reacts with HO
2,N O 2 and NO in the atmosphere. 2 This intermediate
can also undergo self-reaction 4 and epoxidation reactions. 65 These reactions
lead to the formation of hydroperoxide adducts, alkoxy radical and O 3, and
also OH radical regeneration. The alkoxy radical is the second key intermediate
in the VOCs oxidation by OH radical.
2 The alkoxy radical undergoes prompt
decomposition resulting in the oxidation of NO. 66 The characterization of
these secondary reactions is studied extensively using theoretical methods,
but only very few experimental studies are available for such reactions.
19.7 Example: Understanding the Reactivity of
2,3-Dimethylphenol with OH Radical in the
Atmosphere
Dimethylphenols are vital air pollutants and are highly reactive in the atmosphere. The dominant degradation process for dimethylphenols is their reaction
with OH radical. Dimethylphenols are obtained from coal tar or petroleum as
by-products in the fractional distillation and in coal gasiﬁcation.
67 In the atmosphere, dimethylphenols are formed due to the oxidation of xylene by OH
radical. Dimethylphenols present in the atmosphere are responsible for various
health hazards such as cardiovascular, respiratory and skin toxicants. Hence, it
is very important to know whether dimethylphenol remains as such in the atmosphere or is degrading into new products. In our earlier work, we studied the
reaction between six isomers of dimethylphenol and OH radical. The reaction
between dimethylphenol and OH radical is found to proceed in four diﬀerent
ways, as H-atom abstraction from the phenol group, methyl group or aromatic
ring of dimethylphenol by OH radical and also by electrophilic addition of OH
radical to dimethylphenol. The H-atom abstraction from methyl group is found
to occur dominantly in 2,3-dimethylphenol.
As shown in Figure 19.2, the reaction between 2,3-dimethylphenol and OH
radical is initiated through abstraction of an H atom present in the methyl
group of 2,3-dimethylphenol by OH radical. The H-atom abstraction from the
methyl group results in the formation of an alkyl radical. The initially formed
alkyl radical will react more rapidly with O
2 and a peroxy radical is formed,
which is the major transformation reaction in the troposphere. 68,69 These two
reaction channels were studied in our previous work. 70 At lower temperatures,
this peroxy radical is stabilized by bath gas collisions and is able to participate
in further bimolecular reactions.
The peroxy radical is the key intermediate in the reaction mechanism and has
excess energy to undergo further reactions with HO
2 in the atmosphere leading
to the formation of new compounds. 71 The reaction between peroxy radical
and HO 2 proceed in two diﬀerent ways. One of the reactions involves the
formation of hydroperoxide adduct along with O 2.
70 In the next possible way,
Theoretical Studies on Mechanism and Kinetics of Atmospheric Chemical Reactions 471
 17/11/2013 09:29:44.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00462
View Online

## Page 492

the reaction between peroxy radical and HO 2 leads to the formation of formic
acid, m-cresol and O 2. The formic acid so formed contributes signiﬁcantly to
the acidity of atmospheric precipitation and is further degraded by atmospheric
heterogeneous transformations.
72 The m-cresol further undergoes oxidation
reaction with O 2 leading to the formation of organic compounds such as
dicarbonyls, aldehydes and alcohols. These products can further decompose to
glyoxal and butanedial, which are the principal oxidation products of aromatic
compounds.
73,74 Here we study the subsequent atmospheric chemistry resulting
from the reaction of m-cresol with O 2 in detail.
The secondary reactions of peroxy radical with HO 2 slow down the free
radical driven photochemical oxidation reactions and reduce the formation of
ozone. In addition, these reactions represent an important chemical sink for
HO
X radicals in the troposphere. Hence, the reactions of peroxy radical with
HO2 are of comparable importance in the atmospheric fate of dimethylphenols.
Previous studies on the reactions of dimethylphenol with OH radical have focused only on the initial H-atom abstraction step and its kinetics.
75,76 Hence,
this work focused mainly on the study of possible secondary reactions of the
reaction between 2,3-dimethylphenol and OH radical. Theoretical calculations
assess the feasibility of diﬀerent reaction channels and provide thermochemical
data for the reaction system.
19.7.1 Computational Methodology
The geometry of the reactant, intermediates, transition states and products on
the potential energy surface of the reaction system of 2,3-dimethylphenol with
Figure 19.2 Reaction scheme for the oxidation of 2,3-dimethylphenol by OH
radical.
472 Chapter 19
 17/11/2013 09:29:44.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00462
View Online

## Page 493

OH radical were optimized using the density functional theory methods
B3LYP,77,78 M06-2X,79 and MPW1K, 80 with 6-311 þþ G(d,p) basis sets. The
same methods were used to obtain vibrational frequencies and zero-point energy (ZPE) corrections and to characterize the stationary points as minima or
ﬁrst order saddle points. All local minima were conﬁrmed with all positive
frequencies, while each transition state had one imaginary frequency conﬁrming their maxima in one reaction coordinate. IRC calculations
81 were
carried out in each case to conﬁrm that the transition state structures connect
the designated reactant and product. The paths were computed following the
second-order algorithm of Gonzalez and Schlegel.
82 The energy derivatives
including gradients and Hessians at geometries along the MEP were also obtained. The enthalpy of reaction and Gibb’s free energy are calculated by including thermodynamic corrections to the energy at 298.15 K and at 1
atmospheric pressure. All the electronic structure calculations were performed
using the Gaussian09 program package.
83 As described above, the potential
energy surface, gradients and Hessians obtained from the electronic structure
calculations are used directly to calculate the rate constants. The theoretical
rate constants for the reactions are calculated using canonical variational
transition state theory (CVT).
84,85 The tunneling correction deﬁned as the
Boltzmann average of the ratio of the quantum and classical probabilities was
calculated using the small curvature tunneling (SCT) method.
86,87 The generalized normal-mode analysis is performed in Cartesian coordinates. The lowest
vibrational mode of the transition state is treated as a hindered rotation, and all
other vibrations are treated harmonically. The hindered rotor approximation
88
is used to calculate the partition function of the lowest vibrational mode. The
rate constant calculations were performed by using the GAUSSRATE 2009A
89
program, which is an interface between the Gaussian09 and POLYRATE
2010A
87 programs.
19.7.2 Results and Discussions
19.7.2.1 Reaction Mechanism and Reaction Paths
The structure of stationary points on the ground-state potential energy surface
of the 2,3-dimethylphenol þ OH reaction system optimized at the M06-2X/6311þþ G(d,p) level of theory is shown in Figure 19.3. The relative energy
proﬁle corresponding to diﬀerent pathways of the reaction system obtained
from M06-2X/6-311 þþ G(d,p) level of theory is shown in Figure 19.4. The
barrier height ( DE), relative enthalpy ( DH) and Gibb’s free energy ( DG) of the
reactive species calculated using the diﬀerent methods are summarized in
Table 19.1. The structural parameters obtained with the three methods diﬀer
slightly. For instance, the average root mean square deviation (rmsd) between
the internal coordinates obtained from M06-2X and B3LYP functionals for the
most favorable pathway is 0.07 A˚ ,0 . 0 4A˚ and 0.2 A˚ for the reactants, transition
state and product, respectively. The rmsd between the internal coordinates for
the reactants, product and transition state of the most favorable reaction

## Page 495

comparable within 2 to 3 kcal mol /C0 1. However, the results obtained from the
B3LYP method do not coincide with the other two methods. This is because the
B3LYP method with a higher basis set underestimates the energetics for
H-atom abstraction reactions and also for reactions with loose transition
states.
70 On comparing the results obtained for various reaction paths, it is
observed that the relative importance of the various reaction channels does not
change with respect to the methods of calculation. The favorable reaction path
in the studied reaction pathways is the same in all the three methods. In recent
studies, the M06-2X functional has been shown to give reliable barrier heights
and the rate constant calculated using the energetics obtained with M06-2X
functional is in good agreement with experimental results.
90,91 Hence, the
structure and energetics obtained using M06-2X functional with 6311þþ G(d,p) basis set are discussed in detail and are used in further kinetic
calculations. The reactant, intermediate, transition state and product are labeled as R, I, TS and P, respectively followed by a number.
The OH group of 2,3-dimethylphenol is a strong ortho–para director. This is
because one of the two lone pairs on the oxygen atom interacts strongly
with the p electrons of the aromatic system and inﬂuences the ortho and para
positions to possess greater electron density than the meta position. Hence, the
OH group of 2,3-dimethylphenol directs the reacting OH radical to attack at
these electron-rich centers. This results in the abstraction of an H atom from
the methyl group of 2,3-dimethylphenol by OH radical.

## Page 497

an alkyl radical (I1) and a water molecule. This abstraction occurs through a
transition state TS1 with an energy barrier of 6.72 kcal mol /C0 1. In TS1, the angle
between the H atom of the CH 3 group, which is being abstracted, and the
reacting OH radical is found to be 130 1 which was 92 1 in the reactants. This
initial reaction is exothermic by –22.32 kcal mol /C0 1, as observed for other
H-atom abstraction reactions, 4,5 and this reaction is exoergic with a free energy
of /C0 23.45 kcal mol/C0 1. The initially formed radical, I1, is found to react more
rapidly with O 2 resulting in the formation of a peroxy radical intermediate,
I2, in a barrierless reaction. The heat of formation of this peroxy radical is –
3.76 kcal mol
/C0 1.
The peroxy radical thus formed further reacts with HO 2 leading to the formation of 3-methylphenol ( m-cresol), formic acid and O 2. These products are
formed via a transition state, TS2 with a barrier of 25.32 kcal mol /C0 1. In the
transition state structure TS2, the bond between the H and O atoms of HO 2 is
elongated by 1 A˚ and a weak hydrogen bonding is observed between the O
atom of HO 2 and the H atom of the phenol group. After the formation of the
transition state structure, the O atom of peroxy group in TS2 abstracts the H
atom of HO
2 and the distance between C2 and C7 is increased by 2 A ˚ , and the
bond between them is cleaved (for labeling of atoms, see Figure 19.5). Thus, the
formic acid is formed and, as shown in Figure 19.3, the remaining H atom in
the radical makes a new bond with the C atom in the second position of
C
6H3OH(CH3). Thus, the intermediate m-cresol is formed along with formic
acid and O 2. As given in Table 19.1, this reaction occurs in a highly exothermic
process with an enthalpy of –73 kcal mol /C0 1 and a free energy of –69.54 kcal
mol/C0 1. This high exothermicity implies that the products formed in this reaction are highly activated with suﬃcient energy to proceed with secondary
reactions to form new decomposition products. The m-cresol formed in this
reaction serves as a key intermediate for the formation of ring cleavage and ring
rearrangement products. Several studies have focused on the degradation of
aromatic hydrocarbons in the atmosphere.
92,93 However, the reaction mechanism for the atmospheric oxidation of m-cresol is not studied in detail elsewhere. The m-cresol can predominantly undergo subsequent reactions with O 2
present in the atmosphere. The possible reactions are characterized by eight
degradation pathways and are discussed below.
The structure of stationary points on the ground-state potential energy
surface of the m-cresol þ O2 reaction system optimized at the M06-2X/6311þþ G(d,p) level of theory is shown in Figure 19.5. The relative energy
proﬁle corresponding to diﬀerent pathways of the reaction system obtained
from M06-2X/6-311 þþ G(d,p) level of theory is shown in Figure 19.6. The
reaction between m-cresol and O 2 leads to the formation of 6-hydroxymethyloxa-bicyclo [4,1,0] hepta-2,4-diene-2-ol (P1) through a transition state TS3 with
an energy barrier of 55.77 kcal mol
/C0 1.
As shown in Figure 19.5, in the transition state structure TS3, one of the O
atoms of O 2 forms a triangular structure with the C1 atom and with its
neighboring C2 atom from which the formic acid group was eliminated. In
addition, the other O atom forms a triangular structure with the C4 and C6

3 group binds with this O atom and thus an alcoholic product is formed.
