–65.02 kcal mol/C0 1. As reported in Table 19.1, this product channel is exoergic
by –62.05 kcal mol/C0 1.
The second pathway involves the formation of 2-hydroxy-cyclopenta-2,4dienecarboxylic acid methyl ester (P2). This reaction is characterized by a
transition state TS4 with an energy barrier of 66.12 kcal mol
/C0 1. In the transition
state structure TS4, the C2 ¼C6 bond loses its double bond character and the
O2 molecule binds with the C4 atom and thus a peroxy group is formed. As
shown in Figure 19.5, after the formation of transition state structure TS4, the
bond between the C4 and C6 atoms is cleaved, and one of the O atoms of O
2
binds with the C4 and C6 atoms while the other O atom makes a double bond
with the C2 atom. Thus, a neutral product is formed. This product channel is
formed exothermically with a reaction enthalpy of –84.68 kcal mol
/C0 1 and the
reaction is exoergic with a free energy of –82.15 kcal mol /C0 1.
The next reaction is the aromatic ring opening reaction and a neutral product
2-hydroxy-cyclohept-4-ene-1,3-dione (P3) is formed. This ring opening and
rearrangement reaction occurs through a transition state, TS5, with an energy
barrier of 55.07 kcal mol
/C0 1. The transition state structure TS5 is an aromatic
structure in which the reacting O 2 molecule dissociates into two O atoms and
one of the O atoms binds with the C1 and C2 atoms and the other O atom binds
with the C3 and C5 atoms. After the formation of transition state TS5, the
bond between the C4 and C7 atoms in TS5 is broken, and the C7 atom makes
new bond with the C6 atom. In addition, the H8 atom in TS5 shifts its position
and binds with the C6 atom and therefore a circular structure is formed as a
product. As given in Table 19.1, this reaction is the highest exothermic and
exoergic reaction with reaction enthalpy of –93.57 kcal mol
/C0 1 and a free energy
of –90.91 kcal mol/C0 1. This high exothermicity further implies that the oxidation
Figure 19.6 Relative energy proﬁle corresponding to the formation of ring cleavage
and ring rearrangement products from the reaction of m-cresol with O 2.
480 Chapter 19
 17/11/2013 09:29:44.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00462
View Online

## Page 501

of 2,3-dimethylphenol by OH radical is potentially involved in autoignition and
combustion processes.
The next pathway includes continuous association and disassociation processes in which an open chain product, 7-hydroxy-hepta-4,6-diene-2,3-dione
(P4), is formed. This reaction occurs via a transition state, TS6, with a potential
barrier of 18.95 kcal mol
/C0 1. In the transition state, TS6, the reacting O 2 molecule dissociates and one of the O atoms becomes bonded between the C1 and
C2 atoms of m-cresol, while the other O atom forms a triangular structure with
the C4 and C6 atoms. Further, this transition state structure disassociates into a
neutral product. This reaction channel is highly exothermic by –79.51 kcal
mol
/C0 1 and the reaction occurs in an exoergic process with a free energy of –
80.04 kcal mol/C0 1. This open chain product can again degrade to important
products such as methyl glyoxal and butanol. 73,74
The next expected reaction is breaking of the C–C bond between the C4 and
C2 atoms, and the formation of a C–C bond between the C2 atom and C atom
of the methyl group in m-cresol. This reaction channel is characterized by a
transition state, TS7, with an energy barrier of 11.16 kcal mol
/C0 1. In the transition state structure TS7, the O 2 molecule is aligned in plane with respect to
the C1 and C6 atoms of m-cresol. After this transition state formation, the
reacting O 2 molecule in TS7 dissociates and one of the O atoms becomes
bonded with the C4 and C6 atoms, and the other O atom binds with the
C2 atom with double bond character. Thus an aldehyde product, 3-hydroxy-2methyl-2H-pyran-2-carbaldehyde (P5), is formed. This product channel
is also exothermic by –80.42 kcal mol
/C0 1 and the reaction is exoergic with DG ¼
/C0 77.56 kcal mol/C0 1.
The next signiﬁcant pathway is the formation of 7-methyl-2,8-dioxa-bicyclo[5,1,0]octa-3,5-diene-3-ol (P6). This product channel is formed through a
transition state TS8 with a barrier of 11.16 kcal mol
/C0 1. The structures of TS7
and TS8 are similar except for a small diﬀerence in the C6–O3 bond length.
After the formation of the transition state, the reacting O 2 molecule in TS8
dissociates and one of the O atoms binds with the C1 and C2 atoms, and the
other O atom binds with the C2 and C4 atoms as a triangular structure. This
product is formed in an exothermic reaction with DH ¼ –64.86 kcal mol
/C0 1 and
the reaction is exoergic by –61.76 kcal mol /C0 1. The reaction paths corresponding
to the formation of P5 and P6 product channels are the most favorable due to
the small energy barrier.
As shown in Figure 19.5, the reaction between m-cresol and O
2 can also lead
to an adduct formation in which one of the O atoms of the reacting O 2 molecule
in m-cresol becomes attached between the C1 and C2 atoms and the other O
atom binds with the C4 atom. This product formation is associated with a
transition state TS9 with an energy barrier of 32.87 kcal mol
/C0 1. In the transition
state structure TS9, the C–O bond distance is greater by 0.5 A ˚ than that of
the product. Thus, the product 2-methyl-[1,4] dioxocin-5-ol (P7) is formed.
This compound is relatively stable global pollutant and very hazardous.
This reaction is exothermic by –57.98 kcal mol
/C0 1 and is exoergic with a free
energy of /C0 55.43 kcal mol/C0 1.
Theoretical Studies on Mechanism and Kinetics of Atmospheric Chemical Reactions 481
 17/11/2013 09:29:44.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00462
View Online

## Page 502

The next pathway involves a complicated structural rearrangement of mcresol. The product formation, 1-(1-hydroxy-2-oxa-bicyclo [2,2,0] hex-5-en-3yl)-ethanone (P8), occurs through a transition state TS10 with a barrier of
14.54 kcal mol
/C0 1. As shown in Figure 19.5, the transition from the reactants to
the product involve the formation of a ﬁve-membered ring, in which the CH 3
group attached in the fourth position of m-cresol shifts its position and binds
with the C2 atom. Also, the reacting O 2 molecule dissociates and one of the O
atoms makes a bond with C1 atom and the other O atom binds with the C4
atom. During the product formation, the bond between the O atom and the
C4 atom contracts and becomes a double bond, and the other O atom attached
with the C1 atom bifurcates the bond between the C1 and C2 atoms. The
distance between the ﬁrst and sixth position C atoms contracts and a new bond
is formed between them, resulting in the formation of 1-(1-hydroxy-2-oxabicyclo [2,2,0] hex-5-en-3-yl)-ethanone (P8). This reaction is the least
exothermic channel among all the studied reactions with exothermicity of
/C0 50.74 kcal mol
/C0 1; it is exoergic by –48.63 kcal mol /C0 1.
Thus, eight pathways are identiﬁed for the secondary reactions of OH with
2,3-dimethylphenol. Of all the pathways studied, pathways 5 and 6 leading to
the formation of 3-hydroxy-2-methyl-2H-pyran-2-carbaldehyde (P5) and 7methyl-2,8-dioxa-bicyclo[5,1,0]octa-3,5-diene-3-ol (P6) are found to be equally
most favorable due to small barrier of 11.16 kcal mol
/C0 1.
19.7.2.2 Reaction Path Properties and Rate Constant
Calculations
The results discussed in the previous section reveal that the reaction rate corresponding to the formation of major by-products of the oxidation reaction is
important to determine the lifetime of dimethylphenol in the atmosphere. The
rate constants are calculated using canonical variational transition state theory
(CVT) with small curvature tunneling (SCT) corrections over the temperature
range of 278–350 K. As described in Figure 19.2, the formation of product
channels consists of four reaction channels. The rate constants for the formation of alkyl radical (I1), peroxy radical (I2), m-cresol and the product
channels are designated as k
I1, kI2, km and kP, respectively, and are summarized
in Tables 19.2 and 19.3. The reaction path properties and rate constant obtained for the most favorable product channels, P5 and P6, are discussed in
detail.
Since the motion along the reaction coordinate ðRÞ is coupled with overall
translations and rotations of the reacting systems, the contribution of entropy
plays an important role in determining the classical potential energy, V
MEPðRÞ.
While calculating the rate constants of the reactions with high energy barriers,
the quantum tunneling corrections should be included. In such cases, the rate
constant is calculated using the adiabatic potential energy VG
a ðRÞ, instead of
VMEPðRÞ, which is the sum of classical potential energy and the total vibrational zero point energy. Figure 19.7 represents the ground state vibrational
482 Chapter 19
 17/11/2013 09:29:44.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00462
View Online

## Page 503

adiabatic potential energy paths for the I1 þ H2O, I2, m-cresol þ
HCOOH þ O2, P5 and P6 product channels, according to the equation:
VG
a ðsÞ¼ VMEPðsÞþ ZPE ð19:14Þ
where VG
a ðsÞ is the ground-state adiabatic potential energy, VMEPðsÞ is the
classical potential energy path and ZPE is the zero-point vibrational energy at
s. Since, VG
a ðsÞ is obtained by summing the VMEPðsÞ and ZPE, the shape of the
ground state adiabatic surface is determined by the variation in VMEPðsÞ and
ZPE with respect to reaction coordinate, s. In the case of H-atom abstraction
reaction (I1), the drop in the zero-point energy is unique. This may be due to
the eﬀect of tunneling on the reaction rate, in which various vibrational levels
are participating in the determination of partition function.
The forward rate constant for this initial methyl H-atom abstraction reaction
at 298 K is 8.23 /C2 10
/C0 13 cm3 molecule/C0 1 s/C0 1. Experimental studies on H-atom
abstraction from 2,3-dimethylphenol show that the rate constant for this abstraction reaction is of the order of 10
/C0 11 to 10/C0 12 cm3 molecule/C0 1 s/C0 1.94,95 The
calculated reverse rate constant for this reaction is 2.16 /C2 10/C0 31 cm3 molecule/C0 1
s/C0 1. According to these values, the reversibility of the reaction is negligible, as
reﬂected in the equilibrium constant value of 6.07 /C2 1014. The transmission
coeﬃcient for this reaction is 7.11 at 298 K, which shows that the tunneling
eﬀect is signiﬁcant in this H-atom abstraction step. The variational correction,
i.e. the ratio between CVT and TST rate constants for this reaction, is about 1.5

H2O, I2, m-cresol þ HCOOH þ O2, P5 and P6.

## Page 504

over the whole temperature range. The variational transition state is located at
s ¼ –0.118 a 0.
Since, the peroxy radical is formed without any potential barrier, the rate
constant for the formation of I2 is calculated by variationally moving the
reference position along the minimum energy path. The calculated rate constant k
I2 for the formation of peroxy radical is 8.10 /C2 10/C0 13 cm3 molecule/C0 1 s/C0 1
at 298 K. The chemical bonding between the C 6H5OHCH3(CH2) and OO
fragments in peroxy radical is weak and hence the reaction is reversible under
tropospheric conditions, and leads to a chemical equilibrium between I1 and
O
2. The reverse rate constant for the decomposition of the peroxy radical is
1.18/C2 10/C0 14 cm3 molecule/C0 1 s/C0 1, which shows that the forward reaction is more
favorable than the reverse reaction. The equilibrium constant calculated at
298 K for the formation and decomposition of the peroxy radical is 0.14. The
variation of k
I2 with temperature is very small, which suggests that the activation energy around 298 K is close to zero. The tunneling eﬀect is negligible in
this reaction. The rate constant k
m calculated at 298 K for the formation of
m-cresol, along with formic acid and O 2 is 6.63/C2 10/C0 14 cm3 molecule/C0 1 s/C0 1. For
this reaction, tunneling is signiﬁcant and a tunneling factor of 6.2 is observed.
The rate constant in this reaction channel is calculated using an adiabatic energy barrier of 11.2 kcal mol
/C0 1. The variational transition state is located at
s ¼ 0.0023 a 0. As observed from Table 19.2, the variation eﬀect is negligible in
the rate constant calculation for the channels, I2 and m-cresol þ HCOOH þ O2.
As summarized in Table 19.3, the rate constants calculated for the P1, P2, P3,
P4 and P7 product channels are of low magnitude, indicating that the formation of these product channels in the atmosphere is a slow process. The
variational eﬀect is negligible in the ﬁrst four product channels (P1–P4) and in
the P7 product channel. The variational transition states corresponding to the
P1, P2, P3, P4 and P7 product channels are located at s values equal to 0.0008
a
0, –0.008 a 0, 0.0004 a 0, 0.0006 a 0 and –0.012 a 0, respectively, over the whole
temperature range. The tunneling eﬀect is found to be small for all these reaction channels.
The rate constants kP5, kP6 and kP8 calculated for the product channels P5,
P6 and P8 are 1.01 /C2 104 s/C0 1, 0.58 /C2 106 s/C0 1 and 0.6 /C2 103 s/C0 1, respectively. The
large magnitude of the rate constant for these product channels reveals that the
formation of P5, P6 and P8 product channels in the atmosphere is a fast process. The variational eﬀect is negligible in the P5 and P6 product channels and,
in the P8 product channel, the variational correction varies from 9.65 to 0.95
over the temperature range of 278–350 K. The zero point vibrational energy for
the P5 reaction channel is 3.79 kcal mol
/C0 1 and that for P6 reaction channel is
4.02 kcal mol/C0 1. For the P5, P6 and P8 product channels, the transmission
coeﬃcients are 2.85, 2.45 and 1.0, respectively. The variational transition state
for P5, P6 and P8 channels are located at s ¼ –0.0008 a
0, –0.0003 a 0 and –0.012
a0, respectively. On comparing the barrier heights for the formation of the
channel m-cresol þ HCOOH þ O2 and the product channels P5 and P6, it is
observed that the formation of the m-cresol þ HCOOH þ O2 channel is the rate
determining step. The calculated rate constant for primary reactions reveals

that the initial H-atom abstraction channel is the rate determining step in the
reaction mechanism. This observation show that the calculation of rate constant depends not only on the energy barrier, but also on other thermodynamic
parameters such as entropy, Gibb’s free energy and zero point vibrational
energy.
The inﬂuence of temperature on the rate of chemical reaction is interpreted in
terms of Arrhenius activation energy, E
a, given by the relation:
k ¼ Ae/C0 Ea RT= ð19:15Þ
where A is known as the pre-exponential factor. The Arrhenius plot for the
favorable reaction channels is shown in Figure 19.8 where it can be seen that
the Arrhenius plot corresponding to the I1 þ H
2O channel is slightly curved,
while that corresponding to all the other studied reaction channels is linear.
E
a is calculated at any temperature from the slope of the tangent to the
Arrhenius plot. The Arrhenius activation energy calculated for the I1 þ H2O,
I2, m-cresol þ HCOOH þ O2, P5 and P6 reaction channels at 298 K is 7.94, –
0.82, 0.08, 9.43 and 7.11 kcal mol /C0 1, respectively. A positive temperature dependence of the rate constant is observed for the I2 and mcresol þ HCOOH þ O2 reaction channels and the rate constant of the other
channels studied show negative temperature dependence.
The branching ratios corresponding to the diﬀerent product channels at
298 K are calculated and summarized in Table 19.4. The branching ratio is
calculated as the ratio between the rate constant for particular product channels and the sum of the rate constants of all possible product channels studied.
For example, for product channel, P1 the branching ratio is:
G

where i runs from 1 to all possible product channels studied. According to the
branching ratios summarized in Table 19.4, the contribution of P6 product
channel to the atmospheric degradation of m-cresol is signiﬁcant compared
with the other product channels.
19.7.2.3 Tropospheric Implications
The potential energy surface, thermochemical and kinetic data for the reaction
of 2,3-dimethylphenol with OH radical reveal several important aspects of alkylated aromatic compounds in the atmospheric chemistry. The reaction between 2,3-dimethylphenol and OH radical is initiated by H-atom abstraction
from a methyl group and the resulting alkyl radical further reacts with O
2 to
form a peroxy radical, a key intermediate in the reaction mechanism. This
peroxy radical has excess energy to undergo further reaction with the atmospheric species.
The reaction between the peroxy radical of dimethylphenol and HO
2
proceeds by the loss of peroxy group, leading to the formation of m-cresol,

488 Chapter 19
 17/11/2013 09:29:44.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00462
View Online

## Page 509

formic acid and O 2. The m-cresol further undergoes oxidative degradation
reaction with O 2, leading to the formation of ring cleavage and ring rearrangement products. The reaction pathway corresponding to the formation
of 3-hydroxy-2-methyl-2H-pyran-2-carbaldehyde and 7-methyl-2,8-dioxabicyclo[5,1,0]octa-3,5-diene-3-ol are found to be the most favorable due to the
small barrier of 11.16 kcal mol
/C0 1. All the studied reaction channels are found to
be exothermic at 298.15 K. The highest exothermicity of 93.57 kcal mol /C0 1 is
observed for the formation of 2-hydroxy-cyclohept-4-ene-1,3-dione, which may
facilitate autoignition and combustion processes in the troposphere.
The results presented in this study allow for the elucidation of the reaction
mechanism and kinetics of the major products of alkylated aromatic compounds in the troposphere. The results show that the peroxy radical chemistry
achieves the conversion of dimethylphenol to unsaturated dicarbonyls and aldehydes, thereby aiding oxidation and combustion processes that either releases large amount of energy or form reactive free radicals.
19.8 Conclusions and Outlook
1. The theoretical studies on atmospheric chemical reactions are vital to
study the degradation of pollutants in the atmosphere. These studies will
provide information about the formation of new reactive species and their
lifetime in the atmosphere.
2. Experimental determination of the reaction pathways is tedious because
the isolation of transition states and intermediates is very diﬃcult due to
their instability. Electronic structure calculations through theoretical
methods are useful in modeling and characterizing the reaction pathways.
3. Density functional theory (DFT) based calculations are widely used
methods to study chemical reactions. The wave function based methods
provide accurate reaction pathways, but the computational cost for such
methods is expensive.
4. Transition state theory (TST) and Rice–Ramsperger–Kassel–Marcus;
(RRKM) theory are the theories most widely used to calculate the rate
constant. TST is used to study bimolecular reactions and RRKM theory is
the generalization of TST which is applicable for unimolecular reactions.

Theoretical Studies on Mechanism and Kinetics of Atmospheric Chemical Reactions 489
 17/11/2013 09:29:44.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00462
View Online

## Page 510

5. In the atmosphere, VOCs are emitted as pollutants. The degradation of an
aromatic VOC, 2,3-dimethylphenol by reaction with OH radical is studied
as a special case. The secondary reactions from the initial oxidation reaction are studied in detail and the lifetime of m-cresol resulting from the
secondary reaction is determined.
6. Several research groups have focused on studying atmospheric chemical
reactions using theoretical techniques. Results from theoretical calculations are also used to evaluate radiative forcing and global warming potential for hazardous materials emitted into the atmosphere. The
atmospheric data evaluation groups make use of theoretical rate constants in the attainment of ﬁrm data for rate constants. The theory provides an eﬀective communication between laboratory scientists,
atmospheric modelers and ﬁeld observatories in order to model the atmospheric reaction mechanisms. This multidisciplinary approach will
help to develop a clear picture about the degradation of pollutants in the
atmosphere.

CHAPTER 20
Computation of Intrinsic RRKM
and Non-RRKM Unimolecular
Rate Constants
AMIT KUMAR PAUL, SUJITHA KOLAKKANDY,
SUBHA PRATIHAR AND WILLIAM L. HASE*
Department of Chemistry and Biochemistry, Texas Tech University,
Lubbock, TX 79409-1061, USA
*Email: bill.hase@ttu.edu
20.1 Introduction
The most widely used model for calculating unimolecular rate constants is
Rice–Ramsperger–Kassel–Marcus (RRKM) theory.
1–3 RRKM theory is a
limiting model of unimolecular kinetics, which assumes a microcanonical
ensemble of states is maintained for the energized unimolecular reactants A* as
they decompose. Apparently, the microcanonical model for unimolecular
decomposition was ﬁrst derived by Bohr and Wheeler.
4,5 However, it was not
until the 1950s that RRKM was developed in a manner amendable for use in
interpreting chemical kinetics experiments. 1–3 Applications of RRKM theory
by Rabinovitch and co-workers were important for tests of the theory and
facilitating its general use.
6–8 Within the ion-molecule community, RRKM
theory became known as quasi-equilibrium theory (QET). 3 Early computer
simulations were performed by Bunker 9,10 to relate the microcanonical
assumption of RRKM to the actual atomic-level dynamics of molecules.
RSC Theoretical and Computational Chemistry Series No. 6
Reaction Rate Constant Computations: Theories and Applications
Edited by Keli Han and Tianshu Chu
r The Royal Society of Chemistry 2014
Published by the Royal Society of Chemistry, www.rsc.org
494
 17/11/2013 09:29:55.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00494

## Page 515

This work led to the understanding of intrinsic RRKM and non-RRKM
dynamics for unimolecular reactions. 11
The research presented in this chapter addresses the fundamental atomistic
dynamics of unimolecular reactions and their relationships to the RRKM
theoretical model. The presentation follows previous reviews of this topic.
12–16
This chapter includes: a derivation of the expression of the RRKM unimolecular rate constant; discussions of intrinsic RRKM and non-RRKM
unimolecular dynamics, and how these dynamics are aﬀected by the classical
phase space structure of energized molecules; examples of intrinsic non-RRKM
dynamics from experiments and simulations; the role of anharmonicity in
calculating accurate RRKM rate constants; and quantum scattering and intrinsic RRKM and non-RRKM dynamics.
20.2 The RRKM Rate Constant for a Microcanonical
Ensemble
20.2.1 Derivation of the RRKM Rate Constant
The RRKM rate constant is derived from classical statistical mechanics and is
the classical statistical mechanical model for the unimolecular decomposition
of energized reactants A* at total energy E.
17 To derive the RRKM unimolecular rate constant k(E), a dividing surface is placed in the classical phase
space, which separates the unimolecular reactants from products. The assumptions of RRKM are that a microcanonical ensemble of states exists for the
decomposing reactants and that if a trajectory crosses the dividing surface, in
the direction of reactants to products, it does not recross the dividing surface
and reform reactants. Thus, the one way ﬂux across the dividing surface for this
ensemble gives the unimolecular rate constant k(E). In the vicinity of the dividing surface the Hamiltonian for the system may be separated without approximation into two parts:
H ¼ H
1 þ H 0 ð20:1Þ
where H1 deﬁnes the energy for the conjugate coordinate q1 and momentum p1
for motion across the dividing surface and H0 gives the energy for the remaining
s – 1 conjugate coordinates and momenta, which are orthogonal to q1 and p1.
The dividing surface has a value for q1 equal to q1
z and microcanonical transition states have a value for q1 which lie between q1
z and q1
z þ dq1
z. The potential energy at the transition state, with H0 ¼ 0, is the classical threshold for
unimolecular reaction identiﬁed as E0. The coordinate q1
z for the transition
state (TS) is called the ‘reaction coordinate’.
The assumption of RRKM theory is that the total phase space of the
unimolecular reactant, including that for q1
z þ dq1
z, is populated statistically to
form a microcanonical ensemble of states at total energy E. This permits the
ratio of molecules at the dividing surface to the total number of molecules
[dN(q1
z,p1
z)/N] to be expressed as the ratio of the phase space volume at the

20.3 Intrinsic RRKM and Non-RRKM Unimolecular
Dynamics
20.3.1 Rate Expressions
RRKM theory assumes that a microcanonical ensemble is maintained as the
energized A* molecules decompose so that, at any time t; kðEÞ is given by: 17,23
/C0 dN tðÞ
dt ¼ kEðÞ NtðÞ ð 20:14Þ
As a result of the time-independent kEðÞ ; NðtÞ decays exponentially, i.e.
NtðÞ ¼ N 0ðÞ e/C0 kEðÞ t ð20:15Þ
An important quantity in studying unimolecular dynamics is the lifetime
distribution PtðÞ which equals /C0½ dNðtÞ=dt/C138=Nð0Þ:10 According to RRKM
theory, PtðÞ is given by:
PtðÞ ¼ kEðÞ e/C0 kEðÞ t ð20:16Þ
so that both the t ¼ 0 intercept of PðtÞ and its time-dependence give the RRKM
rate constant kEðÞ . A molecule which behaves in this manner is said to be intrinsically RRKM. 11 An example of intrinsically RRKM unimolecular decomposition is Al 6 ! Al5 þ Al;24 which is illustrated in Figure 20.1.
For the microcanonical ensemble assumed by RRKM theory, each state in
the energy interval E ! E þ dE has equal probability of being populated.
Figure 20.1 Trajectory Al 6 - Al5 þ Al lifetime distribution P(t), following microcannonical sampling. The histogram plot represents the number of Al6
dissociations per unit time interval. The dashed line represents the
random lifetime distribution of eqn (20.16). The total energy is 40 kcal
mol/C0 1 and the angular momentum is zero.
Adapted from ref. 24.
498 Chapter 20
 17/11/2013 09:29:55.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00494
View Online

## Page 519

RRKM theory assumes the microcanonical ensemble exists at t ¼ 0 and is
maintained during the unimolecular decomposition. For the latter to occur,
repopulation of the decomposing states results from rapid intramolecular vibrational energy redistribution (IVR). The states are strongly coupled.
Intrinsic non-RRKM occurs when the initial microcanonical ensemble is not
maintained during the decomposition.
9–11 The unimolecular rate constant is
then time-dependent, i.e.
/C0 dN tðÞ
dt ¼ kE ; tðÞ NtðÞ ð 20:17Þ
NðtÞ is no longer a single exponential as assumed by RRKM theory, but may
be represented by a multi-exponential function as: 11
NtðÞ
Nð0Þ ¼
X
i
fie/C0 ki t ð20:18Þ
where the sum of fi equals unity. The lifetime distribution for the nonexponential NðtÞ is:
PtðÞ ¼
X
i
fikie/C0 ki t ð20:19Þ
with P 0ðÞ ¼ P
i fiki equal to the RRKM rate constant kðEÞ, since a microcanonical ensemble exists at t ¼ 0. Thus for intrinsic non-RRKM
decomposition, the intercept of PðtÞ gives the RRKM rate constant.
Intrinsic non-RRKM dynamics occurs when transitions between individual
molecular rovibrational states are slower than transitions leading to products.
As a result, the vibrational states do not have equal dissociation probabilities.
In terms of classical phase space dynamics, slow transitions between the states
occur when the reactant phase space consists of several regions, which are
weakly coupled on the timescale of the unimolecular reaction, and when there is
at least one bottleneck in the phase space other than the one deﬁning the TS.
For non-exponential decay, one distinguishes an initial fast decay (faster than
the RRKM rate) and a slower decay component which sets in at later times.
The initial decay is faster than the RRKM prediction, because the reactive
region of phase space is smaller than the total phase space for this initial
decay.
25 Thus the decomposing molecule behaves as a smaller molecule, a
dynamical property ﬁrst described by O. K. Rice in 1930. 26
An extreme illustration of intrinsic non-RRKM dynamics occurs when one
part of the phase space is totally decoupled from the reaction coordinate. 14 The
resulting relative NðtÞ is:
NðtÞ
Nð0Þ ¼ f1e/C0 k1t þ f2 ð20:20Þ
and the lifetime distribution PðtÞ is:
PtðÞ ¼ f1k1e/C0 k1t ð20:21Þ
Computation of Intrinsic RRKM and Non-RRKM Unimolecular Rate Constants 499
 17/11/2013 09:29:55.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00494
View Online

## Page 520

so that the RRKM rate constant is f1k1. However, the decomposition occurs
via a rate constant k1, which is larger than the RRKM rate constant. For this
model k1 is a microcanonical-like rate constant, i.e. k1 ¼ Nz=r1, with the decomposition transition state sum of states Nz and a restricted density of states
equal to f1 times the total density r.
20.3.2 Phase Space Structure and Dynamics
As discussed below, there are two limiting types of motion for an energized
molecule, i.e. regular (quasiperiodic) and irregular (chaotic). 27 Both of these
motions may exist for the trajectories selected in preparing a microcanonical
ensemble of state for a unimolecular reactant.
14 However, the RRKM assumption that a microcanonical ensemble of states is maintained during the
unimolecular reaction requires chaotic motion, so that the dynamics is ergodic
on the timescale of the unimolecular reaction. As regions of the reactant phase
space become unoccupied, as a result of unimolecular decomposition, the
ergodic dynamics of the unimolecular reaction will reﬁll these regions, maintaining a microcanonical ensemble. The diﬀerent types of intramolecular motions for a unimolecular reactant are considered below.
At low energies, with inﬁnitesimal displacement from the equilibrium
geometry, the molecule’s motion is described by the normal mode Hamiltonian:
H ¼
X
i
Hi ¼
X
i
ðP2
i þ liQ2
i Þ=2 ð20:22Þ
for which the energy is a sum of energies in the individual normal modes. Since
the Hamiltonian is separable, each normal mode moves independent of the
other modes with no energy transfer between the modes. The classical motion is
regular giving rise to quasiperiodic trajectories. Each trajectory moves in a
restricted region of the phase space, which has a shape of an s-dimensional
torus for a Hamiltonian with s normal modes.
27
If this Hamiltonian is extended to energies above the unimolecular threshold
(i.e. the Slater theory), 20 reaction is assumed to have occurred if a particular
internal coordinate q1 such as a bond length attains a critical extension qz. With
the normal mode model, the displacement of internal coordinates and normal
mode coordinates are related through the linear transformation Dq ¼ LQ.A ta
particular time, the normal mode coordinates may phase together so that q1
exceeds qz and decomposition occurs. If a microcanonical ensemble is prepared
for the normal mode Hamiltonian, at t ¼ 0 the initial unimolecular rate constant will be that of RRKM theory, but the ensuing decomposition will be
intrinsically non-RRKM.
10,11 Energy does not ﬂow between the normal modes,
and only trajectories with proper energies in the normal modes and proper
phase relationships between the normal modes will react. Many of the trajectories will be unreactive and remained trapped in the reactant region of
phase space.

## Page 521

The normal mode Hamiltonian is rigorously accurate for small displacements from equilibrium and, thus, only for low energies. As the energy increased, a coupling term H
0
between the normal modes becomes important for
the molecular Hamiltonian, i.e. H ¼ Hnm þ H
0
, with Hnm remaining a good
zero-order representation of the full Hamiltonian. 17,27 However, with addition
of the H
0
coupling term, some of the trajectories for a microcanonical ensemble
at H ¼ E exhibit irregular (chaotic) motion instead of regular (quasiperiodic)
motion. For the former, chaotic motion energy ﬂows freely between the modes
of the molecule. Regular and irregular motion for a two-dimensional H /C0 C /C0
C ! H þ C ¼ C Hamiltonian
28 are distinguished in Figure 20.2.
At low energies this additional coupling only causes a small fraction of the
trajectories to become irregular, with the vast majority remaining regular and
moving on s-dimensional tori as deﬁned by the normal mode Hamiltonian. 27
As the energy is increased, the chaotic trajectories become more prevalent,
coexisting with the quasiperiodic trajectories. Of interest at these higher energies is that the tori for the regular trajectories may no longer be well described
by the normal mode Hamiltonian and another coordinate, momentum representation, is required. As the energy is continually increased, tori—to which the
quasiperiodic trajectories are conﬁned—are destroyed. At a suﬃciently high
energy all the tori are destroyed, with only chaotic trajectories so that the dynamics is ergodic.
The dynamics of the transition from quasiperiodic to chaotic motion have
been addressed. As identiﬁed by numerical simulations the presence of the
coupling H
0 alone, for H ¼ Hnm þ H
0
, is insuﬃcient to destroy the tori for the
quasiperiodic trajectories and yielding ergodic dynamics. 27,29 For free exchange
of energy between the vibrational modes resonance conditions are required for
the energy transfer, which for a two-dimensional Hamiltonian are speciﬁed by
n
1o1 /C0 n2o2 /C25 0.27,29 Such frequency matchings are known as ‘internal’ or
‘anharmonic’ resonances and they begin to destroy the tori of the regular
quasiperiodic trajectories as the energy is increased. The role of the resonances
is explained by the Kolmogorov–Arnold–Moser (KAM) theorem,
30 which
states that if there are no resonances among a set of oscillators, the addition of
a perturbation, that is suﬃciently small compared with the total energy, does
not make the system ergodic.
The tori destroyed ﬁrst are those wit h commensurate frequency, which
satisfy resonance conditions as that given above. Tori with incommensurate
frequencies are not destroyed and only s lightly deformed by the perturbation.
An important ﬁnding is that, for a period of time, the trajectories in the
vicinity of regular trajectories retain s ome degree of regularity in their motion
and are said to move on ‘vague tori’.
31,32 For a multi-dimensional Hamiltonian, the regions of phase space with the chaotic trajectories intermingle with
the tori for the regular trajectories and the vague tori for the ‘somewhat’
regular trajectories. These chaotic trajectories are connected via an Arnold
web of anharmonic chaotic resonances.
29,33 As the energy is increased the
fraction of quasiperiodic trajectories i n the phase space becomes smaller, and

## Page 523

A classical microcanonical ensemble for an intrinsic non-RRKM molecule
consists of chaotic, ‘vague tori’ and quasiperiodic trajectories. Such a complex
non-ergodic phase space structure leads to a non-exponential P(t). As an
application of the KAM theorem, Oxtoby and Rice
35 have shown that the
intrinsic non-RRKM dynamics that Bunker found for model triatomic
Hamiltonians9,10 results from insuﬃcient internal resonances to yield ergodic
dynamics.
20.3.3 Vibrational/Rotational Energy Levels
20.3.3.1 Quantum Mechanical
Since unimolecular reaction dynamics begins with the excitation of the reactant
molecule to its high energy levels, the detail descriptions of this reaction dynamics requires a basic understanding of these energy levels. In this section
molecular vibrational and vibrational/rotational energy levels are described,
with a brief sketch of the underlying theory.
The calculations are based on the Born–Oppenheimer principle which separates the electronic motion from the vibrational and rotational motion of the
nuclei by considering that the light electrons move much faster in a molecule
than the heavy nuclei, i.e.
^HC ¼ ^T
N þ ^Te þ VNN þ Vee þ VNe
/C0/C1
C ¼ EC ð20:23Þ
where ^TN and ^Te are the kinetic energy operators of the nuclei and the
electrons, respectively, VNN and Vee are the repulsive electrostatic potential
energy of the nuclei and the electrons, respectively, and VNe is the attractive
electrostatic potential energy between nuclei and electrons. According to the
Born–Oppenheimer expansion, one can write the total wavefunction C as a
product of an electronic wavefunction C
e and a nuclear wavefunction CN as:
C ¼ CeðR; rÞCN ðRÞð 20:24Þ
The electronic wavefunction Ce is a solution of the electronic Schro ¨dinger
equation:
ðTe þ Vee þ VNeÞCeðR; rÞ¼ EeðRÞCeðR; rÞð 20:25Þ
and the nuclear wavefunction CN is the solution of the nuclear Schro ¨dinger
equation:
½TN þ VðRÞ/C138CN ðRÞ¼ ECN ðRÞð 20:26Þ
where V(R) ¼ VNN(R) þ Ee(R).
The center of mass translational energy may be removed from TN in
eqn (20.26) to give a Hamiltonian for vibrational/rotational motion, which may
be expressed in Cartesian, internal or normal mode coordinates. The

If there is a principal ci in the linear combination for eqn (20.32), the

Cn. A low levels of excitation, where the vibrational Hamiltonian is nearly
separable in normal mode coordinates and forms a good zero-order Hamiltonian, there will be only one principal normal mode basis function in the linear
combination. However, at higher levels of excitation where anharmonic
couplings between the normal mode coordinates occur, there may not be one
principal normal mode basis function in c
n, and it may be impossible to assign
normal mode quantum numbers to the energy levels. If this is the case it is
important to determine whether basis functions associated with another type of
zero-order Hamiltonian would give assignable wavefunctions. If there is no
zero-order Hamiltonian and associated basis functions that yield one principal

Using perturbation theory, Watson 47 has derived an expression, similar to
eqn (20.31) but with rotation included, for a polyatomic molecule’s vibration/
rotation energy levels. The analytic form for a prolate symmetric top has been
given.
48 This approach assumes the molecule has ‘good’ vibration/rotation
quantum numbers and, if this is not the case or if more accurate values for the
energy levels are required, the variational method may also be used to determine vibrational/rotational energy levels. For this approach the wavefunction
C

CJ ¼

where ci is a suitable vibrational wavefunction ( e.g. the normal mode wavefunction), RJK is a symmetric top wavefunction and cJ
iK is the variational
parameter. Vibrational/rotational coupling will mix the (2 J þ 1) K-levels for a
particular J. If this coupling is extensive so that all K-levels are mixed, K will no
longer be a meaningful quantum number for an energy level. 49,50 For such a
situation, and if it is also impossible to assign vibrational quantum numbers,
the levels are intrinsically unassignable and the levels are only identiﬁed by their
energy and total angular momentum j.
20.3.3.2 Semi-classical
If the classical motion is quasiperiodic or ‘somewhat’ regular, Einstein–
Brillouin–Kramers (EBK) semi-classical quantization is applicable for ﬁnding
the energy levels.
51,52 For N coupled modes, the quantum conditions are:
Z
Ci
XN
k¼1
pkdqk ¼ ni þ 1
2
/C18/C19
hi ¼ 1t o NðÞ ð 20:36Þ
where ni are integers, and qk and pk are the coordinates and their conjugate
momenta, respectively. Ci are topologically independent closed paths, which
need not be along actual trajectories. These are the paths of the N-dimensional
torus on which the quasiperiodic trajectory moves. For a two-mode Hamiltonian this torus has the shape of a tube. For a chaotic trajectory the invariant
curves C
i, and thus torus, do not exist and EBK semi-classical quantization
cannot be applied.
20.3.3.3 Regular and Irregular Spectra
Spectra that can be assigned approximate quantum numbers are called ‘regular’
spectra. As discussed above, when the energy is increased mode couplings
become important and the spectrum may lose its patterns and progressions
associated with a regular structure.
53 If a zero-order Hamiltonian and basis
506 Chapter 20
 17/11/2013 09:29:55.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00494
View Online

## Page 527

cannot be found to represent the eigenstates Cn, the spectra are intrinsically
unassignable and identiﬁed as ‘irregular’.
Regular and irregular spectra may be distinguished by considering the distributions of nearest neighbor energy level spacings. 54–57 For regular spectra,
the energy level positions are well approximated by a total energy E which is a
sum of individual mode energies, i.e. E ¼ E1 þ E2 þ E3 ... . For such a system
the energy levels behave as a random sequence, since there is no correlation
between the individual mode energies.
58,59 The resulting distribution of energy
level spacings between the nearest neighbors is given by the Poisson, or random, distribution:
PsðÞ ¼ expð/C0 s=hsiÞ=hsið 20:37Þ
where hsi is the average spacing between the energy levels. The Poisson distribution peaks at zero spacing, i.e. the levels tend to cluster.
As discussed above, for an irregular spectrum there is strong mixing in any
zero-order representation and the level energy is no longer a sum of individual
mode energies. For such a system the energy levels may be calculated by a
variational method, which involves diagonalizing a matrix whose elements are
coupling integrals such as the one in eqn (20.34). If the spectrum is irregular, the
elements of this matrix are independent and truly random.
50 From numerical
studies of random matrices, 54,57 it was found that the distribution of energy
level spacings is very closely approximated by the Wigner surmise: 60
PsðÞ ¼ ps expð/C0 ps2=4hsi2Þ=2hsi2 ð20:38Þ
Thus, for an irregular spectrum the distribution of energy level spacings no
longer peaks at zero as for a regular system. This is understood by recognizing
that the couplings between the zero-order energy levels cause levels with similar
energies to repel.
20.3.4 Phase Space Dynamics and Energy Levels, Classical/
Quantum Correspondence, and RRKM Theory
As described above, and summarized in Figure 20.3, there is a close relationship
between the nature of the phase space dynamics for a vibrational/rotational
Hamiltonian and the Hamiltonian’s energy levels.
61 The increase in the fraction
of phase space consisting of chaotic trajectories, resulting in ergodic dynamics
at high energy, is illustrated in Figure 20.3. Concomitant with this increase in
chaotic trajectories, with increase in energy, is a smaller fraction of trajectories
in an energy interval E ! E þ DE which may be used to identify energy levels
by EBK semi-classical quantization.
There is a good correspondence between the above classical description of
the phase space dynamics and quantum dynamics. Classically there is an increase in the fraction of trajectories which are chaotic with increase in energy,
while quantum mechanically all the states are assignable at low energy, then
Computation of Intrinsic RRKM and Non-RRKM Unimolecular Rate Constants 507
 17/11/2013 09:29:55.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00494
View Online

## Page 528

some become unassignable at intermediate energies, and at most a small
number of states are assignable at high energies. In general, energy levels which
are assignable have quasiperiodic or ‘somewhat’ regular motion, while unassignable levels are associated with irregular chaotic motion. NO
2 is an illustrative example of these dynamics. 62,63 However, there may be diﬀerences in
the classical energy regime for the transition from regular to irregular motion
and the quantum energy regime from a regular to irregular spectrum. Irregular
dynamics may occur at a lower energy classically than quantum mechanically,
as found for HOCl energy levels near the dissociation threshold.
64 Work by
Davis65 suggests that it may be possible to enhance the classical–quantum
correspondence for the transition from regular to irregular dynamics. He
presented an approximate EBK semi-classical quantization method which can
be used to determine accurate eigenvalues, with quantum numbers, well into
the chaotic energy regime.
As shown in Figure 20.4, the nature of the spectrum, near the unimolecular
threshold, indicates whether the unimolecular dynamics will be intrinsically
RRKM or non-RRKM. RRKM theory assumes irregular chaotic dynamics
and, if this is the nature of the spectrum near the dissociation threshold, intrinsic RRKM behavior is expected. In contrast, if an appreciable fraction of
the spectrum is regular near the dissociation threshold, the dynamics should be
intrinsically non-RRKM.
Figure 20.3 Illustration of the fraction of trajectories in the phase space, which
are chaotic versus energy, and the relationship of the fraction to the:
(1) ability to determine energy levels by EBK semi-classical quantization;
(2) assign quantum numbers to the vibrational levels; and (3) nature of
the spectrum, i.e. regular or irregular.

## Page 529

20.4 Examples of Intrinsic Non-RRKM Dynamics
20.4.1 Experiments
Low-barrier isomerization reactions appear to be a general class of reactions
that have intrinsic non-RRKM dynamics. Thermal experiments, and monoenergetic experiments at thermal energies, give rate constants much smaller
than the RRKM values. This work was initiated by Bauer and co-workers,
66
and the molecules studied include aziridine, 66 trans-stilbene,67 2-ﬂuoroethanol,68 methyl and ethyl cyclohexanone, 69 N-acetyl tryptophan methyl
amide70 and cyclopropane carboxaldehyde. 71 The results are summarized in
Table 20.1. Considering the discussion in the previous section, and the relationship between intrinsic RRKM and non-RRKM dynamics as depicted in
Figure 20.4, the intrinsic non-RRKM dynamics for these isomerizations is
overall not surprising. The isomerization barriers are low and a regular spectrum is expected for these molecules at energies near their barriers. As shown in
Figure 20.4, this property is consistent with intrinsic non-RRKM dynamics.
It should be recognized that there is an ambiguity in the reported experimental isomerization rate constants. Following the discussion in section 20.3,
the intrinsic non-RRKM isomerization lifetime distribution P(t) and reactant
population N(t) are non-exponential with multiple rate constants. A possible
model for the isomerization dynamics is that a microcanonical ensemble of
reactant states has a P(t) with an initial small component which has a rate
constant k
1 much larger than the RRKM value and a much larger longer-time
Figure 20.4 Relationship between the nature of the vibrational spectrum versus
energy and intrinsic RRKM and non-RRKM unimolecular dynamics.
Computation of Intrinsic RRKM and Non-RRKM Unimolecular Rate Constants 509
 17/11/2013 09:29:55.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00494
View Online

## Page 530

component with a rate constant k2 much smaller than that of RRKM theory.
The average rate constant f1k1 þ f2k2 is then that of RRKM theory, with
f1 /C28 f2 /C25 1. In a collision environment, P(t) is averaged over the collision
probability and the resulting rate constant is pressure dependent. 20,72–75 For the
above two-component model for P(t), the low pressure rate constant is k2.
Collision-free time domain experiments of these isomerizations, in which the
number of reactant and/or product molecules is followed in time, would be very
valuable for identifying details of the unimolecular dynamics. Non-exponential
P(t)a n d N(t) are expected.
20.4.2 Simulations
Both experiments and simulations have shown that the chemical dynamics of
gas-phase X/C0 þ CH3Y ! XCH3 þ Y/C0 SN2 nucleophilic substitution reactions
are non-statistical. 76–79 Reactions, such as Cl/C0 þ CH3Br ! ClCH3 þ Br/C0 ,
have X/C0 --- CH3Y and XCH3 --- Y/C0 ion–dipole complexes separated by a
central barrier (Figure 20.5) and the unimolecular dynamics of these complexes
are intrinsically non-RRKM.
75,80–87 These dynamics arise in part from weak
couplings between the three low frequency intermolecular modes of the complex and the complex’s much higher frequency nine intramolecular modes.
Chemical dynamics simulations were performed to study the unimolecular
decomposition of microcanonical ensembles versus energy of the Cl/C0 --- CH3Br
ion–dipole complex. 82,83 An analytic potential energy surface was used for the
simulations. The complex has two unimolecular reaction paths, i.e. dissociation
to Cl/C0 þ CH3Br or isomerization to the ClCH3---Br/C0 ion–dipole complex. The
simulations were performed for energies of 30–80 kcal mol /C0 1 and the resulting
non-exponential N(t)/N(0) were ﬁt by a sum of three exponentials, i.e. eqn
(20.18). The resulting fi and ki ﬁtting parameters are listed in Table 20.2.

/C0 --- CH3Br unimolecular dynamics. The non-exponential dynamics is more pronounced for Pr1. The ClCH3---Br/C0 isomerization product
has three prominent components in its non-exponential ﬁt for each energy. In
contrast for Pr2, Cl/C0 þ CH3Br formation, there are only two dominant components in the non-exponential for E ¼ 40 kcal mol /C0 1 and a single dominant
component at the higher energies of 60 and 80 kcal mol /C0 1. In addition, the
range in the rate constants for the Pr1 non-exponential is larger than for the Pr2
non-exponential.
The diﬀerent dynamics observed in the simulations for forming the Cl/C0 þ
CH3Br dissociation and ClCH3---Br/C0 isomerization products are qualitatively
consistent with a model of intermolecular and intramolecular complexes 76,79
for Cl/C0 ---CH3Br, depending on which modes are excited. The intermolecular
and intramolecular complexes preferentially dissociate to Cl/C0 þ CH3Br, Pr2
and isomerize to ClCH3 --- Br/C0 , Pr1, respectively. If the phase space regions for

20.5 Calculating an Accurate RRKM Rate Constant
20.5.1 Variational RRKM Theory
In deriving the expression for the RRKM rate constant, it is assumed that the
rate at which reactant molecules cross the transition state, in the direction of
products, is the same rate at which the reactants form products. Any system
passing through the transition state does so only once, i.e. no recrossing of the
transition state (TS) is possible. Thus, the best position for TS along the reaction coordinate is where the ﬂux through the TS is minimized.
91 This TS will
give the smallest unimolecular rate constant and thus minimize the eﬀect of
possible TS recrossings. This model is called variational RRKM theory.
92 For
unimolecular reactions with a well-deﬁned barrier and associated saddle point,
the variational TS is close to or at this saddle point. However, the variational
model is crucial for unimolecular dissociation reactions which do not have a
Figure 20.6 Relative number of products, Pr1 (ClCH3----Br/C0 ) and Pr2 (Cl/C0 þ CH3Br)
versus time t (in ps), determined by the analytic PES simulations. The
energy, E, is in units of kcal mol /C0 1.
Computation of Intrinsic RRKM and Non-RRKM Unimolecular Rate Constants 513

## Page 534

saddle point and for which there is no barrier for association of the unimolecular fragments, i.e. CH4 - H þ CH3.92
To minimize the ﬂux at the TS, the TS’s sum of states is minimized versus the
reaction coordinate q1
dNz E /C0 E0ðq1Þ½/C138
dq1
¼ 0 ð20:41Þ
where E0ðq1Þ is the potential energy as a function of q1. The location of the TS
at q1 ¼ qz
1, where Nz[E – E0(q1)] takes the minimum, is expected to be the best
bottleneck for the reaction. The minimum in Nz, for a dissociation reaction like
CH4 - H þ CH3, results from two competing factors. One is the increase in the
potential energy E0 with increase in q1, which has the eﬀect of decreasing
the sum of states Nz. The second is the transition of vibrational modes of the
reactants to rotational and translational modes as q1 is increased; modes that
are called ‘transitional modes’. 93–95 This latter factor has the eﬀect of increasing
Nz with increase in q1. The combination of these two factors gives a minimum
in N z. For CH 4 - H þ CH3, three CH 4 vibrational modes are lost during the
dissociation; i.e. a C–H stretch, the reaction coordinate and two H–CH 3 bends.
These modes become H-atom translations, for the products. For C 2H6 -
2CH3 six C 2H6 vibrational modes are lost during the dissociation, which become three translations and three rotations of the products. These six modes
are the C–C stretch, the H
3C–CH3 torsion and the four CH 3 rocking motions.
To perform a variational RRKM calculation for a dissociation reaction as
CH4 - H þ CH3 or C2H6 - 2CH3 requires knowing how the potential energy
and properties of the transitional modes change as the reaction coordinate q1 is
increased. This information may be obtained from an electronic structure
calculation and there are two models for the ensuing variational RRKM calculation. For the vibrator model,
95–97 the intrinsic reaction coordinate (IRC) 98
or analytic potential model 96 may be used to determine q1 and its associated
properties. The IRC is deﬁned by the path of steepest decent on the potential
energy surface in mass-weighted coordinates. As described by the reaction path
Hamiltonian,99,100 this IRC calculation gives the reaction coordinate q1 along
the reaction path, and the potential energy E0, moments of inertia I, and vibrational frequencies m associated with q1. The m are the 3 N-7 vibrational frequencies orthogonal to q1. The same information may be determined from the
analytic potential model. This information may be used to determine harmonic
oscillator/rigid rotor energy levels versus q
1, from which the minimum in Nz[E –
E0(q1)] may be obtained. This method is expected to be appropriate if the
translational modes are well-described as harmonic oscillators, instead of
hindered or free rotors.
The second model for the variational TS is a rotor model, for which the
Hamiltonian is written as: 101
H ¼ Hn1 þ Hn2 þ Tr1 þ Tr2 þ l2
2mr2 þ Et þ V ð20:42Þ
514 Chapter 20

Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00494
View Online

## Page 535

where the Hv’s and Tr’s are the vibrational Hamiltonians and rotational kinetic
energies of the reaction fragments respectively, l is the orbital angular
momentum, m is the reduced mass of the fragments, r their center of mass
separation, Et is the relative translational energy projected on the fragments’
center of mass separation, and V is the fragments’ intermolecular potential.
The unimolecular system’s total angular momentum is the vector sum of the
rotational angular momenta j1 and j2 for the two fragments and the orbital
angular momentum, i.e. j ¼ j1 þ j2 þ l.
Diﬀerent rotor TS models have been advanced and, for each, the standard
assumption is that Hn1 and Hn2 are ‘conserved’ as the fragments associate to
form the variational TS, i.e. the vibrational frequencies of the fragments are
constant and do not change (although these frequencies may be changed as a
function of r if considered important). For the phase space theory (PST) model
of Klotz,
102 the variational TS is assumed to be in the product limit, with the
two fragments freely rotating. As a result the l2=2mr2 and V terms in eqn (20.42)
do not contribute to the TS sum of states. For the orbiting transition state
(OTS)/PST model, r is taken as the reaction coordinate q
1 and the intermolecular potential V is assumed to be isotropic. Variational TSs are found
versus E , j and l, and the minimum in the sum of states versus r is at the centrifugal barrier. The ﬂexible TS model of Wardlaw and Marcus 94,103 is the same
as OTS/PST, except that the fragments’ intermolecular potential is no longer
assumed to be isotropic, so that the full anisotropy in the potential is included.
A more complete rotor model is the variable reaction coordinate (VRC)
model,
104,105 which uses the anisotropic intermolecular potential as the ﬂexible
model, but uses a generalized reaction coordinate q1 that may contain contributions from bending and stretching motions, as well as the fragment’s centerof-mass separation. The latter is q1 for the OTS/PST and ﬂexible TS models.
The VRC model has given good agreement with experiment.
20.5.2 Anharmonic Correction
The nearly universal approach for calculating the RRKM rate constant k(E)o r
k(E, J) is to assume harmonic vibrational frequencies for both the unimolecular
reactant and the TS. This is an uncertain assumption and the harmonic rate
constant, kh E; JðÞ is related to the actual anharmonic rate constant by:
kanh E; JðÞ ¼ fanh E; JðÞ kh E; JðÞ ð 20:43Þ
where fanh E; JðÞ is the anharmonic correction term, which is given by the ratio
of the anharmonic corrections for the sum and density of states; i.e.

signiﬁcantly more important than for Nz E /C0 E0ðÞ , and fanh E; JðÞ is well approximated by 1

TS is expected also to become important.
There are very few experimental studies, relevant to unimolecular dynamics,
of the eﬀect of anharmonicity on a molecule’s density of states. This requires
obtaining spectroscopic information at very high energies. Two molecules
which have been investigated are H
2CO and H–C /C17C–H.44,107 For H 2CO at
80 kcal mol /C0 1, the experimental ranh(E) is 11 times larger than rh(E).107 For
H–C/C17C–H at 77 kcal mol /C0 1, ranhðEÞ=rhðEÞ is 6.44 In general it is not possible
to directly observe energy levels for the TS and thus spectroscopic methods may
not be used to study the importance of anharmonicity for the TS. Theoretical/
computational approaches are needed to investigate anharmonic eﬀects for
the TS.
An extensive amount of information concerning anharmonicity has come
from classical mechanical calculations. The reactant anharmonic classical
density of states is given by the phase space integral:

These may be combined to give the anharmonic RRKM rate constant kanh(E,J)
and correction fanh(E,J) in eqns (20.43) and (20.44). A more direct approach to
obtain kanh(E,J), as described in section 20.3, is to determine it directly by
simulating the decomposition of a microcannonical ensemble of states.
Illustrative classical mechanical calculations include those for the fragment
H–C–C, CH 4, and the S N2 ion dipole complex Cl /C0 ---CH3Cl. For the H–C–C
study, an analytic potential was used consisting of the two Morse stretches and
an H–C–C bend whose force constant is attenuated as the H–C and/or C–C
bonds are stretched.
108 The dissociation energy is 90 kcal mol /C0 1 and, at this
energy, ranhðEÞ=rhðEÞ¼ 3–5 for diﬀerent model parameters. Both the Morse
and bend anharmonicities are important. The dissociation reaction CH 4 -
H þ CH3 was studied using on analytic PES and the resulting value of fanh(E)
was found to be 0.39–0.21 for the 120–150 kcal mol /C0 1 energy range. 82 Simulations of Cl /C0 ---CH3Br decomposition, utilizing an analytic PES, gave fanh
which increased from 0.17 to 1.34 as the energy was increased from 30 to 60
kcal mol /C0 1.82 The fanh(E) values of 0.17–0.25 for E of 30–40 kcal mol /C0 1 are
similar to the rhðEÞ=ranhðEÞ value of 0.5 found for the Cl /C0 ---CH3Cl complex at
E of 35–37 kcal mol /C0 1.75 For the highest energy of 60 kcal mol /C0 1, fanh(E) is 1.34
and greater than unity, indicating that anharmonicity has become more important for the TS than for the Cl /C0 ---CH3Br reactant.

The above are classical mechanical calculations of anharmonic corrections.
Approximate quantum mechanical anharmonic corrections have been obtained

20.6 Quantum Dynamics
The energy spectrum of a molecule which can dissociate is not actually discrete
as the relative motion of the product fragments is unbounded. If the dissociation probability of the molecule is suﬃciently small, the concept of quasibound states can be introduced. Such states are commonly referred to as
‘resonances’ since the energy of the dissociative continuum is in resonance with
the bound states of unimolecular reactants. Resonance widths ( G
n) are established from the imaginary part of the Sigert eigenvalues. 117 In the case of
isolated and non-overlapping resonances, unimolecular decay rates are generally related to the resonance widths by:
kn ¼ Gn
/C22h ð20:54Þ
Eqn (20.54) is valid when the coupling between the quasi-bound states and the
dissociative continua is weak, i.e. the average width of resonances is less than
the spacing between them. This is true when the density of states r is small
(Gnr o 1). In this case the resonance widths can be measured directly from the
experimental spectra. As the coupling becomes extensive, the decay rate and the
resonance widths are no longer related by such a simple expression, since resonance widths cannot be directly extracted from experimental results when
the resonances overlap ( G
nr 4 1). It has been seen that, in this regime, where
the quasi-bound states interacts signiﬁcantly with the dissociative continua, the
dissociation rate can be well described by RRKM theory. In the following
isolated resonances are ﬁrst discussed, followed by a discussion of overlapping
resonances.
20.6.1 Isolated Resonances
For the resonance of a unimolecular reactant, the energy is complex and
given by:

The quantity cnðtÞjj 2 gives the probability of ﬁnding the unimolecular reactant
in the resonance state. Thus, Gn gives the lifetime of the resonance state, with
the state speciﬁc unimolecular rate constant:
kn ¼ Gn
/C22h ¼ 1
tn
ð20:58Þ
where tn is the resonance’s lifetime. Taking the appropriate Fourier transform
of cnðtÞjj 2 gives the absorption spectrum for the resonance, which has a
Lorentzian line-shape. Its full-width at half maximum is Gn.
There are two limiting case for resonances’ wavefunctions, cn, as discussed in
section 20.3.3. For one, quantum numbers may be assigned to cn and the
unimolecular dissociation from the resonance state may be viewed as ‘mode
speciﬁc’. Example molecules exhibiting mode speciﬁcity are HCO
118 and
HOCl.119 For mode speciﬁcity, the unimolecular rate constant for the resonance depends on the speciﬁc mode excite; i.e. the resonance’s quantum numbers. To illustrate, for HCO dissociation, resonances within an energy interval
DE have long lifetime if the number of quanta in the H–C stretch is zero, but
much shorter if there are more quanta in this mode. Resonance states with large
amount of quanta in the C–O stretch, but zero quanta in the H–C stretch, have
orders of magnitude larger lifetimes than the RRKM prediction.
The other limiting case for c
n is the situation for which they are intrinsically
unassignable. As a result, the rate constant kn for cn may not be related to any
particular mode excitation of the unimolecular reactant. What is found is that
the distribution of k
n for cn in an energy interval E ! E þ DE, is as statistical
as possible. 120 Such unimolecular dynamics is referred to as ‘statistical state
speciﬁc’.121
A microcanonical ensemble of isolated resonance states excited incoherently
decays according to:
Nt ; E ! E þ DEðÞ ¼
X
n
exp /C0 kntðÞ ð 20:59Þ

average:
kn ¼

where M is the number of resonance states in the E ! E þ DE energy interval.
An important ﬁnding is that kn for statistical state speciﬁc decomposition is
in excellent agreement with the RRKM rate constant. This is illustrated in
Figure 20.8 for HO
2! H þ O2, whose dissociation is statistical state speciﬁc. 122
The rate constants in the ﬁgure are those for the resonances kqm, the averages
of the kqm within E ! E þ DE energy intervals, and the RRKM rate
constants.
For mode-speciﬁc decomposition, kn is also often found to approximate the
RRKM rate constant. 14 However, for mode speciﬁc decomposition, the ﬂuctuation in kn for an E ! E þ DE energy interval is usually orders of magnitude

20.6.2 Overlapping Resonances
Several theoretical studies have related resonance widths and the unimolecular
rate constant for overlapping resonances.
123–131 The following discussion focuses on studies based on the random matrix version of Feshbach’s optical
model,
133,134 especially the results of Peskin et al. 123
The transition from a non-overlapping regime to an overlapping regime
occurs approximately at Gnr ¼ 1. The analytical expression 123 for the resonance
energy corresponds to this transition for a one-dimensional potential with a
smooth barrier (within the WKB approximation
132), i.e.
E ¼ V0 þ /C22hob ð20:61Þ
where V0 is the barrier height and ob is its frequency within the harmonic
oscillator approximation. For a multidimensional system with F vibrational

E ¼ V0 þ F !ðÞ 1=F /C22h /C22oz ð20:62Þ

In Figure 20.9, the decay rate for a random initial state is plotted as a
function of the average resonance width. It is clear that keff approaches the TST
limit as N, the number of continuum states, becomes larger. The decay rate
saturates as the average resonance width increases. In the non-overlapping
regime, the rate limiting step for the decay process is the state-to-state transitions from a speciﬁc Q state to a speciﬁc P state. As the density of states in the
Q space increases, the total decay rate is limited by number of molecular states
per energy and the number of independent open channels in P. This saturation
phenomenon of the decay rate at larger values of h/C22Gi can also be understood
from the TST interpretation of the optical model. Eqns (20.73) and (20.74)
explain the two limiting cases. In contrast to the TST interpretation, the decay
rate is found to decrease when the condition h/C22Gir 4 N is reached. Bifurcation
of the resonances has been reported at identical conditions for the optical
model.
126,137,138 Someda et al. have reported126 that GRRKM was almost close to
the h/C22Gi at the point of critical overlap density, but overestimating at the
overlapping resonance regime. The reason for this is explained as the recrossing
trajectories or the premature recrossing trajectories, which go out across the
dividing surface without entering the molecular region, contributing to the
outgoing ﬂux at the dividing surface but not to the molecular dissociation.
There are diﬀerent points of view on the origin of the saturation of the decay
rate at large r. Rotter
124 has a diﬀerent perspective than that made above by
Peskin et al. 123 Rotter suggests that the saturation of the decay rates at high
level density is due to a ‘trapping eﬀect’. This involves the bifurcation of resonance widths to diﬀerent timescales and the decay rate is related to the long
lived subgroup of resonance widths. The point of Peskin et al.
125 is that the

## Page 545

decay rate tends to saturate even before the bifurcation of the resonance widths
starts and thus the trapping eﬀect is not the reason for saturation. Instead it can
account for the decrease in the decay rate in the saturation limit of the optical
model. The non-physical behavior associated with the trapping eﬀect is actually
associated with an improper sampling of the spectrum of H
eff within the ﬁnite
representation of the Q space.

## Page 550

CHAPTER 21
Molecular Dynamics Simulation
of Kinetic Isotope Eﬀects in
Enzyme-Catalyzed Reactions
JIALI GAO
Department of Chemistry and Supercomputer Institute, University of
Minnesota, 207 Pleasant Street S.E., Minneapolis, MN 55455-0431, USA
Email: gao@jialigao.org
21.1 Introduction
The ability to accurately compute kinetic isotope eﬀects (KIEs) for chemical
reactions in solution and in enzymes is important because the measured
KIEs provide the most direct probe to the nature of the transition state and
the computational results can help rationalize experimental ﬁndings.
1 This is
illustrated by the work of Schramm and co-workers, 2 who have used the
experimental KIEs to develop transition state models for the enzymatic process
catalyzed by purine nucleoside phosphorylase (PNP), which in turn were used
to design picomolar inhibitors. In principle, Schramm’s approach can be
applied to other enzymes; however, in order to establish a useful transition state
model for enzymatic reactions, it is often necessary to use sophisticated computational methods to model the structure of the transition state and to match
the computed KIEs with experiments. The challenge to theory is the diﬃculty in
accurately determining the small diﬀerence in free energy of activation due
to isotope replacements, especially for secondary and heavy isotope eﬀects.
Furthermore, unlike studies of reactions in the gas phase, one has to consider
RSC Theoretical and Computational Chemistry Series No. 6
Reaction Rate Constant Computations: Theories and Applications
Edited by Keli Han and Tianshu Chu
r The Royal Society of Chemistry 2014
Published by the Royal Society of Chemistry, www.rsc.org
530
 17/11/2013 09:30:01.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00530

## Page 551

the dynamic ﬂuctuations of enzymes 3–7 through statistical mechanical sampling
in molecular dynamics simulations, particularly for systems involving large
amplitude motions. In this chapter, we present a coupled path integral-free
energy perturbation and umbrella sampling method (PI-FEP/UM) to determine KIEs of chemical reactions in solution and in enzymes.
8,9
Quantum mechanics is essential for modeling enzyme kinetics and there are
two major challenges in these studies. 10,11 First, electronic structural theory is
required to describe the potential energy surface (PES) to model the bondmaking and bond-breaking process in an enzyme active site. Although it could
be straightforward to use molecular orbital theory and density functional
theory (DFT) on a cluster model for the active site, it is often necessary to
include the full electrostatic and steric eﬀects of the rest of the protein and
solvent system through dynamical simulations. Currently, the most popular
approach is to represent a small region of the system, namely the active site, by
quantum mechanics (QM), which is embedded in the rest of the system modeled
classically by molecular mechanics (MM).
12–14 Such a combined QM/MM
approach oﬀers both accuracy and computational eﬃciency, and methods
that can treat the entire protein–solvent system with a quantum mechanical
representation are being actively pursued by a number of research groups.
15–17
A major advantage of the latter approach is the possibility to include
polarization and charge transfer eﬀects on the PES.
17–19
The second challenge to theory is to incorporate explicitly nuclear quantum
eﬀects (NQEs) into the time-evolution of macromolecular systems, including
zero-point energy, non-adiabatic coupling and quantum mechanical tunneling.
The inclusion of NQEs is essential in the study of hydrogen transfer processes
and the computation of KIEs.
11 A variety of methods have been developed
to treat NQEs for gas-phase reactions. 20 In principle, these techniques can
be directly extended to condensed-phase systems; however, the size and complexity of enzyme systems make it intractable computationally. One approach
successfully applied to enzymatic reactions is the ensemble-averaged variational
transition state theory with QM/MM sampling (EA-VTST-QM/MM).
10,11 This
technique extends the VTST method developed for gas-phase reactions into
condensed-phase reactions through ensemble averaging over conﬁgurations
sampled through dynamics simulations. In this approach, a frozen-bath
approximation is used to model the instantaneous protein–solvent eﬀects on
the reaction rate in the active site.
10,11,21 In another work, a grid-based hybrid
approach was used to model NQEs by numerically solving the vibrational
wavefunction of the quantized nucleus. 22 The latter method has only been used
to quantize one particle because of increased computational costs. 23
The discretized Feynman path integral method has been used in a variety of
applications in solution and biological systems. 24–32 In principle, centroid path
integral simulations 26,27,30 can be directly used to determine KIEs by carrying
out two separate calculations for the heavy and light isotope, respectively.
However, the convergence of the computed free energy barrier from dynamics
simulations has much greater ﬂuctuations than the accuracy needed to
determine KIEs, especially for heavy atom and secondary eﬀects. To this end,
Molecular Dynamics Simulation of Kinetic Isotope Eﬀects 531
 17/11/2013 09:30:01.
Published on 18 October 2013 on http://pubs.rsc.org | doi:10.1039/9781849737753-00530
View Online

## Page 552

we have developed a free energy perturbation technique, called PI-FEP/UM, 33
by perturbing the atomic mass from light to heavy isotopes in a bisection path
integral sampling scheme, 8,34–36 and this has tremendously reduced the
statistical uncertainty in the computed KIEs. It oﬀers an eﬃcient and general
approach for treating NQEs in condensed-phase simulations and the method
has been applied to a number of reactions in solution and enzyme-catalyzed
processes.
8,9,37–41
The PI-FEP/UM method involves two computational steps. 33 This procedure was ﬁrst used in the work by Sprik and co-workers to estimate NQEs in
a lattice of hard spheres,
25 and was explored by Warshel and co-workers to
model enzymatic reactions. 42–45 First, classical molecular dynamics simulation
is carried out to obtain the potential of mean force along the reaction
coordinate for a given reaction. Then, centroid path integral simulations are
performed to determine the NQEs. The most signiﬁcant feature of these studies
is that classical and quantum simulations are fully separated, making it
particularly attractive and eﬃcient for enzymatic reactions. The special feature
in the PI-FEP/UM method is to use a free energy perturbation scheme to
obtain accurate KIEs for chemical reactions by changing the atomic mass from
one isotope into another in path integral sampling.
33
Although the methods discussed above to incorporate electronic structure
theory to model the PES and NQEs to determine the rate process are based on
very diﬀerent theories, a common strategy is to estimate approximately the
quantum mechanical rate constant by introducing a quantum correction factor
to bridge with the classical transition state theory:
k /C17k
qm ¼ g /C1kTST ð21:1Þ
where kTST is the transition state theory rate constant and g ¼ kG is the
generalized transmission coeﬃcient, 20 which includes the classical dynamic
recrossing factor, G, and the quantum correction factor k, which is deﬁned as
follows:
k ¼ kqm
kTST
¼ e/C0 bðDF a
qm /C0 DF a
TST Þ ð21:2Þ
In eqn (21.2), b ¼ 1=kBT with kB being Boltzmann’s constant and T the temperature, and DF a
qm and DF a
TST are the quantum and classical free energy of
activation, respectively. Here we have implicitly assumed that the classical and
quantum G factors are identical. The diﬀerent methods applied to enzymatic
reactions to incorporate nuclear quantum eﬀects diﬀer in the speciﬁc
approximations to estimate the free energy diﬀerence in eqn (21.2).
In this chapter I ﬁrst summarize the theoretical methods developed in my
group for enzyme kinetics modeling, which include both electronic and nuclear
quantum eﬀects. Then the methods are illustrated through applications to
three energy systems, namely, alanine racemase, nitroalkane oxidase and
dihydrofolate reductase.

21.2 Methods
21.2.1 Quantum Transition State Theory and Path Integral
Simulations

To enhance convergence of free-particle sampling in centroid path integral
simulations, a bisection sampling technique was used for a ring of beads by
extending the original approach of Pollock and Ceperley for free particle

## Page 555

sampling in which the initial and ﬁnal beads are not connected. 35,36 Since the
free-particle distribution is known exactly at a given temperature, each ringbeads distribution can be directly generated according to this distribution.
36
Furthermore, in this construction, each new conﬁguration is created independently, starting from a single initial bead position, allowing the new
conﬁguration to move into a completely diﬀerent region of conﬁgurational
space. This latter point is especially important in achieving convergence by
avoiding being trapped in a local region of the classical potential in the path
integral sampling. This method has been thoroughly tested in a number of
applications.
8,33,34,37,52
21.2.2 Potential Energy Surface
The potential energy function describes the energetic change as a function
of the variations in atomic coordinates, including thermal ﬂuctuations and
rearrangements of the chemical bonds. The accuracy of the potential energy
function used to carry out molecular dynamics simulations directly aﬀects
the reliability of the computed w
qmð/C22zÞ.10,12,53,54 To this end, we have developed
a combined quantum mechanical and molecular mechanical (QM/MM)
approach to model the potential energy surface for chemical and enzymatic
reactions in solution.
In combined QM/MM methods, the system is partitioned into a QM region,
consisting of the substrate and residues directly participating in the chemical
process, and an MM region, including the rest of the system.
12,14,55 The QM/
MM potential is given by:
Utot ¼ CðSÞhj Ho

(MM) potential energy for the MM region, and Hqm=mmðSÞ is the interaction
Hamiltonian between the two regions. The wave function CðSÞ of the QM
subsystem is optimized to minimize the energy of the electronic Hamiltonian
H

energy terms purely from the QM region and MM region, plus the interaction
energy between the two regions:
55
Utot ¼ Eo

Eo

and DEqm=mmðSÞ is the interaction energy corresponding to the energy change

The QM/MM PES is a preferred choice for large molecular systems such as a
fully solvated protein because the method combines both the generality of
quantum mechanical methods for treating chemical processes and the computational eﬃciency of a force ﬁeld for large systems.
56 This is important because the dynamic ﬂuctuations of the enzyme and aqueous system have a major
impact on the polarization of the species involved in the chemical reaction
which, in turn, aﬀects the chemical reactivity.
14,55,57
21.2.3 Integrated Path Integral-Free Energy Perturbation/
Umbrella Sampling (PI-FEP/UM) Method for
Computing KIEs
A straightforward procedure to determine kinetic isotope eﬀects for enzymatic
reactions is to carry out separate centroid path integral simulations of diﬀerent
isotopic reactions. However, the statistical errors associated with each separate
calculation are typically greater than the free energy diﬀerence for diﬀerent
isotopic reactions, especially on secondary and heavy isotope eﬀects, and they
are too large to be useful for accurately estimating KIEs.
The centroid path integral method described above enable us to conveniently
determine KIEs by directly computing the ratio of the quantum partition
functions for two diﬀerent isotopes through free energy perturbation (FEP)
theory.
33 The use of ‘mass’ perturbation in free-particle bisection sampling
scheme results in a major improvement in computation accuracy for KIE
calculations such that secondary kinetic isotope eﬀects and heavy atom isotope
eﬀects can be reliably obtained.
33,58 The PI-FEP/UM method is the only
practical approach to yield computed secondary KIEs suﬃciently accurate to
be compared with experiments. 39
The light-to-heavy kinetic isotope eﬀect, kL=kH , is rewritten in terms of the
ratio of the partial partition functions at the centroid reactant and transition
state, and is given by:
33
KIE ¼ kL
kH ¼

partition functions between two isotopes.
