In eqn (21.18), we obtain the free energy (inner average) diﬀerence between
the heavy and light isotopes by carrying out the bisection path integral sampling with the light atom and then perturbing the heavy isotope positions according to eqn (21.16). The free energy diﬀerence (the ratio of the partition
function) between the light and heavy isotope ensembles is weighted by a
Boltzmann factor for each quantized conﬁguration.
33 Consequently, only a
simulation with just one isotope is needed to obtain KIEs. This is in contrast to
other approaches reported in the literature using centroid path integral
simulations.

21.3 Illustrative Examples
21.3.1 Alanine Racemase
Alanine racemase (AlaR) catalyzes the interconversion of L-a n d D-alanine, the
latter being an essential component in the peptidoglycan layer of the bacterial
cell wall. The chemical transformation is illustrated in Scheme 21.1, which has
been modeled by a combined QM/MM potential in molecular dynamics
simulations.
Employing the PI-FEP/UM method, Major and co-workers computed the
primary KIEs for both the forward and reverse processes.
38,52 In these calculations, the semi-empirical AM1 formalism was used to describe the active site,
which includes the pyridoxal 5
0-phosphate (PLP) cofactor bound substrate and
the acid and base residues Lys39 and Tyr265 0, where the prime indicates a
residue from the second subunit of the dimeric enzyme. In this study, the AM1
model was re-parametrized to model the AlaR-catalyzed racemization, yielding
a highly accurate Hamiltonian that is comparable to mPW1PW91/6311þþ G(3df,2p) results. Stochastic boundary molecular dynamics simulations
were carried out for a system of 30 A ˚ sphere about the center of the active site
and a series of umbrella sampling simulations were performed, cumulating a
total of 24 ns statistical sampling (with 1 fs integration step) to yield the classical potential of mean force [the outer average in eqn (21.10)]. Then the
transferring proton, the donor and acceptor heavy atoms for each process are
quantized by centroid path integral with 32 beads for each particle. About
15 000 conﬁgurations saved from these trajectories in regions corresponding to
the Michaelis complex reactant state, transition state and product state were
extracted, each of which was subjected to 10 bisection free-particle sampling to
yield the centroid PI quantum corrections to the classical free energy proﬁle.
The quantum mechanical PMFs displayed in Figure 21.1 incorporate the
quantum mechanical corrections to the classical PMF from molecular dynamics simulations. For the
L-D alanine racemization in AlaR, the ﬁrst proton abstraction step by Tyr265 0 is rate limiting, and thus the observed rate
constant is directly related to this reaction step, kobs ¼ k1, and the KIE is
computed using this rate constant. Figure 21.1 shows that inclusion of
NQEs in the computed classical PMF lowers the free energy barrier by 2.6 and
1.7 kcal mol/C0 1 for proton and deuteron transfer to Tyr265 0 phenolate ion in

## Page 559

AlaR. This leads to a computed intrinsic KIE of 4.2 for the a-proton
abstraction in the L-D alanine conversion. However, the computational result
is much greater than the experimental value of 1.9. 59 The origin of this
discrepancy is still unclear; it may be a result of one or a combination of factors,
including the complexity involved in the analysis of experimental kinetic data,
the possibility that the experimental KIE was not exactly the intrinsic value,
and computational uncertainty.
21.3.2 Nitroalkane Oxidase
The ﬂavoenzyme nitroalkane oxidase (NAO) catalyzes the conversion of
nitroalkanes to nitrite and aldehydes or ketones.
60 The a-proton abstraction of
the small substrate nitroethane by Asp402 is rate limiting. The enzymatic rate
enhancement is 10
9 over the uncatalyzed reaction in water. 61 Of particular
interest is the ﬁnding that the deuterium KIEs are noticeably greater for the
enzymatic reaction (9.2) than that in water (7.8),
61,62 suggesting that tunneling
contribution may play a greater role in the enzyme than that in water. The
proton transfer reaction between nitroethane and Asp402 catalyzed by
nitroalkane oxidase provides an excellent opportunity for a comparative study
because the enzymatic process can be directly modeled by the reaction of
nitroethane and acetate ion in water.
The PI-FEP/UM simulation technique was used to model the NQEs.
33
Moreover, the PES was described by a combined QM/MM method. 37,55

Thus, both the electronic structure of the reacting system and the nuclear dynamics are treated quantum mechanically. The crystal structure 2C12 with the
inhibitor spermine was used to construct the Michaelis complex (MC) model
through a combined minimization and molecular dynamics simulation technique.
63 As it turns out, the computational MC structure was in excellent accord with a parallel study of the D402N-nitroethane X-ray structure. 39
The centroid PMFs from PI-FEP/UM simulations for the nitroethane
deprotonation reaction in NAO and in water (Scheme 21.2) are shown in
Figure 21.2. The reaction coordinate is deﬁned as the diﬀerence between the
breaking (donor-proton) and forming (acceptor-proton) bond distances
(Scheme 21.2). The computed free energies of activation are 15.9 and
24.4 kcal mol
/C0 1 for the enzymatic and the uncatalyzed proton transfer reaction
in water, respectively, in accord with experimental results (14.0 and
24.8 kcal mol/C0 1).61

540 Chapter 21
 17/11/2013 09:30:01.

View Online

## Page 561

The computed primary and secondary KIEs for the nitroethane deprotonation reaction in water and in the enzyme are listed in Table 21.1, along with
the total KIEs determined for the perdeuterated substrate nitroethane at the C
a
position.61 In PI-FEP/UM simulations, the computed nuclear quantum eﬀects
include both zero-point energies and nuclear tunneling and their contributions
to the computed KIEs are not separable. 33 Thus, it is useful to use semiclassical methods20 to estimate the tunneling transmission coeﬃcient to gain an
understanding of the origin of NQEs in NAO catalysis. Consequently, we have
utilized the ensemble-averaged variational transition state theory with multidimensional tunneling (EA-VTST/MT) approach
10,11,21 to separate tunneling
from the overall quantum eﬀects (Table 21.1).
The results in Figure 21.2 and Table 21.1 present overwhelming support that
NQEs make greater contributions to the enzymatic reaction in nitroalkane
oxidase than the uncatalyzed process in water. First, there are greater NQEs to
lower the classical barrier of the enzymatic process than that of the uncatalyzed
model reaction. Second, the diﬀerence in quantum eﬀects between proton and
deuteron transfer is more signiﬁcant for the enzymatic reaction than the
uncatalyzed one. Third, both experiment and computation show enhanced
primary KIEs in the enzyme over that in water. Fourth, the mixed Swain–
Schaad exponent for the enzymatic reaction is greater than the semi-classical
limit without tunneling and it is increased for the enzyme process. Finally, the
tunneling transmission coeﬃcient was found to be about three times greater for
the enzyme reaction than the model reaction in water from EA-VTST/MT
analysis, an entirely diﬀerent approach from the PI-FEP/UM simulation
method. Analyses of the tunneling paths in EA-VTST reveal that the origin of
the diﬀerence may be attributed to a narrowing eﬀect in the eﬀective potentials
for tunneling in the enzyme than those in aqueous solution.
39 These studies
demonstrate that diﬀerential quantum tunneling contributions are utilized in
certain enzymatic catalysis as illustrated by nitroalkane oxidase.

## Page 562

21.3.3 Dihydrofolate Reductase
Dihydrofolate reductase (DHFR) is a small, ﬂexible protein that catalyzes the
nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide phosphate (NADPH) dependent reduction
of 7,8-dihydrofolate to 5,6,7,8-tetrahydrofolate.
6,64,65 Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) experiments established that conformational changes of ﬂexible
loops spread out of the protein play a critical role in modulating ligand speciﬁcity and catalytic turnover. 6,65–68 Intriguingly, single and double mutations
at the M42 and G121 sites and other locations that are remote from the
catalytic center can have profound, non-additive eﬀects on reaction rate, activation parameters and the temperature dependence of KIEs.
69–78 Computational studies helped identify networks of interatomic distance variations along
the reaction coordinate and distal mutations can induce structural perturbations that alter these conformational change.
76,77,79–82 Other studies have
probed various aspects of DHFR catalysis. 83–88 We found that restriction in
conformational ﬂexibility of the M20 loop at the transition state in the M42W/
G121V double mutant is responsible for the impaired catalytic activity by increasing the entropic barrier.
To model the DHFR reaction, we developed a combined QM/MM approach,
14,55 employing the semi-empirical AM1 Hamiltonian along with a
simple valence bond correction term speciﬁcally designed for the DHFR system.
82 Using the same QM/MM methodology as in our earlier studies, 53,57,89,90
we obtained the potentials of mean force for the hydride transfer from NADPH
to DHF catalyzed by wild-type (wt)-DHFR and the M42W/G121V double
mutant (dm) DHFR at 5, 25 and 45 1C (Figure 21.3). In both cases, the free
energy of activation, DG
a , increases with temperature. At 25 1C, the computed
DG a for the hydride transfer is 16.4 kcal mol /C0 1 for wt-DHFR, in agreement
with the experimental value (16.7 kcal mol /C0 1) determined at pH ¼ 9, under
which the hydride transfer step is rate limiting. 71 The activation free energy is
increased by 1.2 kcal mol /C0 1 in the M42W/G121V mutant, somewhat smaller
than the corresponding experimental data (2.3 kcal mol /C0 1). Inclusion of NQEs
reduces the computed barrier by about 3 kcal mol /C0 1 for both wt- and dmDHFR at 25 1C.82,89 The estimated free energies of reaction, DGrxn, are about
–5 and –2 kcal mol /C0 1 for wt- and dm-DHFR, respectively, reﬂecting that there
is a small change in driving force for the hydride transfer due to the M42W/
G121V mutations. This is in accord with experimental data on the M42W/
G121S mutant.
69,80 A similar eﬀect of raising the driving force and a correlation
between free energy barrier and DGrxn for a number of single and double
mutant DHFR enzymes has been noted. 78
Wild-type E. coli DHFR exhibits temperature-independent KIEs for the
hydride transfer step. 91 Intriguingly, the KIEs for the hydride transfer in
M42W/G121V dm-DHFR become steeply temperature-dependent. 71 A large
number of enzymes have been found to show temperature independence of
KIEs in their optimal operating temperature range, but the KIEs are changed
to temperature-dependent outside this temperature range or as a result of
mutations.
92,93 Several studies have proposed that the wild-type enzyme has

evolved to optimize the average donor–acceptor distance for tunneling at the
transition state sampled by the mass-independent thermal activation, giving
rise to temperature-independent KIEs (provided that tunneling is the dominant
contribution to the observed KIEs).

## Page 564

There are two major contributions to the overall NQEs responsible for the
observed KIEs: the change in quantum vibrational free energy, predominantly
zero-point eﬀects; and tunneling.
10,11,53,89 For reactions where tunneling is
dominant, such as the hydrogen atom transfer catalyzed by soybean lipoxygenase,
96 the tunneling mechanism above is quite reasonable. For the hydride
transfer catalyzed by DHFR where the observed intrinsic primary KIEs are
only about 3 both in the wt-DHFR and in mutants, 71,75,91 it would be important for a mechanism to also account for the contribution and change from
vibrational free energy. Using ensemble averaged-transition state theory including semi-classical multidimensional tunneling (EA-VTST/MT),
10,21 we
identiﬁed two general features responsible for the observed temperature independence (or small temperature dependence) of KIEs in wt-DHFR: variation
of the transition state position; and temperature-dependence of the eﬀective
potential for tunneling.
89 In particular, we found that the location of the
transition state for the hydride transfer coordinate is slightly shifted from
/C0 0.205 A˚ at 5 1Ct o /C0 0.165 A˚ at 45 1C (Figure 21.4). 89 As a result, the difference in vibrational free energy between H-transfer and D-transfer is also
increased at higher temperature, resulting in a nearly temperature invariant
Boltzmann factor. In this sense, there are greater NQEs at higher temperatures.
It is interesting to note that the diﬀerence in vibrational free energy is predominantly responsible for the computed H/D KIEs, with a net value of 2.83

and 2.81 (without tunneling) at 5 1C and 45 1C; inclusion of tunneling increases

The temperature dependence of the KIEs for the hydride transfer both in
wt-DHFR and the M42W/G121 dm-DHFR have also been examined using
PI-FEP/UM simulations.
41 Unlike the EA-VTST approach, 89 vibrational free
energy and nuclear tunneling are not separable in path integral simulations, but
the total NQEs as well as KIEs are obtained directly from statistical simulations.
Nuclear quantum eﬀects lower the free energy barrier from classical molecular
dynamics simulations by about 2.8 /C6 0.5 kcal mol
/C0 1 for both wt-DHFR and the
dm-DHFR at 25 1C, in good accord with results (3.2 kcal mol/C0 1)c o m p u t e du s i n g
a completely diﬀerent theoretical approach, 82,89 namely the EA-VTST/MT

the hydride atom was quantized. 97,98 Arrhenius plots of the calculated and

wt-DHFR both in the absolute value of KIEs and in the temperatureindependent behavior. The agreement for the M42W/G121V double mutant is
also good, although the slope of the temperature dependence plot is smaller than
that measured experimentally. Since the average donor–acceptor distances do
not diﬀer between wt-DHFR and the M42W/G121V mutant, suggesting similar
zero-point eﬀects, we attribute the change in temperature-dependence of the
KIEs in the double mutant to an altered potential energy surface for tunneling.

## Page 566

This is consistent with structural analyses and computed change in the entropy of
activation for the wild-type and double mutant DHFR.
21.4 Concluding Remarks
In this chapter, quantum mechanical methods developed for enzyme kinetics
modeling in our group have been presented, including the treatment of the
potential energy surface for reactive system and the incorporation of nuclear
quantum eﬀects in dynamics simulations. Two aspects are emphasized:
(1) The potential energy surface is represented by combined QM/MM
method to describe bond forming and breaking processes.
(2) A free energy perturbation approach in Feynman centroid path integral
simulations has been developed to incorporate nuclear quantum eﬀects.
The latter method, called the PI-FEP/UM approach, allows accurate primary
and secondary kinetic isotope eﬀects to be computed for enzymatic reactions.
These methods are illustrated by applications to three enzyme systems, namely,
the proton abstraction and reprotonation process catalyzed by alanine racemase, the enhanced nuclear quantum eﬀects in nitroalkane oxidase catalysis,
and the temperature (in)dependence of the wild-type and the M42W/G121V
double mutant of dihydrofolate dehydrogenase. These examples show that
incorporation of quantum mechanical eﬀects is essential for enzyme kinetics
simulations and that the methods discussed in this chapter oﬀer a great opportunity to more accurately model the mechanism and free energies of enzymatic reactions.
Acknowledgements
I wish to thank my many co-workers and collaborators for their invaluable
contributions to the project. Their names are listed in the references cited in this
chapter. This work has been supported in part by the National Institutes of
Health (GM46736) and by the National Science Foundation (CHE09-57162).
